cases.title: Belly Pain That Moved to the Right
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening abdominal pain over the past day, now focused in the right lower quadrant.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents with abdominal pain that began roughly 18 hours ago as a vague, dull ache around the umbilicus. Over the last several hours the pain has migrated to the right lower quadrant and become sharp and constant, worsened by movement and by the bumpy car ride to the hospital. He reports nausea with one episode of vomiting and complete loss of appetite, noting he could not finish breakfast. He has had no diarrhea, no dysuria, no hematuria, and no prior similar episodes. His last bowel movement was yesterday and was normal. He denies penile discharge and is sexually active with one female partner using condoms. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no daily medications and has taken only one dose of acetaminophen 500 mg today with minimal relief. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is notable for a father with hypertension. He works as a college student and is otherwise healthy.
cases.exam: The patient lies still on the stretcher and is reluctant to move. Abdomen is flat with normal bowel sounds. There is focal tenderness at McBurney point with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness localized to the right lower quadrant. Rovsing sign is positive, and the psoas sign reproduces his pain. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness, no inguinal hernia, and the testes are normal and nontender bilaterally. There is no flank ecchymosis or periumbilical discoloration. Bowel sounds are present but hypoactive in the right lower quadrant. The remainder of the abdomen is soft and nondistended.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation is most specific for acute appendicitis?
options: Generalized periumbilical aching at onset as the first step today
Migration of pain from the periumbilical region to the right lower quadrant
Low-grade fever of 37.9 °C as the first step today
A single episode of vomiting as the first step today
stages.explanation: Migration of pain from the periumbilical area to the right lower quadrant is the single most discriminating historical feature for appendicitis, reflecting the shift from visceral (T10 referred) to parietal peritoneal irritation as inflammation progresses. Early periumbilical aching (option B) is sensitive but nonspecific, occurring in many causes of early visceral pain. A single episode of vomiting (option D) is common in gastroenteritis, biliary colic, and many other conditions. Low-grade fever (option E) supports an inflammatory process but is nonspecific and often absent early; high fever should actually prompt concern for perforation or an alternative diagnosis. The migratory pattern combined with anorexia and focal RLQ signs raises the pretest probability substantially.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step in this hemodynamically stable young man?
options: Abdominal plain radiographs as the primary diagnosis
Immediate exploratory laparotomy as the primary diagnosis
Reassurance and discharge with outpatient follow-up
CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast plus CBC and urinalysis
stages.explanation: In a stable adult with an equivocal-to-likely appendicitis picture, CT of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast is the most accurate imaging test (sensitivity and specificity >90%) and helps confirm the diagnosis and exclude mimics; basic labs including CBC and urinalysis support the workup and rule out urinary causes. Immediate laparotomy (option C) is excessive when imaging can confirm the diagnosis and guide a laparoscopic approach. Plain radiographs (option D) rarely show an appendicolith and miss most cases. Discharge (option E) is unsafe given classic findings and risks progression to perforation. Note that in children and many young women, ultrasound is preferred first to avoid radiation; in young men, CT remains the standard confirmatory study.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL
Platelets 268 ×10³/µL
Na 139 mEq/L
K 4.1 mEq/L
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
CRP 48 mg/L
Urinalysis: no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no blood
CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated appendix 11 mm with wall thickening, periappendiceal fat stranding, and a small appendicolith; no free air or abscess

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Percutaneous drain placement as initial therapy
ERCP as initial therapy
IV antibiotics alone with planned discharge in 24 hours
Laparoscopic appendectomy with perioperative IV antibiotics
stages.explanation: Laparoscopic appendectomy with perioperative IV antibiotics is the standard of care for uncomplicated acute appendicitis, offering definitive source control, low complication rates, and faster recovery than an open approach. Antibiotics-alone (option B) is an emerging nonoperative option in highly selected patients without an appendicolith, but recurrence rates approach 25-40% within a year and this patient has an appendicolith, which predicts antibiotic failure and complications, making surgery preferred. Percutaneous drainage (option C) is reserved for a contained periappendiceal abscess, which is absent here. ERCP (option E) is a biliary procedure with no role in appendicitis. Prompt appendectomy minimizes the risk of progression to perforation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After an uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy for non-perforated appendicitis, what is the most appropriate postoperative antibiotic plan?
options: No additional postoperative antibiotics
IV antibiotics for a full 7-day inpatient course
A single repeat IV dose only if fever recurs
Oral antibiotics for 7 days after discharge
stages.explanation: For uncomplicated, non-perforated appendicitis, perioperative antibiotics alone are sufficient and no further postoperative antibiotics are needed; continuing them does not reduce infection and promotes resistance and C. difficile risk. A 7-day oral course (option C) or a prolonged IV course (option D) is appropriate only for complicated (perforated or gangrenous) appendicitis with peritonitis or abscess, where 4-7 days of antibiotics are warranted after source control. Reactively dosing only if fever recurs (option E) is not a guideline-based strategy and risks undertreatment. This patient had a non-perforated appendix, so he can be discharged the same or next day with no antibiotics and routine wound care.

cases.diagnosis: Acute uncomplicated appendicitis
pearls: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with anorexia is the most specific history for appendicitis.
An appendicolith on imaging predicts failure of antibiotics-alone therapy, favoring appendectomy.
Uncomplicated, non-perforated appendicitis needs only perioperative antibiotics, not a postoperative course.
CT with contrast is the confirmatory test in adult men; ultrasound is first-line in children and pregnancy.

cases.title: Right-Sided Belly Pain Late in Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening right-sided abdominal pain with nausea at 32 weeks of pregnancy.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation (G2P1) presents with one day of progressively worsening abdominal pain. The pain began near the umbilicus but is now most intense in the right upper and mid-right abdomen rather than the right lower quadrant, which she finds confusing. She has nausea and two episodes of vomiting and has not felt like eating. She reports good fetal movement and denies vaginal bleeding, leakage of fluid, contractions, dysuria, or back pain radiating to the groin. Her first pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. Her current pregnancy has been normal with appropriate weight gain and a recent normal glucose tolerance test. She takes a prenatal vitamin daily and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use drugs. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she notes mild subjective chills today but no diarrhea. Her blood type is O positive and prenatal labs have been unremarkable. She is anxious about the safety of testing for her baby.
cases.exam: The gravid abdomen is consistent with 32 weeks. Fundal height is appropriate. There is tenderness in the right flank and right upper-to-mid abdomen, higher than the classic McBurney point, with mild guarding; rebound is difficult to assess given the gravid uterus. Bowel sounds are present. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness and Murphy sign is negative. The uterus is soft and nontender between palpations, with no palpable contractions. Cervix is closed and long on sterile exam with no bleeding or pooling. Fetal heart tones are reassuring at 148 bpm. There is no flank ecchymosis. Extremities show trace symmetric edema without calf tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Why is the abdominal pain located higher than expected in this pregnant patient?
options: Pregnancy eliminates parietal peritoneal pain signaling
The enlarging gravid uterus displaces the appendix cephalad
The appendix is destroyed and pain is now diffuse
The pain must instead be biliary in origin as the first step today
stages.explanation: As pregnancy advances, the enlarging uterus displaces the appendix upward and laterally, so appendiceal pain may localize to the right mid-abdomen or right upper quadrant rather than the classic right lower quadrant, especially in the third trimester. This anatomic shift is a major reason appendicitis is harder to diagnose and more often perforated in pregnancy. A destroyed appendix with diffuse pain (option B) would suggest perforation with peritonitis, not supported by her focal exam. Pregnancy does not abolish parietal pain (option D). While biliary disease (option E) is on the differential, the cephalad appendiceal displacement directly explains the atypical location and should not be dismissed; her negative Murphy sign argues against cholecystitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study to evaluate for appendicitis in this pregnant patient?
options: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as the primary diagnosis
Graded-compression abdominal ultrasound of the right lower/mid abdomen
Plain abdominal radiograph as the primary diagnosis
Diagnostic laparoscopy before any imaging as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Graded-compression ultrasound is the preferred first-line imaging in pregnancy because it avoids ionizing radiation and can identify a noncompressible, dilated appendix. When ultrasound is nondiagnostic, MRI without gadolinium is the next study of choice and is highly accurate. CT (option B) involves ionizing radiation and is generally avoided in pregnancy when MRI is available, though it is acceptable if MRI is unavailable and diagnosis is critical. Plain radiographs (option D) are insensitive and add radiation without benefit. Proceeding directly to diagnostic laparoscopy (option E) before any imaging is overly invasive when noninvasive tests can confirm or refute the diagnosis; surgery is reserved for confirmed or high-probability cases.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.8 ×10³/µL (note: mild leukocytosis is physiologic in pregnancy)
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Platelets 230 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
AST 22 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 180 U/L (elevated, placental source)
Lipase 38 U/L
Urinalysis: no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, trace ketones
Graded-compression ultrasound: nondiagnostic, appendix not visualized
MRI abdomen/pelvis without contrast: dilated fluid-filled appendix 9 mm with periappendiceal edema; no abscess

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the MRI findings, what is the most appropriate management?
options: IV antibiotics alone and avoid surgery until after delivery
Immediate cesarean section followed by appendectomy
Laparoscopic appendectomy with obstetric monitoring
Discharge with outpatient surgical follow-up in one week
stages.explanation: Confirmed appendicitis in pregnancy requires prompt appendectomy regardless of trimester, because delay markedly increases perforation, which raises fetal loss and maternal morbidity. Laparoscopic appendectomy is safe and preferred in pregnancy in experienced hands, with perioperative obstetric monitoring of fetal heart tones and uterine activity. Antibiotics-alone (option B) risks progression and perforation and is not standard for confirmed appendicitis in pregnancy. Cesarean section (option C) is not indicated solely to access the appendix at 32 weeks with a reassuring fetus; delivery is driven by obstetric indications. Discharge (option E) is dangerous given confirmed appendicitis. Perioperative tocolysis is not given routinely, and corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity may be considered given the preterm gestation if delivery becomes likely.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why does appendicitis in pregnancy carry a higher rate of perforation and fetal loss than in nonpregnant patients?
options: Diagnostic delay from atypical pain location
Pregnancy accelerates antibiotic clearance, making medical therapy fail
The appendix is structurally weaker during gestation in this presentation
Fetal demand depletes maternal white blood cells as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The leading driver of worse outcomes is diagnostic delay: the cephalad-displaced appendix shifts pain location, and nausea, vomiting, and mild leukocytosis are common in normal pregnancy, all of which blunt suspicion and postpone surgery, allowing progression to perforation. Perforation increases the risk of preterm labor and fetal loss several-fold. Pregnancy does not meaningfully accelerate antibiotic clearance in a way that causes appendicitis treatment to fail (option B). The appendix is not structurally weakened by gestation (option D). Maternal leukocyte counts actually rise physiologically in pregnancy rather than being depleted by the fetus (option E); the resulting overlap with infection is part of why diagnosis is delayed. Early imaging with ultrasound or MRI and a low threshold for surgery mitigate this risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate perioperative obstetric management during her appendectomy at 32 weeks?
options: Intraoperative and postoperative fetal heart-rate and uterine-activity monitoring, with a course of antenatal corticosteroids considered for fetal lung maturity
Withhold all analgesia postoperatively to avoid fetal harm with appropriate follow-up
Routine emergent cesarean delivery at the time of appendectomy with appropriate follow-up
Routine prophylactic tocolytics for all pregnant surgical patients with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: At a viable, preterm gestation, the appropriate plan is perioperative fetal heart-rate and uterine-activity monitoring to detect distress or preterm labor, plus consideration of a course of antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity given the risk of preterm delivery. Routine cesarean at the time of appendectomy (option B) is not indicated when the fetus is reassuring; delivery is reserved for obstetric indications. Routine prophylactic tocolytics (option D) are not given to all pregnant surgical patients and may be considered only for documented preterm contractions after weighing risks. Withholding analgesia (option E) is wrong and harmful; untreated maternal pain and stress are detrimental, and pregnancy-appropriate analgesia should be provided. Coordinated surgical and obstetric care optimizes both maternal and fetal outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Acute appendicitis in pregnancy (third trimester, cephalad-displaced appendix)
pearls: The gravid uterus displaces the appendix cephalad, so third-trimester appendicitis often presents as RUQ or right flank pain.
Ultrasound is first-line in pregnancy; MRI without contrast is the next step when ultrasound is nondiagnostic.
Mild leukocytosis and nausea are physiologic in pregnancy, contributing to diagnostic delay and higher perforation rates.
Confirmed appendicitis in pregnancy warrants prompt appendectomy in any trimester; delay increases fetal loss.

cases.title: Right Upper Belly Pain After a Fatty Meal
cases.chiefComplaint: Constant right upper quadrant pain with fever since last night.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with steady right upper quadrant pain that began about 14 hours ago, several hours after a large fried-chicken dinner. She has had similar but briefer episodes over the past few months, usually after fatty meals, that resolved within an hour or two; this episode has not let up and feels worse. The pain radiates to the right scapula and is associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. She reports subjective fevers and chills today. She denies jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Past medical history includes obesity and two prior pregnancies. Her only medication is a multivitamin; she takes no oral contraceptives currently. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine occasionally. Family history includes a mother who had her gallbladder removed. She works as a teacher. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no diarrhea and no urinary symptoms.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable. Sclerae are anicteric. The abdomen is soft but markedly tender in the right upper quadrant. Murphy sign is positive: she abruptly halts inspiration during deep right subcostal palpation. There is voluntary guarding over the right upper quadrant but no rebound elsewhere and no diffuse rigidity. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no palpable mass, no costovertebral angle tenderness, and no peripheral edema. The remainder of the abdomen is nontender. Skin shows no jaundice and no spider angiomata.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding best distinguishes acute cholecystitis from simple biliary colic in this patient?
options: Nausea and vomiting as the first step today
A history of pain after fatty meals
Fever with a positive Murphy sign
Pain that radiates to the scapula in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Acute cholecystitis is distinguished from biliary colic by signs of gallbladder inflammation: persistent pain lasting more than several hours, fever, leukocytosis, and a positive Murphy sign indicating peritoneal irritation of the inflamed gallbladder. Biliary colic, by contrast, is self-limited (usually under a few hours) and lacks fever and inflammatory signs. Scapular radiation (option B) and pain after fatty meals (option C) occur in both biliary colic and cholecystitis and do not differentiate them. Nausea and vomiting (option E) are nonspecific and accompany both conditions. The combination of a sustained attack, fever, and a positive Murphy sign strongly points to acute cholecystitis rather than uncomplicated biliary colic.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line imaging study?
options: CT abdomen with contrast
MRCP as the primary diagnosis
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
HIDA scan as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the first-line study for suspected acute cholecystitis: it is rapid, radiation-free, and identifies gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign. CT (option C) is less sensitive for stones and is reserved for unclear cases or complications. MRCP (option D) excels at evaluating the bile ducts for choledocholithiasis but is not the initial test for cholecystitis. A HIDA scan (option E) is the most specific test, showing nonvisualization of the gallbladder due to cystic duct obstruction, but it is slower and typically reserved for cases where ultrasound is equivocal. Ultrasound first is the standard, cost-effective approach.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL
AST 30 U/L, ALT 28 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 96 U/L
Lipase 35 U/L
CRP 62 mg/L
RUQ ultrasound: multiple gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening to 5 mm, pericholecystic fluid, and a positive sonographic Murphy sign; common bile duct 4 mm (normal caliber)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient with acute cholecystitis?
options: ERCP with stone extraction as initial therapy
Outpatient ursodeoxycholic acid to dissolve the stones as initial therapy
Elective cholecystectomy scheduled in 6 weeks after the inflammation resolves
IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics,
stages.explanation: The standard of care for acute calculous cholecystitis is supportive care (IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics) followed by early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission, ideally within 72 hours of symptom onset, which lowers complications and overall length of stay compared with delayed surgery. Ursodeoxycholic acid (option B) dissolves only small cholesterol stones slowly and has no role in acute cholecystitis. Interval cholecystectomy weeks later (option D) is an older strategy now reserved for patients who present too late or are too unstable for early surgery. ERCP (option E) treats bile duct stones, but this patient has a normal-caliber CBD (4 mm) and normal bilirubin, so there is no evidence of choledocholithiasis requiring ERCP.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If this patient were instead frail, septic, and a poor surgical candidate with acute cholecystitis, what is the most appropriate temporizing intervention?
options: Immediate open cholecystectomy under general anesthesia
Long-term oral antibiotic suppression alone
ERCP with sphincterotomy based on current risk
Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube drainage
stages.explanation: For patients who are too unstable or high-risk for surgery, percutaneous cholecystostomy provides source control by draining the infected, obstructed gallbladder and can be a bridge to later cholecystectomy once they recover. Immediate open cholecystectomy (option C) under general anesthesia carries unacceptable risk in a frail, septic patient and is not the preferred temporizing measure. Antibiotics alone (option D) do not relieve the underlying obstruction and risk progression to gangrene or perforation. ERCP with sphincterotomy (option E) addresses bile duct obstruction, not cystic duct obstruction causing cholecystitis, so it does not decompress the gallbladder. Cholecystostomy buys time for the patient to stabilize before definitive surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Acute calculous cholecystitis
pearls: Persistent pain >6 hours, fever, leukocytosis, and a positive Murphy sign distinguish cholecystitis from biliary colic.
RUQ ultrasound is first-line; wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, and sonographic Murphy sign confirm it.
Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the index admission (ideally <72 h) is the definitive treatment.
Percutaneous cholecystostomy is the temporizing option for septic patients unfit for surgery.

cases.title: Fever, Yellow Eyes, and Confusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever, right upper quadrant pain, and yellowing of the eyes for two days, now increasingly confused.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man is brought to the ED by his daughter for two days of fevers with shaking chills, right upper quadrant pain, and yellow eyes. Today he has become confused and lethargic, prompting the visit. He had an episode of gallstone-related pain a few months ago but declined surgery. He denies recent travel, sick contacts, or new medications. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cholelithiasis. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks one beer most evenings and quit smoking ten years ago. Family history is noncontributory. According to his daughter he has eaten little for two days and his urine has turned dark while his stools appear pale. On arrival he is oriented only to person and is intermittently drowsy. There is no history of prior biliary instrumentation, and he has had no abdominal surgeries.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, jaundiced, and lethargic but arousable, oriented to person only. Scleral icterus is evident. The abdomen is soft with tenderness in the right upper quadrant; Murphy sign is equivocal and there is no rebound or rigidity. There is no palpable gallbladder and no shifting dullness. Skin is warm with delayed capillary refill. He has no asterixis appreciated, no spider angiomata, and no peripheral edema. Mucous membranes are dry. Heart is tachycardic and regular without murmurs; lungs are clear. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This patient's fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, plus hypotension and confusion correspond to which classic clinical pattern?
options: Beck triad as the first step today
Charcot triad only
Reynolds pentad
Cushing triad in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Charcot triad (fever, RUQ pain, jaundice) indicates acute cholangitis. When hypotension (septic shock) and altered mental status are added, the picture becomes Reynolds pentad, signifying severe, suppurative cholangitis and a surgical emergency requiring urgent biliary decompression. Choosing Charcot triad alone (option B) understates the severity by omitting the shock and confusion now present. Cushing triad (option D) refers to hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations from raised intracranial pressure and is unrelated. Beck triad (option E) describes hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension in cardiac tamponade. Recognizing Reynolds pentad mandates immediate resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and emergent drainage rather than a routine workup.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the first hour?
options: CT head before any other intervention as initial therapy
Emergent laparoscopic cholecystectomy as initial therapy
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Oral antibiotics and admission for observation
stages.explanation: The immediate priorities in cholangitis with septic shock are hemodynamic resuscitation with IV fluids and prompt broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes, followed by urgent biliary decompression. Emergent cholecystectomy (option C) does not relieve the obstructed common bile duct and is inappropriate in an unstable, septic patient; the gallbladder is not the locus of obstruction in cholangitis. Oral antibiotics with observation (option D) is dangerously inadequate for a patient in shock. CT head first (option E) misattributes the confusion to a neurologic cause when the altered mentation is part of the septic pentad; obtaining it first would delay life-saving resuscitation and decompression. Stabilize and start antibiotics immediately while arranging decompression.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Total bilirubin 6.8 mg/dL (direct 5.1 mg/dL)
Alkaline phosphatase 410 U/L
AST 142 U/L, ALT 156 U/L
Lipase 48 U/L
Lactate 3.6 mmol/L
Creatinine 1.7 mg/dL
INR 1.4
RUQ ultrasound: dilated common bile duct to 12 mm with an obstructing distal stone; gallstones present, gallbladder wall not thickened

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After resuscitation and antibiotics, what is the most appropriate definitive intervention for biliary decompression?
options: Immediate open common bile duct exploration in this setting
Continue antibiotics alone and reassess in 48 hours
Urgent ERCP with sphincterotomy
Percutaneous cholecystostomy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Urgent ERCP is the treatment of choice for acute cholangitis from choledocholithiasis: it both confirms the obstruction and relieves it via sphincterotomy with stone extraction or stent placement, providing biliary decompression. In severe cholangitis decompression should occur urgently, typically within 24 hours and as soon as the patient is stabilized. Antibiotics alone (option B) fail to relieve the obstruction and risk ongoing sepsis and death. Open CBD exploration (option D) is reserved for cases where ERCP fails or is unavailable, given its higher morbidity in a septic patient. Percutaneous cholecystostomy (option E) drains the gallbladder, not the obstructed bile duct, and does not address the cholangitis; percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage would be the alternative if ERCP is not feasible.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful ERCP and clinical recovery, what is the recommended definitive management of his gallstones?
options: No further treatment; ERCP cured the problem with appropriate follow-up
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission or shortly after recovery
Lifelong ursodeoxycholic acid with appropriate follow-up
Repeat ERCP every 6 months indefinitely with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: ERCP relieves the duct obstruction but leaves the stone-filled gallbladder, the source of further stones and recurrent biliary events. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is recommended after recovery, ideally during the same hospitalization or soon afterward, to prevent recurrent cholangitis, cholecystitis, and choledocholithiasis. Doing nothing (option B) ignores the persistent stone source and risks recurrence. Ursodeoxycholic acid (option C) is not effective definitive therapy for symptomatic gallstone disease in this setting. Indefinite repeat ERCP (option E) is not a substitute for removing the gallbladder and exposes the patient to repeated procedural risk. Cholecystectomy after the acute illness resolves is the standard plan, with timing individualized to the patient's recovery and operative fitness.

cases.diagnosis: Acute ascending cholangitis (Reynolds pentad) due to choledocholithiasis
pearls: Charcot triad (fever, RUQ pain, jaundice) plus hypotension and confusion equals Reynolds pentad and severe cholangitis.
Initial management is fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics, followed by urgent biliary decompression.
ERCP with sphincterotomy is the decompression method of choice; PTBD is the backup if ERCP fails.
After recovery, perform cholecystectomy to remove the stone source and prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Yellow Skin and Dark Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of yellowing skin, dark urine, and intermittent right upper quadrant pain.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents with four days of progressive yellowing of her skin and eyes, dark cola-colored urine, and pale stools. She has had intermittent right upper quadrant and epigastric pain over the past two weeks, often after meals, with some episodes radiating to the back. The pain is colicky and waxes and wanes rather than being constant. She denies fevers, chills, rigors, or confusion. She has had no weight loss, no pruritus until recently, and no prior jaundice. Past medical history includes obesity and hypothyroidism. Medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; she takes no other drugs and has no known allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and uses no herbal supplements. Family history includes gallstones in her sister. She has never had abdominal surgery or biliary procedures. On review of systems she notes mild nausea but no vomiting and no diarrhea. She is concerned because her coworker noticed her yellow eyes.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and afebrile. There is obvious scleral icterus and mild jaundice of the skin. The abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium; Murphy sign is negative. There is no rebound, guarding, or rigidity, and no palpable mass or hepatomegaly. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no asterixis and no spider angiomata or palmar erythema. There are a few excoriations on the forearms from scratching. No peripheral edema and no costovertebral angle tenderness. The gallbladder is not palpable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and an elevated direct bilirubin best indicates which type of process?
options: Gilbert syndrome as the first step today
Isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from a hemolytic anemia
Prehepatic (hemolytic) jaundice as the first step today
Obstructive hyperbilirubinemia
stages.explanation: Dark urine, pale (acholic) stools, and a predominantly direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia indicate obstruction to bile flow: conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and spills into urine (darkening it) while failing to reach the gut (lightening stools). This pattern fits choledocholithiasis obstructing the common bile duct. Prehepatic/hemolytic jaundice (option B) and isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (option E) raise indirect bilirubin and do not darken urine, because unconjugated bilirubin is not water-soluble. Gilbert syndrome (option D) causes mild, intermittent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, typically without dark urine or pale stools. The conjugated, obstructive pattern combined with her biliary-type pain points toward a stone in the bile duct.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Right upper quadrant ultrasound
CT of the head
Diagnostic ERCP
Tagged red blood cell scan
stages.explanation: Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the appropriate first study: it assesses for gallstones, measures the common bile duct caliber, and looks for ductal dilation suggesting obstruction, all without radiation. ERCP (option C) is therapeutic and carries risks (pancreatitis, perforation, bleeding) and should not be used purely diagnostically when noninvasive tests can stratify risk; MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound is preferred when the diagnosis is uncertain. CT of the head (option D) is irrelevant to a jaundiced, neurologically intact patient. A tagged red cell scan (option E) is used to localize GI bleeding, not biliary obstruction. Ultrasound first guides whether further ductal imaging or direct intervention is warranted.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 5.2 mg/dL (direct 4.0 mg/dL)
Alkaline phosphatase 360 U/L
GGT 290 U/L
AST 110 U/L, ALT 130 U/L
Lipase 40 U/L
RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis with a dilated common bile duct measuring 9 mm; a stone is suspected in the distal CBD; no pericholecystic fluid and normal gallbladder wall

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: With a dilated CBD, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and a suspected ductal stone, this patient is at HIGH probability of choledocholithiasis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Observation with repeat liver enzymes in two weeks in this setting
Proceed directly to laparoscopic cholecystectomy without addressing the duct
ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
MRCP to further confirm before any intervention in this setting
stages.explanation: With high-probability predictors of choledocholithiasis (a CBD stone seen on ultrasound, a dilated CBD, and a markedly elevated bilirubin), ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is the appropriate next step, clearing the duct. MRCP (option B) is reserved for intermediate-probability patients to confirm the need for ERCP and would add delay here without changing management. Proceeding to cholecystectomy without clearing the duct (option D) leaves the obstructing stone and risks ongoing obstruction or cholangitis. Observation (option E) is unsafe with active obstruction and rising enzymes. In high-probability cases, ERCP first to clear the duct, then cholecystectomy, is the standard sequence.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful ERCP clears the duct stone, what is the recommended timing of cholecystectomy?
options: No cholecystectomy is needed once the duct is cleared with appropriate follow-up
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission or within the same hospitalization
Cholecystectomy only if she develops another episode of jaundice based on current risk
Wait at least 6 months before any surgery with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After ERCP clears a ductal stone in a patient with gallstones, early laparoscopic cholecystectomy, ideally during the same admission, is recommended to remove the gallbladder reservoir of stones and prevent recurrent biliary events, including recurrent choledocholithiasis and cholangitis. Omitting cholecystectomy (option B) leaves a high recurrence risk because stones can pass again from the gallbladder. Waiting six months (option D) needlessly exposes the patient to interval complications. A wait-and-see approach until another jaundice episode (option E) is inferior because recurrent stones may cause cholecystitis, pancreatitis, or cholangitis. Same-admission cholecystectomy after duct clearance is the guideline-endorsed strategy for fit patients.

cases.diagnosis: Choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) with obstructive jaundice
pearls: Dark urine, pale stools, and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia signal obstructive (post-hepatic) jaundice.
RUQ ultrasound showing a dilated CBD (>6-7 mm) plus a visualized stone marks high probability of choledocholithiasis.
High-probability patients go straight to ERCP; intermediate-probability patients get MRCP or EUS first.
Clear the duct with ERCP, then perform early laparoscopic cholecystectomy, ideally during the same admission.

cases.title: Crampy Belly Pain and Vomiting in an Older Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, distension, and vomiting.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman presents with three days of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, progressive abdominal distension, and bilious vomiting. The pain comes in waves and has been waxing and waning, which she initially attributed to constipation. She has not passed gas or stool for over a day. She recalls episodes of right upper quadrant pain over the past few weeks that she ignored. She denies fever today but had chills earlier in the week. Past medical history includes gallstones (never treated), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and osteoarthritis. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and acetaminophen as needed. She has no known drug allergies and, importantly, has never had abdominal surgery. She lives alone, does not smoke, and drinks no alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she reports decreased appetite and mild dehydration. She has had no hematemesis or melena. Her daughter notes she has seemed weaker and less interactive over the past two days.
cases.exam: The patient appears dehydrated with dry mucous membranes. The abdomen is distended and tympanitic with high-pitched, intermittently rushing bowel sounds. There is diffuse tenderness, greatest in the upper abdomen, without rigidity, rebound, or peritonism. No surgical scars are present. No hernias are palpable at the inguinal or femoral regions or umbilicus. There is no flank ecchymosis. Rectal exam reveals an empty vault with no gross blood. Skin turgor is reduced. There is no jaundice. Lungs are clear and heart is regular but tachycardic. No palpable abdominal masses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given crampy pain, distension, vomiting, obstipation, and high-pitched bowel sounds in a patient with NO prior surgery and no hernia, what should be high on the differential?
options: Ileus from electrolyte derangement as the first step today
Adhesive small bowel obstruction as the first step today
Mechanical small bowel obstruction from an unusual cause such as gallstone ileus
Acute gastroenteritis as the first step today
stages.explanation: She has clear mechanical small bowel obstruction (crampy pain, distension, vomiting, obstipation, high-pitched bowel sounds), but the two most common causes, adhesions and hernias, are effectively excluded by the absence of any prior surgery and no palpable hernia. This should raise suspicion for an unusual mechanical cause such as gallstone ileus, especially given her untreated gallstones and recent biliary symptoms. Adhesive obstruction (option B) is unlikely without prior surgery. Gastroenteritis (option D) causes diarrhea and hyperactive sounds, not obstipation with obstructive features. A paralytic ileus from electrolytes (option C alternative, option E) typically shows absent/hypoactive bowel sounds and no transition point, whereas her high-pitched rushes indicate a mechanical process. The history points toward a gallstone eroding into the bowel.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the cause of obstruction?
options: Barium enema in this clinical context
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
Abdominal ultrasound only
Upper GI endoscopy
stages.explanation: CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the test of choice for suspected mechanical obstruction and is highly sensitive for gallstone ileus, demonstrating the Rigler triad: pneumobilia (air in the biliary tree from a cholecystoenteric fistula), an ectopic gallstone (often in the distal ileum), and small bowel obstruction. Ultrasound (option B) can show gallstones but poorly evaluates the bowel and obstruction level. A barium enema (option D) evaluates the colon and is contraindicated in suspected obstruction with possible perforation. Upper endoscopy (option E) cannot reach a stone lodged in the distal small bowel and does not assess the obstruction. CT defines the level, cause, and presence of a fistula, guiding operative planning.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.2 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL
Na 133 mEq/L
K 3.3 mEq/L
Chloride 96 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 30 mEq/L
BUN 32 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis: pneumobilia, a 3.5 cm calcified gallstone impacted in the distal ileum, dilated proximal small bowel with air-fluid levels, and a cholecystoduodenal fistula

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT findings of pneumobilia, an ectopic gallstone, and small bowel obstruction are known collectively as what?
options: Charcot triad in this clinical context
Beck triad in this clinical context
Rigler triad
Virchow triad in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Rigler triad describes the radiographic hallmark of gallstone ileus: pneumobilia, evidence of small bowel obstruction, and an ectopic (aberrantly located) gallstone, classically lodged at the ileocecal valve or distal ileum where the lumen narrows. The stone reaches the bowel through a cholecystoenteric (here cholecystoduodenal) fistula formed by chronic gallstone erosion. Charcot triad (option B) is fever, RUQ pain, and jaundice in cholangitis. Virchow triad (option D) refers to stasis, hypercoagulability, and endothelial injury in venous thrombosis. Beck triad (option E) describes cardiac tamponade. Recognizing Rigler triad clinches the diagnosis of gallstone ileus and explains the mechanical obstruction in a patient without prior surgery or hernia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After resuscitation with IV fluids and electrolyte correction, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Emergent total colectomy as initial therapy
ERCP to extract the ileal stone as initial therapy
Continued nasogastric decompression and observation alone
Surgical enterolithotomy to remove the obstructing stone
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of gallstone ileus is surgical enterolithotomy: a longitudinal enterotomy proximal to the impacted stone to remove it and relieve the obstruction. In elderly, frail patients, enterolithotomy alone is often preferred, deferring cholecystectomy and fistula takedown to a later operation (or omitting it) because the combined one-stage procedure carries higher mortality. ERCP (option B) cannot reach a stone impacted in the distal ileum. NG decompression and observation alone (option C) will not relieve a stone large enough to obstruct (typically >2.5 cm) and risks bowel compromise. Total colectomy (option D) is not indicated; the obstruction is in the small bowel from an ectopic stone, not a colonic process. Source control of the obstruction is the priority.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During enterolithotomy, why must the entire small bowel be carefully inspected before closing?
options: To biopsy the bowel for malignancy in every case as the leading concern
To search for additional ectopic gallstones proximally, which can cause recurrent obstruction if missed
To routinely resect the terminal ileum as the leading concern
To confirm the appendix is normal as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Multiple gallstones can pass through the cholecystoenteric fistula, so the entire small bowel must be palpated and inspected ("running the bowel") to find and remove any additional proximal stones; a retained second stone is a well-recognized cause of recurrent or persistent obstruction after enterolithotomy. Routine biopsy for malignancy (option B) is not indicated in a clearly mechanical, benign process. Routine resection of the terminal ileum (option D) is unnecessary and is performed only if a segment is nonviable. Confirming a normal appendix (option E) is incidental and not the purpose of the inspection. Thoroughly clearing the bowel of all stones at the index operation prevents avoidable reoperation and is a key technical and safety step.

cases.diagnosis: Gallstone ileus from a cholecystoduodenal fistula (Rigler triad)
pearls: Suspect gallstone ileus in mechanical small bowel obstruction without prior surgery or hernia, especially in older women.
Rigler triad on CT: pneumobilia, small bowel obstruction, and an ectopic (often distal ileal) gallstone.
The stone enters the gut via a cholecystoenteric fistula, usually cholecystoduodenal.
Enterolithotomy relieves the obstruction; one-stage fistula repair is often deferred in frail elderly patients.

cases.title: Severe Upper Belly Pain Boring to the Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back with vomiting since this morning.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman presents with severe, constant epigastric pain that began this morning and radiates straight through to her back. The pain is partially relieved by leaning forward and is worsened by lying flat. She has vomited several times and feels she cannot keep anything down. She reports a few episodes of shorter right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals in recent months. She denies hematemesis, chest pain, or shortness of breath. She drinks alcohol only rarely, about one glass of wine a month, and denies binge drinking. Past medical history includes obesity and hyperlipidemia. Medications are atorvastatin 20 mg daily and a multivitamin; she does not take fibrates. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke. Family history includes gallstones in her mother. She has had no recent abdominal trauma, no new medications, and no prior pancreatitis. On review of systems she notes nausea, anorexia, and subjective low-grade fever. She is otherwise healthy and works as an accountant.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable, lying still and preferring to sit forward. The abdomen is soft with marked tenderness in the epigastrium and voluntary guarding; there is no rigidity or rebound, and bowel sounds are diminished. Murphy sign is negative. There is no flank or periumbilical ecchymosis. Sclerae are anicteric. There is no palpable mass and no hepatomegaly. Heart is regular and tachycardic; lungs are clear with no basilar crackles. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. No costovertebral angle tenderness. No lower extremity edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory test is most appropriate to confirm the suspected diagnosis of acute pancreatitis?
options: Serum amylase in this clinical context
Serum lipase
Serum gastrin in this clinical context
Liver biopsy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Serum lipase is the preferred confirmatory test for acute pancreatitis: a level at least three times the upper limit of normal, in the setting of characteristic pain, satisfies the diagnostic criteria. Lipase is more sensitive and specific than amylase and remains elevated longer. Amylase (option B) supports the diagnosis but is less specific, rising with many other intra-abdominal and salivary conditions, and normalizes faster. Liver biopsy (option C) has no role in diagnosing pancreatitis. Serum gastrin (option D) is used to evaluate gastrin-secreting tumors (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome), not pancreatitis. Diagnosis of acute pancreatitis requires two of three: characteristic pain, lipase/amylase >3x normal, or imaging findings; lipase elevation plus her classic pain confirms it.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Lipase 1180 U/L (normal <60)
WBC 13.0 ×10³/µL
ALT 220 U/L, AST 180 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L
Triglycerides 150 mg/dL
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL
Glucose 128 mg/dL
RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis, common bile duct 5 mm (normal), no pericholecystic fluid; pancreas partially obscured by bowel gas

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly points to a GALLSTONE (biliary) etiology of her pancreatitis?
options: A calcium of 9.2 mg/dL as the primary diagnosis
Her rare alcohol use as the primary diagnosis
A markedly elevated ALT with gallstones on ultrasound
Triglycerides of 150 mg/dL as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A markedly elevated ALT, particularly greater than three times the upper limit of normal, is the single most useful predictor of a biliary (gallstone) etiology of acute pancreatitis, especially combined with gallstones on ultrasound; transient passage of a stone through the ampulla causes the transaminase spike. A triglyceride level of 150 mg/dL (option B) is normal and far below the ~1000 mg/dL threshold that causes hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis. Her rare alcohol use (option D) makes alcoholic pancreatitis unlikely. A normal calcium of 9.2 mg/dL (option E) argues against hypercalcemia as a cause. The combination of gallstones, elevated ALT, and elevated bilirubin establishes a biliary etiology, guiding the need for cholecystectomy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of initial management for this patient with acute pancreatitis?
options: Immediate ERCP for all patients with gallstone pancreatitis as initial therapy
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Strict prolonged bowel rest with total parenteral nutrition for one week
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia,
stages.explanation: Initial management of acute pancreatitis centers on goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (commonly lactated Ringer's), adequate analgesia, antiemetics, and early enteral nutrition once nausea and pain allow, which improves outcomes versus prolonged fasting. Immediate ERCP for all gallstone pancreatitis (option B) is not indicated; urgent ERCP is reserved for concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction, and this patient has neither (normal CBD caliber, no cholangitis). Prophylactic antibiotics (option C) are not recommended in acute pancreatitis without proven infection. Prolonged bowel rest with TPN (option D) is outdated; early enteral feeding is preferred and reduces infectious complications. Supportive care with fluids, pain control, and early feeding is the foundation of treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her mild gallstone pancreatitis resolves over a few days. What is the recommended definitive intervention before discharge?
options: Lifelong low-fat diet instead of surgery
No intervention; recurrence is unlikely
Schedule elective cholecystectomy in 3 months
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission
stages.explanation: For mild gallstone pancreatitis, laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same hospitalization, after the pancreatitis resolves, is recommended to prevent recurrent biliary events, which are common and can be severe if surgery is delayed. Doing nothing (option B) leaves a high risk of recurrent pancreatitis or other biliary complications. Delaying surgery for three months (option D) exposes the patient to interval attacks and is reserved for severe or necrotizing pancreatitis where surgery is deferred until inflammation settles. A low-fat diet alone (option D alternative, option E) does not address the gallstones and does not reliably prevent recurrence. Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild biliary pancreatitis is the guideline-endorsed approach.

cases.diagnosis: Acute gallstone (biliary) pancreatitis, mild
pearls: Diagnose acute pancreatitis with 2 of 3: characteristic pain, lipase/amylase >3x normal, or imaging findings.
An ALT >3x normal with gallstones strongly suggests a biliary etiology.
Goal-directed fluids, analgesia, and early enteral feeding are the cornerstones; antibiotics are not prophylactic.
Mild gallstone pancreatitis warrants same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Worsening Pancreatitis With New Fever in the ICU
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent severe abdominal pain and new fever during an ICU admission for pancreatitis.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is in the ICU on day 12 of admission for severe acute pancreatitis (alcohol-related). His early course required aggressive fluid resuscitation and he had transient organ dysfunction. He had been slowly improving but over the last 48 hours has developed worsening epigastric pain, new high fevers, rising white count, and increasing oxygen requirement. He has not tolerated enteral feeds well today. Past medical history includes heavy alcohol use, hypertension, and prior alcoholic gastritis. Outpatient medications were amlodipine 5 mg daily; he has had no alcohol since admission. He has no known drug allergies. He is a current smoker with a 30-pack-year history. Family history is noncontributory. He lives with his partner and works in construction. On review of systems he reports chills, anorexia, and abdominal fullness. He has had no hematemesis or melena. The team is concerned about a new infectious process superimposed on his necrotizing pancreatitis given the deterioration after an initial improvement, the so-called second deterioration.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing and diaphoretic. The abdomen is distended with marked epigastric tenderness and fullness; there is involuntary guarding in the upper abdomen but no diffuse rigidity. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. There is no flank (Grey Turner) or periumbilical (Cullen) ecchymosis appreciated today. He is tachypneic with bibasilar crackles. Skin is warm and flushed. Mucous membranes are dry. There is no jaundice. Peripheral pulses are intact but he has cool extremities with a capillary refill of 3 seconds. No focal neurologic deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He improved initially and is now deteriorating with fever and rising WBC around day 12. What complication of necrotizing pancreatitis is most concerning?
options: Simple pancreatic pseudocyst
Infected pancreatic necrosis
Sterile pancreatic necrosis only
Alcohol withdrawal alone
stages.explanation: A secondary clinical deterioration with new fever, leukocytosis, and worsening organ function typically two or more weeks into severe necrotizing pancreatitis strongly suggests infected pancreatic necrosis, which carries high mortality and changes management toward intervention. Sterile necrosis (option B) can cause systemic inflammation early but does not usually produce a new septic deterioration after initial improvement and is generally managed conservatively. A pseudocyst (option C) is a later (>4 week) encapsulated fluid collection and does not typically cause this acute septic picture. Alcohol withdrawal (option E) can cause tachycardia and agitation but does not explain high fever, rising WBC, and worsening abdominal findings; the timeline and systemic signs point to infected necrosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding on contrast-enhanced CT would most specifically confirm INFECTED necrosis (rather than sterile necrosis)?
options: Peripancreatic fat stranding
A dilated common bile duct
Gas bubbles within the necrotic collection
Nonenhancing pancreatic parenchyma
stages.explanation: Extraluminal gas within a necrotic peripancreatic collection on CT is the most specific sign of infected necrosis, indicating gas-forming organisms or a connection that has allowed bacterial infection. Nonenhancing parenchyma (option B) defines necrosis itself but does not distinguish sterile from infected. Peripancreatic fat stranding (option D) is a nonspecific inflammatory finding seen in pancreatitis generally. A dilated CBD (option E) suggests biliary obstruction, not infection of necrosis. When gas is absent but infection is still strongly suspected clinically, image-guided fine-needle aspiration for Gram stain and culture can confirm infection, though many centers proceed on clinical grounds; the presence of gas, however, is essentially diagnostic.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 22.4 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Lactate 3.8 mmol/L
Procalcitonin markedly elevated
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (up from baseline)
Glucose 240 mg/dL
Lipase 90 U/L (now near-normal, as expected late in course)
Contrast-enhanced CT: extensive nonenhancing pancreatic and peripancreatic necrosis with multiple gas bubbles within a walled-off collection; no free intraperitoneal air

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy for this infected walled-off necrosis?
options: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) as initial therapy
Antibiotics alone indefinitely with no drainage as initial therapy
Broad-spectrum antibiotics plus a step-up approach starting with image-guided percutaneous drainage
Immediate open surgical necrosectomy as the first intervention as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The standard of care for infected necrotizing pancreatitis is broad-spectrum antibiotics with carbapenem-level pancreatic penetration plus a step-up approach: begin with image-guided percutaneous catheter drainage or endoscopic transmural drainage, escalating to minimally invasive (e.g., video-assisted retroperitoneal or endoscopic) necrosectomy only if the patient fails to improve. The step-up strategy reduces major complications and death versus primary open necrosectomy. Immediate open necrosectomy (option B) is now reserved for failures of less invasive measures and is associated with higher morbidity. Antibiotics alone without drainage (option D) are insufficient for established infected necrosis causing sepsis. A Whipple procedure (option E) is an oncologic/anatomic resection with no role in managing infected necrosis. Intervention is also ideally delayed until the collection is walled off (~4 weeks), which has occurred here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Why is the timing of intervention (waiting for a walled-off collection when possible) important in necrotizing pancreatitis?
options: Delaying until the necrosis is walled off makes drainage/debridement safer
Timing has no effect on outcomes as the immediate next step
Waiting allows the antibiotics to fully sterilize the necrosis without any drainage in this setting
Early intervention is always superior regardless of encapsulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Whenever the patient's stability allows, intervention is delayed until the necrotic collection becomes walled off (encapsulated), typically around four weeks, because debridement and drainage of a mature, demarcated collection are technically easier, more complete, and carry substantially lower mortality than operating on early, ill-defined necrosis. Antibiotics alone cannot sterilize established infected necrosis without source control (option B). Early intervention is not uniformly superior (option D); premature necrosectomy increases bleeding, incomplete debridement, and death, so it is reserved for patients who deteriorate despite drainage. Timing clearly affects outcomes (option E). If a patient is septic and unstable before encapsulation, drainage may be performed earlier as a temporizing step-up measure, but the principle is to delay definitive debridement when feasible.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As he recovers, which nutritional strategy is most appropriate for this patient with necrotizing pancreatitis?
options: Enteral nutrition , preferred over parenteral nutrition when the gut can be used
Total parenteral nutrition as the routine first choice with appropriate follow-up
High-fat oral diet to maximize calories immediately with appropriate follow-up
Strict prolonged NPO status until all necrosis fully resolves with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Enteral nutrition is preferred in severe and necrotizing pancreatitis because it maintains gut mucosal integrity, reduces bacterial translocation, and lowers infectious complications and mortality compared with parenteral nutrition; it can be delivered orally if tolerated or via a nasogastric/nasojejunal tube if not. Prolonged strict NPO (option B) is outdated and worsens outcomes by promoting gut atrophy and translocation. Total parenteral nutrition (option D) is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate or access enteral feeding (e.g., persistent ileus or intolerance), not as the routine first choice, and it carries higher infectious and catheter-related risks. An immediate high-fat diet (option E) is not the goal; feeding is advanced as tolerated. Using the gut whenever feasible is the guiding principle of nutrition in pancreatitis.

cases.diagnosis: Infected necrotizing pancreatitis (walled-off necrosis)
pearls: A secondary deterioration with fever and rising WBC ~2 weeks into necrotizing pancreatitis suggests infected necrosis.
Gas within a necrotic collection on CT is the most specific sign of infected necrosis.
Use a step-up approach: antibiotics, then percutaneous/endoscopic drainage, then minimally invasive necrosectomy if needed.
Delay definitive debridement until the necrosis is walled off (~4 weeks) when the patient can tolerate waiting.

cases.title: Persistent Upper Belly Fullness After Pancreatitis
cases.chiefComplaint: Ongoing epigastric fullness, early satiety, and a palpable upper abdominal mass weeks after pancreatitis.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man presents to clinic six weeks after hospitalization for acute pancreatitis (alcohol-related). He reports persistent epigastric discomfort, a sensation of upper abdominal fullness, early satiety, and intermittent nausea. He has noticed he feels full after only a few bites and has lost a few pounds. He denies high fevers, rigors, jaundice, hematemesis, or melena. He has abstained from alcohol since discharge and is attending counseling. Past medical history includes prior alcohol use disorder and gastritis. Medications are a proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 20 mg daily) and a multivitamin. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker who quit one year ago. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he reports a vague epigastric mass he can feel when lying down. He has had no recurrent severe pain episodes and no diarrhea or steatorrhea. He is working again and his appetite, when he can eat, is fair. He is worried the pancreatitis is coming back.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and afebrile. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness and a vague, smooth, nonpulsatile fullness/mass in the epigastrium that is mildly tender. There is no rebound, guarding, or rigidity. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no scleral icterus and no jaundice. There is no flank ecchymosis. The liver edge is not enlarged and the spleen is not palpable. No peripheral edema. No lymphadenopathy. Heart and lung exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a persistent epigastric mass and fullness about six weeks after pancreatitis, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute fluid collection (early, <4 weeks)
Pancreatic pseudocyst
Pancreatic adenocarcinoma
Splenic abscess in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A pancreatic pseudocyst is the most likely diagnosis: it is an encapsulated collection of pancreatic fluid surrounded by a non-epithelialized wall of granulation/fibrous tissue that matures roughly four or more weeks after an episode of pancreatitis, often presenting with persistent epigastric fullness, early satiety from gastric compression, and a palpable mass. Pancreatic adenocarcinoma (option B) is less likely given the clear preceding pancreatitis and the subacute, benign-feeling fluid mass, though it remains on the differential and imaging characteristics help distinguish them. An acute peripancreatic fluid collection (option C) is the precursor lesion seen within the first four weeks before a wall forms, whereas his timeline (six weeks) fits a matured pseudocyst. A splenic abscess (option D) would typically cause fever and left-sided findings and is uncommon here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to characterize the suspected pseudocyst?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
Barium swallow as the immediate next step
No imaging; proceed directly to surgical exploration
Plain abdominal radiograph in this setting
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen is the appropriate study to characterize a pseudocyst, defining its size, location, wall maturity, and relationship to the stomach and duct, and helping exclude a cystic neoplasm; MRI/MRCP adds detail on ductal communication and is useful when planning drainage. A plain radiograph (option B) cannot adequately evaluate a pancreatic fluid collection. A barium swallow (option C) assesses esophageal/gastric anatomy and mucosa, not the pancreas. Proceeding directly to surgery without imaging (option D) is inappropriate; characterization guides whether drainage is needed and which route is safest, and confirms the lesion is a pseudocyst rather than a neoplasm or pseudoaneurysm before any intervention.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL
Amylase 130 U/L (mildly elevated)
Lipase 90 U/L
Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL
Albumin 3.9 g/dL
CEA and CA 19-9 within normal limits
Contrast-enhanced CT: a well-circumscribed 7 cm fluid collection with a mature enhancing wall in the lesser sac abutting the posterior stomach; no solid components, no internal gas, and no mural nodularity

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This pseudocyst is 7 cm, symptomatic with early satiety, and adjacent to the posterior stomach wall. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation alone since most pseudocysts resolve spontaneously
Immediate percutaneous aspiration with a small needle and removal of the needle
Endoscopic ultrasound-guided cystgastrostomy
Distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A symptomatic, mature pseudocyst that is large and abuts the stomach is well suited to endoscopic ultrasound-guided cystgastrostomy, creating a transmural drainage tract into the stomach; this minimally invasive approach is now first-line for accessible, wall-adherent pseudocysts and avoids external drains and open surgery. Observation (option B) is appropriate for small, asymptomatic pseudocysts, many of which resolve, but this one is large and symptomatic. Simple needle aspiration without a drain (option C) has a very high recurrence rate because the fluid reaccumulates. Distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy (option D) is far too aggressive for an uncomplicated pseudocyst and is reserved for selected surgical anatomy or complications. Endoscopic transmural drainage offers definitive decompression with low morbidity.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before draining a pancreatic pseudocyst, which complication must be specifically excluded because drainage could be catastrophic?
options: A small pleural effusion as the leading concern
Concurrent gastritis as the leading concern
A pseudoaneurysm within or adjacent to the cyst
Mild hyperamylasemia as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A pseudoaneurysm, classically of the splenic artery, can form adjacent to or within a pseudocyst from enzymatic erosion of the vessel wall; draining or instrumenting a pseudocyst that harbors a pseudoaneurysm can cause massive, life-threatening hemorrhage, so it must be excluded (with contrast CT/CTA or Doppler) and treated, typically by angioembolization, before drainage. Mild hyperamylasemia (option B) is expected and not a contraindication. Concurrent gastritis (option C) is common and does not preclude drainage. A small pleural effusion (option D) can accompany pancreatitis and is not a reason to avoid pseudocyst drainage. Identifying and managing a pseudoaneurysm first is a critical safety step before any pseudocyst intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Pancreatic pseudocyst (mature, symptomatic)
pearls: A pseudocyst is an encapsulated pancreatic fluid collection with a non-epithelialized wall maturing ~4+ weeks after pancreatitis.
Image with contrast CT or MRI/MRCP to confirm maturity, plan drainage, and exclude a cystic neoplasm.
Drain symptomatic or large pseudocysts; EUS-guided cystgastrostomy is first-line when the cyst abuts the stomach.
Always exclude an adjacent pseudoaneurysm (e.g., splenic artery) before drainage to avoid catastrophic hemorrhage.

cases.title: Sudden Knife-Like Upper Belly Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe epigastric pain that started abruptly an hour ago and is now spreading across the abdomen.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with sudden, severe, knife-like epigastric pain that began abruptly about an hour ago while he was at home. The pain rapidly became generalized across the abdomen, and he reports it is worse with any movement, so he is lying very still. He has had several weeks of intermittent burning epigastric pain relieved by antacids, which he attributed to indigestion. He takes ibuprofen 600 mg several times daily for chronic low back pain and has done so for months. He denies prior diagnosed ulcers, hematemesis, or melena, though he notes his recent stools were dark. Past medical history includes osteoarthritis and hypertension. Medications are ibuprofen as above, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and over-the-counter antacids. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes one pack per day and drinks two to three beers most nights. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he reports nausea but no vomiting. He has not taken any acid-suppressing prescription medication. He appears acutely distressed.
cases.exam: The patient lies rigidly still and resists any movement. The abdomen is rigid and board-like with diffuse tenderness, involuntary guarding, and rebound tenderness consistent with generalized peritonitis. Bowel sounds are absent. There is no abdominal distension early on. There is no flank ecchymosis. He is tachycardic with a regular rhythm. Mucous membranes are dry. There is no scleral icterus. Rectal exam shows dark stool that is guaiac positive. He has no palpable pulsatile abdominal mass. Lungs are clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A board-like rigid abdomen with diffuse peritonitis after sudden severe epigastric pain in an NSAID user most strongly suggests what?
options: Uncomplicated gastritis
Acute cholecystitis in this clinical context
Acute pancreatitis in this clinical context
Perforated peptic ulcer with peritonitis
stages.explanation: Sudden, severe epigastric pain that rapidly generalizes with a board-like, rigid abdomen and diffuse peritonitis is the classic presentation of a perforated peptic ulcer, especially in an NSAID user with weeks of dyspepsia; spillage of gastroduodenal contents causes chemical and then bacterial peritonitis. Acute pancreatitis (option B) causes severe epigastric pain but typically does not produce a board-like rigid abdomen this abruptly and is usually not associated with sudden free-perforation peritonitis. Uncomplicated gastritis (option C) causes burning pain but not peritonitis or rigidity. Acute cholecystitis (option D) localizes to the RUQ with a positive Murphy sign rather than diffuse peritonitis. The abrupt onset and rigid abdomen point to perforation, a surgical emergency.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate and fastest initial imaging study to confirm a perforated viscus?
options: Barium upper GI series as the primary diagnosis
Upright chest x-ray looking for free air under the diaphragm
Nuclear medicine gastric emptying study as the primary diagnosis
Right upper quadrant ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An upright chest radiograph is the fastest, most accessible first study and can show free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum), confirming a perforated hollow viscus; an upright or left lateral decubitus abdominal film is an alternative. If the upright film is negative but suspicion remains high, CT with contrast is more sensitive and can localize the perforation. Ultrasound (option B) is not the test of choice for free intraperitoneal air or perforation. A barium upper GI series (option C) is contraindicated when perforation is suspected because barium spillage into the peritoneum causes severe peritonitis; water-soluble contrast is used if a study is needed. A gastric emptying study (option D) is irrelevant to an acute perforation. Free air on the upright film clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Lipase 70 U/L (mildly elevated, can occur with perforation)
Lactate 2.8 mmol/L
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
Upright chest x-ray: free air under both hemidiaphragms (pneumoperitoneum)
CT abdomen/pelvis (confirmatory): free intraperitoneal air and fluid with a focal duodenal/pyloric wall defect; no pancreatic necrosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this perforated peptic ulcer with peritonitis?
options: Therapeutic upper endoscopy with clipping as the sole treatment
IV proton pump inhibitor and observation alone as initial therapy
Outpatient H. pylori testing and treatment as initial therapy
Urgent surgical repair with resuscitation and antibiotics
stages.explanation: A perforated peptic ulcer with generalized peritonitis requires urgent operative repair, classically a Graham omental (omentopexy) patch over the perforation, alongside fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, IV proton pump inhibitor, nasogastric decompression, and analgesia. PPI and observation alone (option B) is reserved only for highly selected, stable patients with a contained, sealed perforation and no peritonitis, which does not apply to this peritonitic patient. Outpatient H. pylori testing (option C) addresses the underlying ulcer cause but is not the acute treatment for a free perforation; H. pylori eradication and stopping NSAIDs follow recovery. Endoscopic clipping alone (option D) does not reliably treat a free perforation with peritonitis. Source control via surgery is the priority.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After surgical repair and recovery, which measures are most important to prevent recurrent peptic ulcer disease in this patient?
options: Test for and eradicate H. pylori and discontinue NSAIDs, with PPI therapy
Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Continue ibuprofen but add an antacid as needed based on current risk
No further treatment is needed after the patch repair
stages.explanation: Long-term prevention targets the two main causes of peptic ulcer disease: test for and eradicate Helicobacter pylori if present, and discontinue NSAIDs (here, his chronic high-dose ibuprofen), with proton pump inhibitor therapy to promote healing. Continuing ibuprofen with an antacid (option B) leaves the major ulcerogenic insult in place and risks recurrence and re-perforation. Doing nothing after the patch (option C) ignores the underlying etiology and is inadequate. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics (option D) are not indicated and do not address ulcer pathophysiology. Counseling on smoking and alcohol cessation further reduces risk. Addressing H. pylori and NSAID exposure is the cornerstone of preventing recurrent ulcers after repair.

cases.diagnosis: Perforated peptic ulcer with generalized peritonitis
pearls: Sudden severe epigastric pain with a board-like rigid abdomen in an NSAID user suggests a perforated peptic ulcer.
An upright chest x-ray showing free air under the diaphragm is the fast first confirmatory test; CT is more sensitive.
Avoid barium studies when perforation is suspected; use water-soluble contrast if needed.
Treatment is urgent surgical repair (Graham omental patch) plus resuscitation, antibiotics, and a PPI.

cases.title: A Small Lump in the Front of the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless lump in the middle of the neck noticed by her mother several weeks ago.
cases.history: An 8-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother, who noticed a soft lump in the front of her daughter's neck about six weeks ago while helping her wash her hair. The mass has not obviously changed in size and is not painful. There is no difficulty swallowing, no voice change, no stridor, and no shortness of breath. About two weeks ago the girl had an upper respiratory infection, and her mother thought the lump looked slightly larger during that illness before settling back down. There has been no fever, night sweats, or weight loss. She is growing and developing normally, is up to date on immunizations, and has no chronic medical problems. She takes no daily medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger brother and attends third grade. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with hypothyroidism; there is no family history of thyroid cancer or endocrine tumors. The mother specifically asks whether this could be cancer and whether it needs to be removed.
cases.exam: A well-appearing, playful child in no distress. There is a smooth, soft, nontender 2 cm midline neck mass located just below the hyoid bone. The mass moves upward with swallowing and notably elevates when the child is asked to stick out her tongue. There is no overlying erythema, warmth, fluctuance, or draining sinus. No lateral neck or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is appreciated. The thyroid gland is palpable in its normal position and is of normal size without nodules. The oropharynx is clear and there is no lingual mass at the tongue base. Cranial nerve and remainder of the head and neck exam are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical exam finding is most characteristic of this midline neck mass?
options: It is pulsatile and has an audible bruit
It elevates with tongue protrusion
It is firm, fixed, and matted to surrounding tissue
It transilluminates brightly with a penlight
