stages.explanation: A midline neck mass that elevates with tongue protrusion (and with swallowing) is the classic sign of a thyroglossal duct cyst, because the cyst remains connected to the foramen cecum of the tongue base via a tract that runs through or near the hyoid bone. Transillumination (option A) suggests a cystic hygroma or other fluid-filled lateral lesion and is not the defining sign here. A pulsatile mass with a bruit (option B) points to a vascular lesion such as a carotid body tumor or aneurysm, which would be lateral, not midline. A firm, fixed, matted mass (option C) raises concern for malignant lymphadenopathy and is not typical of a benign congenital cyst. The combination of a midline location near the hyoid and movement with tongue protrusion is essentially diagnostic.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to confirm the diagnosis and evaluate the thyroid?
options: Fine-needle aspiration of the mass
CT neck with IV contrast
Radionuclide thyroid (technetium) scan
Neck ultrasound
stages.explanation: Neck ultrasound is the preferred initial study for a suspected thyroglossal duct cyst in a child: it confirms the cystic nature and midline location and, critically, documents the presence of a normal, orthotopically positioned thyroid gland. This last point is essential because in a small number of patients the cyst contains the only functioning thyroid tissue (ectopic/lingual thyroid), and resecting it would render the child hypothyroid. CT with contrast (option B) gives more anatomic detail but adds radiation and is reserved for atypical or recurrent cases. Fine-needle aspiration (option C) is not routinely needed in a classic pediatric presentation and risks infection or tract disruption. A radionuclide thyroid scan (option D) can localize functioning tissue but is not first-line; ultrasound answers the key questions noninvasively.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL (normal)
Free T4 1.2 ng/dL (normal)
Neck ultrasound: well-circumscribed, thin-walled anechoic cystic structure 1.9 cm just inferior to the hyoid bone in the midline
Normal-sized thyroid gland in the normal anatomic position with no nodules
No abnormal cervical lymphadenopathy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive surgical treatment for this lesion?
options: Incision and drainage with packing as initial therapy
Sistrunk procedure
Total thyroidectomy as initial therapy
Simple enucleation of the cyst alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The Sistrunk procedure is the standard of care: it removes the cyst together with the central body of the hyoid bone and the epithelial tract extending up to the foramen cecum at the tongue base. Removing the central hyoid and tract is what dramatically lowers recurrence (to roughly 1-4%) compared with simple cyst excision. Simple enucleation (option B) leaves the tract and hyoid attachment behind and carries a high recurrence rate (up to ~40-50%). Total thyroidectomy (option C) is inappropriate because the thyroid gland is normal and is not the source of the lesion. Incision and drainage (option D) is only a temporizing measure for an acutely infected cyst and does not provide definitive treatment; ideally surgery is performed after any active infection has resolved.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which complication is the most common reason a thyroglossal duct cyst recurs after surgery?
options: Incomplete removal of the tract
Malignant transformation of residual cyst lining in this clinical context
Postoperative hypothyroidism from removing all thyroid tissue
Injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Recurrence is most often caused by incomplete excision, specifically leaving behind the epithelial tract or failing to remove the central portion of the hyoid bone, which is precisely why the Sistrunk procedure (rather than simple cyst removal) is the operation of choice. Postoperative hypothyroidism (option A) only occurs if the cyst contained the patient's sole functioning thyroid tissue, which is why preoperative ultrasound confirming a normal orthotopic thyroid is important; it is not a cause of recurrence. Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (option C) is a concern in thyroid surgery, not in midline thyroglossal duct surgery, since the operation does not approach the tracheoesophageal groove. Malignant transformation (option D) is rare (usually papillary carcinoma) and is not the typical cause of recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Thyroglossal duct cyst
pearls: A midline neck mass that moves up with tongue protrusion and swallowing is a thyroglossal duct cyst until proven otherwise.
Always confirm a normal orthotopic thyroid on ultrasound before excision, because the cyst occasionally contains the only functioning thyroid tissue.
The Sistrunk procedure (cyst + central hyoid + tract) is definitive and minimizes recurrence; simple excision recurs frequently.
Thyroglossal duct cysts often enlarge or become infected during an upper respiratory illness; operate after infection resolves.

cases.title: An Unexpected Finding at the Tip of the Appendix
cases.chiefComplaint: Intraoperative discovery of a firm nodule at the appendiceal tip during laparoscopic appendectomy.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman underwent laparoscopic appendectomy six hours ago for classic acute appendicitis: roughly 24 hours of periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant with anorexia and a CT showing a dilated appendix with fat stranding. She has no significant past medical history, takes only an oral contraceptive pill, and has no known drug allergies. She has never had flushing, episodic diarrhea, wheezing, or palpitations, and reports no weight loss. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable for endocrine tumors or colon cancer. During the operation the surgeon noted a firm, yellow-tan 1.4 cm nodule at the very tip of the appendix that was not appreciated on preoperative imaging; the base of the appendix and mesoappendix appeared grossly normal, and there was no carcinomatosis or mucinous material. A standard appendectomy was completed and the specimen sent for permanent pathology. The team is now reviewing the final histopathology to decide whether any further operation is warranted. The patient is recovering well and is hemodynamically stable.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable in the post-anesthesia care unit. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with no murmur, no jugular venous distention, and clear lungs. The abdomen is soft with three laparoscopic port sites that are clean, dry, and intact; there is mild expected periincisional tenderness but no peritoneal signs, distention, or rebound. There is no flushing of the face or neck, no telangiectasias, and no wheezing. There is no hepatomegaly on palpation. Bowel sounds are present. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: FINAL PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (carcinoid) of the appendix
Tumor size: 1.4 cm, located at the appendiceal tip
Depth: invasion into the subserosa; no mesoappendiceal invasion identified
Margins: appendiceal base margin negative
Lymphovascular invasion: not identified
Ki-67: 1% (grade 1)
No goblet cell or mixed adenoneuroendocrine features

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the pathology, what is the most appropriate management for this 1.4 cm grade 1 tip carcinoid with negative margins and no high-risk features?
options: Simple appendectomy is sufficient
Completion right hemicolectomy in this clinical context
Total colectomy as the primary diagnosis
Adjuvant chemotherapy with capecitabine and oxaliplatin
stages.explanation: For an appendiceal neuroendocrine tumor that is less than 2 cm, grade 1, with negative margins and no high-risk features (no mesoappendiceal invasion beyond a few millimeters, no lymphovascular invasion, not at the base), simple appendectomy is curative and no further surgery is required. This tumor is 1.4 cm at the tip with negative margins and Ki-67 of 1%, placing it squarely in the low-risk group. Completion right hemicolectomy (option A) is reserved for tumors greater than 2 cm or smaller tumors with high-risk features. Total colectomy (option B) has no role for a localized appendiceal carcinoid. Adjuvant chemotherapy (option D) is not indicated for a localized, completely resected, low-grade NET; well-differentiated NETs are also relatively chemoresistant.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which set of findings would have changed the recommendation toward completion right hemicolectomy?
options: Tumor greater than 2 cm, OR involvement of the appendiceal base/margin, OR deep mesoappendiceal invasion, lymphovascular invasion, or intermediate/high grade
A preoperative chromogranin A at the upper limit of normal as the immediate next step
A single episode of postoperative nausea as the immediate next step
Tumor at the tip, 1.4 cm, Ki-67 1%, margins negative as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Right hemicolectomy is recommended when an appendiceal NET is greater than 2 cm, involves the base or resection margin, shows deep mesoappendiceal invasion (commonly cited as greater than 3 mm), demonstrates lymphovascular invasion, or is intermediate/high grade, because these features substantially raise the risk of lymph node metastasis. The findings in option A describe exactly the low-risk tumor that does not warrant more surgery. Postoperative nausea (option B) is a nonspecific recovery symptom unrelated to oncologic staging. A borderline chromogranin A (option C) is neither sensitive nor specific enough to drive a decision for hemicolectomy and is influenced by PPIs and renal function; the operative decision is based on histopathologic risk features, not a single biomarker.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient asks whether she needs any biochemical or imaging surveillance after a curative appendectomy for this tumor. What is the most appropriate response?
options: For a completely resected low-risk NET under 2 cm, routine intensive surveillance is not required
Lifelong annual 5-HIAA, octreotide scans, and CT every 3 months as initial therapy
Begin long-acting octreotide injections indefinitely as initial therapy
Schedule a screening colonoscopy every year for life as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A completely excised appendiceal NET smaller than 2 cm, grade 1, with negative margins and no high-risk features has an excellent prognosis approaching that of the general population, and guidelines do not recommend intensive long-term biochemical or imaging surveillance for these low-risk lesions. Aggressive surveillance with serial 5-HIAA, octreotide imaging, and frequent CT (option A) is appropriate for metastatic or high-risk disease, not a cured low-risk tumor, and adds cost and radiation. Long-acting octreotide (option C) is a treatment for symptomatic or progressive metastatic NET, not adjuvant therapy for a resected localized tumor. Annual colonoscopy for life (option D) is not warranted by this diagnosis alone; she should follow age- and risk-appropriate colorectal cancer screening guidelines.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the intraoperative tip lesion had instead measured 2.4 cm with extension into the mesoappendix, what would be the correct definitive operation?
options: Long-acting octreotide as primary therapy
Right hemicolectomy with regional lymphadenectomy
Observation with serial imaging in this setting
Repeat appendectomy with wider appendiceal margins
stages.explanation: An appendiceal NET that is 2 cm or larger, or one with deep mesoappendiceal invasion, carries a meaningfully higher risk of regional lymph node metastasis, so the correct definitive operation is a right hemicolectomy with regional lymphadenectomy to remove the draining nodal basin and achieve oncologic clearance. Repeat appendectomy with wider margins (option B) does not address the nodal basin and is inadequate for a high-risk tumor. Observation with serial imaging (option C) undertreats a tumor with a substantial metastatic risk and would miss the window for curative resection. Long-acting octreotide as primary therapy (option D) is used for symptomatic or unresectable metastatic NET, not as a substitute for curative surgery in localized, resectable high-risk disease. The size and mesoappendiceal invasion are precisely the features that mandate hemicolectomy rather than appendectomy alone.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this tumor had instead been a 1.0 cm goblet cell adenocarcinoma (goblet cell carcinoid) of the appendix, how would management differ?
options: It behaves more aggressively than a classic NET,
It mandates immediate pelvic exenteration as the leading concern
It would be treated identically to a typical small NET with appendectomy alone
It requires only endoscopic surveillance as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Goblet cell adenocarcinoma (formerly goblet cell carcinoid) is a distinct, more aggressive entity that behaves biologically more like an adenocarcinoma than a well-differentiated NET, with a greater propensity for nodal and peritoneal spread. Because of this, right hemicolectomy is generally recommended even for smaller tumors, and bilateral oophorectomy is often considered in women given the risk of ovarian (Krukenberg-type) spread. Treating it like a small classic NET with appendectomy alone (option A) would undertreat an aggressive cancer. Endoscopic surveillance (option C) has no role for an appendiceal tumor. Pelvic exenteration (option D) is a radical procedure for locally advanced pelvic malignancy and is not the standard operation for a localized goblet cell tumor of the appendix.

cases.diagnosis: Appendiceal neuroendocrine tumor (carcinoid), 1.4 cm tip lesion, grade 1
pearls: Carcinoid is the most common appendiceal tumor and is usually an incidental finding at appendectomy, most often at the tip.
Size drives management: less than 2 cm with no high-risk features is cured by appendectomy; greater than 2 cm warrants right hemicolectomy.
High-risk features that push toward hemicolectomy include base/margin involvement, deep mesoappendiceal invasion, lymphovascular invasion, and higher grade.
Goblet cell adenocarcinoma is a more aggressive variant treated like adenocarcinoma, often with hemicolectomy even when small.

cases.title: Episodes of Flushing and Watery Diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent facial flushing and several months of watery diarrhea.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man presents to clinic with about eight months of intermittent facial flushing and loose, watery, nonbloody diarrhea occurring four to six times daily. The flushing episodes come on suddenly, last a few minutes, involve the face and upper chest with a warm sensation, and are sometimes triggered by stress, a glass of red wine, or large meals. He has also noticed occasional wheezing during these episodes despite no history of asthma. Over the past few months he reports mild fatigue and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. He has noticed his ankles swelling and gets short of breath climbing stairs. His past medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two to three beers weekly, quit smoking ten years ago, and works as an accountant. Family history is notable for a brother with type 2 diabetes; there is no known family history of endocrine tumors or colon cancer. He was previously evaluated for irritable bowel syndrome and rosacea without improvement on dietary changes, prompting referral for further workup.
cases.exam: An alert, thin man who flushes visibly across the cheeks and upper chest during the visit. Cardiac exam reveals a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, along with elevated jugular venous pressure and a prominent v wave. There is 2+ pitting edema of both ankles and mild hepatomegaly with a palpable, slightly nodular liver edge two finger-breadths below the costal margin. Lungs have scattered expiratory wheezes. The abdomen is soft and nontender without ascites. There are facial telangiectasias. No lymphadenopathy is appreciated. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The constellation of flushing, secretory diarrhea, wheezing, and right-sided cardiac findings most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Pheochromocytoma as the first step today
VIPoma as the first step today
Systemic mastocytosis as the first step today
Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor
stages.explanation: The combination of episodic flushing, secretory (watery) diarrhea, bronchospasm/wheezing, and right-sided valvular heart disease is the classic tetrad of carcinoid syndrome, caused by serotonin and other vasoactive mediators released into the systemic circulation, typically once a midgut NET has metastasized to the liver. Pheochromocytoma (option A) causes episodic hypertension, palpitations, headache, and diaphoresis rather than secretory diarrhea and right-heart valve disease. Systemic mastocytosis (option C) can cause flushing and diarrhea but is associated with urticaria pigmentosa, pruritus, and tryptase elevation, not carcinoid heart disease. VIPoma (option D) causes profuse watery diarrhea with hypokalemia and achlorhydria (WDHA syndrome) but does not produce the flushing-plus-right-heart-valve picture characteristic of carcinoid.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial biochemical test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Plasma free metanephrines
24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid
Fecal elastase as the primary diagnosis
Serum gastrin level in this clinical context
stages.explanation: 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA, the major metabolite of serotonin, is the standard confirmatory biochemical test for carcinoid syndrome and is both sensitive and specific when patients avoid serotonin-rich foods (bananas, tomatoes, avocados, walnuts) and certain drugs before collection. Plasma free metanephrines (option A) test for pheochromocytoma, not carcinoid. Serum gastrin (option C) is used to evaluate Zollinger-Ellison syndrome/gastrinoma, which presents with peptic ulcers and diarrhea, not flushing and right-heart disease. Fecal elastase (option D) assesses pancreatic exocrine insufficiency and would not establish a neuroendocrine tumor. Chromogranin A is a useful adjunct tumor marker, but urinary 5-HIAA is the specific test for the serotonin-driven syndrome described here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
24-hour urinary 5-HIAA 78 mg/24h (markedly elevated; normal <6)
Chromogranin A 540 ng/mL (elevated)
NT-proBNP 1850 pg/mL (elevated)
CT abdomen/pelvis: 2.6 cm enhancing mesenteric mass in the distal ileum with a desmoplastic reaction; multiple hypervascular hepatic lesions consistent with metastases
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT: intense somatostatin-receptor uptake in the ileal primary and multiple liver lesions
Echocardiogram: thickened, retracted tricuspid valve leaflets with severe tricuspid regurgitation and a dilated right ventricle

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which agent is the cornerstone of medical therapy to control both the symptoms and tumor growth in this patient?
options: High-dose loperamide alone as initial therapy
A long-acting somatostatin analog
Oral prednisone as initial therapy
A nonselective beta-blocker as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Long-acting somatostatin analogs such as octreotide LAR or lanreotide are the cornerstone of treatment: they bind somatostatin receptors (confirmed avid on DOTATATE PET) to inhibit secretion of serotonin and other mediators, controlling flushing and diarrhea, and they also have an antiproliferative effect that slows tumor progression in well-differentiated NETs. Loperamide alone (option B) may modestly help diarrhea symptomatically but does nothing for flushing, mediator release, or tumor growth. Prednisone (option C) is not standard therapy for carcinoid syndrome and would not control serotonin-driven symptoms. A nonselective beta-blocker (option D) does not treat the underlying syndrome and could worsen bronchospasm; moreover, in carcinoid patients, perioperative stress can precipitate carcinoid crisis, which is prevented with octreotide, not beta-blockade.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The patient is scheduled for hepatic debulking. Which life-threatening perioperative complication must be specifically prevented, and how?
options: Carcinoid crisis, prevented with perioperative intravenous octreotide
Thyroid storm, prevented with propranolol and methimazole
Malignant hyperthermia, prevented by avoiding succinylcholine
Addisonian crisis, prevented with stress-dose hydrocortisone
stages.explanation: Patients with carcinoid syndrome can develop carcinoid crisis during anesthesia induction, tumor manipulation, or embolization, with profound flushing, bronchospasm, and hemodynamic instability (severe hypotension or hypertension) from a massive surge of vasoactive mediators. This is prevented and treated with perioperative intravenous octreotide. Thyroid storm (option B) relates to thyrotoxicosis, not NETs. Addisonian crisis (option C) results from adrenal insufficiency and is unrelated to this tumor. Malignant hyperthermia (option D) is a pharmacogenetic reaction to volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine, distinct from carcinoid crisis; while anesthetic planning matters, the specific catastrophic risk in this patient is mediator-driven carcinoid crisis, mitigated by octreotide. Right-heart carcinoid valve disease also markedly increases anesthetic risk and may require valve intervention.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For his progressive, somatostatin-receptor-avid liver metastases that are not fully resectable, which additional treatment option is most appropriate to consider?
options: Immediate cardiac transplantation with appropriate follow-up
Systemic doxorubicin as first-line therapy with appropriate follow-up
Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy and/or liver-directed therapy such as hepatic artery embolization
Whole-brain radiation therapy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: For well-differentiated, somatostatin-receptor-positive (DOTATATE-avid) NETs with progressive liver-dominant metastatic disease, appropriate options include peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) such as Lu-177 DOTATATE, which delivers targeted radiation to receptor-expressing tumor, and liver-directed therapies like hepatic artery embolization/chemoembolization or debulking. Whole-brain radiation (option A) is irrelevant absent brain metastases. Systemic doxorubicin as first-line (option B) is not standard for well-differentiated NETs, which are relatively chemoresistant; cytotoxic chemotherapy is generally reserved for high-grade or refractory disease. Cardiac transplantation (option D) is not the treatment for metastatic carcinoid; severe carcinoid heart disease is managed with valve surgery in selected patients, not transplant. Somatostatin analogs remain the backbone, with PRRT and liver-directed therapy escalating control of receptor-avid hepatic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic small bowel (ileal) neuroendocrine tumor with liver metastases and carcinoid heart disease
pearls: Carcinoid syndrome usually appears only after a midgut NET metastasizes to the liver, bypassing first-pass hepatic clearance of serotonin.
The classic tetrad is flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm, and right-sided valvular heart disease (tricuspid regurgitation/pulmonic stenosis).
Confirm with 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA; localize and stage with Ga-68 DOTATATE PET; somatostatin analogs control symptoms and slow growth.
Always give perioperative IV octreotide to prevent carcinoid crisis during surgery, anesthesia, or hepatic embolization.

cases.title: Hundreds of Polyps Found on Screening
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after a colonoscopy showed innumerable colonic polyps.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man is referred to colorectal surgery after a screening colonoscopy, prompted by his family history, revealed hundreds of polyps carpeting the colon and rectum. He has had occasional bright red blood on the toilet paper but no significant abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. His father died of colon cancer at age 42, and a paternal aunt was diagnosed with colon cancer in her late thirties; several relatives have reportedly had "colon polyps" or colectomies at a young age. He has no significant past medical history, takes no daily medications, and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works in software and is otherwise well. On review of systems he denies bone lumps, skin cysts, or vision problems, though he recalls a dentist mentioning extra teeth on a panoramic film years ago. Genetic counseling has been arranged. Biopsies from the colonoscopy are pending, and the team is counseling him about the underlying diagnosis and the timing of definitive treatment.
cases.exam: A healthy-appearing young man in no distress. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended with no masses or hepatosplenomegaly and normal bowel sounds. Digital rectal exam reveals several small soft polyps in the distal rectum without a discrete mass; there is no gross blood on the glove. There is no lymphadenopathy. Skin exam shows no obvious epidermoid cysts or osteomas, and the eyes appear normal on gross inspection. The remainder of the general physical exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hundreds of colorectal adenomatous polyps in a young patient with a strong family history of early colon cancer is most consistent with which condition, and which gene is responsible?
options: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome due to STK11 mutation
MUTYH-associated polyposis as the most likely cause of hundreds of polyps
Familial adenomatous polyposis due to an APC mutation
Lynch syndrome due to MLH1/MSH2 mutation in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Hundreds to thousands of adenomatous polyps carpeting the colon in a young person with an autosomal dominant family history of early colon cancer is the hallmark of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), caused by germline mutations in the APC tumor suppressor gene. Lynch syndrome (option A) is caused by mismatch repair gene defects and produces only a few adenomas (not hundreds) but with accelerated progression to cancer. Peutz-Jeghers (option B) features hamartomatous polyps and mucocutaneous pigmentation, not hundreds of adenomas. MUTYH-associated polyposis (option D) is autosomal recessive and typically causes tens to a few hundred polyps; it can mimic attenuated FAP, but the classic hundreds-to-thousands phenotype with a clear dominant inheritance pattern points to APC-driven FAP. Note the hint of supernumerary teeth, suggesting Gardner variant features.

stages.label: GENETIC & PATHOLOGY RESULTS
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: >100 polyps throughout the colon and rectum, ranging 2-8 mm; carpeting of the sigmoid
Biopsies: multiple tubular adenomas, some with low-grade dysplasia; no invasive carcinoma identified
Genetic testing: pathogenic germline APC mutation confirmed
Upper endoscopy: a few small fundic gland polyps in the stomach; scattered duodenal adenomas, none high-grade
Ophthalmology: congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium (CHRPE) noted bilaterally

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management for classic FAP, and how should the timing of surgery be approached in this asymptomatic 23-year-old?
options: Chemoprevention with a COX-2 inhibitor as a permanent substitute for surgery as initial therapy
Lifelong colonoscopic polypectomy every 6 months, avoiding surgery as initial therapy
Prophylactic proctocolectomy or total colectomy
Defer all intervention until he develops symptoms or a cancer is found as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because the lifetime risk of colorectal cancer in untreated classic FAP approaches 100%, prophylactic surgery (total colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis, or total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis) is the definitive treatment. Surgery is typically planned electively in the late teens to twenties once the polyp burden is significant but before malignant transformation; this patient's diffuse adenomas with dysplasia make proceeding appropriate. Endoscopic polypectomy alone (option A) cannot keep pace with hundreds to thousands of polyps and does not prevent cancer in classic FAP. Waiting for symptoms or cancer (option C) defeats the purpose of a known near-certain cancer risk. COX-2 inhibitors such as celecoxib (option D) can reduce polyp burden, particularly rectal and duodenal polyps, but are an adjunct, not a replacement for surgery.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The choice between ileorectal anastomosis (IRA) and ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) depends largely on which factor?
options: Whether the patient has CHRPE on eye exam as the immediate next step
The density of rectal polyps
The patient's age regardless of polyp distribution as the immediate next step
The presence of fundic gland polyps in the stomach as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The operative choice hinges chiefly on rectal polyp burden. With relatively sparse rectal polyps, an ileorectal anastomosis preserves the rectum and offers better bowel function but mandates lifelong rectal surveillance because the retained rectum remains at cancer risk. A heavily carpeted rectum or one not amenable to surveillance favors total proctocolectomy with IPAA, which removes essentially all at-risk mucosa. Patient age alone (option A) does not dictate the choice independent of disease distribution. CHRPE (option B) is a benign extracolonic marker of FAP and does not influence the type of resection. Fundic gland polyps (option C) are part of the FAP upper-GI phenotype and prompt upper endoscopic surveillance but do not determine colorectal operative choice.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After colectomy, which extracolonic complication is a leading cause of death in FAP patients and requires ongoing surveillance?
options: Duodenal/periampullary adenocarcinoma
Cutaneous melanoma with appropriate follow-up
Primary lung carcinoma based on current risk
Recurrent colon cancer in the small bowel
stages.explanation: After colectomy, the leading causes of FAP-related death shift to extracolonic disease, chiefly duodenal/periampullary adenocarcinoma and desmoid tumors. This is why FAP patients require lifelong upper endoscopic surveillance (with attention to the ampulla, graded by the Spigelman classification), and why desmoids, often triggered by surgery and abdominal trauma, are monitored and managed carefully. Small bowel "recurrence" of colon cancer (option A) is not the typical pattern; the ileum/pouch is at low risk. Cutaneous melanoma (option C) is not characteristically associated with FAP. Primary lung carcinoma (option D) is not a recognized FAP-related malignancy. Thyroid (papillary) cancer and hepatoblastoma in children are additional FAP-associated tumors worth knowing.

cases.diagnosis: Familial adenomatous polyposis (APC mutation), Gardner variant features
pearls: Classic FAP (germline APC mutation) carries a near-100% lifetime colorectal cancer risk, mandating prophylactic colectomy, usually in the late teens to twenties.
Operative choice (IRA vs total proctocolectomy with IPAA) is driven mainly by rectal polyp burden and the feasibility of lifelong rectal surveillance.
After colectomy, duodenal/periampullary cancer and desmoid tumors become leading causes of death and require ongoing surveillance.
Extracolonic clues to FAP include CHRPE, osteomas, supernumerary teeth, epidermoid cysts (Gardner), papillary thyroid cancer, and childhood hepatoblastoma.

cases.title: Right-Sided Colon Cancer in a Family with Many Cancers
cases.chiefComplaint: Iron-deficiency anemia and a right colon mass found on colonoscopy.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents after workup for fatigue revealed iron-deficiency anemia, prompting a colonoscopy that found a fungating mass in the ascending colon, biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma. She reports mild intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort and dark stools but no overt rectal bleeding, weight loss, or obstruction. Her family history is striking: her mother had colon cancer at 45 and endometrial cancer at 50; a maternal uncle had colon cancer at 48; and a maternal grandmother had ovarian cancer. She herself has had no prior cancers and has had normal Pap smears. Her past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; she takes only an oral iron supplement and a multivitamin, and has no known drug allergies. She has two children, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. She works as a schoolteacher. Given her age and the pattern of cancers in the family, genetic evaluation has been initiated alongside her surgical workup. She is anxious about both her own treatment and the implications for her children.
cases.exam: A pale but well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Conjunctivae are pale, consistent with anemia. Abdomen is soft with mild fullness and tenderness in the right lower quadrant; a vague mass may be palpable in the right flank but no rebound or guarding. There is no hepatomegaly and no ascites. Digital rectal exam shows dark, guaiac-positive stool without a palpable rectal mass. There is no inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam, including pelvic exam, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The pattern of early-onset, right-sided colon cancer with multiple family members affected by colon, endometrial, and ovarian cancers most strongly suggests which hereditary syndrome?
options: Sporadic colon cancer with no hereditary basis
Familial adenomatous polyposis in this clinical context
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome as the first step today
Lynch syndrome
stages.explanation: Early-onset, right-sided (proximal) colorectal cancer in a patient with a family history spanning colon, endometrial, and ovarian cancers across generations is the classic presentation of Lynch syndrome (HNPCC), caused by germline mismatch repair (MMR) gene mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) or EPCAM deletion. FAP (option A) produces hundreds of polyps and would have been evident on colonoscopy; Lynch is nonpolyposis with only a few adenomas that progress rapidly. Peutz-Jeghers (option C) features hamartomas and mucocutaneous pigmentation, not this cancer spectrum. Calling it sporadic (option D) ignores the young age, proximal tumor location, and the Amsterdam/Bethesda-type family pattern. The Amsterdam II and Bethesda criteria are used to flag patients like this for MMR/MSI testing.

stages.label: TUMOR & GENETIC TESTING
stages.content:
Colonoscopy biopsy: moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, ascending colon
Immunohistochemistry: loss of MSH2 and MSH6 protein expression
Microsatellite instability testing: MSI-high
BRAF V600E: not detected
Germline testing: pathogenic MSH2 mutation confirmed
Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no distant metastases; localized right colon mass with a few regional nodes
CEA 3.1 ng/mL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the confirmed Lynch syndrome, what is the most appropriate surgical approach for this localized right colon cancer?
options: Endoscopic resection of the mass as initial therapy
Total proctocolectomy with permanent ileostomy for all Lynch patients as initial therapy
Segmental right hemicolectomy only as initial therapy
Discussion of extended resection given the high metachronous cancer risk, individualized to the patient
stages.explanation: Lynch syndrome patients carry a high risk of metachronous colorectal cancer (reported up to ~16% at 10 years and rising thereafter) in any retained colon, so the surgical discussion should include extended resection such as total/subtotal colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis, weighed against a segmental resection plus intensive surveillance. The decision is individualized considering age, sphincter/bowel function, and patient preference. A segmental resection only (option B) is acceptable in some patients but must be paired with rigorous annual colonoscopy because of the metachronous risk; it is not automatically the best answer here. Routine total proctocolectomy with permanent ileostomy (option C) is more morbid than needed for most Lynch patients. Endoscopic resection (option D) is inadequate for an invasive adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond colon surgery, which additional risk-reducing measure is specifically recommended for women with Lynch syndrome who have completed childbearing?
options: Prophylactic thyroidectomy in this setting
Prophylactic splenectomy in this setting
Prophylactic hysterectomy
Bilateral mastectomy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Women with Lynch syndrome have substantially elevated lifetime risks of endometrial cancer (which can rival or exceed their colorectal risk) and ovarian cancer, so risk-reducing hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is recommended once childbearing is complete; this is often timed with colon surgery if feasible. Bilateral mastectomy (option A) is a strategy for hereditary breast cancer syndromes such as BRCA, not Lynch, which is not strongly associated with breast cancer. Prophylactic thyroidectomy (option C) applies to hereditary medullary thyroid cancer (MEN2/RET), not Lynch. Splenectomy (option D) has no role. Until surgery, endometrial sampling and transvaginal ultrasound surveillance may be considered, though their effectiveness is limited compared with risk-reducing surgery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the recommended colorectal surveillance for this patient's at-risk first-degree relatives and for any retained colon after surgery?
options: Colonoscopy every 1-2 years beginning in the early-to-mid twenties
Annual fecal occult blood testing only with appropriate follow-up
No surveillance is needed once genetic testing is negative in the proband based on current risk
Colonoscopy every 10 years starting at age 50, as in average-risk screening in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Because Lynch-associated adenomas progress to carcinoma rapidly, intensive colonoscopic surveillance every 1-2 years is recommended, typically beginning at age 20-25 (or 2-5 years before the youngest age at diagnosis in the family), for mutation carriers and for any colon remaining after a segmental resection. Average-risk screening every 10 years starting at 50 (option A) badly underscreens a population whose cancers arise younger and faster. FOBT alone (option B) is inadequate surveillance for a high-risk hereditary syndrome. Stating no surveillance is needed (option D) is wrong: relatives should undergo genetic counseling and testing, and those who test positive need intensive surveillance; only relatives who test negative for the known familial mutation can follow average-risk screening.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because this tumor is MSI-high (deficient mismatch repair), which systemic therapy consideration is most relevant if the cancer were metastatic or recurrent?
options: It is uniformly chemoresistant and only radiation should be used
It responds best to 5-fluorouracil monotherapy, which is more effective in MSI-high tumors
It requires anti-EGFR therapy regardless of RAS status as the leading concern
It is highly responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab)
stages.explanation: MSI-high/deficient-MMR tumors carry a high mutational (neoantigen) burden and are characteristically responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab, which are now standard for MSI-high metastatic colorectal cancer and increasingly used earlier in selected cases. The notion that MSI-high tumors respond especially well to single-agent 5-FU (option B) is incorrect; in fact stage II MSI-high tumors derive little benefit from 5-FU monotherapy. Calling them uniformly chemoresistant and treating with radiation only (option C) is wrong; they are not radiation-managed and do respond to immunotherapy. Anti-EGFR agents (option D) require left-sided, RAS/BRAF wild-type tumors and are not indicated based on MSI status, so blanket anti-EGFR therapy is inappropriate here.

cases.diagnosis: Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) with MSI-high, MSH2-deficient right-sided colon adenocarcinoma
pearls: Suspect Lynch syndrome with early-onset, right-sided colon cancer and a family history of colon, endometrial, and ovarian cancers (Amsterdam/Bethesda criteria).
Tumor testing shows MSI-high and loss of an MMR protein (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2); confirm with germline testing.
Discuss extended colectomy given the high metachronous cancer risk, plus risk-reducing hysterectomy/BSO for women, and surveil any retained colon every 1-2 years.
MSI-high colorectal cancers are highly responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors and respond poorly to 5-FU monotherapy in stage II disease.

cases.title: Something Coming Out During Bowel Movements
cases.chiefComplaint: A mass that protrudes from the rectum with bowel movements and now must be pushed back in.
cases.history: A 76-year-old woman presents to clinic distressed about tissue that protrudes from her bottom. For the past year she has noticed a reddish mass coming out during bowel movements; initially it reduced on its own, but over recent months she has had to manually push it back in. She reports mucous discharge, some bright red spotting on the tissue, a sensation of incomplete evacuation, and worsening fecal incontinence with occasional leakage. She has a long history of chronic constipation with straining and three vaginal deliveries in the past. She denies severe pain, fever, or weight loss, and reports no overt large-volume rectal bleeding. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. Medications include levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and as-needed acetaminophen; she takes a fiber supplement intermittently. She has no known drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and lives independently. Family history is noncontributory. She is embarrassed and reports the protrusion is now interfering with daily activities, prompting her to finally seek evaluation.
cases.exam: A well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended without masses. On perineal inspection, when she is asked to strain (Valsalva), a full-thickness segment of rectum protrudes through the anus as a red, moist mass with concentric (circular) mucosal rings. The protrusion reduces with gentle pressure. There is no incarceration, ischemia, or necrosis. Digital rectal exam reveals decreased resting and squeeze sphincter tone. There is mucous staining of the perineum. No discrete rectal mass or polyp is palpable, and stool is guaiac negative aside from superficial blood on the prolapsed mucosa.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: On exam, the protruding tissue shows concentric (circular) mucosal rings. How does this distinguish full-thickness rectal prolapse from prolapsing hemorrhoids?
options: Hemorrhoids show concentric rings, whereas rectal prolapse shows radial grooves as the first step today
Rectal prolapse never protrudes through the anus as the first step today
Both show identical concentric rings and cannot be distinguished as the first step today
Full-thickness rectal prolapse shows concentric circular rings, whereas prolapsing hemorrhoids show radial grooves separating the cushions
stages.explanation: Full-thickness rectal prolapse (procidentia) protrudes as concentric, circular mucosal folds because the entire rectal wall telescopes through the anus. Prolapsing internal hemorrhoids, by contrast, bulge as separate cushions creating radial grooves (a furrowed, segmented appearance) rather than smooth circular rings. Saying hemorrhoids show concentric rings and prolapse shows radial grooves (option A) reverses the correct pattern. Claiming they are indistinguishable (option B) is incorrect; the ring pattern is a classic bedside discriminator. Stating that rectal prolapse never protrudes through the anus (option D) is false; full-thickness prolapse by definition extrudes through the anus, often elicited by having the patient strain or squat. Recognizing concentric rings supports the diagnosis of full-thickness prolapse and guides appropriate workup and repair.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Beyond confirming the prolapse, which evaluation is important before planning surgical repair in this patient?
options: Brain MRI as the primary diagnosis
Immediate emergency laparotomy as the primary diagnosis
No further evaluation is needed; proceed directly to surgery as the primary diagnosis
Colonoscopy to exclude a colorectal neoplasm or lead point, plus assessment of bowel function/continence
stages.explanation: Before elective repair, colonoscopy (or other colon evaluation) is important to exclude a colorectal neoplasm or other lesion that could act as a lead point or coexist, particularly given her age and rectal bleeding; assessment of continence and constipation helps tailor the operation and set expectations, since incontinence may or may not improve after repair. Proceeding directly to surgery without colon evaluation (option A) risks missing a synchronous cancer. Emergency laparotomy (option C) is unwarranted because the prolapse is reducible and not incarcerated or ischemic; this is an elective problem. Brain MRI (option D) is irrelevant to rectal prolapse. Anorectal physiology testing and defecography are sometimes used selectively, but excluding malignancy and characterizing function are the key preoperative steps here.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: no mass, polyp, or other mucosal lesion; mild diverticulosis only
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL (normal on levothyroxine)
Defecography: full-thickness rectal intussusception/prolapse with no obstructing lesion
Anorectal manometry: reduced resting and squeeze pressures

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering the difference between perineal and abdominal approaches, which factor most favors an abdominal repair (e.g., rectopexy with or without sigmoid resection) over a perineal procedure?
options: Active incarceration with gangrenous bowel as initial therapy
Very advanced age with multiple severe comorbidities and high operative risk as initial therapy
A younger or fit patient who can tolerate an abdominal operation, in whom rectopexy offers lower recurrence
Patient preference for the least invasive option regardless of recurrence as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Abdominal repair (rectopexy, with sigmoid resection added when there is significant constipation) provides the lowest recurrence rates and the most durable result, so it is favored in younger or medically fit patients who can tolerate an abdominal operation and general anesthesia. Conversely, very advanced age with severe comorbidities and high operative risk (option A) favors a perineal approach (e.g., Altemeier perineal rectosigmoidectomy or Delorme procedure), which is less invasive and can be done under regional anesthesia but has higher recurrence. Incarceration with gangrenous bowel (option C) is an emergency requiring resection (often a perineal rectosigmoidectomy emergently), not an elective elective-approach decision. Choosing the least invasive option regardless of recurrence (option D) is not a sound surgical principle; the approach is matched to fitness, function, and durability goals.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient is relatively fit for her age and elects an abdominal rectopexy with sigmoid resection. What should she be counseled about regarding her associated fecal incontinence?
options: Incontinence always worsens after rectopexy with appropriate follow-up
Incontinence may improve over time after repair but does not always fully resolve
Incontinence is unrelated to the prolapse and needs no discussion with appropriate follow-up
Incontinence will definitely resolve immediately after surgery with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Patients should be counseled that fecal incontinence frequently improves after correction of the prolapse, often gradually over months as the rectum and sphincter complex recover, but it does not always fully resolve, particularly when there is chronic pudendal/sphincter dysfunction; pelvic floor physical therapy and biofeedback can further help. Promising immediate complete resolution (option B) overstates the outcome and risks disappointment. Saying incontinence always worsens (option C) is incorrect; repair more often improves it. Claiming incontinence is unrelated to the prolapse (option D) is wrong, since chronic prolapse stretches the sphincters and contributes directly to incontinence, making it an important part of preoperative counseling and postoperative expectations.

cases.diagnosis: Full-thickness rectal prolapse (rectal procidentia)
pearls: Full-thickness rectal prolapse protrudes as concentric circular rings; prolapsing hemorrhoids show radial grooves.
Evaluate for a colorectal neoplasm (colonoscopy) and characterize continence/constipation before elective repair.
Approach selection: abdominal rectopexy (often with sigmoid resection) for fit patients (durable, low recurrence); perineal procedures (Altemeier, Delorme) for high-risk elderly patients.
Associated fecal incontinence often improves after repair but may not fully resolve; pelvic floor rehabilitation can help.

cases.title: Severe Belly Pain in a Woman Who Hasn't Moved Her Bowels in Days
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening lower abdominal pain and distension in a chronically constipated nursing-home resident.
cases.history: An 84-year-old nursing-home resident is brought to the ED with two days of worsening lower abdominal pain and distension. Per staff, she has not had a bowel movement in roughly nine days despite her usual scheduled laxatives, and over the last several hours she has become more lethargic and stopped eating. She has a long history of chronic constipation, dementia, and limited mobility. She has had no vomiting but the abdomen has become progressively firm. Her medications include scheduled docusate, intermittent senna, oxycodone 5 mg as needed for chronic back pain, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and donepezil 10 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, does not drink alcohol, and is at her baseline severe cognitive impairment, nonverbal at baseline. Family history is noncontributory. Staff note that her recent opioid use for back pain had increased. On arrival she appears uncomfortable, guarding her abdomen, and is more somnolent than her caregivers describe as normal, prompting concern for an acute intra-abdominal process.
cases.exam: An ill-appearing, somnolent elderly woman who moans with abdominal palpation. The abdomen is markedly distended and tense, with diffuse tenderness most pronounced in the left lower quadrant, involuntary guarding, and rebound tenderness; bowel sounds are absent. A large, firm mass of stool is palpable in the left lower quadrant, and digital rectal exam reveals a rectal vault impacted with hard stool. There is no obvious external hernia. She is tachycardic with cool, mottled extremities and delayed capillary refill. Lungs have shallow breaths but are clear. The skin is dry with poor turgor. She does not localize pain consistently given her baseline dementia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the fever, tachycardia, hypotension, peritoneal signs, and a rectum impacted with hard stool, what is the most concerning diagnosis to evaluate for urgently?
options: Acute uncomplicated diverticulitis
Stercoral colitis with possible perforation
Opioid-induced ileus alone
Simple fecal impaction without complication
stages.explanation: The combination of long-standing severe constipation, a colon and rectum loaded with hard stool, plus systemic signs of sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension) and peritonitis points to stercoral colitis with likely perforation: impacted fecal material exerts pressure on the bowel wall, causing ischemic pressure necrosis that can progress to perforation and feculent peritonitis. Simple fecal impaction (option A) causes distension and pain but not peritonitis or septic shock. Uncomplicated diverticulitis (option B) would not typically present with diffuse peritonitis and a stool-impacted rectum driving the picture, and uncomplicated disease is not associated with shock. Opioid-induced ileus alone (option C) explains constipation and distension but cannot account for fever, peritoneal signs, and hemodynamic instability, which signal a surgical catastrophe.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis and identify perforation in this hemodynamically borderline patient?
options: MRI of the abdomen
Abdominal ultrasound
Barium enema in this clinical context
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
stages.explanation: CT of the abdomen and pelvis (with IV contrast, and without oral barium) is the test of choice: it can demonstrate a fecal-impacted, dilated colon with bowel-wall thickening, pericolonic fat stranding, and signs of perforation such as free intraperitoneal air, extraluminal stool, or abscess, and it helps define the extent of involvement for surgical planning. Ultrasound (option A) is limited by bowel gas and stool and is poor for detecting free air and the extent of colitis. A barium enema (option C) is contraindicated when perforation is suspected because extravasated barium causes severe chemical peritonitis. MRI (option D) is slower, less available emergently, and offers no advantage over CT in an unstable patient with suspected perforation. Resuscitation should proceed in parallel with imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 22.4 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Lactate 4.8 mmol/L
Na 133 mEq/L
K 5.1 mEq/L
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
HCO3 16 mEq/L
CT abdomen/pelvis: massively stool-filled colon with focal wall thickening and discontinuity of the sigmoid wall; extraluminal air and extraluminal fecal material with free fluid consistent with stercoral perforation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient with stercoral perforation and peritonitis?
options: IV antibiotics alone without surgery as initial therapy
Manual disimpaction and an enema at the bedside, then observation as initial therapy
Emergent exploratory laparotomy with resection of the perforated/necrotic segment
Colonoscopic decompression as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Stercoral perforation with peritonitis is a surgical emergency: the patient needs emergent laparotomy with resection of the perforated, necrotic colonic segment, peritoneal washout, and source control, typically with an end colostomy (Hartmann procedure) rather than a primary anastomosis given gross fecal contamination and instability. Bedside disimpaction and enema (option B) is appropriate for uncomplicated impaction but is dangerous once perforation has occurred and will not treat peritonitis. Antibiotics alone (option C) cannot achieve source control of a perforation with feculent contamination and would be fatal as sole therapy. Colonoscopic decompression (option D) is used for selected nonperforated colonic distension (e.g., Ogilvie syndrome) and is contraindicated with established perforation. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and aggressive resuscitation accompany surgery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the family be counseled regarding prognosis in stercoral colitis complicated by perforation?
options: Outcomes depend only on the choice of antibiotic with appropriate follow-up
It is a low-risk condition with near-universal full recovery based on current risk
Prognosis is identical to that of uncomplicated constipation based on current risk
Mortality is high , especially with perforation, advanced age,
stages.explanation: Stercoral colitis with perforation carries a high mortality, frequently reported in the range of roughly 30-60%, driven by the combination of advanced age, delayed presentation, fecal peritonitis, sepsis, and frequent comorbidities, all of which are present here. Honest counseling about this high risk and goals of care is essential. Claiming a low-risk course with near-universal recovery (option A) misrepresents a lethal surgical emergency. Equating it with uncomplicated constipation (option C) ignores the perforation, peritonitis, and shock that define the danger. Attributing outcomes solely to antibiotic choice (option D) is wrong; source control through surgery, timely resuscitation, and the patient's physiologic reserve are the dominant determinants of survival, and antibiotics are adjunctive.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: For patients who survive stercoral colitis, which intervention most directly addresses the underlying cause and reduces recurrence risk?
options: An aggressive, sustained bowel regimen with minimization of constipating drugs such as opioids
Routine daily opioid escalation for comfort as the immediate next step
Permanent bed rest as the immediate next step
Long-term prophylactic antibiotics as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The root cause of stercoral colitis is severe chronic constipation with fecal impaction, so prevention centers on an aggressive, sustained bowel regimen, scheduled osmotic laxatives (e.g., polyethylene glycol), adequate fiber and hydration, and disimpaction protocols, combined with minimizing constipating medications such as opioids and anticholinergics and encouraging mobility. Long-term prophylactic antibiotics (option A) do not address impaction and are not indicated. Escalating opioids (option B) worsens constipation and would increase recurrence risk. Permanent bed rest (option D) promotes immobility-related constipation and is counterproductive. Addressing the constipation directly, rather than its downstream complications, is what reduces the chance of another life-threatening impaction and stercoral injury in these vulnerable patients.

cases.diagnosis: Stercoral colitis with colonic perforation and feculent peritonitis
pearls: Stercoral colitis results from impacted stool causing pressure necrosis of the colon wall, classically in elderly, immobile, opioid-using, chronically constipated patients.
Peritonitis and sepsis in a chronically constipated patient with a stool-loaded colon should raise immediate concern for stercoral perforation.
CT shows a fecal-impacted, thickened colon; perforation appears as free air and extraluminal stool. Avoid barium enema when perforation is suspected.
Perforated stercoral colitis is a surgical emergency (resection with end colostomy) and carries high mortality.

cases.title: A Hugely Distended Belly After Hip Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive abdominal distension and obstipation on postoperative day 3 after hip surgery.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is on the orthopedic ward three days after open reduction and internal fixation of a hip fracture. Over the past 36 hours his abdomen has become progressively distended, and nursing reports he has not passed flatus or stool since surgery. He has mild diffuse abdominal discomfort but denies severe pain. He has had no vomiting initially, though he now reports nausea. His intake has been poor and he has been mostly bedbound. His past medical history includes Parkinson disease, hypertension, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications include scheduled opioids for postoperative pain, carbidopa-levodopa, amlodipine, and tamsulosin; he received perioperative anticholinergics and has had limited mobilization. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. Electrolytes have not been closely followed since admission. The orthopedic team is concerned about the degree of distension and has asked for surgical evaluation to determine whether this is a mechanical obstruction or a functional problem and how to manage it.
cases.exam: An elderly man lying in bed with a strikingly distended, tympanitic abdomen. The abdomen is soft and only mildly diffusely tender without rebound or guarding; there are no peritoneal signs. Bowel sounds are present but reduced. There is no palpable hernia and no surgical abdominal scar. Digital rectal exam reveals an empty, collapsed rectal vault with no mass and no stool. The hip surgical site is clean and dry. He is comfortable at rest and not in distress. No flank tenderness. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Massive colonic distension after surgery in an elderly, immobile patient on opioids, with an empty rectal vault and no peritoneal signs, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction
Sigmoid volvulus as the first step today
Mechanical large bowel obstruction from a sigmoid cancer
Toxic megacolon from C. difficile
stages.explanation: Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome) is massive colonic dilation in the absence of a mechanical cause, classically in older, hospitalized, postoperative, or debilitated patients, especially with opioids, electrolyte abnormalities, immobility, and conditions like Parkinson disease, all present here. The empty rectal vault and lack of a transition point argue against a mechanical lesion. A mechanical obstruction from sigmoid cancer (option A) usually shows a discrete transition point on imaging and often a mass. Sigmoid volvulus (option B) produces a characteristic coffee-bean/twisted appearance and is a closed-loop mechanical obstruction. Toxic megacolon from C. difficile (option C) would feature diarrhea, fever, leukocytosis, and systemic toxicity, which are absent. Confirming the absence of a mechanical cause is the key first step.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Before considering pharmacologic decompression, what is the most important initial step in management?
options: Supportive care: bowel rest with NPO status, nasogastric
Begin oral osmotic laxatives at high volume immediately as the primary diagnosis
Start high-dose loperamide as the primary diagnosis
Immediate surgical colectomy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Initial management of Ogilvie syndrome is conservative and corrective: keep the patient NPO, decompress with a nasogastric tube (and often a rectal tube), aggressively correct electrolyte abnormalities (potassium, magnesium, calcium, phosphate), discontinue contributing drugs such as opioids and anticholinergics, mobilize the patient, and confirm there is no mechanical obstruction with imaging such as CT or a water-soluble contrast enema. Many patients improve with these measures alone. Immediate colectomy (option A) is reserved for ischemia or perforation, not first-line. Loperamide (option C) is an antimotility agent that would worsen colonic distension. High-volume oral osmotic laxatives (option D) can aggravate distension and risk perforation in a dilated, atonic colon and are not the appropriate first step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
K 3.1 mEq/L
Mg 1.4 mg/dL
Na 138 mEq/L
WBC 8.6 ×10³/µL
Lactate 1.3 mmol/L
CT abdomen/pelvis: marked dilation of the cecum (cecal diameter 11 cm) and right/transverse colon with gas extending to the rectum and no transition point or obstructing lesion; no free air, no pneumatosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After 48 hours of conservative care with electrolyte correction, the cecum remains dilated at 11 cm without ischemia or perforation. What is the most appropriate next pharmacologic step?
options: Intravenous metoclopramide alone as initial therapy
Subcutaneous octreotide as initial therapy
Intravenous neostigmine with cardiac monitoring
Oral erythromycin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When conservative measures fail and there is no ischemia or perforation, intravenous neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that boosts parasympathetic (cholinergic) tone to the colon, is the first-line pharmacologic therapy and produces rapid decompression in most patients. Because it can cause profound bradycardia, it must be given with continuous cardiac monitoring and atropine at the bedside; it is contraindicated with significant bradyarrhythmia, bronchospasm, or mechanical obstruction. Metoclopramide (option B) primarily affects the upper GI tract and is not effective for colonic pseudo-obstruction. Octreotide (option C) has no established role here. Erythromycin (option D) is a motilin agonist used for gastroparesis/upper-gut dysmotility, not for colonic pseudo-obstruction. If neostigmine fails or is contraindicated, colonoscopic decompression is the next step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly indicate that nonoperative management has failed and the patient needs urgent surgical intervention?
options: Mild persistent nausea as the leading concern
Transient bradycardia during neostigmine as the leading concern
Cecal diameter of 8 cm with passage of flatus as the leading concern
Signs of colonic ischemia or perforation
stages.explanation: The feared complication of Ogilvie syndrome is cecal ischemia and perforation; the risk rises with greater cecal diameter (concern increases beyond roughly 10-12 cm) and duration of distension. Signs of ischemia or perforation, peritonitis, worsening focal tenderness, free intraperitoneal air, pneumatosis, or a rapidly enlarging cecum, indicate failure of nonoperative therapy and the need for urgent surgery (e.g., cecostomy or colectomy). A cecal diameter of 8 cm with return of flatus (option A) signals improvement, not failure. Mild persistent nausea (option C) is nonspecific and not an indication for surgery. Transient bradycardia during neostigmine (option D) is an expected, manageable drug effect (reversed with atropine), not evidence of colonic failure requiring an operation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome)
pearls: Ogilvie syndrome is massive colonic dilation without a mechanical cause, typical of elderly, postoperative, immobile patients on opioids with electrolyte derangements.
First exclude mechanical obstruction; then treat conservatively (NPO, decompression, correct K/Mg/Ca/PO4, stop opioids/anticholinergics, mobilize).
IV neostigmine is first-line pharmacologic therapy when conservative care fails; give with cardiac monitoring and atropine ready.
Cecal diameter beyond ~10-12 cm or signs of ischemia/perforation signal the need for colonoscopic decompression or surgery.

cases.title: Profuse Diarrhea and a Failing Gut in the ICU
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe diarrhea, abdominal distension, and shock in a patient recently treated with antibiotics.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman in the ICU developed profuse watery diarrhea (more than ten stools daily) one week ago, three weeks after a course of clindamycin for a dental infection followed by hospitalization for pneumonia treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics. She was started on oral vancomycin for confirmed Clostridioides difficile infection but over the last 24 hours her diarrhea paradoxically decreased while her abdomen became markedly distended and she grew progressively hypotensive, requiring vasopressors. She has become confused. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and recent chemotherapy for breast cancer, leaving her immunocompromised. Medications include the oral vancomycin, insulin, and a proton pump inhibitor; she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. The ICU team notes that despite appropriate medical therapy she is deteriorating, with rising lactate and worsening leukocytosis, and is considering whether surgical intervention is now required. She is intubated for airway protection given worsening mental status.
cases.exam: A critically ill, sedated, intubated woman on vasopressors. The abdomen is markedly distended and tympanitic with diffuse tenderness and decreased bowel sounds; there is involuntary guarding. Extremities are cool and mottled with delayed capillary refill. She is febrile and tachycardic. There is no obvious external hernia or surgical scar. Mucous membranes are dry. Despite reduced stool output, the clinical picture is one of worsening systemic toxicity rather than improvement. No localizing peritoneal point initially, but tenderness is becoming more pronounced on serial exams.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with severe C. difficile infection whose diarrhea suddenly decreases while distension, fever, and shock worsen, what complication should be suspected?
options: New small bowel obstruction as the first step today
Simple opioid-induced constipation
Resolution of the infection as the first step today
Fulminant C. difficile colitis with toxic megacolon
stages.explanation: A sudden decrease in stool output in a severely ill C. difficile patient, accompanied by worsening abdominal distension, fever, and shock, is an ominous sign of fulminant colitis with toxic megacolon and ileus: the colon becomes atonic and dilated, so diarrhea diminishes not because the infection is resolving but because the colon has stopped functioning, while systemic toxicity escalates. Interpreting reduced stool as resolution (option A) is a dangerous misreading and could delay life-saving intervention. Simple opioid-induced constipation (option C) does not cause septic shock and toxic dilation in this context. A new small bowel obstruction (option D) would not explain the C. difficile-driven systemic toxicity and colonic findings; the clinical trajectory clearly points to fulminant colitis with toxic megacolon.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 32.0 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L
Albumin 2.0 g/dL
Creatinine 3.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.6)
Na 134 mEq/L
CT abdomen/pelvis: diffuse colonic wall thickening with 'accordion' and 'target' signs, pericolonic stranding, ascites, and transverse colon dilation to 7 cm consistent with toxic megacolon; no free air at this time

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate intensified medical therapy for fulminant C. difficile colitis at this stage?
options: High-dose oral vancomycin plus intravenous metronidazole, with surgical consultation
Loperamide to slow the diarrhea as initial therapy
Stop all antibiotics and observe as initial therapy
Oral metronidazole monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For fulminant C. difficile colitis (hypotension/shock, ileus, or megacolon), guideline therapy is high-dose oral vancomycin (500 mg four times daily), delivered by nasogastric tube and/or as vancomycin retention enemas when ileus impairs oral delivery, combined with intravenous metronidazole, and urgent surgical consultation should occur in parallel. Oral metronidazole monotherapy (option B) is inadequate for severe or fulminant disease. Stopping all antibiotics (option C) abandons treatment of a lethal infection. Loperamide and other antimotility agents (option D) are contraindicated because slowing colonic transit promotes toxin retention and precipitates or worsens toxic megacolon. Critically, medical therapy must not delay surgical evaluation if the patient continues to deteriorate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite maximal medical therapy, the patient's lactate continues to rise, she requires escalating vasopressors, and her abdomen worsens. What is the definitive surgical management?
options: Segmental sigmoid resection
Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy
Diverting loop ileostomy with appendectomy
Percutaneous drain placement
stages.explanation: When fulminant C. difficile colitis is refractory to medical therapy, with rising lactate, worsening shock, peritonitis, megacolon, or perforation, the definitive operation is total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy (subtotal colectomy), removing the entire diseased colon for source control. Segmental resection (option C) is inadequate because the disease is pancolonic and leaving diseased colon behind leads to ongoing toxicity. Percutaneous drainage (option D) does not address transmural colonic disease. A diverting loop ileostomy with colonic lavage and antegrade vancomycin (a colon-sparing alternative) can be considered in selected, less unstable patients, but the appendectomy phrasing in option A is not the standard description and total colectomy remains the gold-standard rescue in a deteriorating, shocked patient. Early surgery before multiorgan failure improves survival.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which factor is most predictive of the need for colectomy and of higher mortality in fulminant C. difficile colitis?
options: Normal mental status as the leading concern
A single loose stool per day as the leading concern
Markedly elevated lactate, rising WBC , vasopressor-dependent shock,
Mildly elevated WBC of 12 ×10³/µL as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Markers of severe physiologic derangement, markedly elevated serum lactate, very high or rising leukocyte count (often greater than 25-35 ×10³/µL, with extreme counts portending poor outcomes), vasopressor-dependent shock, and evidence of end-organ dysfunction (acute kidney injury, altered mentation), predict failure of medical therapy and the need for colectomy, and they correlate with high mortality. A mildly elevated WBC of 12 (option A) reflects milder disease. A single loose stool daily (option B) is not a feature of fulminant disease and indicates a far less severe course. Normal mental status (option C) is reassuring rather than predictive of deterioration. Recognizing these high-risk features should trigger early surgical involvement before irreversible multiorgan failure sets in.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Had this patient instead survived a non-fulminant course but suffered multiple recurrent C. difficile infections after appropriate antibiotic courses, which therapy is most appropriate to durably prevent further recurrence?
options: Routine prophylactic colectomy
Indefinite continuous metronidazole
High-dose loperamide between episodes
Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT)
stages.explanation: For multiply recurrent C. difficile infection, fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) is highly effective at restoring a protective gut microbiome and durably preventing further recurrence, and it is recommended after the patient has failed appropriate antibiotic strategies (such as a vancomycin taper-pulse or fidaxomicin). Indefinite continuous metronidazole (option A) is discouraged because of cumulative neurotoxicity and limited efficacy and does not restore the microbiome. High-dose loperamide between episodes (option B) is contraindicated, as antimotility agents can precipitate toxic megacolon and do not prevent infection. Routine prophylactic colectomy (option D) is far too morbid for recurrence prevention and is reserved for fulminant, refractory disease. FMT (or, increasingly, approved microbiome-based products) directly targets the dysbiosis underlying recurrent infection.

cases.diagnosis: Fulminant Clostridioides difficile colitis with toxic megacolon
pearls: Fulminant C. difficile colitis presents with shock, ileus, or megacolon; a sudden drop in diarrhea with worsening distension signals colonic atony, not recovery.
Treat with high-dose oral/NG vancomycin (plus rectal vancomycin if ileus) and IV metronidazole; avoid antimotility agents.
Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy is the definitive rescue when medical therapy fails; operate before multiorgan failure for better survival.
High lactate, very high or rising WBC, vasopressor-dependent shock, and end-organ failure predict the need for colectomy and high mortality.

cases.title: Fever and Worsening Belly Pain Five Days After Colon Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: New fever, racing heart, and worsening abdominal pain on postoperative day 5 after a low anterior resection.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is on postoperative day 5 after a low anterior resection for rectal cancer. His early recovery was unremarkable, but over the past 12 hours he has developed worsening lower abdominal pain, a fever, and a racing heart, and he feels generally unwell. He had begun tolerating a diet and passing some flatus, but now has nausea and his abdominal pain has intensified, particularly in the pelvis. His pelvic drain output has changed in appearance. His past medical history includes hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history; he received neoadjuvant chemoradiation for his rectal cancer before surgery. Medications include amlodipine and as-needed opioids; he has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. The covering team is called to the bedside because of the acute change in his condition. Of note, his preoperative course included radiation to the pelvis, and the anastomosis was a low colorectal anastomosis, factors relevant to his current presentation. He looks markedly worse than he did this morning.
cases.exam: An uncomfortable, diaphoretic man who appears ill. He is tachycardic. The abdomen is distended with diffuse tenderness that is most severe in the lower abdomen and pelvis, with involuntary guarding and rebound tenderness; bowel sounds are diminished. The midline incision is intact without surrounding erythema or discharge. The pelvic drain is now draining cloudy, brownish, malodorous fluid where it previously drained serosanguinous fluid. There is no obvious wound dehiscence. He is oliguric per nursing. Extremities are warm early but he is becoming more tachycardic on serial checks.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: New fever, tachycardia, peritonitis, and feculent/cloudy drain output on postoperative day 5 after a low anterior resection most likely indicate which complication?
options: Anastomotic leak
Pulmonary embolism
Wound (superficial surgical site) infection
Postoperative ileus
stages.explanation: The classic timing (around postoperative day 5-7), with new fever, tachycardia, peritonitis, and a change in drain output to cloudy/feculent fluid after a colorectal anastomosis, points to an anastomotic leak, in which bowel contents escape the anastomosis into the peritoneum or pelvis. His risk is heightened by a low (extraperitoneal) anastomosis, neoadjuvant pelvic radiation, and smoking. Postoperative ileus (option A) causes distension and nausea but not fever, peritonitis, or feculent drainage. A superficial wound infection (option B) would show incisional erythema and purulence, but his incision is intact and the problem is intra-abdominal. Pulmonary embolism (option D) causes tachycardia and hypoxia but not peritonitis or feculent drain output. Tachycardia is often the earliest warning sign of a leak.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Lactate 3.2 mmol/L
CRP 240 mg/L
Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
Drain fluid: feculent, malodorous
