Radiation alone followed by observation as initial therapy
Chemotherapy alone with no surgery as initial therapy
Immediate abdominoperineal resection followed by chemotherapy as initial therapy
Local transanal excision of the mass only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For locally advanced (T3-4 or node-positive) rectal cancer, neoadjuvant therapy followed by total mesorectal excision (TME) and adjuvant chemotherapy is standard; modern practice increasingly uses total neoadjuvant therapy. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation downstages the tumor, improves resectability, and reduces local recurrence. Upfront APR (option B) skips downstaging and risks positive margins. Chemotherapy alone (option C) or radiation alone (option E) does not cure locally advanced disease. Local transanal excision (option F) is reserved for early, low-risk T1 tumors and would be inadequate for a T3N1 lesion.

stages.label: SURGICAL PRINCIPLE
stages.question: The patient undergoes total mesorectal excision. Why is removing the intact mesorectal envelope so important in rectal cancer surgery?
options: It guarantees sphincter preservation in all patients as the immediate next step
It shortens operative time compared with blunt dissection as the immediate next step
It prevents the need for postoperative surveillance colonoscopy before discharge planning
It removes the lymphovascular drainage
It eliminates the need for any neoadjuvant therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: TME involves sharp dissection along the mesorectal fascial plane to remove the rectum together with its surrounding mesorectum, which contains the lymphovascular and nodal drainage. Complete excision of this envelope with a clear circumferential resection margin dramatically lowers local recurrence rates. It does not shorten operative time (option B) and is technically demanding. It does not replace neoadjuvant therapy (option C), does not guarantee sphincter preservation (option D) since very low tumors may still require APR, and does not eliminate the need for ongoing surveillance (option F), which continues with CEA, imaging, and colonoscopy.

cases.diagnosis: Locally advanced low rectal adenocarcinoma (T3N1) managed with neoadjuvant chemoradiation and total mesorectal excision
pearls: Any palpable rectal mass on DRE requires colonoscopy with biopsy, not empiric hemorrhoid treatment.
Pelvic MRI is the gold standard for local T/N staging and assessing the circumferential resection margin in rectal cancer.
Locally advanced rectal cancer (T3-4 or node-positive) is treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiation, total mesorectal excision, and adjuvant chemotherapy.
Iron-deficiency anemia in an older adult should prompt evaluation for a colorectal malignancy.

cases.title: Sudden Painless Passage of Large Amounts of Blood
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of passing large volumes of bright red blood per rectum without pain.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after three episodes of passing large amounts of bright red and maroon blood per rectum over the past two hours. The bleeding was painless and not associated with straining. She felt lightheaded and nearly fainted while standing. She denies abdominal pain, vomiting, melena, or recent NSAID use beyond occasional acetaminophen. Her past medical history includes hypertension, atrial fibrillation, osteoarthritis, and known colonic diverticulosis seen on a prior colonoscopy. Medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. She has no known drug allergies. She lives independently, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She has had no prior episodes of GI bleeding and no recent weight loss, fevers, or change in bowel habits. Her last colonoscopy three years ago showed extensive sigmoid diverticulosis without polyps or malignancy.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale and anxious but is alert and oriented. She is tachycardic with a thready pulse; mucous membranes are dry. Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended with normal bowel sounds and no rebound or guarding. There is no palpable mass or organomegaly. Digital rectal examination reveals gross bright red and maroon blood in the vault with no palpable mass and normal sphincter tone. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm. There is no jaundice or stigmata of chronic liver disease.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This hypotensive, tachycardic patient with brisk hematochezia is being triaged. What is the most appropriate first priority?
options: Administer oral activated charcoal as the first step today
Immediate colonoscopy without preparation as the first step today
Send the patient directly to CT angiography before any access is placed
Establish two large-bore IVs, give crystalloid
Reverse anticoagulation only and observe without IV access
stages.explanation: The patient shows signs of hemorrhagic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, cool extremities), so hemodynamic resuscitation comes first: two large-bore IVs, crystalloid, and type-and-cross for blood products. Stabilization precedes diagnostics. Unprepped colonoscopy (option A) is unsafe and poorly visualized in an unstable, unprepared patient. Activated charcoal (option C) has no role in lower GI bleeding. Going to CTA first without access (option D) delays resuscitation in an unstable patient. Reversing anticoagulation without establishing access or resuscitating (option E) ignores the immediate threat of shock; resuscitation and access must occur in parallel.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hgb 8.1 g/dL (baseline 12.5)
Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
INR 1.3
BUN 24 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Lactate 2.4 mmol/L
Type and crossmatch: 2 units PRBC prepared
Apixaban last dose 6 hours ago

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After two units of crystalloid the patient remains tachycardic at 105 with ongoing brisk bleeding. What is the best next step to localize the source?
options: Upper endoscopy in this clinical context
Barium enema as the primary diagnosis
Capsule endoscopy in this clinical context
Tagged red blood cell scintigraphy as the first test
CT angiography of the abdomen
stages.explanation: In a patient with ongoing brisk lower GI bleeding who remains hemodynamically borderline, CT angiography is the preferred first localization test: it is fast, widely available, and can detect active extravasation at rates as low as 0.3-0.5 mL/min, guiding subsequent catheter angiography with embolization. Tagged RBC scan (option B) detects slower bleeding but is slower to perform and only roughly localizes. Upper endoscopy (option C) is considered when an upper source is suspected (e.g., high BUN/Cr ratio with melena), less likely here. Capsule endoscopy (option D) is for stable, obscure bleeding. Barium enema (option E) is contraindicated as it obscures subsequent angiography and endoscopy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT angiography shows active contrast extravasation in the sigmoid colon at a diverticulum. The patient continues to bleed. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Immediate diverting colostomy without addressing the bleeding site
Observation with serial hemoglobin checks as initial therapy
Oral tranexamic acid alone as initial therapy
Emergent total colectomy as initial therapy
Catheter-directed mesenteric angiography with superselective embolization
stages.explanation: When CTA localizes active diverticular bleeding, catheter-directed mesenteric angiography with superselective microcoil embolization can target and stop the bleed while avoiding surgery; success rates are high with low ischemia risk when superselective. Total colectomy (option A) is reserved for failed less-invasive measures or unlocalized massive bleeding. Observation (option B) is unsafe with ongoing hemorrhage. Oral tranexamic acid (option D) is not standard and inadequate for active brisk bleeding. A diverting colostomy (option E) does not control the bleeding source. Colonoscopic therapy is an alternative once the patient is stabilized and prepped.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Embolization successfully stops the bleeding and the patient stabilizes. Regarding her apixaban, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Switch immediately to full-dose warfarin in the ED with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue all anticoagulation permanently regardless of stroke risk with appropriate follow-up
Hold apixaban during acute bleeding, then resume after multidisciplinary assessment of bleeding control
Start a heparin drip while she is still actively bleeding with appropriate follow-up
Resume apixaban immediately at twice the usual dose with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Anticoagulation should be held during active bleeding and resumed once hemostasis is secure, balancing rebleeding risk against her atrial-fibrillation stroke risk; most patients with high stroke risk resume an oral anticoagulant within days after the source is controlled. Doubling the dose (option A) is dangerous. Permanent discontinuation regardless of stroke risk (option B) ignores her substantial thromboembolic risk and is not automatic. Switching abruptly to warfarin in the ED (option D) is unnecessary and adds bridging complexity. Starting heparin during active bleeding (option E) would worsen hemorrhage.

cases.diagnosis: Acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding from a sigmoid diverticulum, controlled with superselective angiographic embolization
pearls: Painless brisk hematochezia in an older adult on anticoagulation is classically diverticular until proven otherwise.
Resuscitate first: two large-bore IVs, crystalloid, and type-and-cross before pursuing localization.
CT angiography is the preferred first localization study in ongoing brisk LGIB and guides catheter embolization.
Hold anticoagulation during active bleeding and resume after a risk-benefit assessment once hemostasis is achieved.

cases.title: Recurrent Anemia and a Heart Murmur in an Elderly Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent fatigue and intermittent dark and bright red blood in the stool with chronic anemia.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man admitted for symptomatic anemia has had three hospitalizations over the past year for transfusion-dependent gastrointestinal blood loss. He reports intermittent painless passage of dark and occasionally bright red blood per rectum and progressive exertional dyspnea and fatigue. Two prior colonoscopies and an upper endoscopy were unrevealing or showed only nonspecific findings. His past medical history includes severe calcific aortic stenosis (followed by cardiology), hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3, and prior iron-deficiency anemia requiring repeated IV iron and transfusions. Medications include aspirin 81 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, and oral ferrous sulfate. He has no known drug allergies. He is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks no alcohol, and lives with his daughter. Family history is unremarkable for bleeding disorders. He denies melena with this episode, weight loss, abdominal pain, or NSAID use. A recent echocardiogram showed an aortic valve area of 0.8 cm² with a peak gradient consistent with severe aortic stenosis. He has required four units of packed red cells over six months.
cases.exam: The patient is pale and chronically ill-appearing but hemodynamically stable. A grade III/VI late-peaking crescendo-decrescendo systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the right upper sternal border with radiation to the carotids and a diminished, delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus). Abdomen is soft, non-tender, non-distended with normal bowel sounds and no mass or organomegaly. Digital rectal exam shows scant dark blood without a palpable mass. Conjunctivae are pale. There is no jaundice, no peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease, and no lymphadenopathy. Mild bilateral lower-extremity edema is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given recurrent GI bleeding with negative standard endoscopy and severe aortic stenosis, which underlying mechanism best explains this presentation?
options: Acquired von Willebrand syndrome with bleeding colonic angiodysplasia
Hemorrhoidal bleeding from chronic constipation as the first step today
Occult colorectal carcinoma missed on colonoscopy in this clinical context
Warfarin-induced coagulopathy as the first step today
Hookworm infestation causing chronic blood loss as the first step today
stages.explanation: Heyde syndrome is the triad of aortic stenosis, an acquired von Willebrand syndrome (type 2A from high-shear destruction of large vWF multimers across the stenotic valve), and bleeding from colonic angiodysplasia. This explains recurrent obscure GI bleeding in a patient with severe AS and unremarkable standard endoscopy. Hookworm (option A) is unlikely in this setting. Hemorrhoids (option C) would not cause transfusion-dependent obscure bleeding with prior negative exams. Missed carcinoma (option D) is possible but does not link to the murmur, and exams were negative. He is on aspirin, not warfarin (option E).

stages.label: LABS & WORKUP
stages.content:
Hgb 7.6 g/dL
MCV 74 fL
Ferritin 9 ng/mL
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
PT/PTT normal
vWF antigen normal but loss of high-molecular-weight multimers on analysis
Fecal occult blood positive
Echocardiogram: aortic valve area 0.8 cm², severe aortic stenosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Standard colonoscopy and EGD were nondiagnostic. What is the best next test to identify the small-bowel or obscure bleeding source?
options: Repeat the same standard colonoscopy a third time
Exploratory laparotomy as the primary diagnosis
Video capsule endoscopy
Empiric subtotal colectomy as the primary diagnosis
Barium small-bowel follow-through as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: For obscure GI bleeding after negative bidirectional endoscopy, video capsule endoscopy is the recommended next step to survey the small bowel for angiodysplasias; if a lesion is identified, device-assisted (deep) enteroscopy allows targeted argon plasma coagulation. Simply repeating standard colonoscopy (option A) is low-yield after prior negative exams. Exploratory laparotomy (option C) is too invasive without localization. Barium follow-through (option D) has poor sensitivity for flat vascular lesions and obscures subsequent studies. Empiric colectomy (option E) is inappropriate without localizing the bleeding source and carries high risk in this comorbid patient.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Capsule endoscopy reveals multiple cecal and small-bowel angiodysplasias, and one is actively oozing on enteroscopy. What is the most appropriate initial endoscopic therapy for the actively bleeding lesion?
options: Systemic anticoagulation to improve mucosal perfusion
Argon plasma coagulation of the bleeding angiodysplasia
Surgical right hemicolectomy as first-line therapy
Observation alone with scheduled transfusions as monotherapy
Long-term high-dose aspirin to promote mucosal healing
stages.explanation: Endoscopic argon plasma coagulation is the first-line treatment for accessible, actively bleeding angiodysplasias, providing effective thermal hemostasis with low complication risk. Up-front hemicolectomy (option A) is reserved for lesions refractory to endoscopic therapy and localized to one segment, given surgical risk. Continuing or escalating aspirin (option C) would worsen bleeding and should be reconsidered. Observation with chronic transfusions (option D) is not definitive and exposes the patient to transfusion risks. Anticoagulation (option E) would exacerbate hemorrhage and has no role here.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: Despite repeated APC sessions, the patient continues to have recurrent angiodysplasia bleeding. Which intervention offers the best chance of durable resolution of the bleeding?
options: Aortic valve replacement to correct the underlying acquired von Willebrand defect
Splenectomy as the immediate next step
Permanent discontinuation of all oral intake as the immediate next step
Lifelong high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy as the immediate next step
Indefinite scheduled transfusions with no other intervention in this setting
stages.explanation: In Heyde syndrome, replacing the stenotic aortic valve (surgical AVR or TAVR) corrects the high-shear destruction of large vWF multimers, restoring hemostasis and resolving or markedly reducing recurrent angiodysplasia bleeding in most patients. Indefinite transfusions (option A) do not address the cause. Stopping oral intake (option C) is not a treatment. Splenectomy (option D) has no role in this pathophysiology. PPI therapy (option E) treats acid-related upper GI disease, not colonic or small-bowel angiodysplasia, and does not correct the vWF defect.

stages.label: ADJUNCT
stages.question: While awaiting valve replacement and between endoscopic treatments, which pharmacologic agent has evidence for reducing bleeding from refractory angiodysplasias?
options: Oral iron alone as definitive hemostatic therapy
High-dose aspirin in this setting
Therapeutic enoxaparin in this setting
Octreotide (a somatostatin analog)
Intravenous heparin infusion
stages.explanation: Octreotide, a somatostatin analog, reduces splanchnic blood flow and has evidence for decreasing transfusion requirements and bleeding from recurrent angiodysplasias when endoscopic therapy is insufficient. Aspirin (option A) and anticoagulants such as enoxaparin (option C) or heparin (option E) would worsen bleeding. Oral iron (option D) replaces losses and treats anemia but does not stop bleeding and is not a hemostatic agent. Thalidomide is another option in refractory cases. Definitive correction still relies on valve replacement, with octreotide serving as a bridge.

cases.diagnosis: Heyde syndrome: bleeding colonic and small-bowel angiodysplasia with acquired von Willebrand syndrome from severe aortic stenosis
pearls: Heyde syndrome links severe aortic stenosis, acquired type 2A von Willebrand syndrome, and bleeding angiodysplasia.
Obscure GI bleeding after negative EGD and colonoscopy warrants video capsule endoscopy, then deep enteroscopy for targeted therapy.
Argon plasma coagulation is first-line for accessible bleeding angiodysplasias; octreotide helps in refractory disease.
Aortic valve replacement corrects the vWF multimer defect and durably resolves Heyde-related bleeding.

cases.title: Crampy Left-Sided Abdominal Pain Followed by Bloody Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden crampy left lower abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhea.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of crampy left lower quadrant abdominal pain that began roughly six hours ago, followed within an hour by an urgent bowel movement containing bright red blood mixed with loose stool. The pain came on after lunch and is moderate, cramping, and localized to the left side. She denies syncope, but felt mildly lightheaded. Her past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and a recent hospitalization for community-acquired pneumonia with an episode of dehydration. Medications include hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and a recently started course for constipation. She has no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago) and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes coronary artery disease. She denies fever, prior similar episodes, recent travel, antibiotic use beyond her pneumonia treatment, or sick contacts. The bleeding has been low-volume, and she remains able to ambulate. She has no known history of inflammatory bowel disease or colorectal cancer.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable but not toxic-appearing. Abdomen is soft with moderate tenderness localized to the left lower quadrant and along the left flank; there is no rebound tenderness, guarding, or rigidity, and bowel sounds are present. There is no palpable mass or abdominal distension. Digital rectal exam reveals soft stool with bright red blood and no mass. There is no peritonitis. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmur. Lower extremities are warm with intact pulses. No CVA tenderness on the right; mild discomfort on left flank palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture of crampy LLQ pain followed by bloody diarrhea in an elderly woman after a hypotensive/dehydration event, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Perforated diverticulitis as the first step today
Clostridioides difficile colitis as the first step today
Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm as the first step today
Acute mesenteric ischemia from SMA embolism as the first step today
Ischemic colitis affecting a watershed region such as the splenic flexure
stages.explanation: Ischemic colitis classically presents in older adults with sudden crampy abdominal pain (often left-sided) followed shortly by bloody or maroon diarrhea, frequently after a low-flow state (dehydration, recent illness). The splenic flexure and rectosigmoid junction are watershed zones vulnerable to hypoperfusion. Acute mesenteric ischemia (option A) typically causes severe pain out of proportion with minimal early bleeding and affects the small bowel. Ruptured AAA (option C) presents with hemodynamic collapse and a pulsatile mass. C. difficile (option D) causes watery, not typically bloody, diarrhea and follows antibiotic exposure. Perforated diverticulitis (option E) would show peritonitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL
Hgb 13.1 g/dL
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Na 138 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: segmental wall thickening and submucosal edema of the splenic flexure and descending colon with pericolic fat stranding; patent SMA and IMA

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After CT suggests segmental colitis at the splenic flexure with patent major vessels, which test best confirms the diagnosis and assesses severity?
options: HIDA scan as the primary diagnosis
Mesenteric angiography to look for an embolus as the primary diagnosis
Colonoscopy showing segmental mucosal pallor, edema,
Upper endoscopy as the primary diagnosis
Exploratory laparotomy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Colonoscopy is the confirmatory test for ischemic colitis: it reveals segmental, sharply demarcated mucosal pallor, edema, petechial hemorrhage, and hemorrhagic ulceration in a watershed distribution, and allows biopsy. Mesenteric angiography (option A) is reserved for suspected acute mesenteric ischemia of the small bowel, where occlusive arterial disease is the target; here CT showed patent vessels. Upper endoscopy (option C) evaluates the upper GI tract, not the colon. Laparotomy (option D) is only indicated if peritonitis or gangrene develops. HIDA scan (option E) evaluates the biliary system and is irrelevant.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Colonoscopy confirms non-gangrenous segmental ischemic colitis without full-thickness necrosis. The patient has no peritonitis. What is the appropriate management?
options: Discharge home with no follow-up as initial therapy
Therapeutic colonoscopy with stent placement as initial therapy
Immediate systemic anticoagulation with heparin as initial therapy
Supportive care: bowel rest, IV fluids, correction of hypoperfusion,
Emergent segmental colectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Most cases of non-gangrenous ischemic colitis are managed conservatively: bowel rest, IV fluid resuscitation, correction of the underlying low-flow state, and broad-spectrum antibiotics, with serial abdominal exams to detect deterioration. Surgery (option A) is reserved for peritonitis, perforation, gangrene, or clinical deterioration. Systemic anticoagulation (option C) is not standard for non-occlusive colonic ischemia and could worsen bleeding. Colonic stenting (option D) treats obstruction, not ischemia. Discharge without follow-up (option E) is unsafe because a subset progresses to gangrene and requires close monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 2, the patient develops worsening diffuse abdominal pain, a rising lactate, fever to 38.9 °C, peritoneal signs, and hemodynamic instability. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat colonoscopy at the bedside as the leading concern
Continue conservative management and recheck labs in the morning
Start oral loperamide to slow the diarrhea
Emergent surgical exploration with resection of necrotic colon
Order an outpatient CT colonography as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Development of peritonitis, rising lactate, fever, and hemodynamic instability signals transmural infarction (gangrenous ischemic colitis) or perforation, which mandates emergent surgical exploration and resection of the necrotic segment. Continuing conservative care (option A) would be fatal in the face of peritonitis. Repeat colonoscopy (option B) risks perforation and delays definitive surgery. Antimotility agents like loperamide (option D) are contraindicated and can worsen ischemic and infectious colitis. Outpatient CT colonography (option E) is wholly inappropriate for an acutely deteriorating, unstable patient.

cases.diagnosis: Ischemic colitis of the splenic flexure watershed region, progressing to gangrenous colitis requiring resection
pearls: Ischemic colitis: crampy abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhea in an older adult after a low-flow state; watershed zones (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid) are most vulnerable.
Colonoscopy confirms the diagnosis with segmental mucosal pallor, edema, and hemorrhagic ulceration.
Most non-gangrenous cases resolve with supportive care, IV fluids, and antibiotics; antimotility agents are contraindicated.
Peritonitis, rising lactate, and instability indicate transmural necrosis and mandate emergent surgical resection.

cases.title: Severe Abdominal Pain With a Surprisingly Benign Belly
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain that is far worse than the abdominal exam suggests.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man with atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with abrupt onset of severe, diffuse, constant abdominal pain that began two hours ago while at rest. The pain is excruciating and poorly localized, and he has had one episode of vomiting and an urgent loose bowel movement. He describes the pain as the worst of his life. His past medical history includes atrial fibrillation, hypertension, congestive heart failure, and prior transient ischemic attack. He admits he stopped taking his apixaban about three weeks ago because he ran out and never refilled it. Other medications include metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, and lisinopril 10 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (40 pack-years) and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. He denies prior similar episodes, recent trauma, melena, hematochezia other than this single loose stool, or chest pain. He has no fever. He reports the pain is unrelenting and not relieved by position.
cases.exam: The patient is in obvious severe distress, writhing and diaphoretic. Strikingly, the abdomen is soft and only minimally tender to deep palpation with no guarding, rebound, or rigidity early in the course; bowel sounds are diminished. This dissociation between severe pain and a benign-appearing abdomen is a hallmark finding. The cardiac rhythm is irregularly irregular without murmur. There is no abdominal distension or palpable mass and no pulsatile mass. Peripheral pulses are intact. Rectal exam shows soft brown stool that is heme-positive. Extremities are warm. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An elderly man with atrial fibrillation off anticoagulation presents with sudden severe pain out of proportion to a benign abdomen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute diverticulitis as the first step today
Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm as the first step today
Acute pancreatitis as the first step today
Acute mesenteric ischemia from a superior mesenteric artery embolism
Small bowel obstruction from adhesions as the first step today
stages.explanation: Sudden, severe pain out of proportion to physical findings in an elderly patient with atrial fibrillation who is off anticoagulation is the classic presentation of acute mesenteric ischemia from an SMA embolism (often cardioembolic from the fibrillating atrium). Early in the course the exam is deceptively benign. Pancreatitis (option B) causes epigastric pain with elevated lipase, not this dissociation. SBO (option C) presents with distension, vomiting, and obstipation rather than pain out of proportion. Ruptured AAA (option D) causes hypotension and a pulsatile mass. Diverticulitis (option E) is localized with focal tenderness and fever.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL
Lactate 5.6 mmol/L
HCO3 16 mEq/L
pH 7.28
Anion gap 20
Amylase 90 U/L (normal)
Lipase 42 U/L (normal)
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL
INR 1.1

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Labs show leukocytosis and metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate. What is the best imaging study to confirm acute mesenteric ischemia?
options: MRI of the brain in this clinical context
CT angiography of the mesenteric vessels
Barium small-bowel follow-through
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
Plain supine abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: CT angiography is the diagnostic test of choice for acute mesenteric ischemia; it rapidly identifies the arterial filling defect (embolus typically lodging a few centimeters distal in the SMA, sparing the proximal jejunal and middle colic branches), bowel wall changes, pneumatosis, and portal venous gas. RUQ ultrasound (option A) evaluates the gallbladder, not mesenteric vessels. Plain radiographs (option C) are insensitive early and show late findings such as thumbprinting or pneumatosis. Brain MRI (option D) is irrelevant. Barium studies (option E) are contraindicated, delay diagnosis, and obscure subsequent angiography. Time is bowel; imaging must not delay surgical consultation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms an embolic occlusion of the SMA with no evidence of bowel necrosis yet. Alongside fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and anticoagulation, what is the most appropriate definitive intervention?
options: Emergent revascularization with assessment of bowel viability
Long-term oral nitrates as monotherapy
Bowel rest and observation only as initial therapy
Elective colonoscopy in one week as initial therapy
Discharge on oral anticoagulation with outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute SMA embolism requires emergent revascularization to restore mesenteric flow before transmural infarction occurs; options include open surgical embolectomy or endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis, with intraoperative assessment of bowel viability and resection of frankly necrotic segments, often with a planned second-look laparotomy. Heparin anticoagulation is started promptly. Discharge (option A) or observation alone (option C) would result in bowel infarction and death. Elective colonoscopy (option D) is irrelevant to small-bowel arterial ischemia. Nitrates alone (option E) do not relieve a fixed embolic occlusion and are inadequate as definitive therapy.

stages.label: INTRAOP DECISION
stages.question: During embolectomy, a 15 cm segment of jejunum appears frankly necrotic, but adjacent bowel is of borderline viability after revascularization. What is the best operative strategy?
options: Resect clearly necrotic bowel, preserve questionable segments,
Perform immediate small-bowel transplantation as the immediate next step
Close the abdomen without resection and observe as the immediate next step
Resect all small bowel regardless of viability to be safe as the immediate next step
Create a permanent ileostomy and resect nothing as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The standard intraoperative approach is to resect clearly nonviable bowel, leave borderline segments after revascularization, and perform a planned second-look laparotomy at 24-48 hours to reassess viability once perfusion has equilibrated. This maximizes preservation of bowel length and avoids short-gut syndrome from over-resection. Resecting all small bowel (option A) causes catastrophic short-gut syndrome. Closing without resecting necrotic bowel (option C) leaves dead tissue and leads to sepsis. An ileostomy without resection (option D) does not address necrosis. Small-bowel transplantation (option E) is not an emergency intervention and is not done acutely.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient recovers after embolectomy and limited resection. What is the most important long-term measure to prevent recurrence?
options: Routine annual mesenteric angiography based on current risk
Therapeutic oral anticoagulation for his atrial fibrillation
Discontinuation of all cardiac medications
Lifelong daily aspirin alone with appropriate follow-up
A low-residue diet only with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The embolus arose from his atrial fibrillation, so long-term therapeutic oral anticoagulation (the very therapy he had stopped) is the key measure to prevent recurrent cardioembolic events, including a repeat mesenteric embolism or stroke. Aspirin alone (option A) is insufficient stroke prophylaxis for atrial fibrillation with this risk profile. Diet (option C) does not address embolic source. Routine annual angiography (option D) is unnecessary and invasive. Stopping cardiac medications (option E) would worsen his rate control and heart failure and increase risk. Patient education on adherence and refills is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Acute mesenteric ischemia from a cardioembolic superior mesenteric artery embolism in atrial fibrillation off anticoagulation
pearls: Pain out of proportion to a benign abdominal exam in an elderly patient with atrial fibrillation is acute mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise.
Elevated lactate with metabolic acidosis and leukocytosis supports bowel ischemia; CT angiography is the confirmatory test.
Treatment is emergent revascularization plus resection of necrotic bowel, with a planned second-look laparotomy for borderline segments.
Long-term therapeutic anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation prevents recurrent cardioembolic mesenteric ischemia.

cases.title: Fear of Eating and Steady Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent dull abdominal pain after meals causing her to avoid eating, with significant weight loss.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents to clinic with several months of dull, crampy central abdominal pain that begins consistently 15 to 30 minutes after eating and lasts one to two hours. The reliable association with meals has made her fearful of eating (sitophobia), and she has lost about 10 kg over four months. She reports early satiety and occasional loose stools but no overt blood. Her past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and peripheral arterial disease with claudication, and she had a coronary stent placed two years ago. Medications include aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg daily, and amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She has a 45-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. She drinks alcohol socially. Family history includes coronary artery disease and stroke. She denies fever, night sweats, dysphagia, jaundice, or recent travel. Prior upper endoscopy and abdominal ultrasound (gallbladder) were unremarkable, and her symptoms have progressed despite acid suppression.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin and somewhat cachectic but is in no acute distress. The abdomen is soft, scaphoid, and non-distended with mild, diffuse, deep tenderness but no rebound, guarding, or palpable mass. An epigastric bruit is audible on auscultation. Bowel sounds are normal. Peripheral pulses are diminished in the lower extremities bilaterally with cool feet and loss of distal hair, consistent with peripheral arterial disease. There is no jaundice, organomegaly, or lymphadenopathy. Rectal exam reveals brown, heme-negative stool. Cardiac exam shows a regular rhythm without murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A thin smoker with postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss, an epigastric bruit, and diffuse atherosclerosis is described. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Chronic mesenteric ischemia
Peptic ulcer disease
Pancreatic adenocarcinoma
Irritable bowel syndrome
Biliary colic as the first step today
stages.explanation: The triad of postprandial abdominal pain, food fear (sitophobia) with weight loss, and a vascular patient (heavy smoker with multivessel atherosclerosis) plus an epigastric bruit is classic for chronic mesenteric ischemia, or intestinal angina, from atherosclerotic stenosis of the mesenteric arteries. Peptic ulcer (option A) was excluded by endoscopy and acid suppression failure. Pancreatic cancer (option C) is a key differential for weight loss but lacks the consistent meal-triggered pain and vascular signature; it should be ruled out on imaging. IBS (option D) does not cause weight loss or a bruit. Biliary colic (option E) was excluded by ultrasound.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hgb 11.8 g/dL
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L (normal at rest)
Lipase 38 U/L (normal)
CT angiography: high-grade stenosis of the celiac and superior mesenteric artery origins with heavy calcification; patent inferior mesenteric artery providing collateral flow

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study best confirms chronic mesenteric ischemia and defines the vascular anatomy for treatment planning?
options: Colonoscopy as the primary diagnosis
Esophageal manometry as the primary diagnosis
CT angiography demonstrating stenosis of mesenteric arteries
Abdominal plain film as the primary diagnosis
HIDA scan as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: CT angiography is the preferred test to confirm chronic mesenteric ischemia, demonstrating atherosclerotic stenosis or occlusion typically involving two or more of the three mesenteric vessels (celiac, SMA, IMA) and defining anatomy for revascularization; mesenteric duplex ultrasound is a noninvasive screening alternative. Chronic mesenteric ischemia usually requires significant disease in at least two vessels because of rich collaterals. Plain film (option A) is nonspecific. HIDA (option C) assesses the biliary tree. Esophageal manometry (option D) evaluates motility disorders. Colonoscopy (option E) does not assess the mesenteric arterial supply to the small bowel.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms high-grade celiac and SMA stenosis in this symptomatic, malnourished patient. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Long-term opioid analgesia without intervention as initial therapy
Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
Immediate small-bowel resection as initial therapy
Reassurance and a low-fat diet only as initial therapy
Mesenteric revascularization, typically endovascular angioplasty with stenting of the SMA
stages.explanation: Symptomatic chronic mesenteric ischemia with weight loss warrants revascularization to relieve symptoms and prevent progression to acute mesenteric ischemia and bowel infarction. Endovascular angioplasty with stenting, usually of the SMA, is now first-line in most patients, with open mesenteric bypass reserved for unfavorable anatomy or failed endovascular therapy. PPIs (option A) treat acid disease, not arterial stenosis. Diet and reassurance (option C) do not address the threat of bowel infarction in a malnourished, progressing patient. Bowel resection (option D) is not indicated without infarction. Opioids alone (option E) mask symptoms without treating the cause.

stages.label: RISK & PREVENTION
stages.question: If this patient's chronic mesenteric ischemia were left untreated, what is the most feared complication, and what perioperative measure most reduces future risk?
options: Development of gallstones; prophylactic cholecystectomy as the immediate next step
Hemorrhoids; topical therapy as the immediate next step
Esophageal stricture; serial dilations as the immediate next step
Progression to acute mesenteric ischemia
Diverticulosis; increased dietary fiber as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The most feared outcome of untreated chronic mesenteric ischemia is acute-on-chronic mesenteric ischemia with bowel infarction, which carries very high mortality. Aggressive risk-factor modification, especially smoking cessation, plus antiplatelet and high-intensity statin therapy and blood-pressure control, slows atherosclerotic progression and protects the revascularization. Gallstones (option B), esophageal stricture (option C), diverticulosis (option D), and hemorrhoids (option E) are unrelated to the pathophysiology of mesenteric arterial atherosclerosis and do not represent the dominant risk in this patient.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic mesenteric ischemia (intestinal angina) from atherosclerotic celiac and SMA stenosis
pearls: Chronic mesenteric ischemia presents with postprandial pain, food fear, and weight loss in a patient with diffuse atherosclerosis.
It usually requires significant stenosis of at least two of the three mesenteric vessels because of collateral circulation.
CT angiography (or mesenteric duplex) confirms the diagnosis; symptomatic disease is treated with revascularization, often SMA stenting.
Untreated disease can progress to acute mesenteric ischemia and bowel infarction; smoking cessation, antiplatelet, and statin therapy are essential.

cases.title: A Groin Bulge That Comes and Goes With Coughing
cases.chiefComplaint: An intermittent groin bulge that appears with straining and disappears when lying down.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents to clinic with a several-month history of a soft bulge in his right groin that becomes prominent when he stands, lifts heavy objects at work, or coughs, and disappears when he lies down. He works in construction and lifts heavy loads daily. He reports mild dragging discomfort in the area by the end of the workday but no severe pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, or change in bowel habits. The bulge has never been painful, red, or stuck. His past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and uses occasional ibuprofen. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily, drinks alcohol on weekends, and is otherwise healthy and active. Family history is noncontributory. He denies testicular pain, urinary symptoms, or weight loss. He is concerned about whether he needs surgery and whether he can keep working. He has had no prior abdominal or groin surgeries and no history of similar problems on the left side.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and comfortable. On standing, a soft, non-tender bulge is visible in the right inguinal region above the inguinal ligament that extends toward the scrotum with Valsalva. With the examining finger invaginating the scrotal skin into the external inguinal ring and asking the patient to cough, a soft impulse is felt at the fingertip, suggesting an indirect hernia. The bulge is easily and completely reducible with gentle pressure, is non-tender, and has no overlying erythema or warmth. There is no scrotal swelling, the testes are normal bilaterally, and transillumination is negative. The abdomen is soft and non-tender. The contralateral groin is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the reducible groin bulge above the inguinal ligament with a cough impulse felt at the fingertip extending toward the scrotum, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Indirect inguinal hernia
Femoral hernia in this clinical context
Inguinal lymphadenopathy
Direct inguinal hernia confined to the floor of the canal
Hydrocele as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An indirect inguinal hernia passes through the deep (internal) inguinal ring lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels, follows the spermatic cord, and can descend into the scrotum; an impulse felt at the fingertip when invaginating into the ring is classic. It lies above and medial to the pubic tubercle. A femoral hernia (option A) lies below the inguinal ligament. A hydrocele (option C) transilluminates and is not reducible. Lymphadenopathy (option D) is a firm, non-reducible node. A direct hernia (option E) bulges through the canal floor (Hesselbach triangle), is felt at the side of the finger, and rarely reaches the scrotum.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: The diagnosis is clinically evident on a reducible, non-tender hernia. What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach at this point?
options: Obtain a scrotal ultrasound to rule out testicular cancer
Send tumor markers as the immediate next step
Order an MRI of the pelvis as the immediate next step
Order an urgent CT abdomen and pelvis as the immediate next step
Diagnosis is clinical
stages.explanation: An uncomplicated, clinically obvious reducible inguinal hernia is diagnosed by history and physical examination alone; routine imaging is unnecessary. Imaging (ultrasound, CT, or MRI) is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, occult hernia with pain but no palpable bulge, or suspected complications. Urgent CT (option A) is not indicated for a stable reducible hernia. Scrotal ultrasound for testicular cancer (option C) is unwarranted with normal testes and a negative transillumination. Pelvic MRI (option D) is excessive. Tumor markers (option E) are irrelevant to a benign reducible hernia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient is a 35-year-old laborer with a symptomatic reducible indirect inguinal hernia causing daily dragging discomfort. What is the best management recommendation?
options: Bed rest for six weeks as initial therapy
Lifelong truss with no surgery as initial therapy
Emergency operation tonight as initial therapy
Watchful waiting indefinitely regardless of symptoms
Elective surgical repair
stages.explanation: For a young, active man with a symptomatic inguinal hernia, elective surgical repair, usually tension-free mesh repair (open Lichtenstein or laparoscopic), is recommended because symptoms are present and repair prevents progression and the small ongoing risk of incarceration; it also allows return to heavy work. A truss (option A) is an obsolete temporizing measure. Watchful waiting (option C) is reasonable only for asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic hernias, especially in older or high-risk patients, not for a symptomatic laborer. Emergency surgery (option D) is unnecessary without incarceration. Prolonged bed rest (option E) is not a treatment.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: The patient asks what warning signs should make him seek emergency care before his scheduled elective repair. Which set of symptoms indicates a possible incarceration or strangulation?
options: Occasional itching of the scrotal skin as the immediate next step
Mild dragging discomfort that resolves with rest as the immediate next step
Slight increase in bulge size only with heavy lifting as the immediate next step
A bulge that becomes firm, painful, irreducible, with nausea, vomiting, or overlying redness
A bulge that reduces easily when he lies down as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Incarceration (an irreducible hernia) and strangulation (compromised blood supply) are surgical emergencies. Warning signs are a hernia that suddenly becomes firm, exquisitely painful, and no longer reducible, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, fever, or erythema over the bulge, suggesting bowel obstruction or ischemia. The other options describe benign features of a stable reducible hernia: mild positional discomfort (option A), easy reduction when supine (option B), size variation with lifting (option D), and scrotal itching (option E) are not emergencies. Clear counseling ensures the patient presents promptly if complications arise.

cases.diagnosis: Reducible indirect inguinal hernia
pearls: Indirect inguinal hernias pass through the deep ring lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels and may reach the scrotum; the impulse is felt at the fingertip.
An uncomplicated reducible inguinal hernia is a clinical diagnosis requiring no routine imaging.
Symptomatic inguinal hernias are repaired electively, usually with tension-free mesh; watchful waiting is an option for minimally symptomatic cases.
A firm, painful, irreducible bulge with nausea, vomiting, or erythema signals incarceration or strangulation and is a surgical emergency.

cases.title: A Painful Groin Lump That Will Not Push Back In
cases.chiefComplaint: A previously reducible groin bulge that is now painful, firm, and cannot be pushed back in.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents to the emergency department because his long-standing right groin bulge, which he could previously push back in himself, became firm and painful this morning and can no longer be reduced. Over the past several hours the pain has worsened and he has had two episodes of vomiting and has not passed gas or stool since the bulge became stuck. He reports the overlying skin now looks reddish. His past medical history includes a known right inguinal hernia for several years that he had deferred repairing, plus hypertension and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily and tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired mechanic, an active smoker (30 pack-years), and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. He denies prior abdominal surgery, fever measured at home, chest pain, or urinary retention. He is anxious and in significant discomfort, holding his groin. He has had no previous episodes of the hernia becoming stuck.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and diaphoretic. There is a tender, firm, irreducible bulge in the right inguinal region extending toward the scrotum, with overlying erythema and warmth and exquisite tenderness to palpation. Attempts at gentle reduction are unsuccessful and very painful. The abdomen is mildly distended with high-pitched then diminishing bowel sounds and generalized tenderness, most marked near the hernia; there is no diffuse rigidity yet. The contralateral groin is normal. Rectal exam shows an empty vault with brown heme-negative stool. The testes are normal. There is no abdominal wall crepitus.

stages.label: INITIAL ASSESSMENT
stages.question: A previously reducible inguinal hernia is now firm, tender, irreducible, with erythema, vomiting, fever, and obstipation. What is the most accurate characterization?
options: A simple reducible hernia that needs only reassurance
Uncomplicated epididymitis as the immediate next step
A hydrocele as the immediate next step
A chronic lipoma of the cord as the immediate next step
An incarcerated hernia with signs of strangulation
stages.explanation: An irreducible (incarcerated) hernia that is now tender with overlying erythema, fever, tachycardia, vomiting, and obstipation indicates strangulation, where the trapped bowel's blood supply is compromised, combined with a bowel obstruction. This is a surgical emergency. A reducible hernia needing reassurance (option A) is excluded by irreducibility and systemic signs. A hydrocele (option C) transilluminates and is painless. Epididymitis (option D) causes scrotal tenderness without bowel obstruction. A cord lipoma (option E) is a soft, chronic, non-tender mass and does not cause obstruction or systemic toxicity.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL
Lactate 3.2 mmol/L
Na 137 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis: right inguinal hernia containing a loop of small bowel with proximal dilation and air-fluid levels; wall thickening and reduced enhancement of the herniated loop and adjacent fat stranding

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms a strangulated inguinal hernia with bowel obstruction and signs of compromised bowel. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Outpatient elective hernia repair in two weeks as initial therapy
Application of a truss to hold the hernia in place as initial therapy
Forceful manual reduction at the bedside followed by discharge as initial therapy
Observation with serial exams overnight as initial therapy
Resuscitation with IV fluids, NG decompression, antibiotics,
stages.explanation: Strangulated hernia with compromised bowel mandates emergent operation: the patient is resuscitated with IV fluids, the bowel is decompressed with a nasogastric tube, broad-spectrum antibiotics are given, and the patient is taken urgently to the operating room to relieve the obstruction, assess bowel viability, resect nonviable bowel, and repair the defect. Forceful reduction (option A) risks reducing dead bowel into the abdomen (reduction en masse) and is dangerous. Elective outpatient repair (option C) and overnight observation (option D) delay treatment of ischemic bowel. A truss (option E) is contraindicated and useless here.

stages.label: INTRAOP DECISION
stages.question: At operation the herniated small-bowel loop is dusky and does not regain pink color, peristalsis, or mesenteric pulsation after release and warm-saline application. What should the surgeon do?
options: Perform mesh repair first and ignore the bowel as the immediate next step
Leave the bowel in the hernia sac as the immediate next step
Close without repairing the hernia and reassess in a week before discharge planning
Return the bowel to the abdomen and close as the immediate next step
Resect the nonviable segment
stages.explanation: Bowel that remains dusky without return of color, peristalsis, or mesenteric pulsation after release and warming is nonviable and must be resected, followed by primary anastomosis (or a stoma if the patient is unstable or there is gross contamination), and then repair of the hernia defect. Returning dead bowel to the abdomen (option A) leads to perforation and sepsis. Leaving it in the sac (option C) is unacceptable. Performing mesh repair while ignoring ischemic bowel (option D) misses the lethal problem; mesh is also often avoided in a contaminated field. Closing without repair (option E) leaves the patient at risk for recurrence and does not address the bowel.

stages.label: TECHNICAL CONSIDERATION
stages.question: Because resection of necrotic bowel created a contaminated field, what is the best approach to repairing the hernia defect?
options: Consider a tissue-based repair or biologic mesh, avoiding permanent synthetic mesh in a grossly contaminated field
Use the resected bowel as a patch as the immediate next step
Leave the defect completely unrepaired permanently as the immediate next step
Pack the wound with synthetic mesh and leave it exposed as the immediate next step
Always place permanent synthetic mesh regardless of contamination as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In a clean field, tension-free synthetic mesh is standard for inguinal hernia repair, but with gross contamination from bowel resection, placing permanent synthetic mesh raises the risk of mesh infection. In that setting surgeons favor a primary tissue (suture) repair or a biologic/absorbable mesh and may stage definitive repair. Always using synthetic mesh regardless of contamination (option A) risks chronic mesh infection. Leaving the defect unrepaired (option C) invites recurrence and obstruction. Exposed synthetic mesh (option D) is unacceptable. Using resected bowel as a patch (option E) is not a real technique.

cases.diagnosis: Incarcerated and strangulated inguinal hernia with small-bowel obstruction requiring emergent repair
pearls: Incarceration means irreducible; strangulation adds compromised blood supply and is signaled by pain, erythema, fever, and obstruction.
A strangulated hernia is a surgical emergency: resuscitate, decompress with an NG tube, give antibiotics, and operate emergently.
Avoid forceful bedside reduction of a strangulated hernia to prevent reducing nonviable bowel en masse into the abdomen.
In a contaminated field after bowel resection, prefer tissue or biologic repair over permanent synthetic mesh.

cases.title: Vomiting and a Small Tender Lump Below the Groin Crease
cases.chiefComplaint: Crampy abdominal pain, vomiting, and a small tender lump in the upper thigh just below the groin.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of progressively worsening crampy central abdominal pain, multiple episodes of bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, and inability to pass gas or stool. This morning she noticed a small, tender lump in her right upper thigh just below the groin crease. She is thin and has lost weight recently after the death of her spouse. Her past medical history includes osteoporosis, hypothyroidism, and prior vaginal deliveries; she has had no prior abdominal surgery. Medications include levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, alendronate weekly, and calcium with vitamin D. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She denies fever measured at home, hematochezia, melena, or urinary symptoms. She initially attributed the symptoms to a stomach bug but sought care when the vomiting became persistent and she noticed the groin lump. She is multiparous and postmenopausal. She has never been told she has a hernia before this presentation.
cases.exam: The patient appears dehydrated and uncomfortable. The abdomen is distended and tympanitic with diffuse tenderness and high-pitched, hyperactive bowel sounds early that are becoming hypoactive; there is no rigidity yet. In the right groin there is a small, firm, exquisitely tender mass located below and lateral to the pubic tubercle and below the inguinal ligament; it is not reducible and has mild overlying erythema. The mass is below the level of a typical inguinal hernia. The contralateral groin and the femoral pulses are normal. Rectal exam reveals an empty vault. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An older multiparous woman has a small bowel obstruction and a tender irreducible mass located below the inguinal ligament and below/lateral to the pubic tubercle. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Saphena varix in this clinical context
Direct inguinal hernia
Femoral artery aneurysm
Indirect inguinal hernia
Femoral hernia
stages.explanation: A femoral hernia protrudes through the femoral canal, presenting as a mass below the inguinal ligament and inferolateral to the pubic tubercle; it is more common in older, thin, multiparous women and has the highest strangulation risk of the groin hernias because of the narrow, rigid femoral ring. This patient's bowel obstruction plus a tender irreducible infra-inguinal mass fits perfectly. Inguinal hernias (options A and 4) lie above the inguinal ligament and superomedial to the pubic tubercle. A saphena varix (option C) is soft, compressible, and disappears when supine. A femoral artery aneurysm (option D) is pulsatile and expansile.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.1 ×10³/µL
Lactate 2.8 mmol/L
Na 134 mEq/L
K 3.3 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL (BUN 38)
CT abdomen/pelvis: small bowel obstruction with a transition point at a right femoral hernia; herniated loop is medial to the femoral vein and shows wall thickening with decreased enhancement

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms a strangulated femoral hernia causing small-bowel obstruction with signs of bowel compromise. What is the most appropriate immediate management before the operating room?
options: Oral laxatives to relieve the obstruction as the immediate next step
Attempt vigorous manual reduction and observe as the immediate next step
IV fluid resuscitation, electrolyte correction, nasogastric decompression, antibiotics,
Discharge with outpatient surgical referral as the immediate next step
CT-guided drainage of the mass as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A strangulated femoral hernia is a surgical emergency. The patient is resuscitated with IV fluids, has her hypokalemia and hyponatremia corrected, undergoes nasogastric decompression for the bowel obstruction, receives broad-spectrum antibiotics, and is taken urgently to the operating room. Discharge (option A) is dangerous with strangulated bowel. Vigorous manual reduction (option C) risks reducing nonviable bowel and is especially difficult and dangerous with the rigid femoral ring. Laxatives (option D) are contraindicated in mechanical obstruction. CT-guided drainage (option E) is meaningless for an incarcerated bowel loop.

stages.label: OPERATIVE PRINCIPLE
stages.question: Why do femoral hernias carry a higher risk of strangulation and more often require emergency surgery than typical inguinal hernias?
options: They are always painless and easily reducible as the immediate next step
The femoral canal is a narrow, rigid space bounded by the lacunar ligament, so herniated bowel is easily compressed
They spontaneously resolve without treatment as the immediate next step
They contain only fat and never bowel as the immediate next step
They occur exclusively in young men as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The femoral canal is a small, rigid space bounded medially by the sharp lacunar ligament; a hernia passing through this tight ring is readily constricted, so incarceration and strangulation are common and frequently present emergently, as in this patient. This is why all femoral hernias should be repaired, even when asymptomatic. They are not fat-only (option A); they commonly contain bowel and can strangulate. They are not painless or easily reducible (option C). They occur predominantly in older women, not young men (option D). They do not spontaneously resolve (option E) and need surgical repair.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Even when a femoral hernia is discovered electively and is asymptomatic, what is the recommended management?
options: Repair only if the patient becomes pregnant as initial therapy
A truss is the treatment of choice as initial therapy
Repair only after a second episode of pain as initial therapy
Watchful waiting indefinitely, like a minimally symptomatic inguinal hernia as initial therapy
Surgical repair is recommended for essentially all femoral hernias because of the high strangulation risk
stages.explanation: Because of the high risk of incarceration and strangulation, surgical repair is recommended for essentially all femoral hernias, including asymptomatic ones, rather than watchful waiting. Repair can be open (e.g., McVay/Cooper ligament repair or a preperitoneal mesh approach) or laparoscopic. Watchful waiting (option A), acceptable for minimally symptomatic inguinal hernias, is not appropriate for femoral hernias. A truss (option C) does not protect against strangulation. Deferring until pregnancy (option D) or a second pain episode (option E) exposes the patient to a potentially catastrophic strangulation event in the interim.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At operation the herniated bowel loop within the femoral ring is frankly necrotic. After resection, what additional precaution is important given the constricting femoral ring?
options: No additional steps are needed once bowel is resected as the leading concern
Pack the femoral canal with gauze permanently as the leading concern
Leave the femoral ring intact and untouched as the leading concern
Avoid repairing the femoral defect to prevent recurrence as the leading concern
Adequately release the constricting lacunar ligament to relieve the obstruction
stages.explanation: The sharp lacunar (or sometimes inguinal) ligament constricting the femoral ring may need careful division to relieve the strangulation and deliver the bowel; the surgeon must then confirm bowel viability, resect any nonviable segment, and close the femoral defect (e.g., approximating the inguinal ligament to Cooper ligament or using preperitoneal mesh). Doing nothing further (option A) leaves the defect open to recurrence. Leaving the ring intact (option C) may not relieve constriction. Permanent gauze packing (option D) is not a repair. Failing to repair the defect (option E) guarantees recurrence and risks future obstruction.

cases.diagnosis: Strangulated femoral hernia presenting as small-bowel obstruction
pearls: Femoral hernias present below the inguinal ligament and inferolateral to the pubic tubercle, most often in older, thin, multiparous women.
They have the highest strangulation risk of groin hernias because of the narrow, rigid femoral canal bounded by the lacunar ligament.
Any groin mass with small-bowel obstruction in an older woman should raise suspicion for an incarcerated femoral hernia.
All femoral hernias warrant surgical repair, even when asymptomatic, given the high risk of strangulation.

cases.title: A Soft Bulge at the Navel
cases.chiefComplaint: A soft bulge at the belly button that has slowly enlarged over the past year.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents to clinic with a soft bulge at her umbilicus that she first noticed about a year ago and that has gradually enlarged. It becomes more prominent when she coughs, strains, or lifts her toddler, and flattens when she lies down. She reports mild intermittent discomfort but no severe pain, nausea, vomiting, redness, or changes in skin color over the bulge. Her past medical history includes obesity (BMI 33), two prior pregnancies, and well-controlled gastroesophageal reflux. Medications include omeprazole 20 mg daily and a prenatal-type multivitamin; she takes no anticoagulants. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has no history of liver disease, hepatitis, or heavy alcohol use. Family history is noncontributory. She denies abdominal distension, obstipation, weight loss, jaundice, or leg swelling. She is concerned about whether the bulge is dangerous and whether it needs surgery. She has had no prior abdominal surgery and no episodes of the bulge becoming hard, stuck, or painful.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and comfortable. There is a soft, non-tender bulge at the umbilicus measuring approximately 2 cm that protrudes with Valsalva and reduces easily and completely with gentle pressure, revealing a palpable fascial defect of about 1.5 cm at the umbilical ring. There is no overlying erythema, warmth, or skin changes, and the bulge is non-tender. The abdomen is otherwise soft and non-distended with normal bowel sounds and no shifting dullness, fluid wave, caput medusae, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, or hepatosplenomegaly. There is no jaundice. The remainder of the abdominal wall is intact without other hernias.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a soft, reducible periumbilical bulge with a palpable fascial defect at the umbilical ring, what is the diagnosis?
options: Incisional hernia
Sister Mary Joseph nodule
Rectus diatasis (diastasis recti)
Reducible umbilical hernia
Epigastric hernia
stages.explanation: A soft, reducible bulge at the umbilicus with a palpable defect in the fascia at the umbilical ring is a reducible umbilical hernia, common in adults with risk factors such as obesity, multiparity, and conditions raising intra-abdominal pressure. An epigastric hernia (option A) occurs in the midline between the xiphoid and umbilicus, not at the ring. Diastasis recti (option C) is midline thinning between the rectus muscles without a true fascial defect or true hernia risk. An incisional hernia (option D) occurs at a prior surgical scar, which she lacks. A Sister Mary Joseph nodule (option E) is a firm, non-reducible metastatic umbilical mass.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before discussing management, which feature in this patient's history would most change the surgical decision-making for an umbilical hernia and should be specifically assessed?
options: A family history of hypertension
Recent dental work as the immediate next step
History of seasonal allergies in this setting
Childhood tonsillectomy in this setting
Presence of cirrhosis with ascites
stages.explanation: Underlying cirrhosis with ascites markedly affects umbilical hernia management: chronically elevated intra-abdominal pressure from ascites enlarges the hernia and raises risks of incarceration, skin breakdown, and rupture, while surgery carries higher morbidity, so ascites should be medically controlled first and repair coordinated with hepatology. Her exam reassuringly lacks stigmata of liver disease, but it is the key factor to assess. Seasonal allergies (option A), family history of hypertension (option C), recent dental work (option D), and a childhood tonsillectomy (option E) have no bearing on umbilical hernia surgical decision-making.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a small, symptomatic but reducible umbilical hernia with no liver disease. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Lifelong abdominal binder and never operate as initial therapy
Elective surgical repair, typically with mesh for defects of meaningful size, given symptoms
No follow-up and complete reassurance with no repair ever offered as initial therapy
Emergency surgery today as initial therapy
Immediate large open repair regardless of size as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a symptomatic, reducible umbilical hernia in a healthy adult, elective repair is appropriate; mesh reinforcement is generally used for defects of meaningful size (often cited as larger than roughly 1-2 cm) to reduce recurrence, while very small defects may be closed primarily. Repair relieves symptoms and prevents enlargement and incarceration. An abdominal binder alone (option A) does not cure the defect. Emergency surgery (option C) is unnecessary without incarceration. Offering no repair ever (option D) ignores her symptoms and progression risk. Committing to a large open repair regardless of size (option E) overtreats a small defect.

stages.label: SPECIAL POPULATION
stages.question: Consider instead a patient with the same umbilical hernia but with cirrhosis and large-volume ascites and overlying skin that is thinning and weeping fluid. What is the best management principle?
options: Optimize ascites control
Ignore it because cirrhotic patients should never have surgery as the immediate next step
Drain ascites at home with a needle as the definitive treatment as the immediate next step
Apply a tight binder over the weeping skin and discharge as the immediate next step
Perform an emergency liver transplant first as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In cirrhotic patients with ascites, umbilical hernias are prone to enlargement, skin breakdown, and rupture (with the risk of catastrophic ascitic leak and infection). The principle is to control ascites medically (sodium restriction, diuretics, and TIPS or paracentesis as needed) and repair the hernia, with urgent repair if there is skin ulceration or impending rupture, coordinated with hepatology. Avoiding surgery entirely (option A) ignores life-threatening rupture risk. Home needle drainage (option C) risks infection and is not definitive. A tight binder over weeping skin (option D) is inadequate. Immediate transplant (option E) is not the acute solution.

cases.diagnosis: Reducible umbilical hernia (with attention to cirrhosis/ascites considerations)
pearls: Adult umbilical hernias are linked to obesity, multiparity, and conditions raising intra-abdominal pressure; diagnosis is clinical with a palpable fascial defect at the umbilical ring.
Symptomatic reducible umbilical hernias are repaired electively, with mesh for defects of meaningful size to reduce recurrence.
In cirrhosis with ascites, control the ascites first; these hernias risk skin breakdown and rupture and need a multidisciplinary, sometimes urgent, approach.
Differentiate a true umbilical hernia from diastasis recti, which has midline thinning but no discrete fascial defect.

cases.title: A Growing Bulge Through an Old Surgical Scar
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressively enlarging bulge along his old abdominal incision over the past two years.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man presents to the general surgery clinic for a midline abdominal bulge that has slowly enlarged over two years. He underwent an open exploratory laparotomy with sigmoid colectomy for a perforated diverticulitis four years ago, complicated by a postoperative wound infection that required prolonged packing. The bulge protrudes when he stands or strains and partially reduces when supine. He reports dull aching and dragging discomfort by the end of the day but denies acute severe pain, nausea, vomiting, obstipation, or skin changes overlying the bulge. PMH: type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.1%), hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea, and obesity (BMI 38 kg/m²). Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former 30-pack-year smoker, quit 1 year ago; drinks alcohol occasionally; works a sedentary desk job. Family history: father with colon cancer at age 70. He is motivated to lose weight and wants a durable repair.
cases.exam: Obese abdomen with a healed midline laparotomy scar. A large reducible bulge (approximately 10 cm wide) protrudes along the mid-to-lower scar with Valsalva, with a palpable fascial defect estimated at 8-10 cm transverse width. The overlying skin is intact without erythema, warmth, or ulceration. The mass is soft, non-tender, and easily reduces when supine with an audible gurgle. No cough impulse at the groin; femoral and inguinal regions are normal. Bowel sounds are normal and active. No peritoneal signs. No incarceration or skin tethering.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to characterize this hernia and plan repair?
options: Reassure the patient and recommend an abdominal binder as definitive management
Obtain a CT scan of the abdomen
Order an abdominal ultrasound as the definitive preoperative study for large defects
Proceed directly to open primary suture repair without imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: For a large complex incisional hernia, a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis is the imaging study of choice. It precisely measures the fascial defect width, identifies hernia contents, assesses for loss of domain (hernia sac volume relative to abdominal cavity), and helps plan whether component separation or mesh will be needed. Direct primary suture repair (choice 1) of a large defect has unacceptably high recurrence and ignores planning needs. A binder (choice 3) is only a temporizing/symptom measure, not definitive. Ultrasound (choice 4) is operator-dependent and inadequate for mapping large midline defects and loss of domain; CT is far superior for surgical planning.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis (without contrast):
Midline ventral incisional hernia with a transverse fascial defect of 9 cm
Hernia sac contains small bowel loops and omentum, easily reducible, no obstruction
No bowel wall thickening, no mesenteric stranding, no transition point
Hernia sac volume approximately 18% of total peritoneal volume (loss of domain not severe)
Rectus muscles laterally retracted; linea alba widened
No free air or fluid collection

stages.label: PREOPERATIVE OPTIMIZATION
stages.question: Before scheduling elective repair, which modifiable factor is most important to optimize to reduce recurrence and wound complications?
options: Discontinue atorvastatin to reduce surgical bleeding risk as the immediate next step
Optimize glycemic control and counsel on weight loss
Start prophylactic broad-spectrum oral antibiotics for two weeks preoperatively
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent perioperative DVT in this setting
stages.explanation: Glycemic control (HbA1c 8.1% is elevated), weight reduction (BMI 38), and confirmed smoking cessation are the highest-yield modifiable factors. Poor glucose control, obesity, and active smoking all markedly increase surgical site infection, wound dehiscence, and hernia recurrence. Statins (choice 1) should be continued and are not a relevant bleeding concern. Prolonged preoperative oral antibiotics (choice 3) do not prevent SSI and promote resistance; perioperative prophylaxis is given at induction. Routine therapeutic anticoagulation (choice 4) is not indicated electively and increases bleeding; mechanical and standard pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis is used perioperatively, not therapeutic dosing.

stages.label: OPERATIVE PLANNING
stages.question: For durable repair of this 9 cm midline defect with laterally retracted rectus muscles, which technique best achieves tension-free midline fascial reapproximation?
options: Posterior component separation with retromuscular sublay mesh
Laparoscopic intraperitoneal bridging mesh leaving the defect open with no fascial closure
Onlay placement of mesh over the skin without closing the fascia in this setting
Open primary suture repair of the fascia without mesh as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A large midline defect with lateralized rectus muscles benefits from component separation to mobilize the abdominal wall and restore the linea alba tension-free, combined with retromuscular (sublay/Rives-Stoppa) mesh reinforcement, which has the lowest recurrence and good wound outcomes. Posterior component separation via transversus abdominis release is a workhorse for this. Primary suture repair alone (choice 1) has very high recurrence for defects this size. Onlay mesh over skin without fascial closure (choice 3) is not a real anatomic repair. Bridging mesh without fascial reapproximation (choice 4) leaves the defect open, restores neither function nor the linea alba, and has higher recurrence/bulging than mesh-reinforced primary fascial closure.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Two days after an elective retromuscular mesh repair with component separation, the patient is recovering. What is the most important early postoperative consideration specific to this repair?
options: Immediate high-intensity core abdominal exercises to strengthen the repair with appropriate follow-up
Monitor for abdominal compartment syndrome and surgical site occurrence
Remove all closed-suction drains within the first 12 hours postoperatively with appropriate follow-up
Keep the patient strictly NPO for one week regardless of bowel function with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After large abdominal wall reconstruction, returning viscera to a tightened cavity can raise intra-abdominal pressure, so monitoring for abdominal compartment syndrome (oliguria, rising airway pressures, abdominal distension) is critical, along with watching for surgical site occurrences (seroma, infection) given the large dissection. Early ambulation and pulmonary hygiene reduce VTE and atelectasis. Heavy core exercise (choice 1) is contraindicated early and risks disruption. Strict prolonged NPO (choice 3) is unnecessary; diet advances with return of bowel function.
