Drains (choice 4) are left until output is low to prevent seroma; removing them at 12 hours is premature and increases fluid collections.

cases.diagnosis: Large midline incisional (ventral) hernia requiring component separation and retromuscular mesh repair
pearls: CT abdomen/pelvis is the best study for large incisional hernias to measure defect width, contents, and loss of domain for surgical planning.
Optimize glycemic control, weight, and smoking cessation before elective repair to cut recurrence and wound complications.
Mesh-reinforced repair with tension-free midline fascial closure (often via component separation/TAR with retromuscular sublay) has the lowest recurrence for large defects.
After major abdominal wall reconstruction, watch for abdominal compartment syndrome and surgical site occurrences.

cases.title: An Elderly Woman With Vomiting and Inner-Thigh Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of crampy abdominal pain, vomiting, and pain shooting down the inside of her right thigh.
cases.history: An 82-year-old thin, frail woman is brought to the ED with two days of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, nausea, and several episodes of bilious vomiting. She has not passed flatus or stool since yesterday. She also reports an aching pain radiating along the inner aspect of her right thigh toward the knee that worsens when she straightens or rotates the leg and eases with hip flexion. She has lost about 7 kg over the past year and describes herself as 'just skin and bones.' PMH: chronic constipation, osteoporosis, hypertension, and prior multiple vaginal deliveries. She has had no prior abdominal surgery. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, calcium with vitamin D, and as-needed senna. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: widowed, lives alone, independent but recently more sedentary; no tobacco or alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She appears thin and uncomfortable, frequently keeping her right hip slightly flexed. There is no visible groin bulge.
cases.exam: Thin, mildly dehydrated woman in intermittent distress. Abdomen is distended and tympanitic with high-pitched, intermittently 'rushing' bowel sounds; diffuse mild tenderness without rebound or guarding. No palpable inguinal or femoral bulge bilaterally; no cough impulse appreciated at the groin. With the right hip extended and internally rotated, she reports medial thigh/knee pain (positive Howship-Romberg sign). Rectal exam: empty vault, no mass, no gross blood. Mucous membranes are dry. No peritoneal signs at present. Distal pulses intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given small bowel obstruction with a positive Howship-Romberg sign in a thin elderly woman without prior surgery, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Colonoscopy to evaluate for an obstructing colonic mass in this clinical context
Reassure and discharge with a laxative regimen for presumed constipation
Upper GI series with barium to evaluate gastric outlet in this clinical context
CT scan of the abdomen
stages.explanation: The presentation—SBO without prior surgery (so adhesions are unlikely), a thin multiparous elderly woman, and a positive Howship-Romberg sign—is classic for an obturator hernia, which is best identified with CT abdomen/pelvis. CT shows a loop of bowel between the pectineus and obturator externus muscles and confirms the level of obstruction. Discharge with laxatives (choice 1) is dangerous given obstruction and strangulation risk. Colonoscopy (choice 3) evaluates colon, not the small-bowel obstruction or hernia, and is risky in acute SBO. An upper GI barium study (choice 4) is inappropriate in suspected mechanical SBO and barium can complicate management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Hgb 12.1 g/dL
Na 133 mEq/L
K 3.3 mEq/L
Cl 92 mEq/L
HCO3 30 mEq/L
BUN 32 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Lactate 2.4 mmol/L
CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels and a transition point in the right pelvis; a loop of small bowel is incarcerated within the right obturator canal between the pectineus and obturator externus muscles; mild surrounding fat stranding; no free air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding on the CT most specifically confirms an obturator hernia as the cause of obstruction?
options: A dilated, fluid-filled appendix with a fecalith as the primary diagnosis
A bowel loop trapped within the obturator canal between the pectineus
Dilated small bowel loops with multiple air-fluid levels as the primary diagnosis
Free intraperitoneal air under the diaphragm as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The pathognomonic CT finding is bowel herniating through the obturator canal, seen as a loop wedged between the pectineus and obturator externus (or externus/internus) muscles—this localizes the hernia precisely. Dilated loops with air-fluid levels (choice 1) confirm obstruction but are nonspecific to cause. Free air under the diaphragm (choice 3) indicates perforation, not the hernia itself, and is absent here. A dilated appendix with fecalith (choice 4) describes appendicitis, an unrelated diagnosis. CT's ability to show the bowel in the obturator canal is why it is the key test for this often-occult hernia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and nasogastric decompression, what is the definitive management?
options: Outpatient follow-up with serial abdominal exams as initial therapy
Prompt surgical exploration with reduction
Therapeutic enema and aggressive bowel regimen as initial therapy
Long-term nasogastric decompression alone until obstruction resolves
stages.explanation: An incarcerated obturator hernia causing bowel obstruction requires prompt operative intervention—reduction of the herniated bowel, resection of any nonviable segment, and repair/closure of the obturator defect (with mesh or primary suture, often via a laparotomy or laparoscopic approach). Strangulation risk is high because the canal is narrow and rigid. Outpatient follow-up (choice 1) risks bowel necrosis and death. NG decompression alone (choice 3) is supportive only; the incarcerated hernia will not resolve and can strangulate. Enemas/bowel regimens (choice 4) treat constipation, not a mechanical incarcerated hernia, and delay needed surgery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Intraoperatively, the incarcerated small bowel segment is dusky and does not regain color, peristalsis, or pulsatile mesenteric flow after warming and reduction. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Perform only a diverting loop ileostomy without resecting the dead segment
Resect the nonviable bowel segment
Return the dusky bowel to the abdomen and close, planning to reassess in clinic
Leave the bowel in the obturator canal to tamponade bleeding as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Bowel that remains dusky with no return of color, peristalsis, or mesenteric pulsation is nonviable and must be resected, typically followed by primary anastomosis in a stable patient, after which the obturator defect is repaired. Returning dead bowel to the abdomen (choice 1) leads to perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis. Leaving bowel in the canal to 'tamponade' (choice 3) is not a recognized maneuver and perpetuates strangulation. A diverting ileostomy without resecting necrotic bowel (choice 4) leaves dead tissue in situ, causing ongoing sepsis—the necrotic segment must be removed.

stages.label: PEARL CHECK
stages.question: Which patient profile is most characteristic of the classic 'little old lady' obturator hernia?
options: A middle-aged man with a longstanding reducible groin bulge
An emaciated, multiparous, elderly woman with recent weight loss
A muscular young athlete with groin strain in this setting
A young, obese man with a chronic cough in this setting
stages.explanation: Obturator hernias classically occur in thin, elderly, multiparous women who have lost weight (loss of the protective obturator fat pad and a wider female pelvis predispose them)—hence the eponym 'little old lady's hernia.' Choice 1 (obese young man with cough) fits risk factors for inguinal hernia, not obturator. A young athlete with groin strain (choice 3) suggests musculotendinous injury or sports hernia. A middle-aged man with a chronic reducible groin bulge (choice 4) describes a typical inguinal hernia. Recognizing this demographic plus the Howship-Romberg sign is key to diagnosing this often-missed hernia early.

cases.diagnosis: Incarcerated right obturator hernia causing small bowel obstruction
pearls: Suspect an obturator hernia in a thin, elderly, multiparous woman with SBO and no prior abdominal surgery.
The Howship-Romberg sign (medial thigh/knee pain worsened by hip extension/internal rotation) reflects obturator nerve compression and is a key clue.
CT abdomen/pelvis is diagnostic, showing bowel in the obturator canal between pectineus and obturator externus.
Obturator hernias have a high strangulation rate due to the narrow rigid canal—prompt surgical repair is required.

cases.title: An Intermittent Bulge on the Side of the Belly
cases.chiefComplaint: A bulge on the lower side of her abdomen that comes and goes, with localized aching.
cases.history: A 57-year-old woman presents with a several-month history of an intermittent bulge along the lower right side of her abdomen, lateral to her belly button. The bulge appears when she stands, coughs, or strains and disappears when she lies down; it is accompanied by a dull aching discomfort at the same spot. She has felt occasional sharp twinges but no nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. She finds the bulge frustrating because it is sometimes there and sometimes not, and a prior provider could not feel anything on exam and attributed it to muscle strain. PMH: obesity (BMI 33), two prior pregnancies, and well-controlled hypertension. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Allergies: sulfa (hives—note thiazide tolerated per chart, longstanding). Social: works as a teacher, lifts boxes frequently; nonsmoker; minimal alcohol. Family history: mother with 'a hernia repair.' She is anxious about whether this is serious and whether it needs surgery. She points consistently to a spot lateral to the rectus muscle, partway between the umbilicus and the iliac crest.
cases.exam: Obese abdomen. At rest, no obvious bulge is seen. With Valsalva while standing, a small, subtle bulge appears along the right semilunar line (lateral border of the rectus, below the level of the umbilicus) with a vague palpable defect and reproducible localized tenderness. The bulge reduces when supine. No overlying skin changes, erythema, or warmth. Bowel sounds normal. No peritoneal signs. Inguinal and femoral regions are normal with no cough impulse. The mass is non-pulsatile and reduces easily without tenderness on reduction.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given an intermittent bulge along the semilunar line that is hard to detect on exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain dynamic abdominal ultrasound , or CT if equivocal, to confirm a fascial defect
Reassure that an intermittent bulge is benign and requires no further workup
Start a muscle relaxant and physical therapy for presumed muscle strain
Refer immediately for emergent surgery without imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: A Spigelian hernia is often occult on exam because the defect is small and lies beneath the intact external oblique aponeurosis. Dynamic ultrasound performed with Valsalva is an excellent first test to demonstrate the fascial defect and protruding sac; CT is used if ultrasound is equivocal and to map anatomy. Reassurance alone (choice 2) misses a hernia with real incarceration risk. Emergent surgery without imaging (choice 3) is inappropriate for an intermittent reducible bulge without obstruction. Muscle relaxants/PT (choice 4) treat the wrong diagnosis and delay identification of a hernia that warrants repair.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Dynamic abdominal ultrasound (with Valsalva):
A fascial defect (approximately 1.8 cm) along the right semilunar line at the level of the arcuate line
Protrusion of preperitoneal fat and a small bowel-containing sac through the defect with Valsalva
The defect lies deep to an intact external oblique aponeurosis (interparietal location)
Sac reduces with recumbency; no free fluid
No bowel wall thickening or hyperemia to suggest strangulation

stages.label: ANATOMY
stages.question: Where does a Spigelian hernia characteristically occur, and why is it often difficult to palpate?
options: Through the Spigelian fascia along the semilunar line, often beneath an intact external oblique aponeurosis
Directly through the floor of Hesselbach's triangle in the groin as the immediate next step
Through the femoral canal below the inguinal ligament, hidden by the femoral sheath in this setting
At the umbilicus through a patent umbilical ring as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A Spigelian hernia protrudes through the Spigelian fascia (the aponeurotic layer along the semilunar line, the lateral border of the rectus), most commonly near the arcuate line. Because the sac frequently remains beneath an intact external oblique aponeurosis (an interparietal/interstitial location), it dissects between muscle layers rather than producing an obvious subcutaneous bulge—making it hard to palpate. Choice 1 describes a femoral hernia. Choice 3 describes an umbilical hernia. Choice 4 (floor of Hesselbach's triangle) describes a direct inguinal hernia. The interparietal nature explains why imaging is often needed to confirm a Spigelian hernia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The hernia is confirmed and reducible without signs of strangulation. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Elective surgical repair, because Spigelian hernias have a relatively high risk of incarceration
Repeat imaging in one year and operate only if it enlarges as initial therapy
Abdominal binder alone as definitive treatment as initial therapy
Observation with watchful waiting and no planned repair, given it is reducible as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Spigelian hernias carry a comparatively high incarceration/strangulation risk because the defect is small with firm fascial edges; elective surgical repair is therefore generally recommended once diagnosed, even if currently reducible. Watchful waiting (choice 1) is reasonable for some small minimally symptomatic ventral hernias but is less appropriate here given the incarceration risk and the patient's symptoms. A binder (choice 3) does not address the defect and is not definitive. Deferring with surveillance imaging (choice 4) exposes the patient to ongoing incarceration risk; repair (often laparoscopic with mesh) is preferred.

stages.label: OPERATIVE APPROACH
stages.question: Which surgical approach is commonly favored for an uncomplicated Spigelian hernia and offers the advantage of confirming the defect and reinforcing it with mesh?
options: Percutaneous drainage of the hernia sac in this setting
Open onlay skin graft over the bulge in this setting
Laparoscopic repair with mesh reinforcement of the defect
Large open midline laparotomy with primary suture closure only
stages.explanation: Laparoscopic repair with mesh is commonly favored for Spigelian hernias: it allows direct visualization to confirm the often-occult defect, enables mesh reinforcement (lower recurrence than suture alone), and avoids a large incision. A midline laparotomy with suture-only repair (choice 1) is unnecessarily morbid and primary suture alone has higher recurrence. Percutaneous drainage (choice 3) is meaningless for a hernia (there is nothing to drain). An onlay skin graft (choice 4) is not a hernia repair. Open repair is also acceptable, but among the options, laparoscopic mesh repair is the best described technique.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If this patient instead presented with the bulge becoming firm, exquisitely tender, irreducible, and associated with vomiting, what would be the appropriate disposition?
options: Schedule elective outpatient repair in several weeks with appropriate follow-up
Prescribe oral antibiotics and reassess in one week with appropriate follow-up
Emergent evaluation and urgent surgical repair for incarceration/possible strangulation
Apply continuous manual pressure at home until it reduces with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A firm, exquisitely tender, irreducible bulge with vomiting signals incarceration with possible strangulation/obstruction—a surgical emergency requiring prompt evaluation, resuscitation, and urgent operative repair (with bowel resection if nonviable). Elective scheduling (choice 1) is unsafe when strangulation is suspected. Continuous home manual pressure (choice 3) risks reducing nonviable bowel and delays care. Oral antibiotics with delayed reassessment (choice 4) do not treat mechanical incarceration and risk bowel necrosis. Recognizing the transition from reducible to incarcerated is essential to timely surgical care.

cases.diagnosis: Spigelian hernia of the right semilunar line
pearls: Spigelian hernias occur through the Spigelian fascia along the semilunar line, typically near the arcuate line, and are often interparietal (beneath an intact external oblique), making them hard to palpate.
Dynamic ultrasound with Valsalva (or CT if equivocal) confirms the often-occult defect.
Because of a relatively high incarceration risk, elective repair is generally recommended once diagnosed.
Laparoscopic mesh repair allows confirmation of the defect and durable reinforcement.

cases.title: An Abnormal Spot on a Routine Screening Mammogram
cases.chiefComplaint: Called back after a routine screening mammogram showed an abnormal area in the breast.
cases.history: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is referred to the breast clinic after a routine screening mammogram flagged a suspicious area in the upper outer quadrant of her left breast. She feels well and has not noticed a lump, pain, nipple discharge, or skin changes herself. She underwent menarche at age 12 and menopause at age 51, had her first child at 30, and never used hormone replacement therapy. PMH: hypertension and hypothyroidism. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social: drinks one glass of wine most nights; never smoked; exercises occasionally. Family history: a maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 65; no known BRCA mutations in the family. She is understandably anxious and wants to know what the next steps are. She has had prior normal screening mammograms and performs occasional self-exams without prior concern.
cases.exam: Both breasts are symmetric without skin dimpling, retraction, erythema, or peau d'orange. On careful palpation of the left upper outer quadrant, there is a vague firmness but no discrete dominant mass; the right breast is unremarkable. Nipples are everted bilaterally with no spontaneous or expressible discharge. No axillary, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy is palpable bilaterally. No chest wall tenderness. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammogram (left breast):
Upper outer quadrant 1.4 cm spiculated mass with associated pleomorphic microcalcifications
No skin thickening; no nipple retraction
BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy)
Targeted ultrasound: 1.3 cm irregular hypoechoic mass with angular margins and posterior acoustic shadowing; no suspicious axillary nodes identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish a tissue diagnosis of this BI-RADS 5 spiculated mass?
options: Fine-needle aspiration for cytology only
Image-guided core needle biopsy of the mass
Six-month follow-up mammogram to assess for change
Excisional biopsy (lumpectomy) as the first diagnostic step
stages.explanation: Image-guided core needle biopsy is the standard next step for a suspicious (BI-RADS 4/5) breast lesion: it provides histology, distinguishes invasive from in situ disease, and yields receptor status (ER/PR/HER2) to guide treatment—all while being minimally invasive. Six-month follow-up (choice 2) is appropriate for BI-RADS 3 (probably benign), not BI-RADS 5. Excisional biopsy as the first step (choice 3) is more invasive and may compromise subsequent oncologic surgical planning; core biopsy should come first. FNA cytology (choice 4) cannot reliably distinguish invasive from in situ disease or provide architecture and is inferior to core biopsy here.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Core needle biopsy (left breast mass):
Invasive ductal carcinoma (invasive carcinoma of no special type), Nottingham grade 2
Estrogen receptor: positive (90%)
Progesterone receptor: positive (70%)
HER2: negative (IHC 0)
Ki-67: 15%
No lymphovascular invasion identified on core

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: With biopsy-proven invasive ductal carcinoma and a clinically negative axilla, which is the appropriate approach to assess regional nodal status at surgery?
options: Routine complete axillary lymph node dissection for all patients
Sentinel lymph node biopsy
Bilateral mastectomy with removal of all axillary nodes regardless of findings
No nodal evaluation is needed for invasive cancer in this setting
stages.explanation: For invasive breast cancer with a clinically and radiologically negative axilla, sentinel lymph node biopsy is the standard to assess nodal status with low morbidity, sparing most patients a full axillary dissection. Routine complete axillary dissection in all patients (choice 1) causes unnecessary lymphedema and is reserved for selected node-positive disease. Skipping nodal evaluation (choice 3) is incorrect—nodal status is critical for staging and prognosis in invasive cancer. Bilateral mastectomy with full axillary clearance (choice 4) is overtreatment for unilateral early-stage cancer without a genetic/clinical indication.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The tumor is 1.3 cm, unifocal, with favorable breast-to-tumor ratio and the patient desires breast preservation. What is the most appropriate local surgical management?
options: Breast-conserving surgery with sentinel node biopsy followed by whole-breast radiation
Total mastectomy without radiation as initial therapy
Observation with endocrine therapy alone and no surgery as initial therapy
Cryoablation of the tumor as definitive curative therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a small, unifocal invasive cancer with adequate breast-to-tumor ratio in a patient who wants breast preservation, breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with sentinel node biopsy plus adjuvant whole-breast radiation gives survival equivalent to mastectomy with better cosmesis. Total mastectomy (choice 1) is more than needed when conservation is feasible and the patient prefers it. Endocrine therapy alone without surgery (choice 3) is not standard curative treatment for operable invasive cancer (it is sometimes used in frail elderly patients, not as first choice here). Cryoablation (choice 4) is investigational and not standard definitive therapy for invasive breast cancer.

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: Given ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma in this postmenopausal woman, which systemic adjuvant therapy is most appropriate?
options: Endocrine therapy with an aromatase inhibitor
No systemic therapy is ever indicated for hormone receptor-positive disease
Trastuzumab-based HER2-targeted therapy as the immediate next step
High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue
stages.explanation: Hormone receptor-positive (ER/PR+) breast cancer is treated with adjuvant endocrine therapy; in a postmenopausal woman, an aromatase inhibitor (e.g., anastrozole, letrozole) is a standard choice to reduce recurrence. Trastuzumab (choice 2) targets HER2 and is only used in HER2-positive disease—this tumor is HER2-negative. Saying no systemic therapy is ever indicated (choice 3) is wrong; endocrine therapy substantially reduces recurrence in ER+ disease. High-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue (choice 4) is not standard for early breast cancer and carries excessive toxicity. Whether to add chemotherapy depends on genomic risk scores, but endocrine therapy is clearly indicated here.

cases.diagnosis: Early-stage invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast (ER+/PR+/HER2-)
pearls: A BI-RADS 4 or 5 mammographic lesion warrants image-guided core needle biopsy, which provides histology and receptor status.
Sentinel lymph node biopsy is standard for staging a clinically node-negative axilla in invasive breast cancer.
Breast-conserving surgery plus whole-breast radiation offers survival equivalent to mastectomy for appropriate early-stage tumors.
ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative disease is treated with adjuvant endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor in postmenopausal women).

cases.title: A Red, Swollen Breast That Won't Improve With Antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly worsening redness, swelling, and warmth of the breast that did not improve with antibiotics.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 5-week history of progressive redness, swelling, warmth, and heaviness of her right breast. Her primary provider initially diagnosed mastitis and prescribed two courses of oral antibiotics (cephalexin then amoxicillin-clavulanate) without improvement; if anything, the breast has enlarged and the skin has taken on a dimpled, orange-peel appearance. She denies fever, recent breastfeeding (her last child is 18), trauma, or insect bite. She reports no discrete lump but notes the whole breast feels firmer and looks different. PMH: type 2 diabetes and obesity. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker; drinks alcohol socially; works as an accountant. Family history: mother had breast cancer at 62. She is frustrated that the antibiotics 'did nothing' and worried because the changes keep advancing. She is perimenopausal with irregular periods and has never had a mammogram outside of routine screening.
cases.exam: The right breast is diffusely enlarged, erythematous, warm, and edematous with prominent peau d'orange (orange-peel) skin texture involving more than one-third of the breast. There is no fluctuant abscess and no purulent nipple discharge; the nipple appears slightly retracted. No single discrete dominant mass is palpable, but the breast is diffusely indurated. Firm, mobile right axillary lymph nodes are palpable. The patient is afebrile and not systemically toxic. The contralateral breast is normal. No supraclavicular adenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a red, swollen breast with peau d'orange that failed antibiotics in a non-lactating 50-year-old, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe a third, broader-spectrum course of antibiotics and reassess in two weeks
Reassure the patient that this is resolving mastitis and observe as the first step today
Promptly obtain diagnostic breast imaging
Incise and drain the breast for presumed deep abscess as the first step today
stages.explanation: A non-lactating woman with rapidly progressive erythema, edema, and peau d'orange that fails antibiotics must be evaluated for inflammatory breast cancer without delay. The next step is prompt diagnostic imaging (mammogram and ultrasound) plus biopsy, including a skin punch biopsy and core biopsy of any underlying lesion, to look for dermal lymphatic invasion and confirm carcinoma. Another antibiotic course (choice 1) only delays diagnosis—the hallmark is failure to improve on antibiotics. Reassurance/observation (choice 3) is dangerous. Empiric incision and drainage (choice 4) is inappropriate when there is no fluctuant abscess and high suspicion for malignancy.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammogram: diffuse skin thickening and increased breast density of the right breast; trabecular thickening; no discrete spiculated mass
Ultrasound: skin thickening with dilated dermal lymphatics; enlarged hypoechoic right axillary lymph nodes
Skin punch biopsy: invasive carcinoma with tumor emboli within dermal lymphatic channels
Core biopsy of breast parenchyma: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3; ER negative, PR negative, HER2 positive (IHC 3+)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which pathologic finding confirms the clinical diagnosis of inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Tumor emboli within dermal lymphatic channels on skin biopsy
Benign ductal hyperplasia without atypia
Granulomatous inflammation with no malignant cells
A 2 cm spiculated mass with microcalcifications
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is a clinicopathologic diagnosis; the classic confirmatory finding is tumor emboli (carcinoma cells) within the dermal lymphatics, which obstruct lymphatic drainage and produce the erythema and peau d'orange. A spiculated mass with calcifications (choice 1) describes a typical invasive cancer but is not the defining feature of IBC, which often lacks a discrete mass. Granulomatous inflammation (choice 3) suggests granulomatous mastitis, not IBC. Benign ductal hyperplasia (choice 4) is not cancer at all. Importantly, even if dermal lymphatic invasion is not seen on a given biopsy, the diagnosis can still be made clinically—but its presence is confirmatory.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Inflammatory breast cancer is confirmed. What is the minimum clinical (cT) stage, and what initial workup is essential before treatment?
options: At least stage IIIB
Stage I; proceed directly to lumpectomy as the immediate next step
Stage IV by definition in all cases; begin hospice discussion
Stage 0 (DCIS); no systemic staging needed as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is classified as T4d, making it at least clinical stage IIIB at presentation (and frequently node-positive). Because of its aggressiveness and high metastatic potential, systemic staging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan, or PET/CT) is essential to evaluate for distant disease before treatment. It is not stage 0/DCIS (choice 1)—it is invasive with dermal lymphatic involvement. It is never stage I or treated with upfront lumpectomy (choice 3); breast conservation is contraindicated in IBC. It is not stage IV by definition (choice 4)—many cases are nonmetastatic (stage III) and treated with curative intent.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows no distant metastases. What is the correct sequence of multimodal therapy for nonmetastatic inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Upfront breast-conserving lumpectomy, then chemotherapy as initial therapy
Radiation alone without surgery or systemic therapy as initial therapy
Neoadjuvant systemic therapy , then modified radical mastectomy, then radiation
Endocrine therapy alone given the patient's age as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Nonmetastatic IBC is treated with a trimodality approach in a specific order: neoadjuvant systemic therapy first (chemotherapy, plus HER2-targeted agents like trastuzumab/pertuzumab for HER2-positive disease), followed by modified radical mastectomy (not breast conservation), then post-mastectomy radiation. Upfront lumpectomy (choice 1) is contraindicated—breast conservation is not appropriate in IBC and systemic therapy must come first. Radiation alone (choice 3) omits essential systemic and surgical therapy. Endocrine therapy alone (choice 4) is inadequate and inappropriate here (the tumor is ER-negative and IBC requires aggressive multimodal treatment).

stages.label: PEARL CHECK
stages.question: Which feature most reliably distinguishes inflammatory breast cancer from simple mastitis at presentation?
options: Presence of breast erythema and warmth as the immediate next step
Rapid progression with peau d'orange that fails to resolve with antibiotics in a non-lactating woman
Mild breast tenderness as the immediate next step
A normal contralateral breast as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Both mastitis and IBC cause erythema and warmth, so those overlap; the discriminating clue is rapidly progressive peau d'orange that does NOT respond to antibiotics, especially in a non-lactating, often older woman—this should trigger evaluation for IBC. Erythema and warmth alone (choice 1) are nonspecific and present in both. Mild tenderness (choice 3) is also nonspecific. A normal contralateral breast (choice 4) is expected in both unilateral mastitis and IBC and does not distinguish them. The teaching point: any 'mastitis' that fails to improve on antibiotics warrants biopsy to exclude IBC.

cases.diagnosis: Inflammatory breast cancer (T4d, HER2-positive)
pearls: Inflammatory breast cancer presents with rapid erythema, edema, and peau d'orange and is frequently mistaken for mastitis—suspect it when 'mastitis' fails antibiotics in a non-lactating woman.
Dermal lymphatic tumor emboli are the confirmatory pathologic hallmark, but IBC remains a clinical diagnosis.
IBC is staged as T4d (at least stage IIIB); always obtain systemic staging for distant metastases.
Treat nonmetastatic IBC with neoadjuvant systemic therapy, then modified radical mastectomy, then radiation—breast conservation is contraindicated.

cases.title: A Painful Lump in the Breast While Breastfeeding
cases.chiefComplaint: A painful, swollen, red lump in one breast that has worsened over several days while breastfeeding.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman who is 6 weeks postpartum and breastfeeding presents to the ED with 4 days of worsening pain, swelling, and redness in her left breast. It began as a tender firm area she thought was a clogged duct, but it has grown more painful, the skin is now red and hot, and she feels a soft, tender spot in the center. She has had subjective fevers and chills and feels run down. She has been continuing to breastfeed on the right but has been avoiding the painful left breast, which feels increasingly engorged. PMH: none. Medications: prenatal vitamins. Allergies: none. Social: married, first baby, no tobacco or alcohol while breastfeeding. Family history: noncontributory. She is worried about whether she can keep breastfeeding and whether the baby is safe. She reports cracked, sore nipples on the left from latch difficulties over the past two weeks.
cases.exam: Left breast with a 4 cm area of erythema, warmth, and induration in the upper outer quadrant; centrally there is a tender, fluctuant area suggesting a fluid collection. The overlying skin is intact without necrosis. The left nipple has a small cracked fissure. There is mild tender left axillary adenopathy. The right breast is engorged but without focal erythema or fluctuance. No peau d'orange of either breast. The patient is uncomfortable but not toxic-appearing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm whether a drainable collection is present?
options: Breast ultrasound
Skin punch biopsy of the erythematous area
MRI of the breast with contrast
Diagnostic mammogram with compression
stages.explanation: Breast ultrasound is the test of choice to evaluate a suspected lactational breast abscess: it confirms a fluid collection, characterizes its size and location, and can guide aspiration. It is fast, radiation-free, and ideal in a young lactating woman. MRI (choice 1) is unnecessary and not first-line for a suspected abscess. Mammography (choice 3) is painful in an acutely inflamed lactating breast, less useful for distinguishing fluid, and not first-line here. Skin punch biopsy (choice 4) would be considered for suspected inflammatory breast cancer in a non-lactating patient who fails antibiotics—not for an acute lactational abscess with clear infectious features.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Breast ultrasound: 3.2 cm well-circumscribed hypoechoic collection with internal debris and posterior acoustic enhancement in the left upper outer quadrant, consistent with an abscess; surrounding hyperemia
No solid suspicious mass identified
WBC 13.0 ×10³/µL
The most likely causative organism is Staphylococcus aureus

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 3.2 cm lactational breast abscess, what is the preferred initial management?
options: Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration plus oral antibiotics, with possible repeat aspiration
Mastectomy of the affected breast as initial therapy
Oral antibiotics alone without any drainage as initial therapy
Wide surgical incision and drainage in the operating room as first-line as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For most lactational breast abscesses (especially smaller, accessible ones), ultrasound-guided needle aspiration combined with antibiotics is preferred—it is less invasive than incision and drainage, preserves cosmesis, and allows continued breastfeeding; aspiration may be repeated if the collection reaccumulates. Formal incision and drainage (choice 1) is reserved for large, multiloculated, or skin-compromised abscesses or aspiration failures, not routine first-line. Antibiotics alone (choice 3) are insufficient once a drainable collection exists. Mastectomy (choice 4) is grossly inappropriate for a benign infectious abscess.

stages.label: ANTIBIOTIC CHOICE
stages.question: Which empiric oral antibiotic best covers the most likely organism in a lactational abscess while remaining compatible with breastfeeding?
options: Oral vancomycin as the immediate next step
Ciprofloxacin as the immediate next step
Dicloxacillin or cephalexin
Metronidazole monotherapy in this setting
stages.explanation: Lactational abscesses are usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus, so a beta-lactamase-stable penicillin (dicloxacillin) or a first-generation cephalosporin (cephalexin) is appropriate empiric therapy, with coverage broadened (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin) if MRSA is a concern—and these are compatible with breastfeeding. Oral vancomycin (choice 2) is not systemically absorbed (used for C. difficile) and would not treat a breast infection. Metronidazole monotherapy (choice 3) targets anaerobes and does not cover S. aureus adequately. Ciprofloxacin (choice 4) is not first-line for staph and fluoroquinolones are generally avoided when better options exist.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate breastfeeding advice for this mother during treatment of her abscess?
options: Continue breastfeeding or expressing milk from the affected breast to maintain drainage, as it is safe to continue
Discard all milk indefinitely and switch the baby to formula permanently as the immediate next step
Breastfeed only from the affected side to 'clear the infection' through the baby as the immediate next step
Stop breastfeeding entirely and suppress lactation immediately as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Mothers should continue breastfeeding or pumping/expressing from the affected breast—this maintains milk drainage, relieves engorgement, speeds resolution, and is safe for the infant even with a treated abscess; emptying the breast is part of therapy. Stopping abruptly (choice 1) worsens engorgement and stasis and can prolong infection. Permanently discarding milk and switching to formula (choice 3) is unnecessary. Breastfeeding 'only' from the infected side as a cure (choice 4) is incorrect framing—the goal is regular drainage of the affected breast, not selectively nursing only on it. Brief interruption is only needed if pain prevents feeding, in which case the breast should still be expressed.

cases.diagnosis: Lactational breast abscess (Staphylococcus aureus)
pearls: Breast ultrasound is the test of choice to confirm and characterize a suspected breast abscess and to guide aspiration.
Most lactational abscesses are managed with ultrasound-guided needle aspiration plus antibiotics; incision and drainage is reserved for large, multiloculated, or skin-compromised abscesses.
Lactational abscesses are usually due to S. aureus—treat with dicloxacillin or cephalexin, broadening for MRSA when indicated.
Encourage continued breastfeeding or expression from the affected breast; it is safe and aids drainage.

cases.title: A Persistent Scaly, Itchy Nipple
cases.chiefComplaint: A scaly, crusted, itchy change of the nipple that has not healed for months.
cases.history: A 61-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 4-month history of a red, scaly, crusted change involving her right nipple that began as itching and mild burning. She tried over-the-counter hydrocortisone and emollients, which gave only transient relief, and the lesion has slowly enlarged to involve the nipple and adjacent areola. She has noticed occasional slight serosanguinous discharge and flaking. She denies a palpable lump, breast pain, or systemic symptoms. PMH: hypertension and osteopenia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social: never smoked, rare alcohol, retired librarian. Family history: sister with breast cancer at 58. She was reassured at an urgent care visit that it was 'eczema,' but it has persisted and now affects the nipple itself. She is worried because it keeps coming back despite creams. Her last mammogram two years ago was reported as normal.
cases.exam: Right nipple-areolar complex with an erythematous, scaly, eczematous, crusted, slightly eroded plaque that involves and is centered on the nipple, extending onto the medial areola; there is mild serous crusting. The nipple is not frankly retracted but is distorted at the lesion. No discrete palpable breast mass and no axillary lymphadenopathy. The left breast and nipple are normal. Importantly, the lesion involves the nipple itself rather than sparing it. No peau d'orange.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A persistent, eczematous, crusted lesion centered on the nipple that has not resolved with topical steroids should prompt which next step?
options: Antifungal cream for presumed candidal dermatitis as the first step today
Full-thickness punch/wedge biopsy of the nipple lesion plus bilateral diagnostic mammography
Reassurance that nipple eczema is benign and observe for a year as the first step today
Another 6-week trial of a more potent topical corticosteroid as the first step today
stages.explanation: A persistent eczematous nipple lesion that fails topical therapy must be biopsied to exclude Paget disease of the nipple. A full-thickness punch or wedge biopsy of the nipple lesion provides diagnosis, and bilateral diagnostic mammography (with ultrasound/MRI as needed) evaluates for underlying DCIS or invasive cancer. Another steroid trial (choice 1) dangerously delays diagnosis—the hallmark is a lesion that does not heal. Reassurance/observation (choice 3) risks progression of an underlying malignancy. Empiric antifungal therapy (choice 4) treats the wrong condition; eczematous nipple changes that persist require tissue diagnosis, not more empiric creams. A key clue: Paget disease involves the nipple and spreads to areola, whereas benign eczema typically spares the nipple.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Nipple punch biopsy: large malignant cells with abundant pale cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli within the epidermis (Paget cells); cells stain positive for CK7 and HER2
Diagnostic mammogram (right): segmental pleomorphic microcalcifications in the retroareolar/upper region
Breast MRI: non-mass enhancement extending from the nipple into the underlying ducts; no discrete invasive mass identified
Stereotactic core biopsy of calcifications: high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), no invasion identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis, and what underlying condition is most commonly associated with it?
options: Contact dermatitis with no malignant association as the primary diagnosis
Eczema associated with atopic dermatitis as the primary diagnosis
Paget disease of the nipple, which is almost always associated with an underlying DCIS or invasive carcinoma
Nipple candidiasis associated with diabetes as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The biopsy showing intraepidermal Paget cells (large cells with pale cytoplasm, CK7/HER2 positive) confirms Paget disease of the nipple. The vast majority of cases are associated with an underlying breast malignancy—most often DCIS, and frequently invasive ductal carcinoma—so the breast must be fully evaluated, as was done here (DCIS found). Contact dermatitis (choice 1) and atopic eczema (choice 4) are benign and would not show Paget cells; they also typically spare the nipple. Candidiasis (choice 3) is a fungal infection, not a malignant intraepidermal process. Recognizing the malignant association is the entire point—Paget disease is essentially a marker of underlying breast cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With Paget disease of the nipple and underlying high-grade DCIS, which surgical options are appropriate?
options: Either total mastectomy, or breast-conserving surgery that removes the nipple-areolar complex plus the underlying disease followed by radiation
Excision of the nipple skin alone without addressing the underlying breast disease as initial therapy
Topical chemotherapy cream to the nipple only as initial therapy
Observation with serial mammograms only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Treatment of Paget disease must address both the nipple lesion and the underlying breast cancer. Options include total (simple) mastectomy, or breast-conserving surgery that resects the nipple-areolar complex together with the underlying DCIS/tumor followed by whole-breast radiation—both are acceptable depending on disease extent and patient preference. Sentinel node biopsy is added if invasive disease is present or mastectomy is performed. Topical chemotherapy alone (choice 1) does not treat underlying breast cancer. Excising only nipple skin (choice 3) leaves the underlying DCIS untreated. Observation (choice 4) is inappropriate for a diagnosed malignancy.

stages.label: DISTINGUISHING FEATURE
stages.question: Which clinical feature best helps distinguish Paget disease of the nipple from benign nipple eczema?
options: There is mild scaling as the immediate next step
The lesion involves the nipple
Itching is present as the immediate next step
The lesion is unilateral as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A classic teaching point: Paget disease starts on and involves the nipple itself, then extends onto the areola, whereas benign nipple eczema usually begins on the areola and characteristically spares the nipple. This pattern, plus a non-healing course despite topical steroids, should prompt biopsy. Itching (choice 1) and scaling (choice 4) occur in both conditions and are nonspecific. Unilaterality (choice 3) can occur in either (eczema can be unilateral too), so it is not reliable. Any persistent unilateral nipple change should ultimately be biopsied regardless, but the nipple-involvement pattern is the most useful clinical discriminator.

stages.label: STAGING ADJUNCT
stages.question: Because invasive disease can be occult in Paget disease, which step is most appropriate when a mastectomy is planned or invasion is confirmed?
options: Skip nodal assessment entirely because DCIS never spreads to nodes
Routine complete axillary dissection in all patients regardless of nodal status
Bilateral mastectomy in every case as the immediate next step
Perform sentinel lymph node biopsy at the time of surgery
stages.explanation: When mastectomy is planned (which precludes later sentinel mapping) or when invasive carcinoma is identified, sentinel lymph node biopsy should be performed to assess the axilla, since occult invasion is common in Paget disease. Routine complete axillary dissection in everyone (choice 2) causes unnecessary lymphedema and is reserved for proven significant nodal disease. Skipping nodal assessment because 'DCIS never spreads' (choice 3) is unsafe here—occult invasion may upstage the disease, and SLNB is especially important when mastectomy removes the option of later sentinel mapping. Bilateral mastectomy in every case (choice 4) is overtreatment without a specific indication.

cases.diagnosis: Paget disease of the nipple with underlying ductal carcinoma in situ
pearls: A persistent, non-healing eczematous/crusted nipple lesion that fails topical steroids requires biopsy to exclude Paget disease.
Paget disease is almost always associated with an underlying breast malignancy (usually DCIS, often invasive ductal carcinoma)—always image and biopsy the breast.
Paget disease involves the nipple and spreads to the areola; benign eczema typically spares the nipple.
Treatment addresses both the nipple and underlying cancer (mastectomy or NAC-excising breast conservation plus radiation); add sentinel node biopsy for invasion or when mastectomy is planned.

cases.title: A Smooth, Movable Lump in a Young Woman's Breast
cases.chiefComplaint: Noticed a small, smooth, movable lump in her breast during a shower.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents after feeling a painless lump in her left breast while showering about three weeks ago. It is small, smooth, and seems to move around under her fingers. It does not appear to change much with her menstrual cycle, and she denies nipple discharge, skin changes, or breast pain. She has no prior breast problems. PMH: none. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. Social: graduate student, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no recreational drugs. Family history: no breast or ovarian cancer; no known BRCA mutation; grandmother had breast cancer at 78. She is anxious because a friend recently had breast cancer, and she wants to know if this lump is dangerous. She has regular menstrual cycles and is not pregnant. She performs occasional breast self-exams and is certain the lump was not there a few months ago.
cases.exam: Left breast with a 1.5 cm well-circumscribed, smooth, firm but rubbery, freely mobile, nontender mass in the upper outer quadrant ('breast mouse' that slips under the examining fingers). No skin dimpling, retraction, erythema, or peau d'orange. Nipples everted bilaterally with no spontaneous or expressible discharge. No axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The right breast is normal. No overlying skin changes or fixation to underlying tissue.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For a discrete breast mass in a 24-year-old, what is the most appropriate initial imaging as part of triple assessment?
options: PET/CT scan as the first step today
Diagnostic mammography as the first-line imaging
Breast MRI with contrast
Breast ultrasound
stages.explanation: In women under ~30, breast ultrasound is the preferred first-line imaging for a palpable mass because dense breast tissue limits mammographic sensitivity and ultrasound reliably distinguishes solid from cystic lesions while avoiding radiation. This is part of the triple assessment (clinical exam, imaging, and tissue sampling if needed). Mammography (choice 1) is first-line in older women but less useful and generally avoided as the initial test in this age group due to dense tissue and radiation. MRI (choice 3) is reserved for problem-solving or high-risk screening, not initial evaluation of a simple palpable mass. PET/CT (choice 4) has no role in evaluating a benign-appearing breast lump.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Breast ultrasound (left, upper outer quadrant):
1.5 cm well-circumscribed, oval, hypoechoic solid mass
Parallel orientation (wider than tall), smooth margins, homogeneous internal echoes
No posterior acoustic shadowing; minimal posterior enhancement
BI-RADS category 3 (probably benign), features consistent with fibroadenoma
No suspicious axillary nodes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the exam and ultrasound, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Invasive ductal carcinoma
Phyllodes tumor with rapid growth
Breast abscess
Fibroadenoma
stages.explanation: A young woman with a smooth, rubbery, freely mobile, nontender mass and an ultrasound showing a well-circumscribed, oval, parallel-oriented hypoechoic solid mass is classic for a fibroadenoma, the most common benign breast tumor in this age group. Invasive ductal carcinoma (choice 1) typically presents as a hard, irregular, fixed mass with suspicious imaging (spiculation, taller-than-wide), uncommon at 24 without risk factors. A breast abscess (choice 3) would be painful, erythematous, and fluctuant—an inflammatory picture, not present here. Phyllodes tumor (choice 4) can mimic fibroadenoma but is rarer, often larger and rapidly growing; the small size, classic features, and slow course favor fibroadenoma.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: To complete the triple assessment and confirm a benign diagnosis before considering observation, what is the most appropriate tissue-sampling step?
options: Repeat ultrasound in 5 years as the immediate next step
No sampling is ever needed for any solid breast mass
Immediate excisional biopsy of the entire mass in this setting
Core needle biopsy for concordant tissue diagnosis
stages.explanation: Triple assessment combines clinical exam, imaging, and tissue sampling; for a solid mass, core needle biopsy (or fine-needle aspiration in some settings) provides a histologic/cytologic diagnosis that should be concordant with the benign clinical and imaging impression. Immediate excisional biopsy (choice 1) is unnecessary for a probably-benign lesion and is more invasive than needed as a first step. Saying no sampling is ever needed (choice 3) is too absolute—solid masses generally warrant confirmation, and biopsy resolves uncertainty. A 5-year interval (choice 4) is far too long for short-term surveillance and is not how probably-benign lesions are followed (typically short-interval imaging at ~6 months).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Core biopsy confirms a fibroadenoma concordant with imaging. The patient is asymptomatic. Which management is appropriate, and when would excision be warranted?
options: Start chemotherapy to shrink the mass as initial therapy
Mandatory excision of all fibroadenomas regardless of size or symptoms as initial therapy
Observation with short-interval follow-up is appropriate
Stop all imaging follow-up and never reassess as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A small, asymptomatic, biopsy-confirmed fibroadenoma concordant with imaging can be safely observed with short-interval follow-up (e.g., clinical exam and ultrasound). Excision is reserved for fibroadenomas that enlarge, are large (often cited >2-3 cm) or symptomatic, cause patient anxiety/preference, or show discordant or atypical/suspicious features (raising concern for phyllodes). Mandatory excision of all fibroadenomas (choice 1) is overtreatment. Chemotherapy (choice 3) is absurd for a benign tumor. Abandoning all follow-up (choice 4) is inappropriate—at least short-interval surveillance is standard to confirm stability.

cases.diagnosis: Fibroadenoma of the breast
pearls: Ultrasound is first-line imaging for a palpable breast mass in women under ~30 due to dense breast tissue.
Fibroadenoma is the most common benign breast tumor in young women: smooth, rubbery, mobile ('breast mouse'), with a well-circumscribed oval mass on ultrasound.
Use triple assessment (clinical exam, imaging, tissue sampling) to confirm benignity before observation.
Observe concordant benign fibroadenomas with short-interval follow-up; excise if enlarging, large, symptomatic, or discordant/atypical.

cases.title: A Painless Lump in the Front of the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: Noticed a painless lump in the front of her neck that moves when she swallows.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents after noticing a painless lump in the lower front of her neck a few weeks ago, which her partner pointed out. It moves up and down when she swallows. She has no pain, hoarseness, difficulty swallowing, or breathing trouble, and no symptoms of hyper- or hypothyroidism (no palpitations, heat intolerance, weight change, fatigue, or constipation). PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a nurse. Family history: no thyroid cancer or endocrine syndromes; mother has Hashimoto thyroiditis. Of note, she received radiation to the neck as a child for an enlarged thymus—she vaguely recalls 'radiation treatments' in early childhood. She is anxious to understand what the lump is and whether it could be cancer. She has never had thyroid imaging before. There is no recent rapid growth and no compressive symptoms.
cases.exam: A firm, nontender 2 cm nodule is palpable in the right thyroid lobe that moves with swallowing. No overlying skin changes. The trachea is midline. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is appreciated. No proptosis, lid lag, or tremor; no signs of thyrotoxicosis. Voice is normal. No stridor. The remainder of the head and neck exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For a newly discovered solitary thyroid nodule, which two initial tests are most appropriate?
options: Serum TSH and thyroid (neck) ultrasound
MRI of the neck and serum thyroglobulin
Radioactive iodine uptake scan and serum calcitonin
CT neck with contrast and thyroid antibody panel
stages.explanation: Initial evaluation of a thyroid nodule starts with serum TSH and a dedicated thyroid/neck ultrasound. TSH assesses thyroid function (a low TSH suggests an autonomously functioning, usually benign nodule and prompts a radionuclide scan), and ultrasound characterizes the nodule's sonographic risk features and guides whether FNA is needed. CT with contrast (choice 1) is not first-line and iodinated contrast can interfere with later radioiodine therapy; antibodies are not the initial step for a nodule. A radioiodine uptake scan (choice 3) is only indicated if TSH is low, not routinely first; calcitonin is not a universal initial test. MRI and thyroglobulin (choice 4) are not appropriate initial evaluation of a nodule.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL (normal)
Free T4 normal
Thyroid ultrasound: 2.1 cm solid hypoechoic right-lobe nodule, taller-than-wide, with irregular margins and punctate echogenic foci (microcalcifications)
ACR TI-RADS: highly suspicious
No abnormal cervical lymph nodes identified
Left lobe and isthmus normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a normal TSH and a solid, hypoechoic, taller-than-wide nodule with microcalcifications, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed directly to total thyroidectomy without biopsy
Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the nodule
Order a radioactive iodine uptake scan in this clinical context
Reassure and rescan in 2 years as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A solid hypoechoic nodule that is taller-than-wide with irregular margins and microcalcifications carries high sonographic suspicion (and the size exceeds the FNA threshold), so ultrasound-guided FNA is the next step to obtain cytology and risk-stratify with the Bethesda system. Reassurance and a 2-year rescan (choice 1) is inappropriate for a high-suspicion nodule. Going straight to total thyroidectomy without cytology (choice 3) skips the diagnostic step that guides the extent of surgery. A radioiodine uptake scan (choice 4) is indicated only when TSH is low (to evaluate a hyperfunctioning nodule); TSH here is normal, so a scan is not the next step.

stages.label: CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
FNA cytology: malignant cells with nuclear enlargement, nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, and powdery 'Orphan Annie eye' chromatin; psammoma bodies present
Bethesda category VI (malignant)
Consistent with papillary thyroid carcinoma

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: FNA confirms papillary thyroid carcinoma in a 2.1 cm nodule in a patient with prior childhood neck irradiation. What is the most appropriate surgical management?
options: Lobectomy alone is mandatory for all papillary cancers regardless of factors
Total thyroidectomy is appropriate, particularly given tumor size >1 cm
Observation (active surveillance) of this malignant nodule as initial therapy
Radioactive iodine ablation instead of any surgery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For papillary thyroid carcinoma, total thyroidectomy is appropriate here given the tumor is >1 cm and especially with a history of childhood neck irradiation, which raises the risk of multifocal disease and recurrence; total thyroidectomy also facilitates radioiodine therapy and thyroglobulin surveillance. (Lobectomy can be acceptable for small, low-risk, unifocal tumors without high-risk features, but those caveats are not met here.) Saying lobectomy is mandatory for all (choice 1) is incorrect. Active surveillance (choice 3) is reserved for very small low-risk papillary microcarcinomas, not a 2.1 cm tumor with a radiation history. Radioiodine alone without surgery (choice 4) is not primary curative therapy for a resectable papillary cancer.

stages.label: POSTOPERATIVE
stages.question: After total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid carcinoma, which postoperative complications and surveillance steps are most important to address?
options: No follow-up is needed once the thyroid is removed as the immediate next step
Begin lifelong high-dose iodine supplementation to prevent recurrence as the immediate next step
Stop all thyroid hormone permanently and avoid replacement as the immediate next step
Monitor for hypocalcemia and voice changes
stages.explanation: After total thyroidectomy, key early complications are hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism (monitor calcium, watch for perioral tingling/Chvostek/Trousseau) and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causing hoarseness/voice change. Patients require levothyroxine replacement (with TSH suppression as indicated), and serum thyroglobulin is used as a tumor marker for recurrence surveillance, often with radioiodine in higher-risk cases. No follow-up (choice 1) is unsafe. Stopping all thyroid hormone (choice 3) would cause hypothyroidism—replacement is required after total thyroidectomy. High-dose iodine supplementation (choice 4) is not a recurrence-prevention strategy and is not indicated.

cases.diagnosis: Papillary thyroid carcinoma
pearls: Initial evaluation of a thyroid nodule is serum TSH plus thyroid ultrasound; a radionuclide scan is added only when TSH is low.
High-suspicion sonographic features (solid, hypoechoic, taller-than-wide, irregular margins, microcalcifications) warrant FNA, risk-stratified by the Bethesda system.
Papillary carcinoma shows nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei, and psammoma bodies; prior neck irradiation is a key risk factor.
After total thyroidectomy, watch for hypocalcemia and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, and follow serum thyroglobulin for recurrence.

cases.title: A Neck Nodule With Flushing and Diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: A firm lump in the neck along with episodes of facial flushing and loose stools.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with a firm nodule in the front of his neck noticed two months ago, accompanied by intermittent episodes of facial flushing and several months of intermittent watery diarrhea. He denies hoarseness or trouble swallowing. He has had occasional headaches and palpitations that he attributed to stress. PMH: kidney stones once. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social drinker, works in IT. Family history: notably, his father died young of a 'thyroid cancer,' and a paternal aunt had a 'tumor of the adrenal gland' and high blood pressure. He is concerned because of the strong family history and his own neck lump. He has not had any prior endocrine workup. He reports no recent weight gain and no skin pigmentation changes, but mentions his lips and tongue have looked a bit bumpy, which he assumed was normal for him.
cases.exam: A firm, fixed 2.5 cm nodule is palpable in the left thyroid lobe, with a few firm ipsilateral cervical lymph nodes. The trachea is midline; voice is normal. He has a tall, slender (marfanoid) habitus with several small mucosal neuromas on the lips and tongue. No proptosis or lid lag. No skin café-au-lait macules. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia. No abdominal masses. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a thyroid nodule with cervical nodes, flushing/diarrhea, mucosal neuromas, and a marfanoid habitus, which tumor marker is most specifically helpful for diagnosis?
options: Serum thyroid peroxidase antibody
Serum calcitonin (and CEA)
Serum thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin
Serum thyroglobulin in this clinical context
stages.explanation: This constellation strongly suggests medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arising from parafollicular C cells, which secrete calcitonin; serum calcitonin (and CEA) are the key tumor markers, elevated in MTC and useful for diagnosis and monitoring. Thyroglobulin (choice 1) is a marker for differentiated (papillary/follicular) thyroid cancer derived from follicular cells, not MTC. Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (choice 3) is a marker of Graves disease, unrelated here. TPO antibody (choice 4) indicates autoimmune thyroiditis (e.g., Hashimoto), not MTC. The clinical clues (mucosal neuromas, marfanoid body habitus, family history) point toward MEN2-associated MTC, for which calcitonin is the specific marker.

stages.label: LABS & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
Serum calcitonin 720 pg/mL (markedly elevated; normal <10)
CEA 38 ng/mL (elevated)
TSH normal
Ultrasound: 2.5 cm solid hypoechoic left thyroid nodule with abnormal ipsilateral cervical lymph nodes
FNA cytology: spindle/plasmacytoid cells in sheets; amyloid stroma (Congo red positive); immunostains positive for calcitonin
Consistent with medullary thyroid carcinoma

stages.label: WORKUP BEFORE SURGERY
stages.question: Before any thyroid surgery in this patient with suspected MEN2-associated MTC, which condition MUST be excluded first?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism only as the immediate next step
Cushing syndrome as the immediate next step
Diabetes insipidus as the immediate next step
Pheochromocytoma , to avoid intraoperative hypertensive crisis
stages.explanation: In MEN2, MTC coexists with pheochromocytoma, which must be excluded BEFORE thyroid (or any) surgery by measuring plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urine metanephrines/catecholamines (and imaging if positive). An undiagnosed pheochromocytoma can precipitate a lethal intraoperative hypertensive crisis; if present, it must be resected first (after alpha-blockade). His hypertension, flushing, palpitations, and headaches are red flags. Primary hyperparathyroidism (choice 1) occurs in MEN2A and should be assessed, but the life-threatening must-exclude-before-surgery condition is pheochromocytoma. Cushing syndrome (choice 3) and diabetes insipidus (choice 4) are not part of the MEN2 syndrome and are not the priority here.

stages.label: GENETIC TESTING
stages.question: Which genetic test should be obtained, given the strong family history and syndromic features?
options: BRAF V600E testing only
RET proto-oncogene mutation testing
BRCA1/2 mutation testing
APC gene testing in this setting
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, especially in MEN2 (MEN2A/MEN2B), is caused by germline activating mutations in the RET proto-oncogene; RET mutation testing should be performed in patients with MTC and is critical for identifying at-risk family members who may need prophylactic thyroidectomy. BRCA1/2 (choice 1) relates to breast/ovarian cancer syndromes, not MEN2. APC (choice 3) is associated with familial adenomatous polyposis, unrelated here. BRAF V600E (choice 4) is relevant to papillary thyroid carcinoma and melanoma, not the diagnosis or hereditary basis of MTC. Identifying a RET mutation also guides cascade testing and the timing of prophylactic surgery in relatives.

stages.label: ADDITIONAL RESULTS
stages.content:
Plasma free metanephrines: elevated; CT abdomen confirms a 3 cm right adrenal mass (pheochromocytoma)
RET proto-oncogene: germline pathogenic mutation identified, consistent with MEN2B
Serum calcium and PTH: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT SEQUENCE
stages.question: With confirmed MTC and a coexisting pheochromocytoma, what is the correct sequence and extent of surgery?
options: Total thyroidectomy first, then treat the pheochromocytoma later as the immediate next step
Treat both tumors with radiation instead of surgery as the immediate next step
Lobectomy of the affected thyroid lobe only, leaving the contralateral lobe as the immediate next step
Resect the pheochromocytoma first , then perform total thyroidectomy with central neck lymph node dissection
stages.explanation: The pheochromocytoma must be addressed first—after adequate preoperative alpha-blockade (then beta-blockade if needed)—to prevent a catastrophic intraoperative hypertensive crisis; the thyroid surgery follows. MTC is not radioiodine-sensitive (C cells don't take up iodine), so the treatment is total thyroidectomy with central compartment (and indicated lateral) lymph node dissection, given MTC's propensity for early nodal spread. Doing the thyroidectomy first (choice 1) risks a lethal crisis from the untreated pheochromocytoma. Lobectomy alone (choice 3) is inadequate for MTC, which is often bilateral/multifocal in hereditary disease and requires total thyroidectomy. Radiation instead of surgery (choice 4) is not curative primary therapy for these tumors.

cases.diagnosis: Medullary thyroid carcinoma in MEN2 (with pheochromocytoma)
pearls: Calcitonin (and CEA) are the tumor markers for medullary thyroid carcinoma, which arises from calcitonin-secreting parafollicular C cells; FNA shows amyloid stroma (Congo red positive).
MTC is associated with MEN2 and germline RET proto-oncogene mutations—test RET and screen family members for prophylactic thyroidectomy.
Always exclude pheochromocytoma (plasma/urine metanephrines) before any surgery in MEN2; resect it first after alpha-blockade.
MTC does not take up radioiodine; treat with total thyroidectomy plus central neck lymph node dissection given early nodal spread.

cases.title: Fatigue, Kidney Stones, and Bone Aches
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent kidney stones and persistent fatigue over the past year.
cases.history: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to surgery clinic after her third calcium-oxalate kidney stone in two years. She reports generalized fatigue, constipation, vague diffuse bone and joint aches, and difficulty concentrating that her family calls 'brain fog.' She also notes increased thirst and frequent urination. She denies neck pain, dysphagia, voice change, or palpable neck lump. PMH is notable for hypertension and a wrist fracture after a minor fall last year. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and a calcium-vitamin D supplement she started for 'bone health,' which she stopped one month ago. No allergies. She drinks one glass of wine weekly, does not smoke. Family history: a sister with kidney stones; no known endocrine tumors or MEN syndromes. Review of systems is positive for low mood and mild abdominal pain. A primary-care DEXA scan last month showed osteoporosis at the distal radius. Routine labs drawn by her PCP showed an elevated serum calcium, prompting this referral.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Neck is supple with no palpable thyroid nodule or cervical lymphadenopathy; no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the suprapubic region without rebound or guarding; no costovertebral-angle tenderness today. Neurologic exam is nonfocal; mood appears mildly depressed. Extremities show no edema; healed surgical scar over the right wrist. No proximal muscle weakness elicited. Skin is dry without striae or hyperpigmentation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given recurrent stones, bone loss, and fatigue with elevated serum calcium, which paired lab studies will most efficiently confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: TSH and free T4 as the first step today
Serum cortisol and ACTH as the first step today
Intact PTH and a repeat serum calcium
Serum gastrin and chromogranin A
stages.explanation: The triad of nephrolithiasis, osteoporosis, and neuropsychiatric 'groans' with hypercalcemia points to primary hyperparathyroidism, best confirmed by an inappropriately normal-to-high intact PTH in the face of hypercalcemia. Confirming calcium (corrected for albumin or as ionized calcium) plus intact PTH establishes PTH-dependent hypercalcemia. Cortisol/ACTH evaluate adrenal disease, not this presentation. TSH/free T4 assess thyroid function—hyperthyroidism can cause bone loss but not this calcium picture. Gastrin/chromogranin A screen for neuroendocrine tumors and are not the initial step here, though MEN1 would later be considered.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 11.6 mg/dL (corrected)
Ionized calcium 1.55 mmol/L
Intact PTH 128 pg/mL (15-65)
Phosphate 2.3 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL
24-hour urine calcium 380 mg/24h
Albumin 4.1 g/dL
TSH 2.1 mIU/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A high serum calcium with an inappropriately elevated PTH and elevated 24-hour urine calcium most strongly supports which diagnosis?
options: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH)
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Vitamin D intoxication in this clinical context
stages.explanation: PTH-dependent hypercalcemia (high calcium with inappropriately high PTH) plus high urinary calcium excretion is classic for primary hyperparathyroidism, usually a single adenoma. FHH would show low urinary calcium (calcium/creatinine clearance ratio <0.01) and typically a positive family history of benign hypercalcemia; the high 24-hour urine calcium argues against it. Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is PTH-independent—PTH is suppressed and PTHrP is elevated. Vitamin D intoxication causes PTH-independent hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH and a high 25-OH vitamin D; her PTH is high and vitamin D is low.

stages.label: PREOPERATIVE PLANNING
stages.question: Surgery is indicated. Which imaging study is most appropriate to localize a single adenoma and enable a minimally invasive, focused parathyroidectomy?
options: CT angiography of the neck vessels in this setting
MRI of the pituitary as the immediate next step
FDG-PET of the whole body as the immediate next step
Technetium-99m sestamibi scan, often with SPECT/CT
stages.explanation: Sestamibi parathyroid scintigraphy (frequently combined with SPECT/CT) plus high-resolution neck ultrasound is the standard preoperative localization strategy. A concordant single focus permits a focused, minimally invasive parathyroidectomy—often with intraoperative PTH monitoring—rather than four-gland exploration. FDG-PET is for oncologic staging and is not the parathyroid localization tool of choice. Pituitary MRI evaluates for pituitary adenoma (relevant to MEN1 screening, not localization). CT angiography of neck vessels does not localize parathyroid tissue and adds radiation/contrast without benefit here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Sestamibi SPECT/CT: focal increased uptake posterior to the inferior pole of the right thyroid lobe, consistent with a single parathyroid adenoma
Neck ultrasound: 1.4 cm hypoechoic ovoid lesion at the right inferior thyroid pole, separate from thyroid parenchyma
Thyroid: no suspicious nodules

stages.label: INTRAOPERATIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: During focused right inferior parathyroidectomy, which intraoperative test best confirms that the hyperfunctioning gland has been removed before closing?
options: Intraoperative intact PTH drop of more than 50% from baseline about 10 minutes after excision
Frozen-section confirming parathyroid tissue only as the immediate next step
Recurrent laryngeal nerve monitoring signal as the immediate next step
Intraoperative serum calcium returning to normal as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Intraoperative PTH monitoring exploits PTH's short half-life (about 3-5 minutes). A drop of more than 50% from the highest pre-excision baseline—ideally into the normal range—roughly 10 minutes after removal confirms cure and excludes additional hyperfunctioning glands, allowing the surgeon to close. Frozen section confirms the tissue is parathyroid but cannot confirm biochemical cure or that no second adenoma remains. Serum calcium falls too slowly (hours to days) to guide the operation. RLN monitoring protects the nerve but says nothing about whether the culprit gland was removed.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hyperparathyroidism due to a single parathyroid adenoma
pearls: PTH-dependent hypercalcemia = high calcium with inappropriately normal/high PTH; the most common cause is a solitary adenoma.
High 24-hour urine calcium distinguishes primary hyperparathyroidism from FHH (low urine calcium).

Surgical criteria in asymptomatic disease include age <50, calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, osteoporosis/fragility fracture, or eGFR <60; symptomatic disease (stones, fractures) is an indication regardless of age.
Concordant sestamibi/SPECT-CT and ultrasound enable focused parathyroidectomy with intraoperative PTH confirming a >50% drop.

cases.title: Spells of Pounding Headache, Racing Heart, and Drenching Sweats
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and profuse sweating.
cases.history: A 46-year-old man is referred for several months of paroxysmal 'attacks' lasting 15-30 minutes: a pounding bitemporal headache, palpitations, drenching diaphoresis, tremor, and a sense of impending doom. Episodes can be triggered by bending over, exertion, or straining at stool, and resolve spontaneously. Between spells he feels well, though he has noticed unintended weight loss and constipation. His blood pressure has been labeled 'hard to control' on three agents. PMH: hypertension, generalized anxiety. Medications: lisinopril 40 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily. No allergies. He does not smoke; rare alcohol. Family history is striking: a paternal uncle had a 'thyroid cancer' and an aunt had 'tumors on both adrenal glands,' raising concern for a hereditary syndrome. He has had no surgeries. An abdominal CT ordered for vague flank pain incidentally revealed a 4.5 cm right adrenal mass, prompting this referral. He denies flushing, diarrhea, or wheezing. He is anxious about the unpredictable spells and asks whether surgery is safe.
cases.exam: Anxious, mildly diaphoretic man with a fine resting tremor. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, with a forceful apical impulse and no murmurs. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender; no bruit, no palpable mass. Neurologic exam nonfocal. Orthostatic check shows a drop from 176/104 mmHg supine to 150/92 mmHg standing with reflex tachycardia. Skin is warm and moist. No café-au-lait macules, no neck mass, no mucosal neuromas; fundoscopy without papilledema. Extremities without edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given episodic headache, palpitations, sweating, and an adrenal mass, which test is the most appropriate first-line biochemical screen?
options: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Plasma free metanephrines
Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio as the first step today
