Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1
Type and crossmatch sent; 2 units PRBC ordered

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He receives a 1 L crystalloid bolus and 2 units of packed red cells. He transiently improves to BP 102/64, HR 104. He is now a transient responder. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge with outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Obtain a contrast-enhanced CT angiogram of the abdomen to localize the bleed
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe as initial therapy
Continue ward observation with serial hemoglobins every 6 hours
stages.explanation: A transient responder who stabilizes after resuscitation but has ongoing concern for splenic hemorrhage should undergo CT angiography to localize active extravasation or a pseudoaneurysm, which determines whether angioembolization can salvage the spleen. Continued passive ward observation after a clear decompensation and hemoglobin drop is unsafe. Discharge is obviously inappropriate during active bleeding. Antibiotics treat infection, which is not the problem here. Because the patient responded to transfusion and regained adequate perfusion, there is a brief, appropriate window to image and pursue a spleen-preserving intervention rather than rushing to splenectomy; a non-responder, by contrast, would go straight to the OR.

stages.label: CT ANGIOGRAM
stages.content:
CT angiogram: grade IV splenic laceration with a 1.5 cm intrasplenic pseudoaneurysm
Active contrast extravasation from the splenic parenchyma
Moderate hemoperitoneum, increased from prior study
No other solid-organ injury; no bowel injury
Patient currently BP 100/62, HR 106 after transfusion

stages.label: INTERVENTION
stages.question: The CT shows a splenic pseudoaneurysm with active extravasation in a now-stabilized (transient responder) patient. What is the preferred next intervention?
options: Splenic artery angioembolization by interventional radiology
Immediate splenectomy as the immediate next step
Repeat CT in 24 hours as the immediate next step
Therapeutic anticoagulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stabilized patient with a splenic pseudoaneurysm and active extravasation, splenic artery angioembolization is the preferred intervention: it controls the bleeding while preserving splenic tissue and immune function, salvaging nonoperative management. Immediate splenectomy is reserved for patients who are hemodynamically unstable or fail/are not candidates for embolization; this patient is currently stable enough for IR. Repeat CT in 24 hours ignores active extravasation and risks exsanguination. Anticoagulation would worsen hemorrhage and is contraindicated. Angioembolization significantly increases the success rate of nonoperative management for high-grade injuries with vascular abnormalities.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful angioembolization, which monitoring plan is most appropriate?
options: Resume full activity and ambulation immediately with appropriate follow-up
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for the pseudoaneurysm with appropriate follow-up
Transfer to a monitored/ICU setting with serial hemoglobin
Immediate discharge home with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Post-embolization, the patient needs close monitoring in a higher-acuity setting with serial hemoglobin and frequent vital signs to detect re-bleeding or embolization failure, with conversion to splenectomy held in reserve if instability recurs. Immediate discharge is unsafe after high-grade injury and active bleeding. Immediate full activity risks splenic re-rupture; activity restriction and bed rest are continued. Anticoagulation is contraindicated in the setting of recent active hemorrhage and would not treat the pseudoaneurysm. Vigilant observation allows early recognition of failure of nonoperative/endovascular management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient had instead failed embolization and required splenectomy, which preventive measure becomes essential before discharge?
options: Routine annual abdominal CT surveillance as the leading concern
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation as the leading concern
Permanent activity restriction from all work as the leading concern
Vaccination against encapsulated organisms
stages.explanation: After splenectomy the patient becomes functionally asplenic and is at lifelong risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) from encapsulated bacteria, so vaccination against Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae type b (typically given about two weeks postoperatively in trauma) is essential, along with education on early antibiotics for febrile illness. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not indicated and is dangerous after major hemorrhage; post-splenectomy thrombocytosis rarely needs treatment. Permanent restriction from all work is unwarranted once recovered. Routine annual CT surveillance has no role after a treated splenic injury and only adds radiation. OPSI prophylaxis is the key discharge consideration.

cases.diagnosis: Delayed rupture of a blunt splenic injury with pseudoaneurysm and active extravasation, salvaged by splenic artery angioembolization
pearls: Delayed splenic rupture can occur days after blunt trauma as a subcapsular hematoma or pseudoaneurysm fails.
A splenic pseudoaneurysm or active extravasation predicts failure of nonoperative management.
Transient responders who stabilize can be imaged and offered angioembolization to preserve the spleen.
Hemodynamic instability that does not respond to resuscitation overrides imaging and mandates splenectomy.

cases.title: Sudden Back Pain and Collapse
cases.chiefComplaint: I had sudden tearing back pain and then I nearly passed out.
cases.history: A 73-year-old man is brought to the ED after the abrupt onset of severe, tearing left flank and lower back pain while gardening, followed by a near-syncopal episode. The pain is constant, radiates to the groin, and is unlike anything he has felt before. EMS reports he was pale and clammy with a weak radial pulse. PMH includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease with prior stent, and a 50 pack-year smoking history (still active). Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. He lives alone and is independent. He drinks two beers most evenings. Family history includes a father who died suddenly of an aortic problem. He has not had recent imaging. On exam he is restless and in obvious distress, gripping his flank. He denies fever, urinary symptoms, hematuria, or recent trauma.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic, pale, and restless. He is alert but anxious. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without murmur. The abdomen is mildly distended with a pulsatile, expansile mass palpable in the epigastrium/periumbilical region; there is tenderness over the mass and the left flank with bruising developing in the left flank (Grey Turner pattern). Femoral pulses are palpable but diminished bilaterally; distal pulses are weak. Skin is cool and clammy with delayed capillary refill. There is no peritonitis with rigidity throughout, no costovertebral angle tenderness to suggest pyelonephritis, and no focal neurologic deficit. Lungs are clear and the trachea is midline.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An older man presents with the triad of sudden back/flank pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
Acute pancreatitis in this clinical context
Renal colic from an obstructing ureteral stone
Perforated peptic ulcer
stages.explanation: The classic triad of acute abdominal/back/flank pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass is a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm until proven otherwise, especially in an elderly male smoker with hypertension. Renal colic causes severe flank pain but not a pulsatile mass or hemorrhagic shock; it is a common and dangerous misdiagnosis in this setting. Acute pancreatitis gives epigastric pain radiating to the back with elevated lipase, not a pulsatile mass with shock from the outset. Perforated ulcer produces a rigid, peritonitic abdomen with free air, not a pulsatile mass. The combination here demands immediate consideration of ruptured AAA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While stabilizing this hypotensive patient with a suspected ruptured AAA, what is the appropriate blood-pressure goal during resuscitation?
options: No fluids at all until the operating room as initial therapy
Permissive hypotension targeting a systolic around 70 to 90 mmHg while maintaining mentation
Aggressive crystalloid to normalize BP to 120/80 mmHg as initial therapy
Vasopressors to drive systolic above 160 mmHg as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In ruptured AAA, permissive (hypotensive) resuscitation targeting a systolic of roughly 70 to 90 mmHg while the patient maintains consciousness avoids dislodging the fragile clot and worsening hemorrhage. Aggressive crystalloid to normalize pressure increases bleeding, dilutes clotting factors, and worsens outcomes. Driving the pressure high with vasopressors would blow off the tamponading clot and accelerate exsanguination. Giving no fluid at all in a patient with altered perfusion is also wrong; the goal is balanced, minimal resuscitation with blood products to preserve cerebral perfusion without provoking more bleeding, alongside rapid transfer for definitive repair.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this patient with a known pulsatile mass remained profoundly hypotensive and unresponsive to resuscitation, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain an MRI of the aorta for detailed anatomy as the immediate next step
Proceed directly to emergent operative/endovascular repair without delay for CT
Admit to the ICU for observation and trend hemoglobin before discharge planning
Send him to CT angiography first to confirm the diagnosis in this setting
stages.explanation: An unstable patient with the classic triad and a pulsatile mass has a ruptured AAA until proven otherwise and should go straight to the operating room or hybrid suite for repair; CT must not delay definitive intervention in the hemodynamically unstable patient. CT angiography is invaluable for planning when the patient is stable or transiently stabilizes, but in persistent shock it costs lifesaving time. ICU observation is inappropriate for ongoing hemorrhage and would be fatal. MRI is far too slow and inaccessible for an exsanguinating patient. Stability, not a desire for perfect imaging, dictates whether there is time to scan before repair.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Bedside ultrasound: 7.8 cm infrarenal aortic aneurysm with periaortic fluid
CT angiogram (obtained as patient transiently stabilized): infrarenal AAA with active retroperitoneal extravasation
Adequate proximal infrarenal neck length; suitable iliac access on CTA
Hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms a ruptured infrarenal AAA with favorable anatomy (adequate proximal neck and iliac access) in a transiently stabilized patient. At a capable center, which repair is generally preferred?
options: Continued medical management with blood-pressure control only
Endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR)
Open surgical aortic repair via laparotomy
Percutaneous coronary intervention in this setting
stages.explanation: For ruptured AAA with suitable anatomy at a center with endovascular capability, EVAR is generally preferred over open repair because it is associated with lower perioperative mortality and morbidity and can sometimes be done under local anesthesia. Open repair remains essential and is used when anatomy is unfavorable (inadequate neck, hostile iliacs) or EVAR is unavailable, but it carries higher physiologic insult. Medical management alone cannot treat an actively bleeding rupture and would be fatal. PCI addresses coronary disease, not aortic rupture. Anatomic suitability and institutional capability drive the EVAR-versus-open decision; either way, repair must be emergent.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several hours after repair, the patient develops abdominal distension, bloody diarrhea, and worsening lactate. Which complication should be suspected?
options: Colonic ischemia from inferior mesenteric artery compromise
Acute cholecystitis as the leading concern
Clostridioides difficile colitis as the leading concern
Small bowel obstruction from adhesions in this presentation
stages.explanation: Colonic ischemia, classically of the sigmoid colon, is a recognized complication after AAA repair due to interruption of inferior mesenteric artery flow and poor collateralization, presenting with bloody diarrhea, distension, and rising lactate within hours to days. C. difficile causes diarrhea but typically days later with antibiotic exposure and is not driven by ischemia/lactate so acutely. Acute cholecystitis gives RUQ pain and fever, not bloody diarrhea. Adhesive small bowel obstruction would be unusual this early after endovascular or even open repair and would not cause bloody stool. Early flexible sigmoidoscopy confirms the diagnosis and guides management.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm repaired emergently with EVAR
pearls: The triad of back/flank pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass is ruptured AAA until proven otherwise.
Use permissive hypotension (SBP ~70-90 mmHg) and avoid aggressive crystalloid before repair.
At capable centers, EVAR is preferred for ruptured AAA with suitable anatomy; open repair for hostile anatomy.
Watch for sigmoid colonic ischemia (bloody diarrhea, rising lactate) after AAA repair.

cases.title: A Surprise on the CT Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: My ER doctor said they found something on my belly scan and I should follow up.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents to clinic to follow up an incidental finding. He went to an urgent care for vague low back pain after lifting, had a CT that showed a kidney stone (which passed), and was told there was also an enlarged aorta. He currently feels well with no abdominal or back pain, no syncope, and no leg symptoms. PMH includes hypertension and COPD. He has a 45 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke about half a pack daily. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and a tiotropium inhaler. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and is retired from truck driving. Family history is notable for a brother who had an aortic aneurysm repaired. He has never been screened for AAA before. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no claudication, no abdominal pain, and no embolic symptoms.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and in no distress. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender with a mildly prominent, nontender, pulsatile fullness in the periumbilical region in this thin man; there is no bruit and no tenderness over the mass. There is no abdominal guarding or rebound. Femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses are all present and symmetric bilaterally with no femoral or carotid bruits. The feet are warm and well perfused with normal capillary refill. There is no peripheral edema and no signs of distal embolization such as blue toes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The report describes a 5.6 cm infrarenal aortic aneurysm. What is the most appropriate imaging to confirm and accurately measure the aneurysm for management planning?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph as the first step today
Abdominal ultrasound, or CT angiography for operative planning
No further imaging is needed as the first step today
Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP)
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the standard, inexpensive, radiation-free tool to confirm and measure AAA diameter and follow it over time; when a diameter reaches the surgical threshold, CT angiography provides precise sizing and anatomy for operative/endovascular planning. Doing nothing is inappropriate for an aneurysm near or at the repair threshold. A plain film cannot reliably measure aortic diameter (only sometimes shows rim calcification). MRCP images the biliary tree, not the aorta. Confirming size with a reliable modality is the key first step before deciding between surveillance and repair.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography: infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm, maximal diameter 5.6 cm
No signs of rupture, dissection, or contained leak
Aneurysm begins below the renal arteries with an adequate proximal neck
Patent iliac and femoral runoff vessels
No significant peripheral aneurysms identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For an asymptomatic infrarenal AAA measuring 5.6 cm in a reasonable surgical candidate, what is the recommended management?
options: Continue annual ultrasound surveillance only
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation
Elective aneurysm repair (EVAR or open)
Immediate emergent surgery tonight
stages.explanation: Elective repair is recommended once an AAA reaches roughly 5.5 cm in men (or 5.0 cm in women), grows >0.5 cm in 6 months, or becomes symptomatic, because rupture risk rises sharply above this threshold. At 5.6 cm and asymptomatic, this patient meets criteria for elective repair, not continued surveillance. Emergent overnight surgery is for rupture or symptomatic/expanding aneurysms, which he does not have. Anticoagulation does not treat or shrink an aneurysm and adds bleeding risk. Elective, planned repair carries far lower mortality than emergency repair after rupture, which is the rationale for intervening at threshold.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which intervention is the single most important to slow aneurysm growth and reduce overall cardiovascular and rupture risk in this patient?
options: Daily vigorous weightlifting
Smoking cessation
A high-protein supplement regimen
Routine prophylactic antibiotics
stages.explanation: Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for AAA formation, expansion, and rupture, so smoking cessation is the single most impactful intervention and also reduces his perioperative and cardiovascular risk. Vigorous weightlifting with heavy Valsalva can transiently spike blood pressure and is not protective; blood-pressure control and statin therapy are part of optimization but cessation is paramount. Protein supplements do not affect aneurysm biology. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role in degenerative AAA. Aggressive risk-factor modification, led by quitting smoking, complements either surveillance or repair.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: If this aneurysm had instead measured 4.2 cm, what would be the appropriate surveillance strategy?
options: Repeat imaging only if he develops symptoms in this setting
No further follow-up ever needed as the immediate next step
Repeat ultrasound surveillance at a defined interval
Immediate elective repair as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A 4.0 to 5.4 cm AAA is managed with periodic ultrasound surveillance (commonly every 6 to 12 months depending on size) plus risk-factor modification, because the rupture risk below 5.5 cm is generally lower than the operative risk of elective repair. Immediate repair is not indicated below threshold in the absence of symptoms or rapid growth. Stopping all follow-up is unsafe because aneurysms enlarge over time and can reach threshold. Waiting only for symptoms is dangerous, since the first symptom may be rupture. Structured surveillance detects growth so repair can be timed electively.

cases.diagnosis: Incidentally discovered asymptomatic 5.6 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm meeting threshold for elective repair
pearls: Elective AAA repair is indicated at ~5.5 cm in men (~5.0 cm in women), rapid growth >0.5 cm/6 months, or symptoms.
AAAs 4.0-5.4 cm are followed with periodic ultrasound surveillance.
Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for AAA growth and rupture.
Elective repair has far lower mortality than emergency repair of a ruptured AAA.

cases.title: A Cold, Painful Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: My right leg suddenly went cold, pale, and numb a few hours ago.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman presents with the abrupt onset, about four hours ago, of severe pain, pallor, coldness, and numbness of her right leg from the mid-thigh down. The onset was sudden while watching television; she now cannot move her toes well and the leg feels heavy. She denies trauma or back pain. PMH is notable for atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and prior transient ischemic attack. She was prescribed warfarin but admits she stopped taking it three weeks ago because of the monitoring hassle. Other medications include metoprolol 50 mg twice daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago) and lives with her husband. Family history includes coronary artery disease. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, dyspnea, prior claudication, or leg ulcers; she had no prior leg symptoms and walks normally at baseline.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and guarding the right leg. Pulse is irregularly irregular. The right lower extremity is pale and cool to touch below the mid-thigh compared with the warm, well-perfused, normally colored left leg. The right femoral pulse is palpable, but the right popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses are absent by palpation and by handheld Doppler; the left leg has triphasic pulses throughout. There is decreased light-touch sensation over the right foot and reduced active dorsiflexion of the toes; the calf is soft and not tense. Capillary refill is markedly delayed in the right foot. No skin breakdown or chronic trophic changes are present, consistent with an acute, not chronic, process.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A sudden cold, pulseless, painful, pale, numb leg in a patient with atrial fibrillation who stopped anticoagulation is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Chronic peripheral arterial disease with claudication
Deep vein thrombosis in this clinical context
Lumbar radiculopathy in this clinical context
Acute limb ischemia from arterial embolism
stages.explanation: The six Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia/coldness, paresthesia, paralysis) with sudden onset in a patient with atrial fibrillation off anticoagulation point to acute limb ischemia from an arterial embolus, classically lodging at a bifurcation. DVT causes a swollen, warm, often cyanotic limb with intact arterial pulses, the opposite of a cold pulseless leg. Chronic PAD causes exertional claudication and trophic skin changes developing over months, not abrupt total ischemia, and this patient had no prior symptoms. Lumbar radiculopathy causes pain and weakness but preserves pulses and warmth. The cardioembolic source plus the acute six-P presentation defines this emergency.

stages.label: IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive care, what is the most important immediate medication to start in this patient with acute limb ischemia?
options: Intravenous antibiotics in this setting
Oral aspirin alone as the immediate next step
Intravenous unfractionated heparin
A statin as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV unfractionated heparin is the cornerstone of initial management: it prevents propagation of the clot and protects collateral circulation while definitive revascularization is arranged. Aspirin alone is insufficient for an acute occlusive embolus. Antibiotics have no role unless infection or established gangrene with sepsis is present. A statin is part of long-term vascular risk management but does nothing for the acute occlusion. Heparin should be started promptly once the diagnosis is suspected and there is no contraindication, in parallel with vascular surgery consultation and limb assessment.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Handheld Doppler: arterial signals absent at the right ankle, venous signals present
Rutherford category IIb: sensory loss beyond toes and mild-to-moderate motor weakness
No audible arterial Doppler at dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial
ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
INR 1.1 (subtherapeutic)
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.4 mEq/L

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: The limb is Rutherford category IIb (threatened, marginally salvageable with prompt treatment) from an embolic occlusion. What is the most appropriate definitive intervention?
options: Urgent surgical embolectomy
Catheter-directed thrombolysis over 24 to 48 hours
Primary amputation as the immediate next step
Observation with continued heparin only
stages.explanation: A category IIb (immediately threatened) limb requires emergent revascularization, and for an embolic occlusion urgent surgical embolectomy with a Fogarty balloon catheter provides the fastest restoration of flow. Catheter-directed thrombolysis takes hours to days and is better suited to viable category I/IIa limbs or thrombotic in-situ occlusions where time permits; a IIb limb cannot wait. Primary amputation is reserved for category III (irreversible) ischemia with profound paralysis, anesthesia, and muscle rigor. Observation alone would allow progression to irreversible ischemia and limb loss. Speed of reperfusion is critical to salvage a threatened limb.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After successful embolectomy and reperfusion, the calf becomes tense and the patient reports severe pain with passive stretch of the foot. What complication must be treated emergently?
options: Simple postoperative wound pain
Deep vein thrombosis requiring an IVC filter
Cellulitis requiring oral antibiotics
Compartment syndrome requiring fasciotomy
stages.explanation: Reperfusion of an ischemic limb causes muscle edema within tight fascial compartments, producing compartment syndrome, signaled by a tense calf and pain out of proportion that worsens with passive stretch. Emergent fasciotomy relieves the pressure and prevents irreversible muscle and nerve necrosis. Cellulitis develops over days with erythema and warmth, not acutely after reperfusion, and is not the issue here. Dismissing it as ordinary wound pain risks limb loss. While DVT can occur, the tense compartment with pain on passive stretch is the hallmark of compartment syndrome, and an IVC filter would not address it. Fasciotomy must not be delayed.

cases.diagnosis: Acute limb ischemia from cardioembolism in atrial fibrillation, treated by urgent embolectomy with attention to reperfusion compartment syndrome
pearls: Remember the six Ps of acute limb ischemia: pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia, paralysis.
Start IV heparin immediately on suspicion of acute limb ischemia to prevent clot propagation.
A Rutherford IIb (threatened) limb needs emergent revascularization; embolectomy is fastest for an embolus.
After reperfusion, watch for compartment syndrome (tense compartment, pain on passive stretch) and perform fasciotomy.

cases.title: Excruciating Groin Pain and Fever
cases.chiefComplaint: My groin and scrotum hurt unbearably and the skin looks terrible.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with two days of rapidly worsening pain and swelling of the scrotum and perineum, now with foul-smelling discharge and a feeling that he might pass out. The pain has been far out of proportion to the skin findings, and he reports fevers and chills. He noticed a small perianal abscess about a week ago that he ignored. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 11.2% last visit), obesity, and chronic kidney disease. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 30 units nightly, and lisinopril 10 mg daily; he admits poor adherence. He has no drug allergies. He smokes half a pack daily and drinks alcohol heavily. He is unemployed and lives alone. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for malaise, decreased urine output, and nausea, and negative for chest pain or recent urinary instrumentation. He looks toxic and is in severe distress.
cases.exam: The patient is toxic-appearing, diaphoretic, and confused at times. The scrotum and perineum are markedly swollen, dusky, and erythematous with patches of gray-black skin discoloration and a few hemorrhagic bullae extending toward the lower abdominal wall. Palpation reveals crepitus (subcutaneous gas) and exquisite tenderness, with pain clearly out of proportion to the visible findings; there is a foul, feculent odor and serous-purulent dishwater drainage. Skin sensation over the affected area is patchy and partly diminished. The abdomen is soft outside the affected region. He is tachycardic with cool extremities and delayed capillary refill, consistent with septic shock. No inguinal hernia is reducible through the edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A diabetic man with perineal pain out of proportion, crepitus, skin necrosis, and septic shock most likely has which condition?
options: Simple scrotal cellulitis
Epididymo-orchitis as the first step today
Necrotizing soft tissue infection
Strangulated inguinal hernia
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion, crepitus from subcutaneous gas, skin necrosis with hemorrhagic bullae, foul drainage, and systemic toxicity define necrotizing soft tissue infection, and in the perineum/genitalia this is Fournier gangrene, classically in diabetic, immunocompromised men. Simple cellulitis causes erythema and warmth without crepitus, necrosis, or this degree of toxicity and pain. Epididymo-orchitis causes a tender testis and dysuria, not gangrenous skin and gas. A strangulated hernia causes a tender irreducible groin mass with obstruction, not crepitant perineal necrosis. The constellation of gas, necrosis, and sepsis marks a surgical emergency.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 22.5 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Glucose 412 mg/dL
Sodium 129 mEq/L
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.3)
CRP 320 mg/L
Lactate 5.1 mmol/L
Hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL
Plain film of pelvis: soft-tissue gas in the perineum
LRINEC score: high

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging shows soft-tissue gas and the patient is in septic shock. After starting resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics, what is the definitive and most time-critical treatment?
options: Emergent surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the primary treatment
Bedside incision and drainage only as initial therapy
Await MRI to delineate fascial planes before any intervention
stages.explanation: Necrotizing soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency; emergent, aggressive wide debridement of all nonviable tissue is the definitive, life-saving treatment and must not be delayed. Imaging can support the diagnosis, but waiting for MRI delays source control and increases mortality; the diagnosis here is already clinical and supported by gas on plain film. Hyperbaric oxygen and antibiotics are adjuncts only and never substitute for surgical debridement. A limited bedside incision and drainage is inadequate because necrotizing infection tracks rapidly along fascial planes far beyond the visible skin and requires thorough operative excision, often with planned re-look procedures.

stages.label: ANTIBIOTICS
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate while preparing for the operating room?
options: Broad-spectrum coverage for gram-positives, gram-negatives,
Antifungal therapy alone as the immediate next step
Oral cephalexin as the immediate next step
A single dose of azithromycin as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Fournier gangrene is typically polymicrobial, so empiric therapy must cover gram-positive cocci (including MRSA), gram-negative rods, and anaerobes, for example a carbapenem or piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin, with clindamycin added for its antitoxin/antiribosomal effect to suppress streptococcal and clostridial exotoxin production. Oral cephalexin is far too narrow and oral therapy is inappropriate in septic shock. Antifungal monotherapy ignores the bacterial pathogens. A single dose of azithromycin provides grossly inadequate coverage. Antibiotics are adjunctive to, not a substitute for, emergent surgical debridement, but the regimen must be broad and given immediately.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial operative debridement, what is the expected ongoing surgical plan for this patient?
options: Planned serial re-exploration
Discharge home on oral antibiotics within 24 hours with appropriate follow-up
Immediate definitive wound closure in the same operation based on current risk
A single operation is curative; no further surgery needed in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Necrotizing infections almost always require planned, serial returns to the operating room every 24 to 48 hours to debride newly necrotic tissue until the wound is clean and the infection controlled. A single debridement is rarely sufficient because necrosis can progress along fascial planes. Immediate definitive closure is inappropriate over an infected, evolving wound; reconstruction comes later once the bed is clean. Discharge within 24 hours is dangerous in a critically ill septic patient who needs ICU-level care, repeated debridement, glucose control, and often urinary/fecal diversion. Mortality remains high and depends on the adequacy and timeliness of repeated source control.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which underlying factor most contributed to this patient's susceptibility and must be aggressively addressed during his hospital course?
options: A remote appendectomy
Poorly controlled diabetes mellitus
Occasional caffeine intake
Mild seasonal allergies
stages.explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 11.2%, glucose 412) impairs neutrophil function and microvascular perfusion, making diabetics the classic hosts for Fournier gangrene; aggressive glycemic control and treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis/hyperglycemia are essential to recovery and wound healing. Seasonal allergies do not predispose to necrotizing infection. A remote appendectomy is unrelated to perineal necrotizing infection. Caffeine has no bearing on infection risk. Addressing the immunocompromising state, alongside surgical source control and antibiotics, is central to improving outcomes in this high-mortality disease.

cases.diagnosis: Fournier gangrene (necrotizing soft tissue infection of the perineum) with septic shock, requiring emergent and serial surgical debridement
pearls: Pain out of proportion plus crepitus and skin necrosis signals necrotizing soft tissue infection.
Treatment is emergent wide surgical debridement; imaging and antibiotics must not delay the OR.
Coverage is broad-spectrum and polymicrobial; add clindamycin for exotoxin suppression.
Plan for serial re-debridement and aggressively control the underlying diabetes.

cases.title: A Tender Lump by the Anus
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a painful swollen lump next to my anus that's getting worse.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with four days of progressively worsening, throbbing pain and swelling next to his anus. The pain is worse with sitting and during bowel movements, and he has noticed a tender lump. He reports low-grade fevers and feeling generally unwell over the last day. He denies significant rectal bleeding, but the area is too tender to examine well himself. PMH is unremarkable; he has no diabetes or inflammatory bowel disease. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He works a desk job, does not smoke, and drinks socially. He has no prior anorectal surgery and no history of similar problems. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative for abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, or perianal drainage prior to this episode. He has been taking ibuprofen with only partial relief and is anxious about the worsening swelling.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable, especially when seated. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without distension. Inspection of the perianal region reveals a tender, erythematous, fluctuant swelling in the perianal area at roughly the 5 o'clock position, with surrounding induration and warmth; the overlying skin is intact without spontaneous drainage. There is exquisite tenderness on gentle palpation with a localized area of fluctuance. Digital rectal examination is markedly limited by pain. There is no crepitus, no extensive necrosis, no spreading cellulitis beyond the local area, and no perianal fistula opening visible. The remainder of the perineal skin is normal, and there is no scrotal involvement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A tender, fluctuant, erythematous perianal swelling with low-grade fever is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Thrombosed external hemorrhoid
Perianal abscess
Rectal prolapse
Anal fissure in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A tender, warm, erythematous, fluctuant perianal mass with fever indicates a perianal abscess, typically arising from an obstructed anal crypt gland. A thrombosed external hemorrhoid is a tense bluish, painful subcutaneous lump at the anal verge but is not fluctuant, warm, or febrile and represents clot, not pus. An anal fissure causes a painful tear with bleeding and a sentinel skin tag, not a fluctuant abscess. Rectal prolapse presents as protruding rectal mucosa, not a localized purulent swelling. The fluctuance, erythema, warmth, and systemic signs distinguish an abscess that requires drainage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for this perianal abscess?
options: Oral antibiotics alone
Sitz baths and stool softeners only
Incision and drainage
Urgent colonoscopy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment of any abscess, including a perianal abscess, is prompt incision and drainage; pus must be evacuated. Antibiotics alone do not penetrate or cure an undrained abscess and are reserved as an adjunct for patients with systemic illness, immunocompromise, diabetes, or surrounding cellulitis. Sitz baths and stool softeners are supportive measures after drainage but cannot treat an established abscess. Colonoscopy is not indicated acutely for a simple perianal abscess and would be unbearable; it may be considered later if inflammatory bowel disease is suspected. Drainage provides immediate relief and source control.

stages.label: PROCEDURE
stages.content:
Incision and drainage performed at bedside under local anesthesia
Approximately 15 mL of purulent material expressed
Wound left open to heal by secondary intention; packing placed
No deep extension or supralevator component identified on exam
Patient afebrile and markedly improved after drainage
Discharge with sitz baths and stool softeners

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: When counseling this patient after drainage, which long-term complication of an anorectal abscess should you specifically mention?
options: Permanent loss of bowel function
Development of an anal fistula
Progression to colon cancer in this setting
Inevitable need for a colostomy
stages.explanation: A significant proportion of drained anorectal abscesses (roughly one third) go on to form an anal fistula, a chronic epithelialized tract between the anal canal and perianal skin that causes recurrent drainage and may require later surgery. The patient should be counseled to return if persistent drainage develops. Permanent loss of bowel function is not an expected consequence of a simple drained abscess. There is no link between a perianal abscess and colon cancer. A colostomy is not inevitable and is reserved for rare severe or complex disease. Fistula formation is the key complication to anticipate and discuss.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which feature, if present, would have indicated that bedside drainage was inadequate and the patient instead needed evaluation in the operating room?
options: A simple, superficial, well-localized perianal abscess with appropriate follow-up
Mild discomfort with sitting with appropriate follow-up
A deep horseshoe or supralevator abscess, extensive cellulitis, or signs of necrotizing infection
A single episode of low-grade fever with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Deep or complex abscesses (ischiorectal with horseshoe extension, supralevator, intersphincteric) and any concern for necrotizing infection or extensive cellulitis, especially in immunocompromised or diabetic patients, require examination and drainage in the operating room for adequate exposure and source control. A simple superficial, well-localized perianal abscess like this one is appropriately drained at the bedside under local anesthesia. Mild discomfort with sitting and a single low-grade fever are expected with a simple abscess and do not mandate the OR. Recognizing complex anatomy or systemic toxicity is what triggers operative management.

cases.diagnosis: Perianal (anorectal) abscess treated with incision and drainage, with counseling on fistula risk
pearls: Anorectal abscesses arise from obstructed anal crypt glands and require incision and drainage, not antibiotics alone.
Add antibiotics for systemic illness, immunocompromise, diabetes, or surrounding cellulitis.
About one third of drained abscesses develop a fistula-in-ano.
Deep, horseshoe, supralevator, or necrotizing infections need drainage in the operating room.

cases.title: Chronic Wetness Near the Anus
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep getting drainage and dampness near my anus that won't go away.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents to clinic with three months of intermittent, malodorous drainage and persistent dampness near the anus, along with occasional itching and mild discomfort. He recalls a painful perianal abscess that was drained in the ED about four months ago; the acute pain resolved, but a small opening near the anus has continued to weep pus and blood-tinged fluid since then. The drainage waxes and wanes, occasionally building up pressure and then discharging. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; he denies diarrhea, weight loss, oral ulcers, or joint symptoms. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks socially. He works in sales and travels frequently. Family history is noncontributory, with no inflammatory bowel disease. Review of systems is negative for fever, significant pain, or systemic illness now, but he is frustrated by the chronic, recurrent drainage staining his clothing.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and afebrile. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Inspection of the perianal region reveals a small external opening with a surrounding granulation-tissue nodule located posterior to the anus, about 2.5 cm from the anal verge, with a small amount of purulent discharge expressible on gentle pressure. A cord-like indurated tract is palpable subcutaneously running from the external opening toward the anal canal. Digital rectal examination reveals a tender point at the dentate line posteriorly suggesting the internal opening. There is no surrounding fluctuance, abscess, spreading cellulitis, or necrosis, and no skin tags or fissures. The remainder of the perineum and the rest of the exam are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Chronic perianal drainage from an external opening following a drained abscess, with a palpable tract, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pilonidal cyst
Hemorrhoidal disease
Anal fissure in this clinical context
Anal fistula
stages.explanation: Chronic drainage from a perianal external opening with a palpable tract after a prior abscess is the classic picture of an anal fistula (fistula-in-ano), an epithelialized connection between the anal canal (internal opening at the dentate line) and the skin. An anal fissure causes painful defecation and bleeding with a posterior midline tear, not a draining external opening. A pilonidal cyst sits in the natal cleft over the sacrococcyx, higher and more midline, not at the anal verge connecting to the canal. Hemorrhoids cause bleeding and prolapse, not chronic purulent drainage with a tract. The abscess-then-fistula sequence is typical.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The external opening lies posterior to the anus. Using Goodsall rule, where is the internal opening expected to be located?
options: There is no predictable relationship
At the posterior midline via a curved tract
Always at the 3 o'clock position
At the anterior midline via a straight radial tract
stages.explanation: Goodsall rule predicts the course of the fistula tract by the location of the external opening relative to a transverse line through the anus. An external opening posterior to this line tracks in a curved path to an internal opening at the posterior midline, whereas an anterior external opening usually connects via a short straight radial tract to the nearest crypt. There is, in fact, a predictable relationship, so claiming none is wrong. The internal opening is not fixed at 3 o'clock. Applying Goodsall rule helps the surgeon locate the internal opening, which is essential for definitive treatment.

stages.label: EXAMINATION FINDINGS
stages.content:
Examination under anesthesia: single tract identified
External opening posterior, curved tract to internal opening at posterior midline crypt
Probe passes through the tract; it traverses a substantial portion of the external sphincter (high transsphincteric fistula)
No secondary tracts or supralevator extension
Anal sphincter tone intact at baseline
No evidence of Crohn disease on prior workup

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The tract is a high transsphincteric fistula crossing a substantial portion of the external sphincter. What is the most appropriate treatment to control sepsis while protecting continence?
options: Placement of a draining (loose) seton
Simple lay-open fistulotomy of the entire tract
No treatment; observe indefinitely
Long-term oral antibiotics alone as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For a high transsphincteric fistula that involves a significant amount of sphincter muscle, dividing the muscle with a primary fistulotomy risks fecal incontinence, so a draining (loose) seton is placed to keep the tract open, control infection, promote fibrosis, and protect continence before a definitive sphincter-sparing procedure (such as a cutting seton, LIFT, advancement flap, or fistula plug). Simple fistulotomy is appropriate only for low fistulas involving minimal sphincter. Doing nothing perpetuates drainage and recurrent abscesses. Antibiotics alone do not cure an epithelialized tract. Balancing sepsis control with continence preservation is the central principle in high fistulas.

stages.label: CONTRAST CASE
stages.question: If this had instead been a simple, low fistula crossing only minimal (subcutaneous) sphincter, what would be the preferred definitive treatment?
options: Diverting colostomy in this setting
Draining seton placement
Continuous antibiotic suppression
Fistulotomy
stages.explanation: For a simple, low fistula that crosses little or no sphincter muscle, fistulotomy, laying the tract open to heal by secondary intention, is the preferred definitive treatment with high cure rates and minimal continence risk. A draining seton is reserved for high or complex tracts where immediate division would threaten continence, so it is not first-line for a simple low fistula. A diverting colostomy is reserved for severe, complex, or refractory disease (often Crohn-related), not a simple fistula. Continuous antibiotics do not cure the epithelialized tract. Matching the procedure to how much sphincter is involved is the key decision.

cases.diagnosis: Anal fistula (fistula-in-ano), high transsphincteric, managed with a draining seton to preserve continence
pearls: Most anal fistulas follow a prior anorectal abscess and connect a crypt internal opening to the skin.
Goodsall rule: posterior external openings curve to a posterior midline internal opening; anterior ones run straight.
Low fistulas with minimal sphincter involvement are treated with fistulotomy.
High/complex fistulas get a draining seton first to control sepsis and protect continence before sphincter-sparing repair.

cases.title: A Sudden Painful Bump at the Anus
cases.chiefComplaint: I suddenly got a very painful lump at my anus yesterday.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents with the sudden onset, about 30 hours ago, of a painful lump at the anal verge. The pain was severe at onset and is worse with sitting and bowel movements. She has been constipated and straining recently and has a history of similar but milder episodes. She denies significant bleeding aside from a few streaks on the toilet paper, and she has no fever or systemic symptoms. She is currently 12 weeks postpartum and breastfeeding. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications are a prenatal vitamin and occasional acetaminophen; she takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. She works from home caring for her newborn and has had a low-fiber diet and reduced fluid intake. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for weight loss, change in stool caliber, diarrhea, or prior anorectal surgery.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable, particularly when sitting. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended. Inspection of the anal verge reveals a single, tense, bluish-purple, well-circumscribed subcutaneous nodule approximately 1 cm in diameter at the anal margin, exquisitely tender to light touch. The overlying skin is intact without ulceration, drainage, fluctuance, or surrounding cellulitis. There is no erythema spreading beyond the lump and no warmth suggesting abscess. Digital rectal examination is limited by tenderness but reveals no internal mass, no fluctuant collection, and normal sphincter tone. There is no active bright-red bleeding, no fissure, and no perianal fistula opening. The remainder of the perineal exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A sudden, exquisitely painful, tense bluish-purple nodule at the anal verge is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Perianal abscess
Anal fissure in this clinical context
Thrombosed external hemorrhoid
Rectal prolapse
stages.explanation: A sudden, tense, bluish-purple, well-circumscribed tender lump at the anal verge is a thrombosed external hemorrhoid, caused by clot within a subepithelial vein of the external hemorrhoidal plexus, often after straining or constipation. A perianal abscess is warm, erythematous, fluctuant, and frequently febrile, reflecting pus rather than a bluish clot. An anal fissure produces a painful tear with bleeding and a sentinel tag, not a discrete bluish nodule. Rectal prolapse presents as protruding rectal mucosa, not a small thrombosed lump. The acute onset, color, and tense character are characteristic of thrombosis.

stages.label: FOCUSED ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Onset: ~30 hours ago (within the early symptomatic window)
Lesion: 1 cm tense bluish nodule at the anal verge, skin intact, not fluctuant
No fever; no surrounding cellulitis or warmth (argues against abscess)
Digital rectal exam: no internal mass, normal sphincter tone
Pain severity: severe and ongoing, limiting sitting
No anticoagulant use; no bleeding diathesis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She presents within 72 hours of onset with severe, ongoing pain from the thrombosed external hemorrhoid. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent formal hemorrhoidectomy of all hemorrhoidal columns
Conservative care only with sitz baths and stool softeners
Excision of the thrombosed hemorrhoid under local anesthesia
Incision and drainage like an abscess as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a patient presenting within about 72 hours of onset with severe, persistent pain, excision of the thrombosed external hemorrhoid (removing the clot and the overlying skin) under local anesthesia provides the most rapid and durable pain relief and lowers recurrence compared with simple incision. Conservative care is appropriate when pain is already improving or beyond the early window, since the thrombus resorbs over a couple of weeks. A formal hemorrhoidectomy of all columns is excessive for a single acute thrombosis. Simple incision and drainage (as for an abscess) only evacuates clot partially, leaves the vein, and has higher recurrence and rebleeding. Timing within the window favors excision.

stages.label: CONTRAST SCENARIO
stages.question: If she had instead presented on day 6 with pain that was already markedly improving, what would be the preferred management?
options: Formal hemorrhoidectomy as the immediate next step
Immediate excision under local anesthesia in this setting
A course of broad-spectrum antibiotics before discharge planning
Conservative management with sitz baths, fiber, stool softeners,
stages.explanation: When a thrombosed external hemorrhoid presents late (beyond ~72 hours) and the pain is already resolving, conservative management with sitz baths, increased fiber and fluids, stool softeners, and analgesia is preferred because the clot is naturally organizing and resorbing, and excising it at this stage offers little benefit over spontaneous resolution. Excision is most useful early in the painful phase, not when pain is subsiding. A formal hemorrhoidectomy is unnecessary and overly aggressive. Antibiotics are not indicated because this is a thrombus, not an infection. Symptom trajectory and timing drive the choice between excision and conservative care.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which advice best helps prevent recurrent thrombosed external hemorrhoids in this postpartum patient?
options: Adopt a long-term low-fiber diet as the immediate next step
Begin daily aspirin therapy as the immediate next step
Increase dietary fiber
Routine prophylactic antibiotics as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Preventing recurrence centers on avoiding constipation and straining: a high-fiber diet, adequate fluids, stool softeners, and not lingering or straining on the toilet reduce venous engorgement and thrombosis, which is especially relevant postpartum when constipation is common. Daily aspirin would not prevent hemorrhoidal thrombosis and adds bleeding risk. A low-fiber diet worsens constipation and increases straining, promoting recurrence. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role since this is a vascular/thrombotic, not infectious, problem. Lifestyle and bowel-habit modification is the evidence-based prevention strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Thrombosed external hemorrhoid, treated with early excision and bowel-habit modification
pearls: A thrombosed external hemorrhoid is an acute, tense, bluish, tender nodule at the anal verge from venous clot.
Excision within ~72 hours of onset gives the fastest pain relief and lowest recurrence.
Late presentation with improving pain is managed conservatively as the clot resorbs.
Prevent recurrence with fiber, fluids, stool softeners, and avoiding straining.

cases.title: Sharp Pain and Bright Red Blood with Bowel Movements
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe knife-like anal pain during and after defecation for the past three weeks.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with three weeks of severe, tearing anal pain that begins with each bowel movement and lingers as a throbbing ache for one to two hours afterward. She notices small streaks of bright red blood on the toilet paper but not mixed in the stool. The pain has become so intense that she now dreads and postpones defecation, which has worsened her constipation. She reports passing hard, infrequent stools for several months, attributing this to a low-fiber diet and inadequate water intake during a busy work schedule. PMH is unremarkable. She takes only an oral combined contraceptive pill and an occasional ibuprofen 400 mg for the pain. No known drug allergies. She denies prior anorectal surgery, anal intercourse, inflammatory bowel disease symptoms, weight loss, fevers, or night sweats. Social history: works as an attorney, drinks alcohol socially, never smoked. Family history is notable for hemorrhoids in her mother but no colorectal cancer or IBD. She is not pregnant and her last menstrual period was two weeks ago.
cases.exam: The patient is in no distress at rest but is visibly apprehensive about the anorectal exam. Gentle separation of the buttocks reveals a linear tear in the anoderm at the posterior midline (6 o'clock position) with a small sentinel skin tag at its distal edge. The base of the fissure shows visible internal sphincter fibers. There is marked anal sphincter spasm and exquisite tenderness; the patient cannot tolerate digital rectal exam or anoscopy. There is no surrounding erythema, fluctuance, induration, or purulent drainage. No external thrombosed hemorrhoids, no perianal fistula opening, and no palpable mass. The abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history and the posterior midline linear tear with sentinel tag, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Perianal abscess
Thrombosed external hemorrhoid
Anorectal fistula
Anal fissure
stages.explanation: An anal fissure is a longitudinal tear in the anoderm distal to the dentate line, classically presenting with severe tearing pain during defecation, bright red blood on the paper, and a sentinel skin tag. Over 90% occur in the posterior midline due to relatively poor perfusion there. A thrombosed external hemorrhoid (wrong) produces a tender bluish perianal lump, not a linear tear. A perianal abscess (wrong) presents with fluctuance, erythema, induration, and often fever, none of which are present. An anorectal fistula (wrong) shows an external opening with chronic drainage and a palpable tract, also absent here. The visible exposed internal sphincter fibers and intense sphincter spasm are characteristic of a fissure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this acute anal fissure?
options: Sitz baths, increased dietary fiber,
Botulinum toxin injection into the internal sphincter
Lateral internal sphincterotomy in this clinical context
Topical hydrocortisone suppositories
stages.explanation: Conservative therapy is first line and heals the majority of acute fissures: warm sitz baths relax the sphincter and improve perfusion, while fiber supplementation, increased fluids, and stool softeners (e.g., docusate or psyllium) break the cycle of hard stools and pain-driven constipation. Lateral internal sphincterotomy (wrong) is definitive surgery reserved for chronic or refractory fissures, not initial therapy. Botulinum toxin (wrong) is a second-line option when topical agents fail, not first line. Topical hydrocortisone (wrong) treats inflammatory conditions and pruritus but does not address the elevated sphincter tone and ischemia underlying a fissure; it is not standard fissure therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She returns at 6 weeks with persistent pain and an unhealed fissure despite excellent compliance with sitz baths and fiber. What pharmacologic agent is most appropriate next?
options: Oral nifedipine extended-release for blood pressure control
Topical nitroglycerin or topical diltiazem ointment
Oral metronidazole as initial therapy
Topical lidocaine alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When conservative measures fail, topical chemical sphincter relaxants are next: topical nitroglycerin (0.4%) or topical calcium channel blocker (diltiazem 2%) lower internal sphincter tone, improve anodermal blood flow, and promote healing. Diltiazem is often preferred because nitroglycerin frequently causes headaches. Oral metronidazole (wrong) is an antibiotic with no role in fissure healing. Topical lidocaine alone (wrong) gives transient analgesia but does not relax the sphincter or correct the underlying ischemia, so the fissure persists. Oral nifedipine for blood pressure (wrong) misframes the goal; the therapeutic target is local sphincter relaxation, and systemic dosing risks hypotension without reliable local benefit.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 8 more weeks of topical diltiazem, the fissure remains painful and chronic in appearance. What is the definitive treatment of choice?
options: Long-term daily topical nitroglycerin indefinitely
Fissurectomy and diverting colostomy in this setting
Lateral internal sphincterotomy
Excision of the fissure with primary closure
stages.explanation: Lateral internal sphincterotomy is the gold-standard surgical treatment for chronic fissures refractory to medical therapy, with cure rates above 90%. Dividing a portion of the hypertonic internal sphincter relieves spasm and restores perfusion, allowing healing; the main risk is minor incontinence to flatus. Simple excision with primary closure (wrong) does not address the high sphincter tone and has high recurrence. Fissurectomy with diverting colostomy (wrong) is grossly excessive for a benign fissure. Indefinite topical nitroglycerin (wrong) is not definitive, causes recurrent headaches, and is impractical long term once medical therapy has already failed. Botulinum toxin is an alternative for patients wishing to avoid surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic anal fissure (posterior midline)
pearls: Over 90% of anal fissures occur in the posterior midline because of relatively poor blood supply in that region.
Lateral or multiple fissures should prompt evaluation for Crohn disease, malignancy, tuberculosis, HIV, or syphilis.
First-line therapy is conservative (sitz baths, fiber, stool softeners); topical diltiazem or nitroglycerin is added for refractory cases.
Lateral internal sphincterotomy is the definitive treatment for chronic fissures, curing over 90% with a small risk of flatus incontinence.

cases.title: Painful Swelling at the Top of the Buttock Cleft
cases.chiefComplaint: A tender, swollen, draining lump just above the buttock crease for the past five days.
cases.history: A 21-year-old man presents with five days of progressively worsening pain and swelling at the top of his buttock cleft. He first noticed a firm, tender bump that enlarged daily; this morning it began spontaneously draining foul-smelling, blood-tinged pus, which transiently relieved the pressure but not the pain. He reports low-grade fevers and difficulty sitting through his college lectures. He recalls similar but milder episodes twice over the past year that resolved on their own. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and used acetaminophen 500 mg today. No known drug allergies. Social history: full-time student, sedentary with long hours seated, drives for a rideshare service on weekends, and is a current smoker (half pack per day for three years). He is obese (BMI 32) and notes he is fairly hirsute. He denies diabetes, perianal pain with defecation, rectal bleeding, or weight loss. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative aside from the localized symptoms and mild malaise.
cases.exam: A well-appearing but uncomfortable young man. Examination of the natal cleft reveals a fluctuant, erythematous, exquisitely tender 3 cm mass in the sacrococcygeal midline, approximately 5 cm cephalad to the anus. There is a small midline pit superior to the mass, and a tuft of hair protrudes from a draining sinus opening with surrounding purulent, malodorous discharge. The surrounding skin is warm and indurated. There is no extension toward the anal verge, no perianal fistula opening on inspection, and the anal canal is normal on gentle digital rectal exam with no tenderness or mass. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the midline sacrococcygeal location, hair-containing sinus, and fluctuant mass, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Perianal abscess from cryptoglandular disease
Pilonidal abscess
Anal fistula with secondary infection
Hidradenitis suppurativa in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A pilonidal abscess is the classic acute presentation of pilonidal disease: a midline natal-cleft pit with hair-containing sinus tracts that become obstructed and infected, producing a tender, fluctuant, draining abscess in the sacrococcygeal region. Young, hirsute, overweight men who sit for long periods are at highest risk. A cryptoglandular perianal abscess (wrong) arises adjacent to the anal canal at the dentate-line glands, not 5 cm cephalad in the midline cleft. Hidradenitis suppurativa (wrong) affects apocrine-bearing skin (axillae, groin, perineum) with recurrent nodules and sinus tracts but is not classically a single midline sacrococcygeal pit with protruding hair. An anal fistula (wrong) connects to the anal canal, which is normal here.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management of this acute, fluctuant pilonidal abscess?
options: Immediate wide excision with flap closure
MRI of the pelvis before any intervention
Incision and drainage off the midline
Oral antibiotics alone and outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: An acute, fluctuant pilonidal abscess is treated with prompt incision and drainage, typically performed off the midline (lateral to the cleft) under local anesthesia; off-midline drainage heals better than midline incisions. Antibiotics alone (wrong) will not resolve a walled-off purulent collection and risk progression. Immediate wide excision with flap closure (wrong) is not appropriate in the acute infected setting because of high complication and wound-failure rates; definitive excision is staged after inflammation resolves. MRI before intervention (wrong) is unnecessary for a clinically obvious superficial abscess and would only delay needed drainage. Drainage provides immediate relief of pain and pressure.

stages.label: PROCEDURE & FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Bedside off-midline incision and drainage performed under local anesthesia
Approximately 15 mL of foul-smelling purulent material and a nest of loose hairs evacuated
Wound packed; patient afebrile and comfortable post-procedure
Wound culture: mixed skin and anaerobic flora
At 4-week follow-up: acute infection resolved, but a chronic midline pit with intermittent serous drainage and a persistent sinus tract remains
Three documented episodes of pilonidal infection in the past 14 months

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His acute infection resolved after drainage, but he now has a chronic, recurrent pilonidal sinus. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Excision of the pilonidal sinus tracts with attention to wound management
Repeat incision and drainage with each flare as initial therapy
Long-term suppressive oral antibiotics as initial therapy
Topical antifungal cream and observation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of chronic or recurrent pilonidal disease is excision of the sinus tracts and pits, combined with good wound management; options include excision with healing by secondary intention, marsupialization, or off-midline flap closure (e.g., Karydakis or cleft-lift), the latter associated with lower recurrence. Repeat I&D with each flare (wrong) treats acute episodes but does not cure recurrent disease. Long-term suppressive antibiotics (wrong) do not eradicate the epithelialized tracts and foster resistance. Topical antifungal cream (wrong) is irrelevant since this is a hair-driven foreign-body and infectious process, not a fungal one. Adjuncts such as meticulous hygiene, hair removal, and weight loss reduce recurrence.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What lifestyle counseling most directly reduces this patient's risk of pilonidal recurrence?
options: Increase dietary fiber and fluid intake in this clinical context
Local hair removal, weight loss,
Begin daily prophylactic antibiotics indefinitely
Avoid all physical activity for six months
stages.explanation: Recurrence is driven by hair entering the natal cleft, friction, moisture, and poor hygiene, so local hair removal (shaving or laser depilation), weight loss, and keeping the cleft clean and dry directly target the underlying mechanism; smoking cessation also aids wound healing. Increasing fiber and fluids (wrong) addresses constipation and anal fissures, not pilonidal disease. Avoiding all physical activity (wrong) is unnecessary and counterproductive to weight management. Daily prophylactic antibiotics (wrong) do not prevent the mechanical hair-entrapment cause and promote resistance. Patient education on these modifiable factors is a key component of long-term success after excision.

cases.diagnosis: Pilonidal disease (sacrococcygeal abscess with chronic hair-containing sinus)
pearls: Pilonidal disease classically affects young, hirsute, overweight men who sit for prolonged periods; recurrent hair entrapment in the natal cleft drives it.
Acute pilonidal abscess is managed with off-midline incision and drainage; off-midline closure heals better than midline.
Chronic or recurrent disease requires excision of sinus tracts, with off-midline flap techniques (Karydakis, cleft-lift) offering the lowest recurrence rates.
Hair removal, weight loss, hygiene, and smoking cessation are essential to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Anal Pain, Bleeding, and a Growing Mass in a Man with HIV
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of anal pain, bleeding, and a palpable lump at the anal verge.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man with well-controlled HIV (on antiretroviral therapy, most recent CD4 480 cells/µL, viral load undetectable) presents with four months of progressive anal pain, intermittent bright red rectal bleeding, pruritus, and a sensation of a mass at the anal verge. He has noticed mild fecal incontinence over the past few weeks and unintentional 5 kg weight loss. PMH includes HIV diagnosed 12 years ago, prior condyloma acuminata treated topically, and a remote history of anal high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion noted on a prior anal cytology that he did not follow up. Medications: bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide once daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: man who has sex with men, 20-pack-year smoking history (still smoking), social alcohol use. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for the symptoms above and negative for fevers, night sweats, abdominal pain, or change in stool caliber proximally. He reports prior HPV exposure and abnormal anal Pap results.
cases.exam: A thin but non-toxic-appearing man. Inspection of the perianal region reveals a firm, ulcerated, partly exophytic mass at the anal verge extending into the anal canal, with contact bleeding. Digital rectal exam confirms a hard, irregular, fixed lesion involving the anal canal; the lesion is tender and friable. There is palpable, firm, nontender right inguinal lymphadenopathy approximately 1.5 cm. No hepatomegaly and no abdominal masses. Anoscopy is limited by pain but confirms the lesion extends above the dentate line. No external thrombosed hemorrhoids and no perianal fistula. The remainder of the skin exam shows no condylomata currently.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the ulcerated anal mass, HIV, prior HPV-related anal dysplasia, and inguinal adenopathy, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Rectal adenocarcinoma
Anal melanoma in this clinical context
Anal squamous cell carcinoma
Crohn-related perianal fistulizing disease
stages.explanation: Anal squamous cell carcinoma is strongly suggested: it is HPV-driven, markedly more common in people with HIV and men who have sex with men, and arises in the anal canal/verge from squamous epithelium and the transition zone. Prior high-grade anal intraepithelial lesion and condylomata are precursors. Rectal adenocarcinoma (wrong) arises in glandular rectal mucosa higher up and presents with change in stool caliber and bleeding, not a verge mass with squamous risk factors. Anal melanoma (wrong) is rare, often pigmented, and would be less likely given the HPV/dysplasia history. Crohn perianal disease (wrong) causes fistulas and skin tags, not a hard ulcerated fixed mass with inguinal nodal spread.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the single most important next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Abdominoperineal resection for definitive treatment
Empiric chemoradiation based on appearance
Excisional biopsy of the inguinal lymph node first
Incisional biopsy of the anal lesion
stages.explanation: Tissue diagnosis is mandatory before any treatment; an incisional biopsy of the anal mass confirms squamous cell carcinoma and its histologic features. Abdominoperineal resection (wrong) is not the primary treatment for anal SCC and should never precede a diagnosis. Empiric chemoradiation without histology (wrong) is unacceptable because management hinges on the specific tissue diagnosis. Excisional inguinal node biopsy first (wrong) is not the priority; the primary lesion is accessible and should be biopsied, and FNA of a suspicious node can later confirm nodal involvement without a morbid excisional procedure. Establishing the primary diagnosis directs the entire treatment pathway.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & STAGING
stages.content:
Anal lesion biopsy: invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive (HPV-associated)
MRI pelvis: 4.2 cm anal canal mass with extension to perirectal soft tissue; enlarged right inguinal node
FNA of right inguinal node: positive for squamous cell carcinoma
CT chest/abdomen: no distant metastatic disease
PET-CT: hypermetabolic anal primary and right inguinal node; no distant uptake
CD4 count 480 cells/µL, HIV viral load undetectable
Clinical stage: T2-T3 N1 M0

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Which imaging modality is most useful for assessing nodal involvement and distant metastases to complete staging of anal squamous cell carcinoma?
options: Abdominal plain radiograph in this setting
Transabdominal ultrasound of the pelvis
Bone scan alone as the immediate next step
PET-CT
stages.explanation: PET-CT is the most useful study for staging anal SCC because it detects metabolically active inguinal/pelvic nodal disease and distant metastases, and it complements pelvic MRI, which best characterizes local (T) extent of the primary; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis also helps exclude distant spread. A plain abdominal radiograph (wrong) provides no useful information about soft-tissue tumor, nodes, or metastases. Transabdominal pelvic ultrasound (wrong) is poor for staging an anal canal tumor and cannot reliably assess nodal or distant disease. A bone scan alone (wrong) evaluates only osseous metastases, which are uncommon at presentation, and would miss nodal and visceral disease. Accurate staging with PET-CT and MRI directs the radiation fields and overall treatment plan.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms p16-positive invasive squamous cell carcinoma, stage T2-T3 N1 M0. What is the preferred first-line treatment?
options: Upfront abdominoperineal resection with permanent colostomy
Concurrent chemoradiation (the Nigro protocol)
Imatinib-based targeted therapy as initial therapy
Local excision of the primary alone as monotherapy
stages.explanation: The standard of care for non-metastatic anal squamous cell carcinoma is definitive concurrent chemoradiation—the Nigro protocol—using 5-fluorouracil plus mitomycin C with radiation. This sphincter-preserving regimen achieves high cure rates and avoids upfront surgery. Upfront abdominoperineal resection with permanent colostomy (wrong) is reserved for salvage after chemoradiation fails or for residual/recurrent disease, not as first-line. Local excision alone (wrong) is inadequate for an invasive T2-T3 N1 tumor and ignores nodal disease. Imatinib (wrong) targets c-KIT-driven GIST and has no role in squamous carcinoma. Chemoradiation is preferred specifically because it preserves anorectal function while controlling locoregional disease, including involved inguinal nodes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Three months after completing chemoradiation, exam and imaging show a persistent biopsy-proven residual anal mass. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Begin palliative hospice care
Salvage abdominoperineal resection
Repeat the identical chemoradiation course immediately
Switch to imatinib based on current risk
stages.explanation: When biopsy-proven disease persists or recurs after definitive chemoradiation, salvage abdominoperineal resection with permanent colostomy is the standard of care and offers the best chance of cure for locoregional residual disease. Importantly, anal SCC can regress slowly, so persistence should be confirmed (often allowing up to ~6 months of follow-up) before declaring failure—here it is biopsy-proven. Immediately repeating the same chemoradiation (wrong) risks excessive cumulative radiation toxicity. Hospice (wrong) is premature for potentially curable locoregional residual disease without metastases. Switching to imatinib (wrong) is irrelevant for squamous histology. Salvage surgery is precisely the indicated role for APR in this disease.

cases.diagnosis: HPV-related anal squamous cell carcinoma (stage T2-T3 N1 M0)
pearls: Anal squamous cell carcinoma is HPV-driven (p16 positive) and markedly more common in people with HIV and men who have sex with men.
Diagnosis requires biopsy of the primary lesion; FNA can confirm suspicious inguinal nodes.
First-line treatment is sphincter-preserving concurrent chemoradiation (Nigro protocol: 5-FU + mitomycin C with radiation), not upfront surgery.
Abdominoperineal resection is reserved for salvage of persistent or recurrent disease after chemoradiation fails.

cases.title: Fatigue, Dark Stools, and an Incidental Stomach Mass
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of fatigue, lightheadedness, and intermittent dark, tarry stools.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents with three weeks of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness. He has noticed several episodes of dark, tarry stools and mild epigastric fullness but denies frank hematemesis or severe pain. He has had no significant weight loss and his appetite is preserved. PMH includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily, which he began on his own a year ago. No NSAID overuse. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired accountant, drinks one glass of wine on weekends, former smoker (quit 15 years ago, 10 pack-years). Family history is unremarkable for gastrointestinal cancers. Review of systems is negative for dysphagia, early satiety to the degree of weight loss, jaundice, or prior peptic ulcer disease. He reports no personal or family history of neurofibromatosis. He takes no anticoagulants beyond aspirin and has never had an endoscopy.
cases.exam: A pale, mildly fatigued-appearing man who is hemodynamically stable. Conjunctivae are pale, consistent with anemia. Cardiovascular exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur and mild tachycardia.
