The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric fullness but no guarding or rebound; no palpable mass is appreciated and there is no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no lymphadenopathy, including no left supraclavicular node. Digital rectal exam reveals dark, melenic stool that is strongly guaiac positive. There is no jaundice, no peripheral edema, and skin shows no café-au-lait macules or neurofibromas. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After resuscitation and stabilization, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step for this patient with melena and iron-deficiency anemia?
options: CT angiography of the abdomen
Colonoscopy in this clinical context
Tagged red blood cell scan
Upper endoscopy (EGD)
stages.explanation: Melena and iron-deficiency anemia point to an upper gastrointestinal source, so upper endoscopy (EGD) is the test of choice; it allows direct visualization, biopsy, and potential hemostasis. Colonoscopy (wrong) evaluates the lower GI tract and is appropriate for hematochezia or after a negative EGD, not as the first study for melena. CT angiography (wrong) requires brisk active bleeding (≥0.5 mL/min) and is reserved for ongoing severe hemorrhage when endoscopy is unrevealing or not feasible. A tagged RBC scan (wrong) detects slow active bleeding but does not localize precisely or allow biopsy. EGD is both diagnostic and potentially therapeutic for an upper source.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
EGD: 4.5 cm submucosal mass along the greater curvature of the gastric body with a central ulceration and overlying clot (recent bleeding source)
Mucosa over the mass appears intact except at the ulcer; lesion appears intramural
Endoscopic ultrasound: hypoechoic mass arising from the fourth (muscularis propria) layer
Biopsy/EUS-FNA: spindle cell neoplasm
Immunohistochemistry: c-KIT (CD117) positive, DOG1 positive, CD34 positive
Mitotic rate: 6 per 50 high-power fields
CT abdomen/pelvis: solitary gastric mass, no hepatic or peritoneal metastases
Hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, MCV 74 fL, ferritin low

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the submucosal mass arising from the muscularis propria with c-KIT (CD117) and DOG1 positivity, what is the diagnosis?
options: Gastrointestinal stromal tumor
Leiomyoma as the primary diagnosis
Gastric adenocarcinoma
Gastric MALT lymphoma
stages.explanation: A gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) is the diagnosis: GISTs arise from the interstitial cells of Cajal in the muscularis propria, present as submucosal masses (often with central ulceration causing GI bleeding), and are characteristically c-KIT/CD117 and DOG1 positive with spindle cell morphology. Gastric adenocarcinoma (wrong) is a mucosal glandular cancer, not a c-KIT-positive intramural spindle cell tumor. MALT lymphoma (wrong) is a lymphoid neoplasm associated with H. pylori, not CD117 positive. Leiomyoma (wrong) is a smooth muscle tumor that is desmin/SMA positive but c-KIT and DOG1 negative. The immunohistochemical profile is the key discriminator pointing squarely to GIST.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 4.5 cm c-KIT-positive gastric GIST without metastases, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Total gastrectomy with extended D2 lymphadenectomy as initial therapy
Chemoradiation with 5-FU and mitomycin C as initial therapy
Surgical resection with negative margins, without routine lymphadenectomy
Endoscopic mucosal resection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Localized resectable GIST is treated with complete surgical resection aiming for negative (R0) margins; because GISTs rarely spread to lymph nodes, routine lymphadenectomy is not required, and a wedge/partial gastrectomy preserving stomach is preferred when feasible. Total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy (wrong) is the operation for gastric adenocarcinoma, not GIST, and unnecessarily aggressive. Chemoradiation with 5-FU/mitomycin (wrong) is the Nigro protocol for anal SCC and has no role in GIST. Endoscopic mucosal resection (wrong) is inadequate for a 4.5 cm intramural tumor arising from the muscularis propria and risks rupture, which seeds the peritoneum. The principle is margin-negative resection while avoiding tumor rupture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The tumor measures 4.5 cm with a mitotic rate of 6 per 50 HPF. What adjuvant therapy is most appropriate given this intermediate-to-high recurrence risk?
options: Adjuvant radiation to the gastric bed
Adjuvant imatinib
Adjuvant FOLFOX chemotherapy in this setting
Observation with no adjuvant therapy
stages.explanation: Imatinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting the constitutively active KIT receptor, is the adjuvant therapy for GISTs at intermediate-to-high risk of recurrence (assessed by size, mitotic rate, location, and rupture); a tumor >3-5 cm with a mitotic rate of 6/50 HPF qualifies, and at least 3 years of adjuvant imatinib improves recurrence-free survival. FOLFOX (wrong) is cytotoxic chemotherapy for colorectal and gastric adenocarcinoma; GISTs are notoriously chemo- and radio-resistant. Radiation (wrong) is likewise ineffective for GIST. Observation alone (wrong) undertreats an intermediate-to-high-risk tumor. Imatinib is also used neoadjuvantly to shrink large or marginally resectable GISTs.

cases.diagnosis: Gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), c-KIT positive
pearls: GISTs arise from the interstitial cells of Cajal in the muscularis propria and are typically c-KIT (CD117) and DOG1 positive; the stomach is the most common site.
They present as submucosal masses, often with central ulceration causing GI bleeding; EUS shows origin from the fourth (muscularis propria) layer.
Treatment is margin-negative resection without routine lymphadenectomy; avoid tumor rupture, which seeds the peritoneum.
Imatinib (a tyrosine kinase inhibitor) is used for high-risk adjuvant therapy and to downsize large or unresectable tumors; GISTs are chemo- and radio-resistant.

cases.title: Unintentional Weight Loss and Feeling Full After a Few Bites
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of early satiety, decreased appetite, and unintentional weight loss.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man presents with three months of progressive early satiety, vague epigastric discomfort, decreased appetite, and a 9 kg unintentional weight loss. He reports occasional nausea and a recent episode of dark stool. He has no dysphagia but feels full after only a few bites. PMH includes chronic Helicobacter pylori gastritis treated years ago, and he emigrated from a region with high gastric cancer incidence. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, which has not relieved his symptoms. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired factory worker, current smoker (40 pack-years), moderate alcohol use, diet historically high in smoked and salted foods. Family history: a brother with gastric cancer. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and the symptoms above, and negative for jaundice, hematemesis, or bowel obstruction symptoms. He denies prior gastric surgery. He has lost interest in eating because of the early fullness and is concerned about his weight loss.
cases.exam: A cachectic-appearing older man with temporal wasting. Abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness with a vaguely palpable, firm epigastric fullness but no obvious discrete mass; no hepatomegaly is appreciated. Crucially, there is a firm, enlarged, nontender left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow node). No periumbilical nodule is palpated and no left axillary node. Rectal exam shows guaiac-positive brown stool without a rectal shelf appreciated. There is no jaundice or ascites. Conjunctivae are mildly pale. The remainder of the cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given weight loss, early satiety, anemia, and a left supraclavicular node, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Barium upper GI series
Abdominal ultrasound
Upper endoscopy with biopsy
Serum gastrin level
stages.explanation: These alarm features in an older patient mandate upper endoscopy (EGD) with biopsy, which directly visualizes the gastric mucosa and provides tissue for diagnosis—essential for any suspected gastric malignancy. A barium UGI series (wrong) may show a mass or linitis plastica but cannot obtain tissue and has been supplanted by EGD. Abdominal ultrasound (wrong) is poor for evaluating the gastric wall and would miss mucosal lesions. A serum gastrin level (wrong) is used to work up gastrinoma/Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, not a suspected gastric adenocarcinoma with a Virchow node. EGD with multiple biopsies is the cornerstone of diagnosis and allows histologic confirmation.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
EGD: large ulcerated, irregular mass in the gastric antrum with heaped, friable margins
Gastric wall appears thickened and somewhat rigid
Biopsy: poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cells
H. pylori testing: negative (prior eradication)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: gastric wall thickening with regional lymphadenopathy; no definite liver or lung metastases
Left supraclavicular node FNA: metastatic adenocarcinoma
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL; albumin 3.0 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Biopsy reveals poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cells, and the supraclavicular node is positive. What does the positive Virchow node signify?
options: Localized disease amenable to curative resection alone
A benign reactive lymph node as the primary diagnosis
Distant metastatic (M1) spread
Regional nodal disease that is still resectable for cure
stages.explanation: A metastatic left supraclavicular (Virchow) node indicates distant metastatic (M1) disease via thoracic duct spread, signifying incurable, stage IV gastric adenocarcinoma. It is not localized disease amenable to resection alone (wrong), because the cancer has spread beyond regional confines. It is not a benign reactive node (wrong); FNA confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma. It is not merely regional nodal disease still resectable for cure (wrong); the supraclavicular location is outside the resectable regional field and defines metastatic spread. Recognizing the Virchow node as a marker of distant metastasis fundamentally changes management from curative surgery toward systemic and palliative therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Suppose instead that staging had shown no distant metastases and the disease appeared locally advanced but potentially resectable. What is the appropriate step before proceeding to gastrectomy?
options: Six months of observation with repeat EGD as initial therapy
Endoscopic mucosal resection of the primary as initial therapy
Diagnostic staging laparoscopy to evaluate for peritoneal disease
Immediate total gastrectomy without further staging
stages.explanation: For locally advanced gastric adenocarcinoma considered for curative resection, diagnostic staging laparoscopy (with peritoneal washings/cytology) is performed before gastrectomy because cross-sectional imaging frequently misses small-volume peritoneal carcinomatosis; detecting it spares patients a non-curative laparotomy. Immediate gastrectomy without staging (wrong) risks operating on patients with occult peritoneal disease. Endoscopic mucosal resection (wrong) is only appropriate for early, well-differentiated, mucosa-confined tumors—not a bulky poorly differentiated signet-ring cancer. Observation with repeat EGD (wrong) inappropriately delays treatment of an aggressive malignancy. Perioperative chemotherapy is also standard for locally advanced disease, but staging laparoscopy is the key step that determines resectability.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the confirmed metastatic (M1) gastric adenocarcinoma with the positive Virchow node, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Curative-intent total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy
Palliative systemic chemotherapy
Concurrent definitive chemoradiation for cure
Esophagectomy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: With distant metastatic (stage IV) gastric adenocarcinoma, treatment is palliative: systemic chemotherapy (e.g., a fluoropyrimidine/platinum doublet, with HER2-targeted therapy if HER2 positive) plus best supportive care to control symptoms and prolong survival. Curative-intent total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy (wrong) is for non-metastatic resectable disease; surgery in M1 disease is generally reserved for palliation of obstruction or bleeding, not cure. Definitive chemoradiation for cure (wrong) does not address widespread metastatic disease. Esophagectomy (wrong) is the operation for esophageal cancer and is irrelevant here. The Virchow node confirms incurability and shifts the goal to disease control and quality of life.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma (signet-ring type) with Virchow node
pearls: Alarm features (weight loss, early satiety, anemia, age >55) warrant prompt EGD with biopsy; risk factors include H. pylori, smoking, salted/smoked foods, and family history.
A left supraclavicular (Virchow) node signifies distant metastatic (M1) disease via thoracic duct spread—incurable stage IV disease.
Staging laparoscopy with peritoneal washings precedes gastrectomy in locally advanced disease to detect occult peritoneal carcinomatosis.
Resectable disease is treated with gastrectomy plus D2 lymphadenectomy and perioperative chemotherapy; metastatic disease is managed with palliative systemic therapy.

cases.title: Trouble Swallowing Solids and a Sticking Sensation Behind the Breastbone
cases.chiefComplaint: Six months of progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods with a sensation of food sticking.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents with six months of progressive dysphagia, initially to dense solids like steak and bread, now to most solid foods, with a sensation of food sticking behind his sternum. He has lost 7 kg unintentionally and modified his diet to softer foods. He reports decades of heartburn and was diagnosed with Barrett esophagus on surveillance endoscopy several years ago but missed his last two follow-ups. He has occasional regurgitation but no odynophagia. PMH includes long-standing GERD, obesity, and hypertension. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: 30-pack-year smoking history (still smoking), regular alcohol use, works in construction. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for weight loss, dysphagia, and chronic reflux; negative for hematemesis, hoarseness, or cough. He notes the dysphagia is steadily worsening rather than intermittent, and liquids remain easy to swallow.
cases.exam: An overweight man with mild temporal wasting suggesting recent weight loss. He is not in distress. Oropharyngeal exam is normal with no thrush or masses. Neck exam shows no palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear without signs of aspiration. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses, hepatomegaly, or ascites. There is no jaundice. No peripheral lymphadenopathy elsewhere. Conjunctivae are not pale. The remainder of the exam, including cardiovascular and neurologic exams, is unremarkable. There is no periumbilical nodule.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this older patient with progressive solid-food dysphagia, weight loss, and Barrett esophagus, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Upper endoscopy (EGD) with biopsy
Barium esophagram alone
Esophageal manometry
24-hour ambulatory pH monitoring
stages.explanation: Progressive solid-food dysphagia with weight loss and a Barrett history is a red flag for esophageal cancer; upper endoscopy with biopsy is the test of choice because it both visualizes any mass/stricture and provides tissue. A barium esophagram alone (wrong) may show a stricture or apple-core lesion but cannot biopsy and would delay definitive diagnosis. Esophageal manometry (wrong) evaluates motility disorders such as achalasia, not an obstructing mass, and is not the first step here. Ambulatory pH monitoring (wrong) quantifies acid reflux for GERD workup and is irrelevant to evaluating alarming dysphagia. EGD with biopsy directly addresses the concern for malignancy in this high-risk patient.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
EGD: ulcerated, partially obstructing mass in the distal esophagus near the gastroesophageal junction, arising in a segment of Barrett mucosa
Biopsy: invasive adenocarcinoma
Endoscopic ultrasound: tumor invades the muscularis propria (T2-T3) with two suspicious periesophageal nodes (N1)
PET-CT: hypermetabolic distal esophageal mass and regional nodes; no distant metastatic uptake
CT chest/abdomen: no liver, lung, or distant disease
Clinical stage: locally advanced (cT3 N1 M0)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma arising in Barrett mucosa at the distal esophagus. Which risk factor is most strongly associated with this histologic type and location?
options: Achalasia as the primary diagnosis
Hot beverage consumption as the primary diagnosis
Human papillomavirus infection as the primary diagnosis
Chronic gastroesophageal reflux with Barrett metaplasia
stages.explanation: Esophageal adenocarcinoma arises in the distal esophagus from Barrett metaplasia caused by chronic GERD; obesity and smoking add risk, and this is now the predominant esophageal cancer type in Western countries. Achalasia (wrong) is a risk factor for squamous cell carcinoma, not adenocarcinoma. Hot beverage consumption (wrong) and tobacco/alcohol predispose to squamous cell carcinoma, typically in the mid/upper esophagus. HPV infection (wrong) is linked to certain squamous cancers (notably anal and oropharyngeal), not esophageal adenocarcinoma. The Barrett (reflux → metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma) sequence in the distal esophagus is the key association driving this diagnosis.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Which modality most accurately determines the depth of tumor invasion (T) and regional nodal status (N) for treatment planning in esophageal cancer?
options: Barium esophagram as the immediate next step
Plain chest radiograph as the immediate next step
Esophageal manometry as the immediate next step
Endoscopic ultrasound , often with FNA of suspicious nodes
stages.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound is the most accurate test for locoregional (T and N) staging of esophageal cancer: it images the layered wall to gauge depth of invasion and can sample suspicious periesophageal nodes with FNA, complementing PET-CT and CT for distant disease. A plain chest radiograph (wrong) cannot assess tumor depth or nodal status. A barium esophagram (wrong) shows luminal narrowing or an apple-core lesion but provides no information on wall-layer invasion or nodes. Esophageal manometry (wrong) measures motility for disorders like achalasia and has no staging role. Accurate EUS-based T/N staging is essential because it determines whether a patient receives neoadjuvant therapy plus surgery versus another approach.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this locally advanced cT3 N1 distal esophageal adenocarcinoma, what is the preferred treatment approach?
options: Upfront esophagectomy alone as initial therapy
Neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by esophagectomy
Definitive chemoradiation with no surgery in all cases
Endoscopic mucosal resection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Locally advanced resectable esophageal adenocarcinoma is best treated with trimodality therapy: neoadjuvant chemoradiation (per the CROSS regimen, carboplatin/paclitaxel with radiation) followed by esophagectomy, which improves R0 resection rates and survival over surgery alone. Upfront esophagectomy alone (wrong) yields inferior outcomes for T3/node-positive disease. Definitive chemoradiation without surgery in all cases (wrong) is reserved for cervical tumors or patients who are unfit or decline surgery, not the default for a resectable distal adenocarcinoma. Endoscopic mucosal resection (wrong) is only appropriate for early, mucosa-confined (T1a) lesions, not a T3 node-positive tumor. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation then surgery is the standard multimodal strategy here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On postoperative day 6 after esophagectomy, he develops fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and increased turbid chest tube output. What is the most likely complication?
options: Pulmonary embolism
Anastomotic leak
Anastomotic stricture
Chylothorax as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Anastomotic leak is the most feared early complication after esophagectomy, presenting with fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and turbid or salivary chest tube drainage, often around postoperative days 5-10; it can cause mediastinitis and sepsis. Diagnosis is confirmed with a contrast esophagram or CT, and management ranges from drainage and NPO/antibiotics to reoperation. Chylothorax (wrong) produces milky, triglyceride-rich pleural fluid from thoracic duct injury but typically lacks the same septic picture. Pulmonary embolism (wrong) causes hypoxia and pleuritic pain, not turbid drainage and leukocytosis. Anastomotic stricture (wrong) is a late complication causing dysphagia weeks to months later, not acute postoperative sepsis. Early recognition of a leak is critical to survival.

cases.diagnosis: Distal esophageal adenocarcinoma arising in Barrett esophagus (cT3 N1 M0)
pearls: Esophageal adenocarcinoma arises in the distal esophagus from Barrett metaplasia driven by chronic GERD, obesity, and smoking; it is now the most common type in Western countries.
Progressive solid-food dysphagia with weight loss is an alarm symptom mandating EGD with biopsy; EUS and PET-CT complete staging.
Locally advanced resectable disease is treated with trimodality therapy: neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS regimen) followed by esophagectomy.
Anastomotic leak (POD 5-10, fever, tachycardia, turbid drainage) is the most dangerous early post-esophagectomy complication.

cases.title: Trouble Swallowing Both Solids and Liquids with Regurgitation
cases.chiefComplaint: A year of difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids with regurgitation of undigested food.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with about a year of slowly progressive difficulty swallowing that affects both solids and liquids from the outset. She regurgitates undigested, non-acidic food, especially when lying down, and has woken at night coughing. She has lost 5 kg and sometimes drinks water or arches her neck to push food down. She denies heartburn responsive to acid suppression and notes her symptoms did not improve on a proton pump inhibitor. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only an occasional antacid and the omeprazole trial that failed. No known drug allergies. Social history: schoolteacher, nonsmoker, rare alcohol use, no recent travel to Central or South America. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for the dysphagia, regurgitation, nocturnal cough, and weight loss; negative for odynophagia, hematemesis, hoarseness, or progressive solid-only dysphagia. She emphasizes that liquids are just as hard to swallow as solids, which has frustrated her for months.
cases.exam: A well-nourished though slightly thin woman in no acute distress. Oropharyngeal exam is normal without thrush, masses, or mucosal lesions. Neck exam reveals no thyromegaly, masses, or lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear without crackles or wheezes, though she reports nocturnal cough. The abdomen is soft, nontender, and without masses or organomegaly. There is no supraclavicular adenopathy. Cardiovascular and neurologic exams are unremarkable. There are no signs of scleroderma such as skin tightening, sclerodactyly, or telangiectasias. She has no halitosis and no neck bulging on swallowing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given dysphagia to both solids and liquids from the start with regurgitation, what initial test is most appropriate first?
options: 24-hour pH monitoring
Barium esophagram
Esophageal manometry
Upper endoscopy
stages.explanation: Dysphagia to both solids and liquids from the onset suggests a motility disorder, and a barium esophagram is a reasonable initial study—classically showing a dilated esophagus with smooth, tapering 'bird-beak' narrowing at the gastroesophageal junction in achalasia. However, upper endoscopy is also essential to exclude a malignancy mimicking achalasia (pseudoachalasia), and many algorithms perform EGD early. Esophageal manometry (wrong as the very first test) is the confirmatory gold standard but typically follows initial imaging/endoscopy. pH monitoring (wrong) evaluates acid reflux, not a motility/obstruction problem. The barium swallow efficiently raises the suspicion of achalasia and guides subsequent confirmatory manometry and mandatory endoscopy.

stages.label: IMAGING & ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Barium esophagram: dilated esophageal body with smooth, tapering 'bird-beak' narrowing at the gastroesophageal junction and retained food/contrast
Upper endoscopy: dilated esophagus with retained food; the scope passes through the GE junction with gentle pressure; no obstructing mass or stricture
No mucosal lesions or malignancy seen (pseudoachalasia excluded)
High-resolution manometry: incomplete lower esophageal sphincter relaxation with elevated integrated relaxation pressure and aperistalsis of the esophageal body

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The manometry shows incomplete LES relaxation with elevated integrated relaxation pressure and absent esophageal peristalsis. What is the diagnosis?
options: Diffuse esophageal spasm
Esophageal carcinoma
Achalasia
Gastroesophageal reflux disease
stages.explanation: Achalasia is defined by impaired relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (elevated integrated relaxation pressure) plus aperistalsis of the esophageal body, caused by degeneration of inhibitory myenteric (Auerbach) plexus neurons. The bird-beak esophagram and these manometric findings are diagnostic. Diffuse esophageal spasm (wrong) shows premature, uncoordinated (simultaneous) contractions—'corkscrew' esophagus—with normal LES relaxation, not aperistalsis. GERD (wrong) involves acid reflux and would not produce bird-beak narrowing or aperistalsis. Esophageal carcinoma (wrong) was excluded on endoscopy; manometry would not show this pattern, and pseudoachalasia from tumor was ruled out. The combination of impaired LES relaxation and absent peristalsis is the manometric hallmark.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this otherwise healthy 45-year-old woman who is a good surgical candidate, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Long-term oral nitrates and calcium channel blockers as initial therapy
Botulinum toxin injection into the LES as initial therapy
Laparoscopic Heller myotomy or peroral endoscopic myotomy
Proton pump inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Durable treatment of achalasia in a good surgical candidate is myotomy of the lower esophageal sphincter—laparoscopic Heller myotomy, typically with a partial (Dor or Toupet) fundoplication to limit reflux, or peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM); both relieve the functional obstruction. Pneumatic dilation is another effective definitive option. Oral nitrates/calcium channel blockers (wrong) provide only modest, temporary relief and are reserved for poor candidates. Botulinum toxin into the LES (wrong) gives transient benefit and is used for elderly or frail patients unfit for definitive therapy. PPIs (wrong) treat acid reflux and do nothing for the impaired LES relaxation and aperistalsis of achalasia. Myotomy (Heller or POEM) is the durable definitive choice here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Because achalasia carries a long-term risk of one esophageal complication, what surveillance or counseling is most appropriate?
options: Begin lifelong prophylactic antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Counsel on a small increased long-term risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma
No long-term concerns; discharge without follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Screen annually for esophageal adenocarcinoma from Barrett esophagus
stages.explanation: Long-standing achalasia carries a small but real increased risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, attributed to chronic stasis and mucosal irritation; patients should be counseled and monitored for new or worsening symptoms. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics (wrong) have no role. Screening for adenocarcinoma from Barrett esophagus (wrong) is the concern in chronic GERD, not achalasia, whose associated cancer is squamous in type. Discharging without any follow-up (wrong) ignores both the chronic nature of the disease and its cancer association, as well as the need to monitor treatment response and possible reflux after myotomy. Appropriate counseling and symptom-directed surveillance are the correct long-term approach.

cases.diagnosis: Achalasia
pearls: Achalasia presents with dysphagia to BOTH solids and liquids from onset, regurgitation of undigested food, and nocturnal cough; it is due to loss of inhibitory myenteric neurons.
Barium swallow shows a dilated esophagus with a 'bird-beak' tapering; manometry (impaired LES relaxation + aperistalsis) is the gold standard, and endoscopy must exclude pseudoachalasia from tumor.

Definitive therapy in good candidates is laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication or POEM; pneumatic dilation is an alternative. Botulinum toxin or nitrates/CCBs are for poor surgical candidates.
Long-standing achalasia carries a small increased risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma.

cases.title: Persistent Heartburn and Regurgitation Despite Daily Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Long-standing heartburn and regurgitation that persist despite daily acid-suppressing medication.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with years of heartburn and acid regurgitation, worse after large meals and when lying flat, with occasional nocturnal cough and a sour taste in his mouth. Despite taking a proton pump inhibitor faithfully for over a year, his symptoms remain bothersome and he is frustrated. He has gained weight and notes a sensation of food and acid coming back up. PMH includes obesity (BMI 34) and well-controlled hypertension. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg twice daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: works in sales, drinks coffee and alcohol regularly, former smoker (quit 5 years ago). Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for heartburn, regurgitation, and intermittent cough; importantly negative for dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, hematemesis, or melena. He has had no alarm features. He is interested in a long-term solution because he dislikes taking medication indefinitely and his reflux disrupts his sleep.
cases.exam: An overweight, well-appearing man in no distress. Oropharynx is normal without erythema or lesions; dentition shows mild enamel changes. Neck exam reveals no masses or lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiovascular exam is normal. The abdomen is obese, soft, and nontender, with no palpable mass or organomegaly and no epigastric tenderness. There are no signs of anemia and conjunctivae are pink. He has no supraclavicular nodes and no abdominal wall hernias. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. There are no neurologic deficits and no signs of scleroderma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has typical reflux symptoms refractory to optimized PPI therapy. Before considering antireflux surgery, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Upper endoscopy to evaluate the mucosa
Proceed directly to Nissen fundoplication
Barium swallow only as the first step today
Empirically double the PPI dose again indefinitely
stages.explanation: In a patient with reflux refractory to optimized PPI therapy who is considering surgery, upper endoscopy is the appropriate first step to assess for esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, strictures, a hiatal hernia, or other mucosal pathology and to exclude alternative diagnoses. Proceeding directly to Nissen fundoplication (wrong) skips the mandatory objective workup that confirms reflux and defines anatomy. Endlessly escalating the PPI dose (wrong) is futile in true refractory disease and delays definitive evaluation. A barium swallow alone (wrong) can show a hiatal hernia but cannot evaluate the mucosa or obtain biopsies. Endoscopy is the foundational study before any antireflux procedure.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: a sliding (type I) hiatal hernia with the gastroesophageal junction displaced above the diaphragmatic hiatus
Los Angeles grade B erosive esophagitis in the distal esophagus
No Barrett metaplasia, stricture, or mass
Biopsies negative for dysplasia and malignancy
No evidence of eosinophilic esophagitis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Before offering antireflux surgery, which two confirmatory studies are most important to obtain after endoscopy?
options: Chest X-ray and electrocardiogram in this clinical context
Ambulatory pH/impedance monitoring
Serum gastrin and H. pylori serology
Gastric emptying study and abdominal CT
stages.explanation: Before antireflux surgery, ambulatory pH/impedance monitoring objectively confirms pathologic acid (and nonacid) reflux and correlates it with symptoms, while esophageal manometry assesses peristalsis to exclude a motility disorder (e.g., achalasia or severe dysmotility) and to tailor the type of fundoplication (a full Nissen versus a partial wrap if peristalsis is weak). A gastric emptying study and CT (wrong) are not routine preoperative reflux studies. Serum gastrin and H. pylori serology (wrong) address Zollinger-Ellison and ulcer disease, not surgical reflux planning. Chest X-ray and ECG (wrong) are general preoperative tests, not reflux-specific confirmatory studies. The pH study confirms the disease, and manometry ensures the patient can tolerate a wrap.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: pH monitoring confirms pathologic acid reflux and manometry shows normal esophageal peristalsis. What is the most appropriate definitive surgical option?
options: Esophagectomy as initial therapy
Heller myotomy as initial therapy
Total gastrectomy as initial therapy
Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication with hiatal hernia repair
stages.explanation: With objectively confirmed pathologic reflux, normal esophageal peristalsis, and a hiatal hernia in a patient refractory to/intolerant of medical therapy, a laparoscopic Nissen (360° complete) fundoplication with hiatal hernia repair (crural closure) is the standard definitive antireflux operation; normal peristalsis makes a full wrap appropriate. A Heller myotomy (wrong) treats achalasia by cutting the LES—exactly the opposite of what is needed for reflux. Total gastrectomy (wrong) is for gastric cancer and grossly inappropriate. Esophagectomy (wrong) is for esophageal malignancy or end-stage disease, not GERD. The Nissen fundoplication recreates a competent lower esophageal sphincter mechanism and reduces the hernia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After counseling, what is the most common side effect he should be warned about following a Nissen fundoplication?
options: Dumping syndrome with hypoglycemia
Temporary dysphagia and gas-bloat syndrome
Recurrent peptic ulcer disease based on current risk
Permanent fecal incontinence based on current risk
stages.explanation: The most common side effects after Nissen fundoplication are early postoperative dysphagia (usually transient as edema resolves) and gas-bloat syndrome—difficulty belching or vomiting and abdominal bloating because the competent wrap traps swallowed air. Patients are counseled to advance diet gradually and avoid carbonated beverages. Permanent fecal incontinence (wrong) is unrelated to an upper GI antireflux procedure. Recurrent peptic ulcer disease (wrong) is not a characteristic complication of fundoplication. Dumping syndrome with hypoglycemia (wrong) follows gastric resections/bypass that disrupt the pylorus, not a fundoplication that preserves the stomach. Counseling on transient dysphagia and gas-bloat sets appropriate expectations and improves satisfaction.

cases.diagnosis: GERD with sliding hiatal hernia (refractory to medical therapy)
pearls: Refractory typical GERD considered for surgery requires objective workup: endoscopy first, then ambulatory pH/impedance monitoring and esophageal manometry.
Manometry excludes a motility disorder and guides the wrap—full Nissen (360°) if peristalsis is normal, partial (Toupet/Dor) if peristalsis is weak.
Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication with hiatal hernia (crural) repair is the standard definitive antireflux operation.
Counsel patients about transient postoperative dysphagia and gas-bloat syndrome (difficulty belching) after fundoplication.

cases.title: Severe Chest Pain After Forceful Vomiting
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe chest and upper abdominal pain that began immediately after several episodes of forceful vomiting.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with sudden, severe, tearing retrosternal and epigastric pain that began immediately after a bout of forceful vomiting and retching following a large meal and heavy alcohol intake at a party. The pain radiates to his back and left shoulder and worsens with swallowing and deep breaths. He feels short of breath and diaphoretic. He vomited several times before the pain started, including some bloody-streaked emesis. PMH includes alcohol use disorder and GERD. Medications: occasional over-the-counter antacids. No known drug allergies. Social history: heavy alcohol use, smokes one pack per day. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for chest pain, dyspnea, odynophagia, and diaphoresis; he denies prior cardiac disease but is anxious and in obvious distress. EMS noted he looked unwell with neck swelling. The vomiting preceded the pain, a sequence he clearly recalls. He has had no recent endoscopy or instrumentation.
cases.exam: An acutely ill, diaphoretic man in moderate respiratory distress. He is tachycardic and tachypneic. Notable finding: subcutaneous crepitus is palpable over the neck and supraclavicular region (subcutaneous emphysema). Auscultation reveals a crunching sound synchronous with the heartbeat over the precordium (Hamman crunch) and decreased breath sounds at the left base. The abdomen is mildly tender in the epigastrium with voluntary guarding but no rigidity or rebound. He appears volume-depleted with cool extremities and a thready pulse. Cardiac exam is otherwise tachycardic without murmurs. He is mentating but uncomfortable and apprehensive.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This acutely ill, tachycardic, hypotensive man arrives in the ED in distress. What is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Administer oral contrast and send him to radiology as the first step today
Assess airway, breathing,
Give a beta-blocker for the tachycardia as the first step today
Obtain a CT scan before any intervention as the first step today
stages.explanation: An acutely ill, hypotensive, tachypneic patient requires immediate ABC assessment with large-bore IV access, fluid resuscitation, and supplemental oxygen before diagnostic workup; this patient shows early sepsis/shock physiology from mediastinal contamination. Obtaining a CT first (wrong) delays life-saving resuscitation in an unstable patient—stabilize before imaging. Giving oral contrast and sending him to radiology (wrong) is premature and unsafe before stabilization and airway assessment, and risks aspiration. A beta-blocker for the tachycardia (wrong) is dangerous because the tachycardia is a compensatory response to hypovolemia/sepsis; blunting it can precipitate cardiovascular collapse. Resuscitation and stabilization always precede definitive diagnosis in the unstable patient.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given forceful vomiting followed by chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, and a Hamman crunch, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Boerhaave syndrome
Acute myocardial infarction
Mallory-Weiss tear
Aortic dissection in this clinical context
stages.explanation: This is the classic Mackler triad of Boerhaave syndrome—vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema—from a full-thickness, barogenic rupture of the esophagus (usually the distal left posterolateral wall) after forceful retching. The Hamman crunch and pneumomediastinum support it. Acute MI (wrong) is in the differential for chest pain but does not cause subcutaneous emphysema or a Hamman crunch and is not precipitated by vomiting in this pattern. A Mallory-Weiss tear (wrong) is only a mucosal tear causing hematemesis, not transmural perforation with mediastinal air. Aortic dissection (wrong) gives tearing pain radiating to the back but not subcutaneous emphysema or the vomiting-then-pain sequence. The triad and air findings point to esophageal rupture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After initial resuscitation (IV fluids, NPO, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and PPI), what is the best confirmatory diagnostic test?
options: Plain chest X-ray alone as the immediate next step
Cardiac catheterization as the immediate next step
Upper endoscopy with insufflation as the immediate next step
Water-soluble contrast esophagram , or CT with oral contrast
stages.explanation: Esophageal perforation is confirmed with a water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram showing extravasation, or CT of the chest/abdomen with oral contrast, which localizes the leak and assesses mediastinal/pleural contamination. Water-soluble contrast is used first to avoid barium-induced mediastinitis; if negative but suspicion is high, dilute barium can follow. Upper endoscopy with insufflation (wrong) risks enlarging the perforation and forcing air into the mediastinum. Plain chest X-ray alone (wrong) may suggest pneumomediastinum, pleural effusion, or hydropneumothorax but is not definitive for the leak site. Cardiac catheterization (wrong) addresses coronary disease, which is not the problem here. Confirming and localizing the perforation directs surgical planning.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 17.8 ×10³/µL
Lactate 3.4 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: pneumomediastinum, left pleural effusion, and a left hydropneumothorax
Gastrografin esophagram: contrast extravasation from the distal esophagus into the left pleural space and mediastinum
CT chest: mediastinal air and fluid with left pleural contamination; perforation of the distal esophagus
Perforation identified within 12 hours of symptom onset
Patient remains hypotensive responsive to fluids; mild tachycardia persists

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The esophagram confirms a distal esophageal perforation with mediastinal and pleural contamination, diagnosed within 12 hours. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Elective repair scheduled in one week as initial therapy
Emergent surgical repair with wide mediastinal
Esophageal stenting as the sole therapy in all cases as monotherapy
Observation with NPO and antibiotics alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An early (within ~24 hours) thoracic esophageal perforation with mediastinal/pleural contamination is a surgical emergency: prompt operative primary repair (often buttressed with surrounding tissue) plus wide drainage of the mediastinum and pleural space, along with antibiotics and nutritional support, offers the best survival. Observation with antibiotics alone (wrong) is reserved only for small, contained perforations in stable patients without ongoing contamination—not this contaminated free leak with sepsis physiology. Stenting as sole therapy in all cases (wrong) is an option in selected stable, contained leaks or poor surgical candidates but does not replace drainage of gross contamination here. Elective repair in a week (wrong) is dangerously delayed; mortality rises sharply with delay beyond 24 hours.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What single factor most strongly determines this patient's prognosis after Boerhaave syndrome?
options: The color of the emesis based on current risk
The patient's body mass index
Time from perforation to definitive treatment
Whether the patient received a PPI
stages.explanation: The time from perforation to definitive treatment is the strongest determinant of outcome in Boerhaave syndrome; mortality climbs steeply once intervention is delayed beyond about 24 hours because mediastinitis and sepsis progress rapidly. Early recognition and prompt repair/drainage dramatically improve survival, which is why this diagnosis must not be missed. Body mass index (wrong) influences operative difficulty but is not the dominant prognostic factor. Whether a PPI was given (wrong) is supportive care, not the key prognostic driver. The color of the emesis (wrong) is clinically incidental and does not predict outcome. The take-home is that early diagnosis and treatment save lives in esophageal rupture.

cases.diagnosis: Boerhaave syndrome (spontaneous esophageal perforation)
pearls: Boerhaave syndrome is a transmural esophageal rupture after forceful vomiting, classically presenting with the Mackler triad: vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema.
A Hamman crunch and pneumomediastinum on imaging are characteristic; confirm with a water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram or CT with oral contrast—never barium first.
Early thoracic perforation (<24 h) with contamination requires emergent primary surgical repair plus wide mediastinal/pleural drainage, antibiotics, and nutritional support.
Time to definitive treatment is the strongest prognostic factor; mortality rises sharply after 24 hours.

cases.title: Difficulty Swallowing with Regurgitation of Old Food and Bad Breath
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of difficulty swallowing with regurgitation of undigested food, bad breath, and a gurgling neck.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man presents with several months of progressive difficulty swallowing accompanied by regurgitation of undigested food hours after eating, sometimes onto his pillow at night. He reports persistent bad breath (halitosis) that embarrasses him, a gurgling sensation in his neck, and intermittent coughing fits, especially at night. He has had two episodes of pneumonia in the past year. He sometimes feels food 'stick' high in his throat and presses on his neck to clear it. He has lost 4 kg. PMH includes hypertension and remote pneumonia. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired carpenter, former smoker (quit 10 years ago), occasional alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for dysphagia, regurgitation of old food, halitosis, nocturnal cough, and recurrent pneumonia; negative for hematemesis, melena, and odynophagia. The regurgitated material is undigested and non-acidic, and his symptoms are worse later in the meal.
cases.exam: An older man, mildly thin, in no acute distress. Oropharyngeal exam is notable for halitosis but no masses, thrush, or mucosal lesions. Neck exam reveals a soft, vaguely palpable left-sided neck fullness that produces a gurgling sound on palpation; no firm mass or lymphadenopathy. Lungs have scattered crackles at the right base, consistent with prior aspiration. Cardiovascular exam is normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without masses. There is no supraclavicular adenopathy and no thyromegaly. Cranial nerve and neurologic exams are intact, with no focal weakness to suggest a neurologic cause of dysphagia. Dentition is fair.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given oropharyngeal dysphagia with regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, and a gurgling neck mass, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Upper endoscopy as the first procedure
Esophageal manometry
Barium esophagram
Neck ultrasound
stages.explanation: A barium esophagram is the initial test of choice and is diagnostic, classically demonstrating a posterior pharyngoesophageal (Zenker) diverticulum—an outpouching through Killian triangle above the cricopharyngeus. Upper endoscopy as the first procedure (wrong) is relatively risky here because the blind scope can inadvertently enter and perforate the diverticulum; if performed, it must be done cautiously and is usually done after the esophagram defines anatomy. Esophageal manometry (wrong) may show cricopharyngeal dysfunction but is not the primary diagnostic study for the pouch. Neck ultrasound (wrong) is not the appropriate modality for a pharyngoesophageal diverticulum. The barium swallow safely and reliably establishes the diagnosis and guides surgical planning.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Barium esophagram: a posterior midline outpouching of the hypopharynx just above the cricopharyngeus muscle, retaining contrast (Zenker diverticulum)
The pouch measures approximately 3 cm and fills with contrast that empties poorly
Mild cricopharyngeal bar (prominence) noted at the upper esophageal sphincter
No esophageal mass or stricture distally
No evidence of malignancy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The esophagram confirms a posterior outpouching above the cricopharyngeus. What is the underlying pathophysiology of a Zenker diverticulum?
options: A false diverticulum due to cricopharyngeal dysfunction
Malignant invasion of the hypopharyngeal wall as the primary diagnosis
A true diverticulum from traction by mediastinal inflammation as the primary diagnosis
A congenital duplication cyst of the esophagus as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A Zenker diverticulum is a false (pulsion) diverticulum: incoordination and incomplete relaxation of the cricopharyngeus (upper esophageal sphincter) raises hypopharyngeal pressure, herniating mucosa and submucosa through Killian triangle, a posterior area of weakness between the inferior pharyngeal constrictor and cricopharyngeus. Because it contains only mucosa/submucosa (not all wall layers), it is a 'false' diverticulum. A true traction diverticulum (wrong) results from external pulling by mediastinal inflammation (e.g., TB) and involves all wall layers, typically mid-esophageal. A congenital duplication cyst (wrong) is a separate developmental anomaly. Malignant invasion (wrong) was excluded on imaging. The pulsion mechanism through Killian triangle explains both the location and the treatment, which targets the cricopharyngeus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this symptomatic 3 cm Zenker diverticulum, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Diverticulectomy alone without addressing the cricopharyngeus
Cricopharyngeal myotomy with diverticulectomy
Observation with serial barium studies as initial therapy
Proton pump inhibitor therapy and dietary modification
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of a symptomatic Zenker diverticulum requires dividing the cricopharyngeus (cricopharyngeal myotomy)—the root cause—combined with management of the pouch by diverticulectomy or diverticulopexy, or via an endoscopic (transoral) stapling/CO2-laser approach that simultaneously divides the common wall (myotomy) and the pouch. PPIs and dietary changes (wrong) do not correct the mechanical/motor problem. Diverticulectomy alone without myotomy (wrong) leaves the high-pressure cricopharyngeal dysfunction uncorrected, leading to high recurrence and suture-line leak. Observation with serial studies (wrong) is inappropriate for a symptomatic patient with aspiration and recurrent pneumonia. The key surgical principle is that the myotomy addresses the underlying cause and prevents recurrence.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which of his symptoms most directly reflects the dangerous complication that makes timely treatment important in elderly patients?
options: Halitosis from retained food with appropriate follow-up
Mild weight loss from dietary changes based on current risk
Recurrent aspiration pneumonia from regurgitated pouch contents
Bad taste in the mouth with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Recurrent aspiration pneumonia from regurgitation of retained, undigested pouch contents is the dangerous complication that makes timely treatment important, particularly in elderly patients in whom aspiration carries high morbidity and mortality. Halitosis (wrong) is bothersome and socially distressing but not dangerous. A bad taste in the mouth (wrong) is a minor symptom. Mild weight loss from dietary changes (wrong) reflects reduced intake but is not the life-threatening complication. By recognizing aspiration as the key risk, the clinician appropriately prioritizes definitive surgical correction with cricopharyngeal myotomy to protect the airway and prevent repeated pneumonias in this older man.

cases.diagnosis: Zenker (pharyngoesophageal) diverticulum
pearls: Zenker diverticulum presents in older adults with oropharyngeal dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, a gurgling neck, nocturnal cough, and aspiration pneumonia.
It is a false (pulsion) diverticulum herniating through Killian triangle due to cricopharyngeal dysfunction; barium esophagram is diagnostic.
Avoid blind upper endoscopy first because of perforation risk; the esophagram defines the anatomy.
Definitive treatment requires cricopharyngeal myotomy (open or endoscopic) plus management of the pouch; myotomy alone addresses the underlying cause and prevents recurrence.

cases.title: Worsening Right Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Crampy right lower quadrant pain for several weeks, now sharper over the past two days.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman presents with intermittent crampy right lower quadrant (RLQ) pain over the past 6 weeks that has acutely intensified over 48 hours. She reports 3-4 loose, nonbloody stools daily for 2 months, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and an unintentional 5 kg weight loss. She has had occasional mouth ulcers. She denies vaginal discharge or abnormal bleeding; last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago and she uses an IUD. Past medical history is notable for two episodes of 'oral aphthous ulcers' and intermittent arthralgias. Medications: oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily and ibuprofen as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a graphic designer, no recent travel or sick contacts. Family history is significant for a maternal aunt with 'colitis.' Review of systems is positive for postprandial bloating and crampy pain partially relieved by defecation, plus episodic perianal soreness. She denies dysuria, hematochezia, and prior abdominal surgery.
cases.exam: She is uncomfortable but nontoxic. Abdomen is soft with focal tenderness in the RLQ and a vague, ill-defined fullness without rebound or guarding; no McBurney point rigidity. Bowel sounds are present. There is no Rovsing or psoas sign. A small, nontender perianal skin tag is noted; no fluctuance or active fistula drainage. Oral exam reveals two healing aphthous ulcers. There is mild conjunctival pallor. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Pelvic exam is unremarkable with no cervical motion tenderness or adnexal mass. No peripheral edema; skin shows no erythema nodosum.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in the ED workup of this patient?
options: Urine beta-hCG, CBC, CMP, CRP,
Empiric metronidazole and discharge home in this clinical context
Immediate appendectomy based on RLQ tenderness
Outpatient colonoscopy referral with no acute imaging
stages.explanation: A urine beta-hCG is mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain to exclude ectopic pregnancy before imaging or surgery, and basic labs (CBC, CMP, CRP) plus a CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast efficiently evaluate both acute appendicitis and an alternative inflammatory process in this subacute presentation with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, anemia, and oral aphthae. Immediate appendectomy is premature without imaging, since the weeks-long course and systemic features point away from simple appendicitis toward inflammatory bowel disease. Outpatient colonoscopy ignores the acute pain and the possibility of obstruction, phlegmon, or abscess that needs urgent evaluation. Empiric metronidazole with discharge risks missing appendicitis, a perforation, or a stricturing complication, provides no diagnosis, and inappropriately delays definitive workup of a potentially deteriorating patient.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 10.4 g/dL
MCV 78 fL
Platelets 466 ×10³/µL
CRP 62 mg/L
Albumin 3.1 g/dL
Urine beta-hCG: negative
CT abdomen/pelvis: mural thickening of the terminal ileum with mucosal hyperenhancement and surrounding fat stranding; a separate inflamed ileal segment 15 cm proximal with normal intervening bowel; mild upstream small-bowel dilation; normal appendix

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging feature most strongly distinguishes this diagnosis from ulcerative colitis and acute appendicitis?
options: Mural thickening of the terminal ileum as the primary diagnosis
A normal-appearing appendix as the primary diagnosis
Discontinuous inflammation with normal intervening bowel
Surrounding mesenteric fat stranding as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Discontinuous skip lesions with normal intervening bowel and transmural small-bowel (terminal ileal) involvement are hallmarks of Crohn disease, which is classically segmental and can affect anywhere from mouth to anus. Ulcerative colitis is continuous, begins in the rectum, and is limited to the colonic mucosa, sparing the small bowel. Isolated mural thickening of the terminal ileum is suggestive but nonspecific (infectious ileitis, lymphoma). Fat stranding indicates inflammation but is not specific to Crohn. A normal appendix rules out appendicitis but does not establish Crohn disease. Crohn classically shows the 'creeping fat' of the mesentery and a thickened, hyperenhancing bowel wall, and on endoscopy cobblestoning and aphthous ulcers; recognizing the segmental, transmural pattern with intervening normal bowel is what reliably separates it from the continuous mucosal disease of ulcerative colitis and from a localized appendiceal process.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the diagnosis and assess disease extent?
options: Barium swallow study
Repeat CT in 6 weeks
Capsule endoscopy as the initial test
Ileocolonoscopy with biopsies
stages.explanation: Ileocolonoscopy with intubation of the terminal ileum and segmental biopsies is the gold standard to confirm Crohn disease, document skip lesions, identify cobblestoning and aphthous ulcers, and obtain tissue showing transmural inflammation with noncaseating granulomas. A barium swallow evaluates the upper tract and is not the test of choice for ileocolonic disease. Repeating CT delays diagnosis and adds radiation without tissue. Capsule endoscopy is reserved for suspected small-bowel disease after colonoscopy and is contraindicated as a first test when a stricture is suspected because the capsule can become impacted. Biopsies also help exclude infectious mimics such as Yersinia or tuberculosis, which can produce a similar terminal ileitis, and establish a baseline for future disease monitoring and therapeutic response.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two months later she returns with colicky pain, abdominal distension, and vomiting. Imaging shows a fibrotic terminal ileal stricture with proximal dilation but no abscess. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent total colectomy as the leading concern
High-dose IV corticosteroids alone as the leading concern
Start an oral aminosalicylate and discharge as the leading concern
Bowel rest, NG decompression, IV fluids,
stages.explanation: A fibrostenotic (fibrotic) stricture causing obstruction reflects scarring rather than active inflammation, so it responds poorly to medical therapy; initial management is bowel rest, NG decompression, and IV fluids, with surgical evaluation for limited resection or strictureplasty. High-dose steroids treat inflammatory, not fixed fibrotic, narrowing and won't relieve a scarred stricture. Total colectomy is inappropriate for isolated small-bowel Crohn disease and is reserved for refractory colonic ulcerative colitis. Aminosalicylates are weak in Crohn disease and useless for a fibrotic obstructing stricture. Distinguishing a fixed fibrotic stricture from an active inflammatory one is essential, because the former is mechanical scar tissue best treated surgically while the latter may improve with anti-inflammatory or biologic therapy; cross-sectional imaging and the clinical course guide that distinction and the timing of operation.

cases.diagnosis: Crohn disease with terminal ileitis (stricturing phenotype)
pearls: Crohn disease causes discontinuous transmural skip lesions anywhere from mouth to anus, classically the terminal ileum; ulcerative colitis is continuous, mucosal, and rectum-based.
Always check a urine beta-hCG before imaging or surgery in any reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain.
Fibrotic (fibrostenotic) strictures do not respond to steroids or biologics and often require resection or strictureplasty, whereas inflammatory strictures may respond to medical therapy.
Noncaseating granulomas, cobblestoning, perianal skin tags/fistulae, and oral aphthae point to Crohn over ulcerative colitis or appendicitis.

cases.title: Critically Ill Man With Bloody Diarrhea and a Distended Abdomen
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening bloody diarrhea, high fever, and progressive abdominal distension.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man with a 4-year history of ulcerative colitis is transferred to the ICU after 10 days of escalating bloody diarrhea (now 12-15 stools/day), crampy abdominal pain, and fevers. Two days ago his outpatient gastroenterologist increased him to prednisone 60 mg daily; today he developed marked distension, has stopped passing stool, and became confused. He recently took loperamide for the diarrhea and a course of ibuprofen for arthralgias. Medications on admission: mesalamine 4.8 g daily, prednisone 60 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, software engineer, no recent travel; no recent antibiotics. Family history: father with ulcerative colitis. Review of systems is positive for lightheadedness, palpitations, and decreased urine output. He denies prior colonic surgery. On transfer he appears toxic and is tachycardic and febrile, prompting concern for a life-threatening colonic complication superimposed on his known inflammatory bowel disease.
cases.exam: He is lethargic and ill-appearing. The abdomen is markedly distended and tympanitic with diffuse tenderness; bowel sounds are absent. There is voluntary guarding but no frank rigidity at presentation. Mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is delayed at 3 seconds. He is mildly confused (oriented to person only). No peritoneal rebound is appreciated yet, though examination is limited by high-dose steroids. Rectal exam shows scant bloody mucus. Extremities are cool with thready pulses. No skin rash or jaundice.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management bundle for this patient?
options: Start oral loperamide and increase mesalamine as the first step today
Obtain an outpatient colonoscopy in the morning as the first step today
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, stop antimotility/anticholinergic agents, NPO with NG decompression, IV steroids,
Give a barium enema to assess mucosal disease as the first step today
stages.explanation: This toxic, septic-appearing patient needs resuscitation with IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, NPO with NG decompression, continuation of IV corticosteroids, cessation of antimotility/anticholinergic/opioid and NSAID agents (which precipitate toxic megacolon), and immediate surgical consultation. Loperamide worsens colonic dilation and is contraindicated. Colonoscopy and barium/contrast enema are contraindicated in suspected toxic megacolon because of high perforation risk. Delaying to the morning ignores the systemic toxicity and impending perforation. Electrolyte repletion, particularly potassium and magnesium, is critical because hypokalemia worsens colonic dilation and atony. Serial abdominal exams and plain films track progression, and the surgical team should be involved early so that the threshold for operating is established before the patient perforates.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 22.6 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Hgb 9.2 g/dL
Platelets 510 ×10³/µL
Na 131 mEq/L
K 2.9 mEq/L
HCO3 18 mEq/L
Lactate 4.1 mmol/L
Albumin 2.4 g/dL
CRP 188 mg/L
C. difficile PCR: negative
Lactate 4.1 mmol/L
Abdominal X-ray: transverse colon dilated to 7 cm with loss of haustration and mucosal islands; no free air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination best establishes the diagnosis of toxic megacolon?
options: Bloody diarrhea plus elevated CRP alone as the primary diagnosis
Transverse colon dilation greater than 6 cm on imaging PLUS systemic toxicity
Free air under the diaphragm on upright film as the primary diagnosis
Any colonic dilation regardless of clinical status as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Toxic megacolon requires both radiographic total or segmental colonic dilation (>6 cm, classically the transverse colon) AND signs of systemic toxicity, per Jalan criteria: fever >38°C, HR >120, neutrophilic leukocytosis, or anemia, plus one of dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, hypotension, or altered mental status. Bloody diarrhea with elevated CRP reflects a flare but is not diagnostic. Colonic dilation without toxicity is simple megacolon, not toxic. Free air indicates perforation, a complication of toxic megacolon, not the defining feature. The Jalan criteria formalize this by requiring radiographic colonic distension plus at least three of fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, or anemia, and at least one of dehydration, altered mental status, electrolyte disturbance, or hypotension. This patient meets the radiographic and systemic-toxicity components, confirming toxic megacolon rather than an uncomplicated flare.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After 36 hours of maximal medical therapy he remains febrile and tachycardic, colonic dilation has increased to 9 cm, and his lactate is rising. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Continue IV steroids for another 72 hours
Emergent total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy
Place a rectal decompression tube and observe
Add infliximab and reassess in 5 days as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Failure of maximal medical therapy (typically reassessed within 24-72 hours), worsening dilation, or any sign of perforation or clinical deterioration mandates emergent total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy and Hartmann/rectal stump—the definitive, life-saving operation. Continuing steroids without improvement risks perforation and death. Starting infliximab and waiting 5 days is unacceptable in a deteriorating patient with rising lactate. A rectal tube does not reliably decompress toxic megacolon and delays definitive surgery. The decision to operate is clinical and time-sensitive: deterioration, increasing colonic diameter, rising lactate, or any suspicion of perforation overrides further medical temporizing. A subtotal/total colectomy with end ileostomy removes the diseased, dilating colon while avoiding a high-risk primary anastomosis in an unstable, steroid-treated, septic patient.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which intraoperative or perioperative complication is the most feared and carries the highest mortality in toxic megacolon?
options: Stoma retraction as the leading concern
Colonic perforation with feculent peritonitis
Postoperative ileus as the leading concern
Wound infection as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Colonic perforation with feculent peritonitis and septic shock is the most lethal complication of toxic megacolon, with mortality rising several-fold once perforation occurs—this is why surgery should precede rather than follow perforation. High-dose steroids can mask peritoneal signs, making early surgical decision-making critical. Wound infection, postoperative ileus, and stoma retraction are real but far less lethal complications and do not drive the urgency of operating before perforation. Because high-dose corticosteroids blunt peritoneal signs, perforation can occur with minimal physical findings, and once feculent contamination produces diffuse peritonitis and septic shock, mortality climbs steeply. This is precisely why the surgical strategy favors colectomy before perforation rather than waiting for unmistakable peritonitis to declare itself.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy, what is the appropriate longer-term surgical plan and counseling?
options: Reversal of the ileostomy within one week regardless of nutrition or healing with appropriate follow-up
Immediate ileal pouch-anal anastomosis at the same operation with appropriate follow-up
Staged reconstruction with completion proctectomy
Lifelong high-dose steroids to avoid further surgery with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: In the emergent setting a subtotal/total colectomy with end ileostomy is performed first; definitive reconstruction with completion proctectomy and ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is done electively after the patient recovers, is off steroids, and is nutritionally optimized, with interim surveillance of the retained rectal stump for disease or dysplasia. A one-stage IPAA in an unstable, steroid-treated, septic patient has unacceptable anastomotic and septic risk. Lifelong high-dose steroids cause major morbidity and do not address the resected colon. Premature ileostomy reversal in a malnourished, recently septic patient invites leak and failure.

cases.diagnosis: Toxic megacolon complicating ulcerative colitis
pearls: Toxic megacolon = colonic dilation >6 cm (often transverse colon) PLUS systemic toxicity; antimotility agents, opioids, anticholinergics, and recent colonoscopy/barium enema can precipitate it.
Colonoscopy and contrast enema are contraindicated in suspected toxic megacolon due to perforation risk; serial plain films and exams guide management.
Failure of 24-72 hours of maximal medical therapy, worsening dilation, or perforation mandates emergent total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy.
High-dose steroids blunt peritoneal signs—operate before perforation, since feculent peritonitis dramatically increases mortality.

cases.title: Painless Bloody Stools in a Preschool Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Two episodes of painless, brick-red blood mixed in the stool today.
cases.history: A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought in after passing two large, painless stools mixed with dark red, almost maroon ("brick-jelly") blood earlier today. His mother denies straining, hard stools, anal pain, or visible fissures. He has had no vomiting, fever, abdominal pain, or weight loss, and is eating and playing normally between episodes, though he looked slightly pale this afternoon. Birth and developmental history are unremarkable; he is fully immunized. No recent travel, sick contacts, antibiotics, or new foods. No NSAID or iron use. Past medical history is unremarkable with no prior bleeding, easy bruising, or surgeries. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory for bleeding disorders or inflammatory bowel disease. Diet is age-appropriate. There is no history of beets or red-dyed foods. The painless, episodic, significant lower GI bleeding in an otherwise well young child without an anal source prompts evaluation for an ectopic mucosal source in the small bowel.
cases.exam: He is alert, interactive, and nontoxic but mildly pale. The abdomen is soft, nondistended, and nontender with no palpable mass, organomegaly, or peritoneal signs; bowel sounds are normal. There is no abdominal guarding. Rectal exam reveals dark red blood in the vault with no fissure, fistula, polyp, or external hemorrhoid; no mass is palpable. Conjunctivae are slightly pale. Capillary refill is 2 seconds. Skin shows no petechiae, purpura, or hemangiomas. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with a soft flow murmur; lungs are clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step in this hemodynamically borderline child with painless lower GI bleeding?
options: Start oral iron and arrange outpatient follow-up as the first step today
Establish IV access, send CBC
Proceed directly to exploratory laparotomy as the first step today
Reassure and discharge with stool softeners as the first step today
stages.explanation: A child with significant painless rectal bleeding and tachycardia needs IV access, a CBC and type & screen, isotonic fluid resuscitation, and close monitoring before any diagnostic test—stabilize first. Discharge with stool softeners ignores potentially substantial blood loss and the tachycardia. Laparotomy is premature without a diagnosis or instability requiring it. Oral iron with outpatient follow-up delays evaluation of active, clinically meaningful bleeding. A type and screen prepares for possible transfusion if the hemoglobin is low or bleeding continues, and continuous monitoring detects ongoing hemorrhage. Only after the child is hemodynamically stabilized should attention turn to localizing the source, since resuscitation always precedes diagnostic maneuvers in active GI bleeding.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 8.6 g/dL (baseline ~12)
MCV 74 fL
Platelets 320 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
Stool: grossly bloody, infectious studies pending
Type & screen: O positive, crossmatch sent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given painless lower GI bleeding in a young child, which study is most appropriate to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Abdominal ultrasound only
Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan
Upper GI series with small-bowel follow-through
Colonoscopy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A technetium-99m pertechnetate scan (Meckel scan) is the test of choice: the radiotracer is preferentially taken up by ectopic gastric mucosa within a Meckel diverticulum, the most common congenital GI anomaly and the leading cause of painless, significant lower GI bleeding in children. Pretreatment with an H2 blocker, pentagastrin, or glucagon can improve uptake and sensitivity. Colonoscopy typically misses a small-bowel diverticulum located beyond reach and is lower yield for this presentation. An upper GI series evaluates luminal anatomy but does not detect ectopic gastric mucosa or active bleeding and would not establish the diagnosis. Ultrasound may reveal intussusception or other pathology but is insensitive for a bleeding Meckel diverticulum. The painless, episodic, brisk hematochezia in an otherwise well young child fits a bleeding Meckel far better than an infectious or anal source, making the pertechnetate scan the highest-yield confirmatory study.

stages.label: NUCLEAR IMAGING
stages.content:
Technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel) scan: focal abnormal radiotracer uptake in the right lower quadrant appearing simultaneously with gastric uptake, consistent with ectopic gastric mucosa in a small-bowel diverticulum
Repeat Hgb after resuscitation: 9.4 g/dL

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: The 'rule of 2s' classically describes this lesion. Which statement is correct?
options: It occurs in 2% of the population, is located ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve,
It is an acquired pulsion diverticulum lacking all bowel wall layers as the immediate next step
It never contains ectopic tissue and bleeds because of arterial malformations as the immediate next step
It occurs in 20% of the population and is located in the proximal jejunum as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The rule of 2s: a Meckel diverticulum occurs in ~2% of the population, is located ~2 feet (within roughly 60 cm) proximal to the ileocecal valve on the antimesenteric border, is about 2 inches long, frequently presents by age 2, and the two common ectopic tissues are gastric and pancreatic mucosa. It is a true diverticulum (all bowel wall layers) arising from a persistent omphalomesenteric/vitelline duct, not an acquired pulsion diverticulum. It is not present in 20% of people or in the proximal jejunum. Bleeding results from acid secreted by ectopic gastric mucosa ulcerating adjacent ileal tissue, not arteriovenous malformations.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for a symptomatic (bleeding) Meckel diverticulum?
options: Surgical resection of the diverticulum
Observation with serial hemoglobin checks as initial therapy
Long-term proton pump inhibitor therapy alone as initial therapy
Endoscopic clipping via colonoscopy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A symptomatic Meckel diverticulum is treated with surgical resection—diverticulectomy, or segmental small-bowel resection when ectopic mucosa or ulceration extends to the diverticular base or adjacent ileum—to remove the acid-secreting ectopic gastric mucosa causing the bleeding. A PPI may transiently reduce acid but does not cure the lesion or stop recurrent bleeding. Endoscopic clipping cannot reliably reach or treat a small-bowel diverticulum. Observation alone is inappropriate for a confirmed, actively bleeding source that has already caused a significant hemoglobin drop. Pretreatment with an H2 blocker, pentagastrin, or glucagon can enhance tracer uptake and improve the scan's sensitivity for ectopic gastric mucosa. Definitive cure, however, is surgical: removing the diverticulum and any adjacent ulcerated ileum eliminates the acid-secreting tissue and prevents recurrent, potentially life-threatening hemorrhage.

cases.diagnosis: Bleeding Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa
pearls: Meckel diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly and the leading cause of painless, significant lower GI bleeding in young children.
Rule of 2s: ~2% of population, ~2 feet from ileocecal valve, ~2 inches long, presents by age 2, and the 2 ectopic tissues are gastric and pancreatic mucosa.
The technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel) scan detects ectopic gastric mucosa; pretreatment with an H2 blocker can improve tracer retention and sensitivity.
Symptomatic Meckel diverticula are resected; bleeding arises from acid-secreting ectopic gastric mucosa ulcerating adjacent ileum.

cases.title: Episodic Crying and Red Currant Stool in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of inconsolable crying with leg-drawing and one red, jelly-like stool.
cases.history: An 18-month-old previously healthy boy is brought in with several hours of intermittent, sudden episodes of inconsolable crying during which he draws his knees to his chest, lasting a few minutes and recurring every 15-20 minutes.
