Fat embolism ARDS (option D) classically follows long-bone fractures with petechiae and neurologic changes 24-72 hours out, not purulent sputum with focal consolidation. Targeted cultures and antibiotics are indicated.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Fever, purulent secretions, leukocytosis, and new consolidation in a contused, ventilated lung indicate pneumonia.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Purulent secretions with a new focal consolidation and fever in a ventilated trauma patient point to pneumonia rather than embolic or aspiration syndromes alone.

cases.diagnosis: Flail chest with underlying pulmonary contusion
pearls: Flail chest = 3+ contiguous ribs fractured in 2+ places, producing a segment that moves paradoxically inward on inspiration.
The pulmonary contusion beneath the flail segment, not the bony injury itself, is the principal cause of hypoxemia.
Management hinges on aggressive analgesia (epidural/paravertebral) and pulmonary toilet; avoid chest-wall strapping and fluid overload.
Consider surgical rib fixation for flail chest with ventilatory failure or severe displacement to reduce ventilator and ICU days.

cases.title: Belly Blow That Left Him Breathless
cases.chiefComplaint: Left-sided chest and abdominal pain with shortness of breath after a high-speed collision.
cases.history: A 37-year-old man presents after a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which he was a restrained front-seat passenger; the vehicle was struck on the left side with significant intrusion. He complains of left lower chest and upper abdominal pain and progressively worsening shortness of breath. He denies head injury or loss of consciousness. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially, does not use tobacco, and denies recreational drug use. He is unsure of his last meal but ate dinner about two hours before the crash. He reports no prior abdominal or thoracic surgery. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival his airway is patent and he is protecting it; he is tachypneic and uncomfortable, with pain worsened by deep inspiration. He has not vomited but feels nauseated. A nasogastric tube was placed during the resuscitation to decompress the stomach. Two large-bore IVs are in place and he has received one liter of crystalloid. He localizes the pain to the left costal margin and left upper quadrant.
cases.exam: Alert, tachypneic man in moderate distress. Breath sounds are diminished at the left base, and on careful auscultation bowel sounds are appreciated over the left hemithorax. The trachea is midline. There is tenderness over the left lower ribs and left upper quadrant without obvious external wound. The abdomen is mildly tender in the left upper quadrant but soft, and it appears slightly scaphoid. There is no peritonitis. Heart sounds are normal without murmur, rub, or muffling, and neck veins are not distended. Pelvis is stable. Extremities are warm with intact distal pulses. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. The patient remains hemodynamically stable but with an oxygen requirement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Auscultation of bowel sounds in the left hemithorax with diminished left breath sounds after blunt trauma should most raise suspicion for which injury?
options: Simple left pneumothorax as the first step today
Traumatic diaphragmatic rupture with herniation of abdominal viscera
Left lower lobe pneumonia as the first step today
Splenic laceration with hemothorax as the first step today
stages.explanation: Bowel sounds heard in the chest with decreased ipsilateral breath sounds after blunt trauma classically indicate diaphragmatic rupture with herniation of abdominal contents into the thorax; the left side is far more commonly recognized because the liver protects and partially seals the right hemidiaphragm. A simple pneumothorax (option A) causes decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance but not bowel sounds in the chest. Pneumonia (option C) does not produce thoracic bowel sounds and is not an acute traumatic finding. A splenic laceration with hemothorax (option D) can cause left-sided dullness and shock but would not place bowel sounds in the chest; importantly, blunt diaphragmatic rupture is frequently associated with concurrent splenic injury, so both must be considered. The scaphoid abdomen further supports herniation of viscera upward.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Thoracic bowel sounds with decreased breath sounds after blunt trauma signal diaphragmatic rupture with visceral herniation.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Bowel sounds in the chest are the key clue: simple pneumothorax, pneumonia, or hemothorax do not relocate the bowel into the thorax.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial diagnostic finding would most strongly confirm diaphragmatic rupture in this stable patient?
options: Kerley B lines on chest radiograph as the primary diagnosis
An elevated serum amylase as the primary diagnosis
Free air under the diaphragm on upright film as the primary diagnosis
The tip of the nasogastric tube coiled within the left hemithorax on chest radiograph
stages.explanation: Seeing the nasogastric tube tip curled up into the left hemithorax on the chest radiograph is essentially pathognomonic for a left diaphragmatic rupture with the stomach herniated into the chest, because the NG tube has followed the stomach above the diaphragm. Free air under the diaphragm (option A) indicates hollow viscus perforation/pneumoperitoneum, a different problem. Kerley B lines (option C) reflect interstitial pulmonary edema, unrelated to diaphragmatic injury. An elevated amylase (option D) is nonspecific and can occur with pancreatic, salivary, or bowel injury and would not confirm a diaphragmatic tear. When the diagnosis is uncertain, CT of the chest and abdomen (with sagittal/coronal reconstructions) is the best confirmatory study, but the herniated NG tube on plain film is a classic, immediately recognizable sign.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. An NG tube coiled in the left chest indicates the stomach has herniated above the diaphragm, confirming rupture.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Look for the stomach (and its NG tube) above the diaphragm; subdiaphragmatic free air, Kerley lines, and amylase point elsewhere.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: nasogastric tube tip coiled in the left hemithorax; gas-filled structure (stomach) in the left chest; elevated/indistinct left hemidiaphragm; mediastinal shift slightly to the right
CT chest/abdomen: discontinuity of the left hemidiaphragm with herniation of the stomach and portion of colon into the thorax ('collar sign'); associated left lower rib fractures; small splenic contusion without active extravasation
Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL
WBC 12.0 ×10³/µL
Lactate 2.0 mmol/L
FAST: no significant free fluid in the abdomen

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management of this acute traumatic diaphragmatic rupture with herniated viscera?
options: Operative reduction of the herniated viscera
Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation as initial therapy
Chest tube placement and observation as initial therapy
Endoscopic repair of the diaphragm as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute traumatic diaphragmatic rupture requires operative repair: the herniated viscera are reduced back into the abdomen and the diaphragmatic defect is closed (primary suture repair, or mesh for large defects). In the acute setting the abdominal approach (laparotomy/laparoscopy) is generally preferred because it allows inspection and treatment of associated intra-abdominal injuries. A chest tube with observation (option A) is dangerous and risks perforating herniated bowel and missing the defect; the diaphragm will not heal spontaneously and the hernia will persist or strangulate. NIPPV (option C) can worsen herniation by forcing more abdominal content into the chest and does not fix the defect. There is no role for endoscopic repair (option D) of an acute traumatic diaphragmatic rupture. Repair prevents the catastrophic late complication of bowel strangulation.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. The herniated viscera must be reduced and the diaphragmatic defect surgically closed, typically via the abdomen acutely.
stages.explanationIncorrect: A torn diaphragm with herniated bowel will not heal on its own; chest tubes and positive pressure can worsen it. Operative repair is required.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If a small diaphragmatic rupture were missed at presentation, what is the most feared delayed complication years later?
options: Phrenic nerve palsy as the leading concern
Recurrent pneumonia only as the leading concern
Strangulation of chronically herniated bowel through the diaphragmatic defect
Pleural effusion as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The most feared late complication of a missed diaphragmatic rupture is incarceration and strangulation of herniated bowel through the defect, which can present years later with bowel obstruction, ischemia, perforation, and sepsis, carrying high mortality. A simple pleural effusion (option A) is far less dangerous and not the principal concern. Phrenic nerve palsy (option C) relates to nerve injury and causes diaphragmatic paralysis but is not the dreaded sequela of an unrepaired rupture. Recurrent pneumonia (option D) can occur from chronic herniation but is not the catastrophic, life-threatening endpoint that strangulation represents. Because defects enlarge over time under the negative intrathoracic pressure gradient, even small acute tears warrant repair to prevent eventual strangulation.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A missed rupture can lead to delayed incarceration and strangulation of herniated bowel, a life-threatening emergency.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The catastrophic late risk is strangulation of bowel herniating through the persistent defect, not a benign effusion or nerve palsy.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic diaphragmatic rupture with visceral herniation
pearls: Bowel sounds in the chest with decreased breath sounds after blunt trauma should trigger suspicion for diaphragmatic rupture.
An NG tube coiled in the left hemithorax on chest x-ray is a classic sign of gastric herniation through a torn diaphragm.
Acute traumatic ruptures are repaired operatively (usually via the abdomen) with reduction of viscera and closure of the defect.
A missed rupture can present years later with strangulated herniated bowel, so even small defects warrant repair.

cases.title: High-Speed Crash and a Wide Shadow on the Film
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe chest and back pain after a high-speed head-on collision.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after a high-speed head-on motor vehicle collision with rapid deceleration; he was the restrained driver and there was significant front-end intrusion with a deployed airbag. He reports severe retrosternal and interscapular pain. He denies focal neurologic deficits. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He has had no prior surgeries. Family history is noncontributory. In the emergency department he was hemodynamically stable after initial resuscitation. He has no abdominal pain and FAST was negative. A trauma CT was obtained. He is alert and oriented, anxious about the pain in his back, which he describes as 'tearing.' He has palpable, symmetric pulses in all extremities. He has received analgesia and a single liter of crystalloid. There is no obvious external chest wound. He underwent the deceleration mechanism that is classically associated with great-vessel injury, and the team is now reviewing his imaging in the unit.
cases.exam: Alert, anxious man in pain. There is tenderness over the sternum and interscapular region. Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally; trachea is midline. Heart sounds are normal without murmur or rub; no muffling. There is a small difference in blood pressure between the right and left arms. Femoral and pedal pulses are palpable bilaterally and symmetric. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Neurologic exam shows intact strength and sensation in all four limbs with no paraplegia. There is an abrasion across the anterior chest from the seatbelt. No external hemorrhage is present. Capillary refill is brisk. He remains hypertensive and tachycardic despite analgesia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which chest radiograph finding in this rapid-deceleration patient should most raise concern for blunt thoracic aortic injury?
options: Bilateral apical pleural caps that resolve with positioning
Elevation of the right hemidiaphragm
A small left pneumothorax as the first step today
A widened mediastinum
stages.explanation: A widened mediastinum (commonly >8 cm on a supine AP film) is the classic and most important plain-film clue to blunt thoracic aortic injury, reflecting a periaortic hematoma; in deceleration trauma the aorta most often tears at the isthmus just distal to the left subclavian artery, where the relatively mobile arch meets the fixed descending aorta tethered by the ligamentum arteriosum. Apical pleural caps (option B) can be a supportive sign but are nonspecific and not the primary screening finding. A small pneumothorax (option C) and right hemidiaphragm elevation (option D) are not specific for aortic injury. Other supportive radiographic signs include loss of the aortic knob contour, depression of the left mainstem bronchus, and a left apical cap, but mediastinal widening is the cardinal alert that demands CT angiography.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A widened mediastinum reflects periaortic hematoma and is the cardinal plain-film alert for blunt aortic injury.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The deceleration mechanism tears the aorta at the isthmus; the plain-film signature of the resulting periaortic hematoma is a widened mediastinum.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory imaging study?
options: Transthoracic echocardiography
CT angiography of the chest
Ventilation-perfusion scan
Plain chest radiograph repeated upright
stages.explanation: CT angiography of the chest is the diagnostic test of choice for blunt thoracic aortic injury: it is rapid, highly sensitive and specific, characterizes the location and grade of injury, and evaluates associated injuries, making it ideal in a stable trauma patient. Transthoracic echocardiography (option A) poorly visualizes the descending thoracic aorta and isthmus; transesophageal echo can be used but is operator-dependent and less available emergently. Repeating a plain chest radiograph (option C) cannot define or exclude the injury. A V/Q scan (option D) evaluates for pulmonary embolism and has no role here. Catheter aortography was historically the gold standard but has been largely replaced by CTA, which now guides decisions about medical management and endovascular repair.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. CTA of the chest is the rapid, accurate confirmatory study for blunt aortic injury and guides management.
stages.explanationIncorrect: TTE and V/Q scans cannot reliably assess the aortic isthmus; CT angiography is the modern confirmatory test of choice.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography chest: focal contained pseudoaneurysm of the proximal descending thoracic aorta just distal to the left subclavian artery (aortic isthmus) with periaortic and mediastinal hematoma; no active free extravasation; intimal flap present
Grade III blunt thoracic aortic injury (pseudoaneurysm)
Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL
Lactate 1.9 mmol/L
No intra-abdominal free fluid
Repeat BP: 158/92 mmHg, HR 102 bpm despite analgesia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive treatment, what is the most important medical intervention to reduce the risk of rupture (impulse control)?
options: Therapeutic heparin infusion as initial therapy
Aggressive crystalloid bolus to maintain a higher blood pressure as initial therapy
Vasopressor support to keep systolic pressure above 160 mmHg as initial therapy
Beta-blockade to lower heart rate
stages.explanation: Anti-impulse therapy with a titratable beta-blocker such as esmolol is the key temporizing measure: lowering heart rate first and then blood pressure reduces the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and wall shear stress, decreasing the chance of rupture before repair. Typical targets are a heart rate near 60 bpm and systolic blood pressure around 100-120 mmHg, balanced against perfusion of other injuries. Aggressive crystalloid (option A) and vasopressors to keep pressures high (option D) would dangerously increase shear stress and rupture risk. Therapeutic heparin (option C) is contraindicated in a multiply-injured trauma patient and risks hemorrhage from other injuries. Beta-blockade is begun first because pure vasodilators alone can cause reflex tachycardia that paradoxically increases dP/dt.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Beta-blockade lowers heart rate and shear stress (dP/dt), reducing rupture risk while definitive repair is arranged.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Raising blood pressure or anticoagulating increases rupture/hemorrhage risk; impulse control means lowering HR and BP, starting with a beta-blocker.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the preferred definitive treatment for most contained blunt thoracic aortic injuries at the isthmus in a suitable patient today?
options: Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR)
Lifelong medical therapy with no procedure
Pericardial window with appropriate follow-up
Open surgical repair with cardiopulmonary bypass
stages.explanation: Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) has become the preferred definitive treatment for most blunt thoracic aortic injuries with suitable anatomy, offering markedly lower mortality and paraplegia rates than open repair and avoiding cardiopulmonary bypass and a thoracotomy in a multiply-injured patient. Open repair (option A) is reserved for unfavorable anatomy or when endovascular options are unavailable. Lifelong medical therapy alone (option C) is appropriate only for minimal (low-grade) intimal injuries; this Grade III pseudoaneurysm requires repair. A pericardial window (option D) addresses pericardial tamponade and is irrelevant to a descending aortic injury. The timing of TEVAR is often delayed (semi-elective) under strict impulse control if the patient is stable and there is no active extravasation, allowing concurrent injuries to be addressed.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. TEVAR is the preferred repair for most isthmic blunt aortic injuries with suitable anatomy, with better outcomes than open surgery.
stages.explanationIncorrect: A Grade III pseudoaneurysm needs repair; the modern preferred approach for suitable anatomy is endovascular (TEVAR), not open bypass surgery or medical therapy alone.

cases.diagnosis: Blunt thoracic aortic injury at the isthmus
pearls: Rapid deceleration tears the aorta at the isthmus distal to the left subclavian; a widened mediastinum is the cardinal plain-film clue.
CT angiography of the chest is the confirmatory study and grades the injury to guide treatment.
Anti-impulse therapy with a titratable beta-blocker (target HR ~60, SBP ~100-120) lowers dP/dt and rupture risk before repair.
TEVAR is the preferred definitive repair for most suitable isthmic injuries, with lower mortality and paraplegia than open surgery.

cases.title: Seatbelt Bruise and a Tender Right Side After the Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Right upper abdominal pain and right shoulder pain after a motor vehicle collision.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU after a motor vehicle collision in which she was the restrained driver in a moderate-speed frontal impact. She complains of right upper quadrant abdominal pain that radiates to the right shoulder. She denies chest pain or shortness of breath. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes a combined oral contraceptive pill and no other medications, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and uses no recreational drugs. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and a urine pregnancy test is negative. She has had no prior abdominal surgery. Family history is noncontributory. In the emergency department she was initially tachycardic but responded to a liter of crystalloid with improvement in her heart rate and blood pressure. A FAST exam showed free fluid in the right upper quadrant and a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. She is now hemodynamically stable on the monitor. She reports the seatbelt 'dug into' her abdomen and her pain is worse with movement and deep breaths. She has not vomited and denies hematuria.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly uncomfortable young woman. There is a transverse seatbelt ecchymosis across the lower abdomen and tenderness in the right upper quadrant with mild voluntary guarding but no rigidity or rebound. Right shoulder is nontender to palpation, consistent with referred (Kehr-type) pain. Breath sounds are clear and equal; the trachea is midline. Heart sounds are normal. The pelvis is stable and there is no gross blood at the urethral meatus. Bowel sounds are present. Extremities are warm and well perfused with symmetric pulses. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. She remains hemodynamically stable after fluid resuscitation with no ongoing signs of decompensation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this hemodynamically stable patient with right upper quadrant pain, seatbelt sign, and free fluid on FAST, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Diagnostic peritoneal lavage as the first step today
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
Immediate exploratory laparotomy as the first step today
Discharge home with return precautions in this clinical context
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable trauma patient, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the test of choice: it identifies and grades solid-organ injury, detects active contrast extravasation (which guides angioembolization), and evaluates for associated injuries, enabling safe nonoperative management. Immediate laparotomy (option A) is reserved for hemodynamic instability not responding to resuscitation or signs of hollow viscus injury/peritonitis, which she does not have. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage (option C) is now rarely used, having been largely replaced by FAST and CT, and it cannot grade the injury or guide angioembolization. Discharge (option D) is unsafe given free fluid and a likely solid-organ injury. The combination of stability, RUQ pain, and free fluid points toward a liver injury amenable to CT-directed nonoperative management.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A stable patient with free fluid should get CT to grade the injury and detect extravasation, enabling nonoperative management.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Stable patients are not rushed to the OR; CT characterizes the injury and guides nonoperative care and possible angioembolization.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: deep parenchymal laceration of the right hepatic lobe >3 cm with a large subcapsular/intraparenchymal hematoma involving >50% of the lobe; focus of active contrast extravasation (blush) within the laceration; moderate hemoperitoneum; spleen, kidneys, pancreas, and bowel appear intact
AAST liver injury grade IV
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (down from 12.5)
WBC 13.0 ×10³/µL
Platelets 232 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Repeat BP after resuscitation: 114/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable but the CT shows a contrast blush (active extravasation) within a high-grade liver laceration. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral antibiotics and discharge as initial therapy
Angiography with transcatheter angioembolization of the bleeding vessel, with continued ICU monitoring
Immediate hepatic lobectomy as initial therapy
Emergent damage-control laparotomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable patient with a high-grade liver injury and a contrast blush, angiography with transcatheter angioembolization is the appropriate next step: it controls arterial bleeding while preserving the liver and supports successful nonoperative management, which is the standard for stable blunt hepatic trauma regardless of grade. Continued ICU monitoring with serial hemoglobin and abdominal exams is essential. Emergent laparotomy (option A) is indicated for hemodynamic instability or peritonitis, not for a stable patient with a controllable blush. Oral antibiotics and discharge (option C) is clearly unsafe with active bleeding. Immediate lobectomy (option D) is overly aggressive, carries high morbidity, and is unnecessary when angioembolization can achieve hemostasis. The key determinant is hemodynamic stability, which permits the liver-sparing, image-guided approach.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A stable patient with a contrast blush is best managed with angioembolization plus ICU monitoring, sparing the liver.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Hemodynamic stability allows nonoperative, liver-sparing care; a blush is controlled with angioembolization, not a rushed laparotomy or lobectomy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Five days into successful nonoperative management she develops a rising fever, leukocytosis, and persistent right upper quadrant pain; ultrasound shows a fluid collection adjacent to the injured liver. What is the most likely complication?
options: Delayed splenic rupture
Perihepatic abscess or infected biloma
Acute cholecystitis
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: A delayed fever, leukocytosis, and a perihepatic fluid collection after a high-grade liver injury most likely represent a perihepatic abscess or an infected biloma (bile leak that becomes infected), classic complications of nonoperative management of hepatic trauma; these are typically managed with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics, with ERCP and stenting if there is an ongoing biliary leak. Delayed splenic rupture (option A) is a recognized entity but the spleen was intact and the collection is perihepatic. Pulmonary embolism (option C) would cause hypoxemia, tachycardia, and pleuritic pain rather than a focal perihepatic collection with fever. Acute cholecystitis (option D) would show gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid around the gallbladder, and a sonographic Murphy sign rather than a collection adjacent to the laceration. Surveillance for such complications is a key part of nonoperative care.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Fever, leukocytosis, and a perihepatic collection after high-grade liver injury suggest abscess or infected biloma.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The collection is perihepatic with systemic signs of infection, pointing to abscess/biloma rather than splenic, embolic, or gallbladder pathology.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which counseling point about activity is most appropriate after successful nonoperative management of a high-grade blunt liver laceration?
options: Resume full contact sports immediately on discharge as the immediate next step
No restrictions are necessary at any point as the immediate next step
Strict permanent bed rest as the immediate next step
Restrict strenuous activity
stages.explanation: After nonoperative management of a high-grade liver injury, patients should restrict strenuous activity and contact sports for several weeks to allow the parenchyma to heal and reduce the risk of delayed bleeding, with the exact timeline individualized and often guided by follow-up imaging and the injury grade. Immediate return to contact sports (option A) risks delayed rupture and hemorrhage. Strict permanent bed rest (option C) is excessive, promotes deconditioning, and increases venous thromboembolism risk. No restrictions at all (option D) ignores the real risk of delayed bleeding from a healing high-grade injury. Counseling on warning signs (worsening pain, dizziness, fainting) and the need to seek care is also important. Graduated return to activity balances healing against the harms of prolonged immobility.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Temporary restriction of strenuous activity/contact sports, guided by healing and imaging, balances rebleeding risk against deconditioning.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Neither immediate full activity nor permanent bed rest is appropriate; a healing high-grade liver injury needs a temporary, graduated activity restriction.

cases.diagnosis: High-grade blunt liver laceration (Grade IV) with active extravasation
pearls: Hemodynamic stability, not injury grade, determines candidacy for nonoperative management of blunt liver trauma.
A contrast blush on CT in a stable patient is an indication for angioembolization rather than laparotomy.
Right shoulder (Kehr-type) pain reflects diaphragmatic irritation from perihepatic blood.
Watch for delayed complications of nonoperative care: biloma/perihepatic abscess and delayed hemorrhage; manage abscess/biloma with drainage.

cases.title: Throwing Up Green After the Handlebar Hit
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated bilious vomiting and abdominal pain two days after falling onto bicycle handlebars.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is admitted to the pediatric ward after he fell off his bicycle two days ago and struck his upper abdomen on the handlebars. He initially had a bruise and mild pain but has now developed repeated episodes of bilious (green) vomiting and worsening epigastric pain, and he is unable to keep down fluids. His mother reports decreasing appetite and a swollen, tender upper belly. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known drug allergies. He is up to date on immunizations. He has had no prior surgeries. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. He has had no fevers, no diarrhea, and no blood in the stool. He localizes the pain to the epigastrium and says it is worse after he tries to eat. On the ward he appears uncomfortable and mildly dehydrated. Vital signs have been stable. The team is concerned about a hollow viscus or solid-organ injury from the focal upper-abdominal blunt force characteristic of handlebar trauma in children, which is notorious for occult injuries.
cases.exam: Alert but uncomfortable school-aged boy lying still. There is a healing ecchymosis over the epigastrium. The upper abdomen is distended and tender to palpation in the epigastrium with mild voluntary guarding but no rigidity, rebound, or peritonitis. A vague fullness is appreciated in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds are present but reduced. There is no flank ecchymosis and no costovertebral angle tenderness. The mucous membranes are slightly dry. Heart and lung exams are normal. There is no jaundice. Extremities are well perfused with normal capillary refill. He is mildly tachycardic but otherwise hemodynamically stable, with signs of a high (proximal) bowel obstruction.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilious vomiting and signs of proximal small-bowel obstruction after focal upper-abdominal blunt trauma in a child should most raise suspicion for which injury?
options: Splenic laceration as the first step today
Gastric volvulus as the first step today
Appendicitis as the first step today
Intramural duodenal hematoma causing duodenal obstruction
stages.explanation: Bilious vomiting with a proximal (high) small-bowel obstruction picture after handlebar-type blunt epigastric trauma is classic for an intramural duodenal hematoma: blood collects within the bowel wall (submucosa), narrowing the lumen and obstructing the second/third portion of the duodenum, typically presenting 24-72 hours after injury. Splenic laceration (option A) causes left upper quadrant pain and hemoperitoneum, not luminal obstruction. Gastric volvulus (option C) is rare and presents with the triad of severe epigastric pain, retching with inability to vomit, and difficulty passing an NG tube, not steady bilious emesis days after trauma. Appendicitis (option D) causes periumbilical-to-RLQ pain and is unrelated to this mechanism and obstruction pattern. The duodenum is fixed and retroperitoneal, making it vulnerable to compression against the vertebral column.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Delayed bilious vomiting and high obstruction after handlebar trauma is classic for intramural duodenal hematoma.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The high-obstruction picture days after focal epigastric trauma points to a duodenal wall hematoma compressing the lumen, not the other entities.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding would best support this diagnosis?
options: Target sign in the right lower quadrant as the primary diagnosis
Free air under the diaphragm on upright film as the primary diagnosis
Sentinel loop in the left upper quadrant only as the primary diagnosis
The 'coiled-spring' appearance of the duodenum on an upper GI contrast study, or a duodenal wall mass narrowing the lumen on CT
stages.explanation: An intramural duodenal hematoma classically produces a 'coiled-spring' or stacked-coin appearance of the mucosal folds on an upper GI contrast study, reflecting the narrowed, compressed lumen, and CT shows a hyperdense intramural mass thickening the duodenal wall and narrowing the lumen without free contrast leak. Free air (option A) would indicate a perforation (full-thickness injury), a different and more urgent problem that must be excluded. A target sign in the RLQ (option C) describes intussusception, not duodenal hematoma. A sentinel loop in the LUQ (option D) is a nonspecific sign of regional inflammation (e.g., pancreatitis) and not diagnostic here. CT also helps evaluate for the commonly associated pancreatic injury, which must be excluded given the shared mechanism and anatomy.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. The coiled-spring sign on UGI (or an intramural mass narrowing the duodenum on CT) supports duodenal hematoma.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Look for the compressed, coiled-spring duodenal lumen; free air signals perforation and the target sign indicates intussusception, not a wall hematoma.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: focal intramural hematoma of the second/third portion of the duodenum with luminal narrowing; no free air; no free fluid; pancreas appears intact without ductal injury
Upper GI contrast study: 'coiled-spring' narrowing of the duodenal lumen with delayed passage of contrast, no extravasation
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
WBC 9.6 ×10³/µL
Lipase 38 U/L (normal)
Amylase 60 U/L (normal)
Lactate 1.5 mmol/L
Electrolytes: mild hypokalemic, hypochloremic picture from vomiting

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms an intramural duodenal hematoma with no perforation and no pancreatic ductal injury. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) as initial therapy
Nonoperative management with nasogastric decompression, bowel rest,
Immediate laparotomy with evacuation of the hematoma as initial therapy
Oral feeding advanced as tolerated immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An isolated intramural duodenal hematoma without perforation is managed nonoperatively in the great majority of cases: nasogastric decompression, bowel rest, and parenteral nutrition allow the hematoma to resorb, with most resolving over 1-3 weeks; serial exams and repeat imaging monitor progress. Immediate operative evacuation (option A) is reserved for cases that fail prolonged nonoperative management (typically beyond ~2 weeks) or when an associated perforation or pancreatic injury is found at exploration. A Whipple (option C) is far too radical for a simple wall hematoma. Advancing oral feeds immediately (option D) is inappropriate while the duodenum is obstructed and would worsen vomiting and the risk of aspiration. The intact pancreatic duct on imaging supports a conservative approach, but vigilance for a missed associated injury is essential.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. An isolated duodenal hematoma without perforation is treated with NG decompression, bowel rest, and TPN until it resorbs.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Without perforation, the hematoma resorbs with conservative care; surgery is reserved for failure of nonoperative management or associated injuries.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given the shared mechanism and anatomy, which associated injury must be specifically excluded in this child because it is commonly missed?
options: Femoral fracture in this presentation
Inguinal hernia in this presentation
Renal cyst as the leading concern
Pancreatic injury
stages.explanation: Pancreatic injury must be specifically excluded because the pancreas lies adjacent to the duodenum and is compressed against the vertebral column by the same handlebar mechanism; pancreatic injuries, especially ductal transection, are notoriously occult on early imaging and easily missed, and a missed ductal injury leads to pseudocyst, fistula, and sepsis. Serial lipase/amylase and repeat imaging (or MRCP) help, and persistent or worsening symptoms should prompt re-evaluation. An inguinal hernia (option B) is unrelated to this blunt epigastric mechanism. A renal cyst (option C) is an incidental, benign finding, not a traumatic concern here. A femoral fracture (option D) is a musculoskeletal injury unrelated to the abdominal mechanism. Vigilance for concurrent pancreatic injury is a core teaching point of pediatric handlebar trauma.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Pancreatic (especially ductal) injury shares the mechanism, is frequently occult, and must be actively excluded.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The injury that travels with duodenal hematoma in handlebar trauma, and is dangerously easy to miss, is pancreatic injury.

cases.diagnosis: Intramural duodenal hematoma from blunt (handlebar) trauma
pearls: Delayed bilious vomiting and high small-bowel obstruction after handlebar trauma is the classic story for duodenal hematoma.
The 'coiled-spring' sign on upper GI study (or an intramural mass narrowing the lumen on CT) supports the diagnosis.
Isolated hematomas without perforation are managed nonoperatively with NG decompression, bowel rest, and TPN until resorption.
Always exclude an associated pancreatic injury, which shares the mechanism and is easily missed on early imaging.

cases.title: Crushing Belly Pain After a Hard Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe epigastric pain after a direct blow to the abdomen.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man is taken to the operating room after sustaining blunt abdominal trauma when he was struck in the epigastrium by a forklift load at work, compressing his upper abdomen against a fixed surface. He developed severe epigastric pain and could not tolerate the secondary survey due to abdominal tenderness. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He has had no prior abdominal surgery. Family history is noncontributory. He last ate about four hours before the injury. In the emergency department he was initially borderline tachycardic; FAST showed free fluid, and a contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a finding concerning for pancreatic injury with peripancreatic fluid. Because of worsening abdominal findings and concern for a high-grade pancreatic injury with possible ductal involvement, the trauma team elected to proceed to operative exploration. He is now intubated in the OR. Two large-bore IVs are in place and he has received crystalloid and is being typed and crossed for blood. The mechanism delivered a focal, high-energy crush to the mid-epigastrium.
cases.exam: Intubated young man under general anesthesia. Prior to induction, the abdomen was diffusely tender, most marked in the epigastrium, with guarding and progressive distension. There was a contusion across the mid-epigastrium. Bowel sounds were diminished. There was no flank ecchymosis and the pelvis was stable. On laparotomy there is a central retroperitoneal hematoma over the pancreas and a moderate amount of cloudy peritoneal fluid; the body/neck of the pancreas shows a transverse parenchymal disruption. The spleen, liver, and bowel are inspected for associated injury. Hemodynamics are stable on the table after initial resuscitation, with no major active arterial hemorrhage identified at the time of exploration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A transverse parenchymal disruption at the neck of the pancreas raises concern primarily for which complication that dictates management?
options: Common bile duct stricture
Splenic vein thrombosis
Portal vein laceration
Injury to the main pancreatic duct
stages.explanation: The single most important determinant of management and outcome in pancreatic trauma is the integrity of the MAIN pancreatic duct: a transection through the neck almost certainly disrupts the duct, and an unaddressed ductal injury leads to pancreatic leak, pseudocyst, fistula, abscess, and sepsis. Splenic vein thrombosis (option A) can complicate pancreatic disease but is not the primary management driver in acute trauma. Common bile duct stricture (option C) relates to biliary, not pancreatic ductal, injury and is not the immediate concern with a neck transection. Portal vein laceration (option D) is a catastrophic vascular injury but is a separate consideration; the defining issue for grading pancreatic trauma is whether the main duct is involved. AAST grading hinges on ductal status and location relative to the superior mesenteric vein.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Main pancreatic duct integrity is the key determinant; a neck transection implies ductal disruption.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Pancreatic trauma management is driven by the main duct; a transection at the neck almost always means ductal injury.

stages.label: OPERATIVE & LAB FINDINGS
stages.content:
Intraoperative findings: complete transection through the neck of the pancreas to the LEFT of the superior mesenteric vein with visible disruption of the main pancreatic duct; no associated major vascular hemorrhage; spleen intact but adjacent to the distal segment
AAST pancreatic injury grade III (distal transection/parenchymal injury with duct involvement)
Lipase 540 U/L
Amylase 410 U/L
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Lactate 2.2 mmol/L
No associated duodenal or biliary injury identified on exploration

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: There is a main pancreatic duct transection at the neck, to the LEFT of the superior mesenteric vein, in a hemodynamically stable patient. What is the most appropriate operative management?
options: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) as initial therapy
Distal pancreatectomy of the segment distal to the transection
Simple suture closure of the duct as initial therapy
Wide drainage alone without resection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a main duct injury in the body/tail (to the left of the superior mesenteric vein), distal pancreatectomy is the standard operation: the distal segment beyond the ductal injury is resected, with splenic preservation considered in stable patients when feasible (though splenectomy is often performed for expediency in trauma). Drainage alone (option A) is appropriate for low-grade injuries WITHOUT ductal disruption, but it will not control a transected main duct and risks a persistent fistula. A Whipple (option C) is a major undertaking reserved for severe combined pancreaticoduodenal/ampullary injuries (proximal head injuries with duodenal devascularization), not a clean distal transection. Simple suture of the duct (option D) is unreliable and will not heal a transected main duct. Resecting the distal segment removes the disrupted duct and prevents ongoing leak.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A distal main-duct transection is treated with distal pancreatectomy (splenic preservation if feasible).
stages.explanationIncorrect: Drainage or suturing will not control a transected main duct; a distal injury is managed by resecting the distal segment (distal pancreatectomy).

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most common complication after operative management of pancreatic trauma that the team must monitor for postoperatively?
options: Wound dehiscence only
Pancreatic fistula/leak
Deep vein thrombosis exclusively
Hypothyroidism
stages.explanation: Pancreatic fistula (a persistent leak of pancreatic secretions, often detected by high amylase in the operative drain output) is the most common complication after pancreatic trauma surgery; placement of closed-suction drains and monitoring drain amylase are central to postoperative care, and most low-output fistulas close with drainage and nutritional support (sometimes octreotide). Wound dehiscence (option B) can occur but is far less specific to pancreatic surgery and not the predominant concern. Hypothyroidism (option C) is unrelated to the pancreas. While DVT prophylaxis is important, DVT (option D) is a general surgical risk and not the characteristic complication of pancreatic injury. New-onset diabetes (endocrine insufficiency) can occur after extensive resection, and pseudocyst/abscess are additional risks, but fistula is the classic, most frequent issue.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Pancreatic fistula/leak is the most common postoperative complication; drain amylase monitoring is key.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The complication most characteristic of pancreatic surgery is a fistula/leak, detected via drain amylase, not the more generic options.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Why is a thorough exploration of the entire pancreas and adjacent structures emphasized in pancreatic trauma?
options: Because pancreatic and associated duodenal/vascular injuries are frequently occult and missed injuries cause high morbidity and mortality
To biopsy for malignancy as the immediate next step
Because the pancreas regenerates if left alone as the immediate next step
To remove the gallbladder routinely as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A meticulous, complete exploration is emphasized because pancreatic injuries, and the commonly associated duodenal and major vascular injuries, are frequently occult; a missed pancreatic ductal or duodenal injury is a leading cause of preventable morbidity and mortality in abdominal trauma, presenting later as fistula, pseudocyst, abscess, or sepsis. Biopsy for malignancy (option A) is irrelevant in acute trauma. Routine cholecystectomy (option C) is not part of pancreatic trauma management. The notion that the pancreas regenerates if left alone (option D) is false and dangerous; a disrupted duct will not heal on its own. Because the retroperitoneal location masks injury, the surgeon must mobilize and inspect the gland fully (and consider intraoperative assessment of ductal integrity) to avoid the catastrophic missed-injury scenario.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Pancreatic and associated injuries are often occult; missing them drives major morbidity and mortality.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The emphasis on full exploration is to avoid missing occult pancreatic/duodenal/vascular injuries, which cause severe delayed complications.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic pancreatic transection at the neck with main duct injury (AAST grade III)
pearls: Main pancreatic duct integrity and injury location relative to the superior mesenteric vein determine grade and management.
Distal (left of the SMV) duct transections are treated with distal pancreatectomy; proximal/combined pancreaticoduodenal injuries may need a Whipple.
Pancreatic fistula is the most common postoperative complication; closed-suction drains and drain-amylase monitoring are essential.
Pancreatic and associated duodenal/vascular injuries are frequently occult, so a thorough exploration is mandatory to avoid missed injuries.

cases.title: Tense Belly and Falling Urine Output After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive abdominal distension, rising ventilator pressures, and decreasing urine output after emergency surgery.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is in the ICU following a damage-control laparotomy for a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm with massive hemorrhage. He received large-volume crystalloid and multiple units of blood products during and after surgery, and his abdomen was left packed with a temporary closure. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes treated with metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. He takes amlodipine 10 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is intubated and sedated. Over the past several hours the ICU team has noted a progressively tense, distended abdomen, rising peak airway pressures on the ventilator, worsening hypoxemia, and a steady decline in urine output despite adequate-appearing volume resuscitation and a mean arterial pressure supported with vasopressors. His most recent labs show a worsening metabolic acidosis. The nurses report the abdominal dressing is increasingly taut. The clinical picture has evolved over a few hours from stable to deteriorating, prompting the team to measure his bladder pressure as a surrogate for intra-abdominal pressure.
cases.exam: Intubated, sedated man. The abdomen is markedly distended, tense, and tympanitic, with a taut temporary closure. Peak inspiratory pressures on the ventilator are climbing and tidal volumes are difficult to deliver. There is jugular venous distension. The extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill and the lower extremities appear mildly mottled. A Foley catheter drains scant, concentrated urine. Heart sounds are distant but regular. Lungs have decreased aeration at the bases. The patient is on increasing vasopressor support. The combination of a tense abdomen, oliguria, and impaired ventilation in the setting of massive resuscitation and damage-control surgery is the central concern, and serial bladder pressures are being trended.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The constellation of a tense distended abdomen, rising airway pressures, and oliguria after massive resuscitation is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Abdominal compartment syndrome
Tension pneumothorax
Cardiogenic shock from myocardial infarction
Acute tubular necrosis alone
stages.explanation: This is the classic presentation of abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS): sustained intra-abdominal hypertension after massive resuscitation/damage-control surgery causes new organ dysfunction, with a tense abdomen, oliguria from renal vein compression and reduced perfusion, elevated airway pressures from cephalad diaphragm displacement, and decreased cardiac output from impaired venous return. Acute tubular necrosis (option B) can contribute to oliguria but does not explain the tense abdomen and high airway pressures, and here the oliguria is driven by the elevated intra-abdominal pressure. Tension pneumothorax (option C) would cause unilateral absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, not a tense abdomen. Cardiogenic shock from MI (option D) would not produce a rigid, distended abdomen with rising airway pressures; the multi-organ picture localized to abdominal hypertension points to ACS.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A tense abdomen, oliguria, and rising airway pressures after massive resuscitation is classic abdominal compartment syndrome.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The unifying cause of the tense abdomen, oliguria, and high airway pressures is elevated intra-abdominal pressure, i.e., abdominal compartment syndrome.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the standard method to confirm intra-abdominal hypertension at the bedside?
options: Direct needle measurement of peritoneal pressure
Measurement of bladder pressure via the Foley catheter
Central venous pressure alone as the primary diagnosis
Abdominal girth measurement with a tape measure
stages.explanation: Intra-abdominal pressure is most commonly and reliably estimated by measuring bladder (intravesical) pressure through the Foley catheter using a standardized technique (supine, end-expiration, with ~25 mL instilled saline, zeroed at the midaxillary line). Intra-abdominal hypertension is defined as a sustained pressure >=12 mmHg, and abdominal compartment syndrome as a sustained pressure >20 mmHg WITH new organ dysfunction. Direct needle measurement (option A) is invasive and not standard. Central venous pressure (option C) is affected by many variables and is not a specific measure of intra-abdominal pressure. Abdominal girth (option D) is insensitive and unreliable for detecting clinically significant intra-abdominal hypertension. The bladder pressure, trended serially, both confirms the diagnosis and guides escalation of therapy.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Bladder (intravesical) pressure via the Foley is the standard bedside surrogate for intra-abdominal pressure.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The reliable, standardized bedside measure is bladder pressure through the Foley; girth and CVP are unreliable and direct needle measurement is not standard.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bladder (intravesical) pressure: 26 mmHg (sustained; up from 14 mmHg three hours earlier)
ABG: pH 7.22, PaCO2 48 mmHg, PaO2 64 mmHg on FiO2 0.7, HCO3 18 mEq/L
Lactate 4.6 mmol/L (rising)
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (up from 1.0)
Urine output: 8 mL/hr over the last 3 hours
Peak airway pressure: 42 cm H2O
Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL
Cardiac index: low; CVP elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His bladder pressure is 26 mmHg with worsening oliguria, acidosis, and ventilation despite medical measures (sedation, neuromuscular blockade, drainage of intraluminal/intra-abdominal fluid, and optimizing fluids). What is the definitive treatment?
options: Increase PEEP and continue current management as initial therapy
Start renal replacement therapy to correct the oliguria
Administer a large fluid bolus to improve urine output
Surgical laparotomy with temporary abdominal closure
stages.explanation: Once abdominal compartment syndrome causes organ dysfunction that fails medical management, the definitive treatment is emergent decompressive laparotomy, releasing the abdominal pressure and managing the abdomen open with a temporary closure (e.g., negative-pressure 'vac' dressing). Increasing PEEP (option A) does not address the underlying intra-abdominal hypertension and can further compromise venous return and cardiac output. Renal replacement therapy (option C) treats a consequence (renal failure) but not the cause; the kidneys are being compressed and decompression is what restores perfusion. A large fluid bolus (option D) is counterproductive: over-resuscitation is a major contributor to ACS and more fluid will worsen visceral edema and intra-abdominal pressure. Medical measures may temporize, but a sustained pressure >20 mmHg with new organ failure mandates surgical decompression.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. ACS refractory to medical measures requires emergent decompressive laparotomy with temporary abdominal closure.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Raising PEEP, more fluid, or dialysis treat symptoms or worsen the problem; the definitive fix for established ACS is surgical decompression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Immediately after decompressive laparotomy, the team should anticipate which physiologic phenomenon?
options: Sudden severe hypertension requiring urgent treatment as the leading concern
Reperfusion with possible hypotension, arrhythmia,
Spontaneous resolution without any hemodynamic change as the leading concern
Immediate complete normalization of renal function with no risks as the leading concern
stages.explanation: After decompression, the sudden release of intra-abdominal pressure restores venous return and reperfuses previously ischemic tissues, which can cause an ischemia-reperfusion response: transient hypotension, arrhythmias, and a washout of acidotic, hyperkalemic, and lactate-laden blood into the central circulation. The team should anticipate this, ensure adequate volume and vasopressor readiness, and monitor potassium and acid-base status closely. Severe hypertension (option A) is not the expected response; decompression typically drops, rather than raises, blood pressure acutely. Renal function may improve as perfusion is restored, but immediate complete normalization without risk (option C) is unrealistic. Decompression is not a no-change event (option D); it produces significant, anticipatable hemodynamic shifts. Recognizing reperfusion physiology prevents avoidable cardiac arrest in the moments after opening the abdomen.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Decompression triggers reperfusion: anticipate hypotension, arrhythmia, and washout of acidotic/hyperkalemic blood.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Releasing the pressure reperfuses ischemic viscera, causing a washout and hemodynamic instability, not hypertension or a risk-free normalization.

cases.diagnosis: Abdominal compartment syndrome after damage-control laparotomy
pearls: Suspect ACS when a tense abdomen, oliguria, and rising airway pressures follow massive resuscitation or damage-control surgery.
Bladder (intravesical) pressure is the standard bedside surrogate; ACS = sustained intra-abdominal pressure >20 mmHg WITH new organ dysfunction.
Over-resuscitation worsens ACS; avoid reflexive fluid boluses, and use medical measures (sedation, paralysis, drainage) as temporizing steps.
Definitive treatment of refractory ACS is decompressive laparotomy; anticipate reperfusion hypotension and hyperkalemia on opening.

cases.title: A Slow-Growing Lump in Front of the Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in front of and below the left ear that has slowly grown over a year.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a painless lump in front of and just below her left ear that she first noticed about a year ago and that has slowly enlarged. It is not painful and does not change with eating. She denies facial weakness, asymmetry, numbness, or difficulty closing her eye. She has no fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She takes no other medications and has no known drug allergies. She has never smoked, drinks wine occasionally, and works as a teacher. She has no history of head and neck radiation and no prior skin cancers. Family history is noncontributory. She has no difficulty swallowing, no hoarseness, and no ear pain or hearing change. She is concerned about whether the lump is cancerous and whether it needs to be removed. She reports the mass feels firm but movable and has remained well-defined. There is no overlying skin change and no recent rapid growth.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. There is a 2.5 cm firm, smooth, mobile, nontender mass in the left parotid region anterior and inferior to the ear, overlying the angle of the mandible. The overlying skin is normal without erythema, ulceration, or fixation. Facial nerve function is fully intact: she has symmetric forehead wrinkling, complete eye closure, symmetric smile, and normal cheek puff (House-Brackmann grade I). There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and no other neck masses. The oral cavity and oropharynx appear normal with no parapharyngeal bulge. There is no trismus. The remainder of the head and neck exam is unremarkable. There is no skin lesion on the ipsilateral scalp, ear, or face to suggest a primary cutaneous malignancy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A painless, firm, mobile mass in the parotid region with intact facial nerve function in a middle-aged woman is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Warthin tumor in this clinical context
Pleomorphic adenoma
Reactive lymphadenopathy
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
stages.explanation: A slowly growing, painless, firm, mobile parotid mass with normal facial nerve function in a middle-aged woman is the classic presentation of a pleomorphic adenoma, the most common salivary gland tumor and the most common parotid neoplasm overall. Warthin tumor (option A) is the second most common benign parotid tumor but classically occurs in older men who smoke and can be bilateral; it is less likely in this nonsmoking woman. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (option C) is the most common malignant salivary tumor, but features suggesting malignancy, such as pain, rapid growth, facial nerve weakness, fixation, or lymphadenopathy, are absent here. Reactive lymphadenopathy (option D) would typically be associated with an inflammatory or infectious source and would not present as a solitary, slowly enlarging firm parotid mass over a year. Painless, mobile, and nerve-sparing all favor a benign mixed tumor.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A painless, mobile parotid mass with intact facial nerve in a middle-aged woman is classic for pleomorphic adenoma.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The demographic and the painless, mobile, nerve-sparing mass point to pleomorphic adenoma rather than Warthin, carcinoma, or reactive nodes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test to characterize this parotid mass?
options: Incisional biopsy of the mass as the primary diagnosis
Fine-needle aspiration
Excisional biopsy by simple enucleation in clinic
PET-CT of the whole body as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA), often with ultrasound guidance and frequently complemented by MRI for deep or large lesions, is the appropriate initial diagnostic test for a parotid mass: it is safe, helps distinguish benign from malignant lesions, and guides operative planning without risking facial nerve injury or tumor seeding. Incisional biopsy (option A) is contraindicated because it risks tumor spillage/seeding and facial nerve injury. Excisional biopsy by simple enucleation (option C) is the wrong operation for a pleomorphic adenoma; enucleation leaves microscopic pseudopod extensions behind, causing high recurrence, which is why formal parotidectomy is required. Whole-body PET-CT (option D) is not an appropriate first step for a localized parotid mass and is reserved for staging known malignancy. FNA provides cytology to inform the surgical plan.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. FNA (with ultrasound/MRI as needed) safely characterizes a parotid mass without risking seeding or nerve injury.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Incisional or enucleation biopsies risk seeding/recurrence and nerve injury; FNA is the safe initial diagnostic test, with imaging as adjunct.

stages.label: FNA & IMAGING
stages.content:
Ultrasound: well-circumscribed, hypoechoic 2.5 cm mass in the superficial lobe of the left parotid gland with posterior acoustic enhancement; no suspicious cervical lymph nodes
Fine-needle aspiration cytology: benign-appearing epithelial and myoepithelial cells within a chondromyxoid (myxoid) stroma, consistent with pleomorphic adenoma; no atypia or malignant features
MRI (when obtained): well-defined mass confined to the superficial lobe, lateral to the plane of the facial nerve, without deep-lobe or parapharyngeal extension

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: FNA confirms a pleomorphic adenoma in the superficial lobe. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Observation with serial imaging only as initial therapy
Simple enucleation of the mass as initial therapy
Primary radiation therapy as initial therapy
Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve identification
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for a superficial-lobe pleomorphic adenoma is superficial (or formal partial) parotidectomy with meticulous identification and preservation of the facial nerve: removing the tumor with a cuff of normal parotid tissue avoids the high recurrence seen with lesser procedures. Observation alone (option A) is inappropriate because the tumor will continue to grow and carries a small but real long-term risk of malignant transformation (carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma). Simple enucleation (option C) is the classic mistake: it leaves microscopic pseudopod extensions, producing high recurrence rates and multinodular recurrent disease that is much harder to treat and more likely to injure the nerve. Primary radiation (option D) is not first-line for a resectable benign tumor and is generally reserved for unresectable or recurrent/malignant disease. Nerve identification and preservation is the central surgical principle.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation is the definitive treatment for a superficial-lobe pleomorphic adenoma.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Enucleation causes high recurrence and observation lets it grow/transform; the correct operation is parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several months after parotidectomy, she notices facial sweating and flushing over the cheek whenever she eats. What is this complication?
options: Salivary fistula
Recurrent tumor
Frey syndrome
Permanent facial nerve paralysis
stages.explanation: Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating) is a well-known late complication of parotidectomy caused by aberrant regeneration of severed parasympathetic (secretomotor) fibers that reinnervate the sweat glands and cutaneous vessels of the overlying skin, so eating triggers sweating and flushing over the cheek; it can be confirmed with the Minor starch-iodine test and treated with topical antiperspirants/anticholinergics or botulinum toxin. Permanent facial nerve paralysis (option A) would present as facial weakness, not gustatory sweating. A salivary fistula (option C) presents as a salivary leak/collection in the early postoperative period, not eating-triggered sweating months later. Recurrent tumor (option D) presents as a new mass, not gustatory sweating. The temporal pattern (sweating specifically with eating, months after surgery) is pathognomonic for Frey syndrome.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Eating-triggered sweating and flushing over the cheek after parotidectomy is Frey syndrome from aberrant nerve regeneration.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Gustatory sweating after parotidectomy is Frey syndrome; facial paralysis, fistula, and recurrence present differently.

cases.diagnosis: Pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland
pearls: Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common salivary/parotid tumor: a painless, firm, mobile mass with intact facial nerve function.
FNA (with ultrasound/MRI as needed) is the initial test; avoid incisional biopsy or enucleation due to seeding and recurrence risk.
Treat with superficial parotidectomy and facial nerve preservation; enucleation causes high recurrence and there is a small risk of malignant transformation.
Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating) is a classic late complication from aberrant parasympathetic reinnervation of sweat glands.

cases.title: A Soft Swelling on the Side of the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump on the left side of the neck that became more noticeable after a recent cold.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents with a painless lump on the left side of her neck that she has been aware of for several months but which became larger and more noticeable after a recent upper respiratory infection. It is not painful and she has had no fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. She denies trouble swallowing, voice change, or breathing difficulty. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes a combined oral contraceptive pill and no other medications, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. She has no history of head and neck radiation. Family history is noncontributory. She reports the lump fluctuated slightly in size with her recent illness and then partially regressed. She has no oral ulcers, no skin lesions, and no recent travel or sick contacts beyond her cold. She is otherwise healthy and very active. She is anxious about the possibility of cancer given her age and wants to know what the lump is and whether it needs to be removed.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman in no distress. There is a 3 cm soft, smooth, mobile, nontender, fluctuant mass in the left lateral neck located anterior to the upper third of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, just below the angle of the mandible. The overlying skin is normal without erythema, sinus tract, or drainage. There is no fixation to deep structures. The remainder of the neck exam reveals no other masses, no thyroid enlargement, and no firm or fixed lymphadenopathy. The oral cavity, oropharynx, and nasopharynx appear normal with no mucosal lesion or tonsillar asymmetry. Cranial nerves are intact and there is no facial weakness. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and no generalized lymphadenopathy. The lesion transilluminates poorly and feels cystic on palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A soft, fluctuant, mobile mass anterior to the upper sternocleidomastoid in a young adult, enlarging after an upper respiratory infection, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Carotid body tumor in this clinical context
Branchial cleft cyst
Thyroglossal duct cyst
Reactive lymph node only
stages.explanation: A second branchial cleft cyst is the classic cause of a soft, fluctuant, mobile lateral neck mass located along the anterior border of the upper-to-middle sternocleidomastoid in a young adult, and it characteristically enlarges or becomes inflamed after an upper respiratory infection. A thyroglossal duct cyst (option A) is a MIDLINE mass near the hyoid that elevates with tongue protrusion and swallowing, not a lateral mass anterior to the SCM. A carotid body tumor (option B, paraganglioma) is a firm, pulsatile mass at the carotid bifurcation that is mobile side-to-side but not vertically (Fontaine sign) and is not fluctuant/cystic. A simple reactive lymph node (option D) would typically be firmer, smaller, and resolve completely after the infection rather than persisting as a fluctuant cyst for months. The location and cystic quality strongly favor a branchial cleft cyst.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A soft, fluctuant, mobile mass anterior to the upper SCM that flares with URIs is classic for a branchial cleft cyst.
stages.explanationIncorrect: A lateral, cystic, anterior-to-SCM mass favors branchial cleft cyst; midline (thyroglossal), pulsatile (carotid body), and fully resolving (reactive node) differ.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Although this is most likely benign, why is it essential to exclude a cystic metastasis before treating it as a simple congenital cyst?
options: Because branchial cleft cysts are radiopaque as the primary diagnosis
Because cysts cannot be aspirated as the primary diagnosis
Because cystic metastasis from HPV-related oropharyngeal carcinoma can mimic a branchial cleft cyst, especially in patients over ~40
Because branchial cleft cysts always require chemotherapy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: It is essential to exclude a cystic metastasis because a cystic lateral neck mass, particularly in adults (especially over ~40), can represent a metastatic lymph node from HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (or papillary thyroid carcinoma) masquerading as a benign branchial cleft cyst; missing this delays cancer treatment. This is why evaluation includes a thorough head and neck exam, imaging, and FNA, with HPV/p16 testing of cyst fluid when malignancy is a concern, and a search for a primary tumor (including the tonsil and base of tongue). Branchial cleft cysts do not require chemotherapy (option B). They are not radiopaque (option C); they appear as well-defined cystic lesions on CT/ultrasound. Cysts certainly can be aspirated (option D), and FNA is part of the workup. Age-appropriate suspicion for malignancy prevents a dangerous misdiagnosis.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. A cystic neck node can be metastatic HPV-related oropharyngeal (or thyroid) cancer mimicking a branchial cleft cyst, especially in older adults.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The reason to exclude malignancy is that cystic metastasis (HPV-related oropharyngeal or papillary thyroid cancer) can imitate a benign branchial cleft cyst.

stages.label: FNA & IMAGING
stages.content:
Ultrasound: well-defined, anechoic cystic mass (3 cm) anterior to the upper sternocleidomastoid, lateral to the carotid sheath, with thin walls; no solid mural nodule; no suspicious lymph nodes
Contrast CT neck: cystic lesion at the mandibular angle along the anterior border of the SCM, displacing the SCM posteriorly and the carotid sheath medially; no enhancing solid component
Fine-needle aspiration: cyst fluid with benign squamous epithelial cells and proteinaceous debris; no malignant cells; p16/HPV testing negative
Thorough oropharyngeal exam (including tonsils and base of tongue): no primary lesion identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Workup confirms a benign branchial cleft cyst with no evidence of malignancy. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Repeated needle aspiration as definitive therapy
Radiation therapy as initial therapy
Complete surgical excision of the cyst
Long-term antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment of a branchial cleft cyst is complete surgical excision, removing the cyst together with any associated epithelial tract/sinus to prevent recurrence and recurrent infection; surgery is ideally performed when the cyst is not acutely inflamed. Repeated needle aspiration (option A) only temporarily decompresses the cyst, which reaccumulates, and is not definitive. Long-term antibiotics (option C) treat secondary infection but do not eliminate the congenital cyst, which will persist and can become infected again. Radiation (option D) has no role in a benign congenital cyst. If the cyst becomes acutely infected, it is first managed with antibiotics (and drainage if abscessed), with definitive excision performed after the inflammation resolves to reduce operative difficulty and the risk of incomplete removal and recurrence.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Complete surgical excision of the cyst and any associated tract is the definitive treatment.
stages.explanationIncorrect: Aspiration and antibiotics are temporizing; a congenital cyst is cured by complete surgical excision, including any tract.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most common complication if the cyst is incompletely excised or if surgery is performed during acute infection?
options: Recurrence of the cyst
Diabetes insipidus as the leading concern
Hypothyroidism as the leading concern
Hyperparathyroidism as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Incomplete excision, particularly when residual cyst wall or tract is left behind or when surgery is done during active inflammation (which distorts tissue planes), leads to recurrence of the cyst and persistent or recurrent infection, the most common complication of branchial cleft cyst surgery; this is why complete removal of the cyst and tract during a quiescent period is emphasized. Hypothyroidism (option B) relates to thyroid pathology, not a lateral branchial cleft cyst. Hyperparathyroidism (option C) involves the parathyroid glands and is unrelated. Diabetes insipidus (option D) is a pituitary/posterior hypothalamic disorder with no connection to neck cyst surgery. Care must also be taken to avoid injury to nearby structures (e.g., facial, hypoglossal, and spinal accessory nerves and the great vessels) during dissection, but recurrence from incomplete excision is the classic teaching point.
stages.explanationCorrect: Correct. Incomplete excision or operating during acute infection most commonly leads to recurrence and recurrent infection.
stages.explanationIncorrect: The classic complication is recurrence from residual cyst/tract; the endocrine options are unrelated to a lateral neck cyst.

cases.diagnosis: Branchial cleft cyst (second branchial cleft)
pearls: A second branchial cleft cyst is a soft, fluctuant, mobile mass anterior to the upper SCM that often flares with upper respiratory infections.
In adults (especially >40), exclude cystic metastasis from HPV-related oropharyngeal or papillary thyroid carcinoma before assuming a benign cyst.
FNA (with p16/HPV testing) and imaging are part of the workup; a thorough search for a mucosal primary is mandatory.
Definitive treatment is complete surgical excision of the cyst and tract during a non-inflamed period; incomplete excision causes recurrence.
